topics.title: Classification of Process Utility Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Process Utility Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Utility Systems** are the essential supporting services (auxiliary facilities) that provide energy, materials, and environmental conditions required for the smooth and continuous operation of a chemical, petrochemical, pharmaceutical, or manufacturing process plant. They form the **non-product, non-reactor** backbone of any process industry, ensuring that the main production unit operates within safe, efficient, and economically viable envelopes.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECHT611)** syllabus framework, process utilities are classified into functional categories based on the *type of service* they deliver to the process core, namely:

1. **Water and Steam Utilities** (Boiler feed water, cooling water, process steam).
2. **Power and Electrical Utilities** (Grid power, captive power, UPS, transformers).
3. **Air and Gas Utilities** (Compressed air, instrument air, nitrogen, oxygen).
4. **Refrigeration and Cryogenic Utilities** (Chilled water, brine systems, LNG).
5. **Fuel Utilities** (Natural gas, furnace oil, coal, biomass).
6. **Waste Treatment Utilities** (Effluent treatment, flue gas desulfurization).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Process Plant like a Human Body:**
> - **Main Reactor** = The Heart (pumps out the product / blood).
> - **Process Utilities** = The Lungs, Kidneys, Digestive System, and Nerves (they don't make the product but keep the body alive).
>
> Just as a hospital needs uninterrupted oxygen, power, water, and air-conditioning for the heart to keep pumping, a chemical plant needs continuous **steam, water, electricity, compressed air, and refrigeration** for the reactor to keep producing.

Without these utilities, the **main process unit (MPU)** cannot function. For instance:
- No **cooling water** → exothermic reactions overheat → thermal runaway.
- No **steam** → distillation columns lose reboiling duty → separation fails.
- No **instrument air** → pneumatic control valves fail → entire plant trips.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics

The following engineering benchmarks define the scale and economic weight of process utilities:

| Metric | Typical Industrial Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Utility Cost Share** | **30% – 40%** of total plant operating cost | Confirms that utilities are the *largest* controllable OPEX line item |
| **Steam Specific Enthalpy** | **~ 2,200 – 2,800 kJ/kg** (saturated/superheated) | Drives reboiler design and heat integration |
| **Power Plant Heat Rate** | **~ 2,000 – 2,500 kCal/kWh** | Benchmarks captive power efficiency |
| **Compressed Air Pressure** | **6 – 8 bar(g)** | Standard for pneumatic instrumentation |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** In KTU valuation, students often neglect to quantify the *economic weight* of utilities. Always mention the **30–40% operating cost** figure — it instantly demonstrates depth of understanding.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic – The "Why" Behind Each Utility

A chemical process typically involves three fundamental requirements:
1. **Heat Addition or Removal** → supplied by steam / cooling water / refrigeration.
2. **Mass Transfer & Reaction Control** → supported by compressed air / inert gases.
3. **Energy Conversion & Distribution** → delivered via electrical utilities and fuels.

Each process utility addresses one or more of these needs. Let us analyze each utility class:

#### 2.1.1 Water and Steam Utilities
- **Why needed?** Water is the universal heat carrier — it has the highest **specific heat capacity** among common liquids, **C_p = 4.186 kJ/kg·K**.
- **Sub-classes:**
  - *Demineralized (DM) Water* — for boiler feed to prevent scaling.
  - *Cooling Water* — for condensers and jacket cooling.
  - *Chilled Water* — for low-temperature heat removal.
  - *Process Steam* — for reboilers, strippers, and direct heating.

#### 2.1.2 Power and Electrical Utilities
- **Why needed?** Motors (pumps, compressors), lighting, instrumentation, control systems.
- **Sub-classes:**
  - *HT (High Tension)* and *LT (Low Tension)* power distribution.
  - *Captive Power Plants* (CPP) — for energy security.
  - *UPS / DG Backup* — for critical instruments.

#### 2.1.3 Air and Gas Utilities
- **Why needed?** Pneumatic actuation, oxidation reactions, inert blanketing, purging.
- **Sub-classes:**
  - *Instrument Air* (oil-free, dry, dew-point controlled).
  - *Plant Air* (general pneumatic tools).
  - *Nitrogen (N₂)* — inerting, blanketing, purging flammable tanks.
  - *Oxygen (O₂)* — for oxidation reactors, enrichment.

#### 2.1.4 Refrigeration Utilities
- **Why needed?** Sub-ambient cooling for crystallization, LNG storage, cold traps.
- **Sub-classes:** Vapor Compression, Vapor Absorption, Cascade systems.

#### 2.1.5 Fuel Utilities
- **Why needed?** Furnace firing, boiler firing, process gas heaters.
- **Sub-classes:** Solid (coal), Liquid (Furnace Oil/LSHS), Gaseous (Natural Gas).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles every governing formula you may need for utility classification calculations in the KTU ESE. Notice the use of `\vert` instead of the pipe character `|` to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept / Formula | Engineering Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer via water/steam | kW |
| 2 | $Q = m \cdot \lambda$ | Latent heat transfer via steam condensation | kW |
| 3 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{steam}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | Boiler thermal efficiency | dimensionless (%) |
| 4 | $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Maximum theoretical refrigeration COP limit | dimensionless |
| 5 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | Coefficient of Performance of refrigeration | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{n} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$ | Power for air/gas compression (ideal) | kW |
| 7 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{f \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot v^2}{2 \cdot D}$ | Pressure drop in utility pipelines | Pa |
| 8 | $S_{salts} = \vert TDS_{feed} - TDS_{limit} \vert$ | Demineralizer salt load | ppm |
| 9 | $\text{SFOC} = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{P_{output}}$ | Specific Fuel Oil Consumption | kg/kWh |
| 10 | $C_{utility} = \sum_{i} (m_i \cdot C_{p,i})$ | Total specific heat capacity cost index | kJ/K |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification of process utilities is the **foundational design step** in any Greenfield or Brownfield project. The hierarchy of importance is:
1. **Safety-critical utilities** (instrument air, fire water, nitrogen purging) → designed first.
2. **Process-critical utilities** (steam, cooling water, power) → sized via pinch analysis.
3. **Comfort/auxiliary utilities** (HVAC, lighting) → designed last.

This ranking drives the *reliability and redundancy philosophy* (e.g., **N+1** compressor sizing, **2 × 100%** pump configuration).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Analytical Matrix & Comparative Derivations

### 3.1 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Utility vs. Function

Since this topic is largely **descriptive-classificatory** rather than computational, the analytical depth is delivered through a *decision matrix* — a tool examiners frequently reward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stepwise Logic for Selecting a Utility in a Given Process Scenario:**
>
> **Step 1:** Identify the *primary physical requirement* (heating? cooling? agitation? inerting?).
> **Step 2:** Map the requirement onto the classification taxonomy (Section 2).
> **Step 3:** Apply *availability, cost, and safety* filters.
> **Step 4:** Decide between utility types (e.g., cooling water vs. chilled water).

| Decision Driver | Recommended Utility | KTU Justification Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| Heat input $T > 150^{\circ}C$ | High-pressure steam | High latent heat, easy distribution |
| Heat removal $5 < T < 80^{\circ}C$ | Cooling water (open/closed loop) | Economical, high $C_p$ |
| Heat removal $T < 5^{\circ}C$ | Refrigeration (chilled water/brine) | Sub-ambient cooling |
| Oxidation reaction required | Oxygen-enriched air | Controlled partial pressure |
| Flammable solvent storage | Nitrogen blanketing | Inerting reduces LEL risk |
| Reactor temperature $T = 250^{\circ}C$ | Thermic fluid (Dowtherm) | High-temp, low-pressure alternative |
| Direct-fired heating | Natural gas / Furnace Oil | High flame temperature |

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example – Selection of Cooling Medium

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):** A reactor releases $Q_{removal} = 500 \text{ kW}$ at $T_{process} = 80^{\circ}C$. Cooling water enters at $30^{\circ}C$ and leaves at $45^{\circ}C$. Compare cooling water vs. chilled water requirement.

> **Step 1: Define Known Variables**

$$\dot{Q}_{removal} = 500 \text{ kW}$$

$$\Delta T_{cw} = T_{out} - T_{in} = 45 - 30 = 15^{\circ}C$$

$$C_{p,water} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg}\cdot K$$

> **Step 2: Mass Flow Rate of Cooling Water**

The governing equation for sensible heat removal is:

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Rearranging for the mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{C_p \cdot \Delta T}$$

Substituting the numerical values (note the unit conversion: $1 \text{ kW} = 1 \text{ kJ/s}$):

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{500 \text{ kJ/s}}{4.186 \text{ kJ/kg}\cdot K \times 15 \text{ K}}$$

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{500}{62.79}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{cw} = 7.96 \text{ kg/s} \approx 28.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}}$$

> **Step 3: Engineering Interpretation**

This cooling water demand of approximately **28.6 m³/hr** is *perfectly feasible* using a standard cooling tower. Therefore, **cooling water is the optimal utility** — chilled water would be technically unnecessary and economically wasteful, as refrigeration cycles are 5–10× more energy-intensive per kW of cooling.

### 3.3 Analytical Check – Why Steam is the Preferred Heat-Transfer Medium

> **Step 1:** Latent heat of saturated steam at 5 bar(g) → $\lambda \approx 2,106 \text{ kJ/kg}$.
> **Step 2:** Compare with cooling water delivering 62.79 kJ/kg (per °C × 15°C) → much lower per unit mass.
> **Step 3:** Therefore, **steam is preferred for heating** because it transfers a large quantity of energy at *constant temperature* during condensation — providing uniform heat flux at the heat exchanger surface.

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{input}}{\lambda} = \dfrac{500}{2{,}106} = 0.237 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = 854 \text{ kg/hr}$$

This demonstrates that **much less steam mass** is needed compared to hot water — a foundational principle behind the dominance of steam as the *primary heating utility* in process industries.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid flowchart presents the complete hierarchical classification of process utility systems as per the KTU 2024 syllabus. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Process Utility Systems] --> rootB[Water and Steam Utilities]
    rootA --> rootC[Energy and Power Utilities]
    rootA --> rootD[Air and Gas Utilities]
    rootA --> rootE[Refrigeration Utilities]
    rootA --> rootF[Fuel Utilities]
    rootA --> rootG[Waste Treatment Utilities]

    rootB --> rootB1[Boiler Feed Water]
    rootB --> rootB2[Cooling Water]
    rootB --> rootB3[Process Steam]
    rootB --> rootB4[Demineralized Water]

    rootC --> rootC1[HT and LT Power]
    rootC --> rootC2[Captive Power Plant]
    rootC --> rootC3[UPS and DG Backup]
    rootC --> rootC4[Transformers]

    rootD --> rootD1[Instrument Air]
    rootD --> rootD2[Plant Air]
    rootD --> rootD3[Nitrogen Blanketing]
    rootD --> rootD4[Oxygen Supply]

    rootE --> rootE1[Chilled Water]
    rootE --> rootE2[Vapor Compression]
    rootE --> rootE3[Absorption Refrigeration]
    rootE --> rootE4[Cryogenic Systems]

    rootF --> rootF1[Solid Fuel Coal]
    rootF --> rootF2[Liquid Fuel Furnace Oil]
    rootF --> rootF3[Gaseous Fuel Natural Gas]
    rootF --> rootF4[Biomass]

    rootG --> rootG1[ETP Effluent Treatment]
    rootG --> rootG2[STP Sewage Treatment]
    rootG --> rootG3[Flue Gas Desulfurization]
    rootG --> rootG4[Solid Waste Handling]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This second diagram illustrates *how* each utility class interfaces with the **Main Process Unit (MPU)**, emphasizing the support-service nature of utility systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ext1[External Grid] --> mpuA[Main Process Unit]
    ext2[Steam Header] --> mpuA
    ext3[CW Header] --> mpuA
    ext4[IA Header] --> mpuA
    ext5[N2 Header] --> mpuA
    ext6[Refrigeration Header] --> mpuA
    ext7[Fuel Header] --> mpuA

    mpuA --> out1[Product Stream]
    mpuA --> out2[Byproduct]
    mpuA --> wt1[Waste Treatment Plant]

    wt1 --> env1[Safe Discharge]

    style mpuA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ext1 fill:#E0F7FA
    style ext2 fill:#E0F7FA
    style ext3 fill:#E0F7FA
    style ext4 fill:#E0F7FA
    style ext5 fill:#E0F7FA
    style ext6 fill:#E0F7FA
    style ext7 fill:#E0F7FA
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Every utility (steam, cooling water, instrument air, etc.) is *fed into* the Main Process Unit. The MPU converts these utility inputs into the product output, while waste streams are directed to the Waste Treatment Plant before safe environmental discharge.

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Utility Importance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hier1[Safety Critical Utilities] --> hier1a[Instrument Air]
    hier1 --> hier1b[Fire Water]
    hier1 --> hier1c[Nitrogen Purging]

    hier2[Process Critical Utilities] --> hier2a[Steam]
    hier2 --> hier2b[Cooling Water]
    hier2 --> hier2c[Electric Power]

    hier3[Comfort Utilities] --> hier3a[HVAC]
    hier3 --> hier3b[Lighting]

    style hier1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style hier2 fill:#FFE0B2
    style hier3 fill:#C8E6C9
```

> **Color Code:** Red → must not fail. Orange → operational necessity. Green → supportive.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*"Define process utilities and list any four major categories used in the chemical process industry."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Process utilities are the essential supporting services such as water, steam, electricity, compressed air, refrigeration, and fuels that are required for the smooth functioning of the main process unit but do not form part of the final product.
>
> **Four Major Categories (2 Marks):**
> 1. Water and Steam Utilities (cooling water, boiler feed water, process steam).
> 2. Power and Electrical Utilities (HT/LT distribution, captive power).
> 3. Air and Gas Utilities (instrument air, nitrogen, oxygen).
> 4. Refrigeration Utilities (chilled water, brine systems).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*"Why are utilities considered the largest controllable operating cost in a chemical plant?"*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Utilities typically contribute **30% – 40% of the total plant operating cost** (1 Mark). They are *controllable* because their demand is directly influenced by **heat integration, pinch analysis, energy recovery, insulation, and process optimization** (1 Mark). Inefficient utility generation (e.g., low boiler efficiency) or distribution losses (e.g., steam leaks) can be identified and minimized through audits, making utilities the primary target for **OPEX reduction** (1 Mark).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, subdivided into **part (a) for 7 marks** and **part (b) for 7 marks**.

---

#### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]*

**Q.A.** *(a)* Classify the process utility systems used in a typical chemical process industry. Discuss in detail the role and application of **steam and cooling water** utilities. *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* A heat exchanger must remove **$Q = 1{,}200 \text{ kW}$** of heat. Cooling water enters at $25^{\circ}C$ and leaves at $40^{\circ}C$. Determine the required cooling water flow rate. If chilled water ($C_p = 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $\Delta T = 5^{\circ}C$) is considered as an alternative, calculate the chilled water mass flow rate and comment on the economic feasibility. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Classification (3 Marks):**
Process utilities are classified into six broad categories:
1. **Water and Steam Utilities** (DM water, cooling water, process steam, condensate).
2. **Power and Electrical Utilities** (HT/LT, DG, UPS, captive power).
3. **Air and Gas Utilities** (instrument air, plant air, N₂, O₂).
4. **Refrigeration Utilities** (chilled water, vapor compression, absorption, cryogenic).
5. **Fuel Utilities** (coal, furnace oil, natural gas, biomass).
6. **Waste Treatment Utilities** (ETP, STP, FGD).

**Role and Application of Steam (2 Marks):**
Steam is the **primary heating medium** because of its high latent heat ($\lambda \approx 2{,}106 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at 5 bar(g)). It is used in:
- Distillation column reboilers.
- Evaporators and heaters.
- Direct steam injection for heating.
- Atomization in oil-fired burners.
- Turbine drives for large rotating equipment.

**Role and Application of Cooling Water (2 Marks):**
Cooling water is the **primary heat-removal medium** due to water's high specific heat capacity ($C_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$). Applications include:
- Condenser cooling in distillation columns.
- Reactor jacket cooling.
- Compressor inter-stage and after-cooler cooling.
- Cooler circuits in heat exchanger networks.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks:**

> **Step 1: Given Data**
>
> $\dot{Q} = 1{,}200 \text{ kW}$, $T_{in,cw} = 25^{\circ}C$, $T_{out,cw} = 40^{\circ}C$, $C_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.

> **Step 2: Cooling Water Mass Flow Rate (3 Marks)**
>
> $$\dot{m}_{cw} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{C_p \cdot \Delta T}$$
>
> $$\dot{m}_{cw} = \dfrac{1{,}200}{4.186 \times 15}$$
>
> $$\dot{m}_{cw} = \dfrac{1{,}200}{62.79} = 19.11 \text{ kg/s}$$
>
> $$\boxed{\dot{m}_{cw} \approx 68.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}}$$

> **Step 3: Chilled Water Mass Flow Rate (2 Marks)**
>
> $$\dot{m}_{chw} = \dfrac{1{,}200}{4.18 \times 5} = \dfrac{1{,}200}{20.9} = 57.4 \text{ kg/s}$$

> **Step 4: Economic Comment (2 Marks)**
>
> Since the cooling water outlet temperature ($40^{\circ}C$) is well above ambient and achievable via a simple cooling tower, **cooling water is the economically feasible choice**. Chilled water would require a **vapor compression refrigeration cycle** consuming 5–10× more electrical power per kW of cooling, making it unjustified unless sub-ambient temperatures are required.

---

#### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model — INTERNAL CHOICE]*

**Q.B.** *(a)* Explain the role of **instrument air** and **nitrogen** utilities. Differentiate between them with respect to their pressure, purity, dew-point, and application. *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* A boiler generates **$1{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$** of saturated steam at 7 bar(g). The feed water enters at $80^{\circ}C$ and the fuel calorific value is $42 \text{ MJ/kg}$. If the fuel consumption is **$60 \text{ kg/hr}$**, calculate the boiler thermal efficiency. Assume specific enthalpy of steam = $2{,}760 \text{ kJ/kg}$ and feed water = $335 \text{ kJ/kg}$. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Instrument Air (3 Marks):**
- **Definition:** Oil-free, dry, compressed air used to actuate **pneumatic control valves, dampers, and instruments**.
- **Pressure:** 6–8 bar(g).
- **Purity:** Free from oil aerosols and particulates; dew point $-40^{\circ}C$.
- **Application:** Final control element actuation, diaphragm motors.

**Nitrogen Utility (3 Marks):**
- **Definition:** Inert, dry gas (N₂) used for **blanketing, purging, and inerting** flammable or oxygen-sensitive materials.
- **Pressure:** 4–6 bar(g).
- **Purity:** 99.9% to 99.999% depending on application.
- **Application:** Tank blanketing, pipeline purging, catalyst transfer protection.

**Differentiation Table (1 Mark):**

| Property | Instrument Air | Nitrogen |
|---|---|---|
| Composition | 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ | 99.9%+ N₂ |
| Function | Actuation | Inerting |
| Reactivity | Supports combustion | Inert |
| Dew Point | $-40^{\circ}C$ | $-60^{\circ}C$ |

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks:**

> **Step 1: Energy Output (Steam Enthalpy) — 3 Marks**
>
> $$\dot{Q}_{output} = \dot{m}_{steam} \times (h_{steam} - h_{fw})$$
>
> $$\dot{Q}_{output} = \dfrac{1{,}000}{3{,}600} \times (2{,}760 - 335)$$
>
> $$\dot{Q}_{output} = 0.2778 \times 2{,}425 = 673.6 \text{ kW}$$

> **Step 2: Energy Input (Fuel Energy) — 2 Marks**
>
> $$\dot{Q}_{input} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times \text{CV}$$
>
> $$\dot{Q}_{input} = \dfrac{60}{3{,}600} \times 42{,}000 = 700 \text{ kW}$$

> **Step 3: Boiler Efficiency — 2 Marks**
>
> $$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{output}}{\dot{Q}_{input}} = \dfrac{673.6}{700}$$
>
> $$\boxed{\eta_{boiler} = 0.9624 = 96.24\%}$$

> **Comment:** A 96% efficiency is exceptionally high, indicating an **economizer + air-preheater** configuration. Typical industrial boilers range 80–92%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Most students forget to convert kg/hr → kg/s before applying the formula. Always show the conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing Q with m:** Writing $Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ without stating $Q$ is in kW and $m$ in kg/s is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Missing the Economic Justification:** In Q.A(b), merely calculating mass flow rates without concluding which utility is economically justified costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 4. **Ignoring Dew Point & Purity:** For Q.B(a), students who write "instrument air and nitrogen are the same" lose 100% marks — *pressure, dew point, and purity* must be differentiated explicitly.
> 5. **Boiler Efficiency Formula:** Writing $\eta = Q_{fuel}/Q_{steam}$ (inverted) is a guaranteed **zero-mark error**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Classification of Process Utility Systems**

- ✅ Process utilities are **non-product supporting services** that account for **30–40% of OPEX**.
- ✅ Six major classes: **Water/Steam, Power, Air/Gas, Refrigeration, Fuel, Waste Treatment**.
- ✅ **Water** has the highest $C_p$ (4.186 kJ/kg·K) among liquids → ideal coolant.
- ✅ **Steam** has high latent heat (~2,100 kJ/kg) → ideal heating medium.
- ✅ **Instrument air** = oil-free, dry ($T_{dew} = -40^{\circ}C$), 6–8 bar(g).
- ✅ **Nitrogen** = inert gas for blanketing, purging, catalyst protection.
- ✅ **Cooling water** is preferred above $5^{\circ}C$; **chilled water** is required only below ambient.
- ✅ **Hierarchy:** Safety-critical > Process-critical > Comfort utilities.
- ✅ Boiler efficiency formula: $\eta = Q_{steam}/Q_{fuel}$.
- ✅ Always convert kg/hr → kg/s in numerical problems.
- ✅ Mention **economic feasibility** in any utility selection answer.
- ✅ Use **Mermaid / block diagrams** to showcase the classification hierarchy.
- ✅ Remember: utilities are designed for **reliability** (N+1 / 2×100% redundancy).
- ✅ Pinch analysis directly governs utility sizing — *mention this in long answers for bonus marks*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Process Utilities in Chemical Industries and Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Importance of Process Utilities in Chemical Industries and Plants # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Process Utilities (KTU 2024 Definition):** *Process utilities* are the **ancillary services and energy streams** — such as **water, steam, electricity, compressed air, refrigeration, fuels, and inert gases** — that are *not part of the primary chemical transformation* but are **indispensable** for the safe, continuous, and economically viable operation of a chemical process plant. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, process utilities are treated as the **"lifeline systems"** of any chemical industry. Without them, even the most elegantly designed reactor is just an empty pressure vessel. The American Institute of Chemical Engineers (AIChE) broadly classifies utilities into three categories: | Utility Category | Examples | Typical Plant Role | |---|---|---| | **Direct Process Utilities** | Steam, cooling water, process water | Heat transfer medium, reactant/diluent | | **Indirect Process Utilities** | Compressed air, nitrogen, refrigeration | Purging, inerting, temperature control | | **Support Utilities** | Electricity, fuel gas, effluent treatment | Power for pumps/compressors, heating, environmental compliance | ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Human Body Analogy:** > Think of a chemical plant like the human body. The **reactor** is the **heart** (where the main action happens), the **pipes** are **blood vessels**, and the **process utilities** are the **lungs, kidneys, and digestive system**. You don't *see* digestion happening, but stop it for 30 minutes and the body collapses. Similarly: > - **Steam** = oxygen being pumped through arteries (energy carrier). > - **Cooling water** = sweat evaporating from skin (heat removal). > - **Compressed air** = the nervous system (signaling and actuation). > - **Nitrogen blanketing** = skin protecting muscles (inert barrier). > > Just as a hospital cannot run without electricity, oxygen, and chilled water, a chemical plant cannot run without its utility network. ## 1.3 Why "Importance" is the First Module Topic The KTU 2024 Scheme deliberately places this topic **first** in Module 1 because: 1. **Utilities typically account for 5% to 40%** of the *total manufacturing cost* of a chemical product. 2. A **single utility failure** (e.g., cooling water pump trip) can cause **plant shutdown within minutes**, leading to losses of **₹10 lakhs to ₹1 crore per hour** depending on plant size. 3. **Energy efficiency** in utilities directly determines whether a process is **profitable, sustainable, and KTU-NEP-2020-aligned** with green-chemistry objectives. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Exam Tag:** Questions on *"importance," "role," "types," and "cost contribution"* of utilities are **repeated almost every semester** as 3-mark or 14-mark questions in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following standard metrics are universally used in process utility design and will appear in KTU numerical problems: - **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C, 1 atm:** $h_{fg} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - **Specific heat of water:** $c_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ - **Typical utility cost contribution in chemical industry:** **10% – 30%** of total manufacturing cost. - **Plant availability target (modern chemical industry):** **> 95%** (i.e., utility uptime > 8740 hours/year). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Process Utilities (Structured Logic) A modern chemical plant depends on a tightly integrated web of six core utilities. Below is the operational logic explaining the **why** and **how** of each: ### Pillar 1 — Water (The Most Voluminous Utility) - **Why needed:** Largest single utility by *mass flow rate*. Used for cooling, heating (as boiler feed), and as a process solvent/reactant. - **How it works:** - **Cooling Water (CW):** Circulates through heat exchangers to absorb waste heat; rejects heat to atmosphere via cooling towers. - **Boiler Feed Water (BFW):** Highly demineralized, deaerated water fed to boilers to generate steam. - **Process Water (PW):** Treated water used directly as a reactant, solvent, or diluent in the process. - **Quality classification:** Raw → Softened → Demineralized (DM) → Deionized (DI). ### Pillar 2 — Steam (The Universal Heat Carrier) - **Why needed:** Provides **clean, controllable, latent heat** for distillation, evaporation, reactor heating, and turbine driving. - **How it works:** Water → Boiler → Saturated/Superheated Steam → Process users → Condensate return → Boiler. - **Pressure classifications used in KTU problems:** - **Low Pressure Steam (LPS):** 3–5 bar (process heating) - **Medium Pressure Steam (MPS):** 10–15 bar (driving turbines) - **High Pressure Steam (HPS):** 40–100 bar (power generation) ### Pillar 3 — Compressed Air & Instrument Air - **Why needed:** Powers pneumatic control valves, actuators, and provides oxidation air for reactions. - **How it works:** Atmospheric air → Multi-stage compressor → After-cooler → Dryer (desiccant/refrigerant) → Receiver → Distribution at **6–8 bar(g)**. - **Critical distinction:** - **Plant Air:** May contain oil, moisture — used for general purposes. - **Instrument Air:** **Oil-free, dry (–40 °C dew point)** — used for instrumentation. ### Pillar 4 — Electricity (The Power Backbone) - **Why needed:** Drives all rotating equipment (pumps, compressors, agitators, mills). - **How it works:** Captive power plant OR grid supply → Transformers → Motor Control Centers (MCC) → Individual motors. ### Pillar 5 — Refrigeration / Chilled Water (Low-Temperature Utility) - **Why needed:** For processes operating below ambient temperature (e.g., crystallization at 5 °C, LNG production at –162 °C). - **How it works:** Vapor Compression Cycle using refrigerants like **NH₃, R-134a, R-717** to produce chilled water at **5–10 °C** or brine at **–5 to –40 °C**. ### Pillar 6 — Inert Gases (Nitrogen, Argon, CO₂) - **Why needed:** **Safety** — to blanket flammable liquids, purge vessels before maintenance, and prevent oxidation. - **How it works:** Cryogenic air separation OR Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA) → On-site storage or pipeline. ## 2.2 The 3-Tier Importance Hierarchy For KTU exam answers, structure the importance of utilities in three tiers: | Tier | Importance Aspect | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---| | **Tier 1 — Economic** | Utilities = 10–30% of product cost | Energy integration can slash cost | | **Tier 2 — Operational** | Any utility failure → plant trip | Redundancy (N+1 design) mandatory | | **Tier 3 — Safety & Environmental** | Steam explosions, inerting, effluent | OSHA / Pollution Control Board norms | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following equations are the *core numerical tools* expected in KTU ESE for Module 1. Memorize these — they appear in nearly every utility-cost calculation.** $$ \text{Utility Cost \%} = \frac{C_{utility}}{C_{raw\,material} + C_{utility} + C_{labor} + C_{overhead}} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Heat Removed by Cooling Water} \quad Q_{cw} = \dot{m}_{cw} \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_{cw} $$ $$ \text{Steam Enthalpy (Energy Released)} \quad Q_{steam} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot \lambda_{steam} $$ $$ \text{Annual Utility Cost} \quad C_{annual} = \dot{m}_{utility} \cdot H_{plant} \cdot d_{operating} \cdot P_{unit} $$ $$ \text{Plant Availability} \quad A = \frac{H_{operating}}{H_{operating} + H_{downtime}} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Specific Energy Consumption} \quad SEC = \frac{\text{Total Energy Input (GJ)}}{\text{Total Product Output (tonnes)}} $$ ### Symbol & Unit Reference Table (No Vertical Pipes Used) | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | |---|---|---| | $\dot{m}_{cw}$ | Cooling water mass flow rate | kg/h | | $c_{p,w}$ | Specific heat of water | kJ/(kg·K) | | $\Delta T_{cw}$ | Temperature rise of cooling water | K | | $\lambda_{steam}$ | Latent heat of steam | kJ/kg | | $H_{plant}$ | Plant operating hours | h/year | | $d_{operating}$ | Operating days per year | days | | $P_{unit}$ | Unit price of utility | ₹/kg or ₹/kWh | | $A$ | Plant availability | % | | $SEC$ | Specific Energy Consumption | GJ/tonne | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the *same equation form* ($\dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$) recurs for cooling water, steam heating, and air heating. This is by design — energy balance is the **single most powerful tool** in utility engineering. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Utility Cost Contribution to Product Cost This derivation is **routinely asked** as a 14-mark numerical question in KTU ESE. We derive the expression for the *fractional contribution of utilities* to the total manufacturing cost. ### Step 1 — Define Total Manufacturing Cost $$ C_{total} = C_{raw} + C_{utility} + C_{labor} + C_{maintenance} + C_{overhead} $$ **Conversion logic:** Total cost is the sum of *all* cost buckets a chemical plant accountant tracks. Each term represents one category of expense. ### Step 2 — Define Utility Sub-Cost Vector $$ C_{utility} = C_{steam} + C_{cooling\,water} + C_{electricity} + C_{compressed\,air} + C_{refrigeration} + C_{fuel} $$ **Conversion logic:** Utilities are not a single line item — they are a *basket* of six cost streams. Each sub-cost must be quantified separately using flow rate × unit price. ### Step 3 — Express Each Sub-Cost in Mass/Energy Terms For steam: $$ C_{steam} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot H_{plant} \cdot P_{steam} $$ For electricity: $$ C_{electricity} = P_{electrical} \cdot H_{plant} \cdot P_{kWh} $$ For cooling water: $$ C_{cooling\,water} = \dot{m}_{cw} \cdot H_{plant} \cdot P_{cw} $$ **Conversion logic:** Each cost is *mass flow (or power) × time × unit price*. This is dimensionally a monetary quantity (₹/year). ### Step 4 — Form the Utility Cost Fraction $$ f_{utility} = \frac{C_{utility}}{C_{total}} $$ $$ f_{utility} = \frac{C_{utility}}{C_{raw} + C_{utility} + C_{labor} + C_{maintenance} + C_{overhead}} $$ ### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem:** A fertilizer plant produces **100 tonnes/day of urea**. Annual operating time = **330 days**. Utility data: > - Steam: $\dot{m} = 5{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$ at ₹2.5/kg > - Cooling water: $\dot{m} = 80{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$ at ₹0.05/kg > - Electricity: $P = 800 \text{ kW}$ at ₹7/kWh > - Raw material cost: ₹50 crore/year; Labor: ₹8 crore/year; Overhead: ₹12 crore/year > > **Calculate:** Annual utility cost and its % contribution. **Step A — Steam cost per year:** $$ C_{steam} = 5{,}000 \times 24 \times 330 \times 2.5 $$ $$ C_{steam} = 5{,}000 \times 7{,}920 \times 2.5 = 9.9 \times 10^{7} \text{ ₹} = \text{₹}9.90 \text{ crore} $$ **Step B — Cooling water cost per year:** $$ C_{cw} = 80{,}000 \times 7{,}920 \times 0.05 = 3.168 \times 10^{7} \text{ ₹} = \text{₹}3.17 \text{ crore} $$ **Step C — Electricity cost per year:** $$ C_{elec} = 800 \times 7{,}920 \times 7 = 4.4352 \times 10^{7} \text{ ₹} = \text{₹}4.44 \text{ crore} $$ **Step D — Total utility cost:** $$ C_{utility} = 9.90 + 3.17 + 4.44 = \text{₹}17.51 \text{ crore} $$ **Step E — Total manufacturing cost:** $$ C_{total} = 50 + 17.51 + 8 + 12 = \text{₹}87.51 \text{ crore} $$ **Step F — Utility cost fraction:** $$ f_{utility} = \frac{17.51}{87.51} \times 100 = 20.01 \% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result Interpretation:** Utilities contribute **~20%** of manufacturing cost — squarely in the typical **10–30% range** for chemical process industries. This validates why energy integration (pinch analysis) is a major KTU Module 4/5 topic. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Utility Criticality Ranking | Utility | Failure Impact | Recovery Time | Cost if Lost (₹/h) | Criticality Rank | |---|---|---|---|---| | Electricity (total) | Complete plant shutdown | 4–24 h | 5,00,000 | **1 (Highest)** | | Cooling water | Loss of heat removal | 5–30 min | 3,00,000 | **2** | | Steam (high P) | Loss of process heat & turbines | 30 min – 2 h | 2,50,000 | **3** | | Instrument air | Loss of valve control → unsafe | 1–10 min | 2,00,000 | **4** | | Chilled water | Loss of low-T control | 1–4 h | 1,00,000 | **5** | | Nitrogen blanketing | Fire/explosion risk | Hours–Days (safety review) | 50,000 + safety risk | **6** | **Reading the matrix:** *Criticality Rank 1 = most critical.* The rightmost column is the *expected KTU 14-mark answer ranking* for the question *"Which utility is most important and why?"* --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Process Utility Network of a Chemical Plant The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of the utility network. Each block represents a *system*, and arrows represent *energy or material flows*. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph INPUT["Raw Energy & Feed Inputs"] A1[Grid Power Supply] A2[Natural Gas / Fuel] A3[Raw Water Intake] A4[Atmospheric Air] end subgraph CONVERSION["Utility Generation Systems"] B1[Boiler House] B2[CW Cooling Tower] B3[Compressor House] B4[Captive Power Plant] B5[Refrigeration Unit] B6[DM Water Plant] B7[Air Separation Unit] end subgraph DIST["Distribution Network"] C1[Steam Header LPS] C2[Steam Header HPS] C3[CW Return Header] C4[CW Supply Header] C5[Instrument Air Header] C6[N2 Distribution Line] C7[Chilled Water Header] end subgraph CONSUM["Process Consumers"] D1[Reactor Jacket] D2[Distillation Column Reboiler] D3[Heat Exchanger Network] D4[Pumps and Agitators] D5[Pneumatic Control Valves] D6[Storage Tank Blanketing] end A2 --> B1 A1 --> B4 A4 --> B3 A4 --> B7 A3 --> B6 A3 --> B2 B1 --> C1 B1 --> C2 B2 --> C4 B3 --> C5 B7 --> C6 B6 --> B1 B4 --> D4 C1 --> D2 C2 --> D2 C4 --> D1 C4 --> D3 C5 --> D5 C6 --> D6 B5 --> C7 C7 --> D3 C3 --> B2 D1 --> C3 D2 --> C1 D3 --> C3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram (KTU Board Tip):** When asked *"Explain the utility network of a chemical plant,"* reproduce **this hierarchical structure** — *Inputs → Generation → Distribution → Consumption → Recycle.* Examiners reward answers that capture the **closed-loop** nature of utility systems (note the return line C3 feeding back to B2). ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Utility Cost Contribution Pie (Qualitative) The relative *typical* cost contribution of each utility in a chemical process plant: ```mermaid flowchart LR P1((Steam
35%)) --> Pie[Total Utility Cost 100%] P2((Electricity
30%)) --> Pie P3((Cooling Water
15%)) --> Pie P4((Fuel
10%)) --> Pie P5((Compressed Air
5%)) --> Pie P6((Refrigeration
3%)) --> Pie P7((Inert Gases
2%)) --> Pie ``` **Reading the pie:** In a typical KTU reference chemical plant (e.g., an ammonia or refinery unit), **steam + electricity together account for ~65%** of utility cost. This is why KTU's later modules focus heavily on *steam system optimization* and *pump/compressor efficiency*. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define process utilities. List any four major process utilities used in a chemical industry.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Process utilities are the supporting services like steam, water, electricity, and compressed air that are *not part of the main reaction* but are essential for smooth plant operation. > **Four Major Utilities (1.5 marks = 0.375 each):** > 1. **Steam** – for heating and as a motive fluid. > 2. **Cooling water** – for heat removal in exchangers. > 3. **Electricity** – for driving motors, pumps, and compressors. > 4. **Compressed/Instrument air** – for pneumatic control and agitation. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Tip:** If you list *more than 4*, you are safe even if one is wrong — KTU examiners follow a *best-of-best* rule for 3-mark definitions. --- ### **Q2. Why are utilities considered critical for the safe and economic operation of a chemical plant?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Utilities are critical because: > 1. **Economic (1 mark):** Utilities contribute **10–30%** of total product cost; any saving directly boosts profit margin. > 2. **Operational (1 mark):** Any utility failure (especially cooling water or instrument air) causes **immediate plant trip**, leading to production loss of ₹1–10 lakh/hour. > 3. **Safety & Environment (1 mark):** Utilities like **nitrogen** prevent explosions; **effluent treatment** utilities ensure Pollution Control Board compliance. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` > **(a)** Explain in detail the **various types of process utilities** used in a typical chemical process industry, with at least **two examples of usage** for each. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A chemical plant consumes **3000 kg/h of steam, 50,000 kg/h of cooling water, and 500 kW of electricity**. Unit prices are: steam ₹3/kg, cooling water ₹0.04/kg, electricity ₹8/kWh. The plant operates **300 days/year, 24 h/day**. Calculate the **annual utility cost** and the **cost per kg of steam equivalent**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q-A(a): The six major process utilities are: | Utility | Function | Two Examples of Usage | |---|---|---| | **Steam** | Heating & motive power | (i) Reboiler of distillation column, (ii) Turbine driver for compressor | | **Cooling water** | Heat removal | (i) Condenser overhead, (ii) Reactor jacket cooling | | **Electricity** | Drives rotating equipment | (i) Centrifugal pump, (ii) Air compressor motor | | **Compressed air** | Actuation & oxidation | (i) Pneumatic control valve actuator, (ii) Fluidized bed aeration | | **Refrigeration** | Sub-ambient cooling | (i) Crystallizer at 5 °C, (ii) Cold storage of intermediates | | **Inert gases (N₂)** | Safety blanketing | (i) Flammable liquid storage tanks, (ii) Reactor purging before startup | **Conclusion (closing line for full marks):** A modern chemical plant cannot operate without the **simultaneous availability** of all six utilities; loss of any one can cause plant trip. #### Model Solution to Q-A(b): **Given:** $\dot{m}_{steam} = 3000$ kg/h, $P_{steam} = ₹3/\text{kg}$ $\dot{m}_{cw} = 50{,}000$ kg/h, $P_{cw} = ₹0.04/\text{kg}$ $P_{elec} = 500$ kW, $P_{kWh} = ₹8/\text{kWh}$ $H = 300 \times 24 = 7200$ h/year **Step 1 — Steam cost:** $$ C_{steam} = 3000 \times 7200 \times 3 = 6.48 \times 10^{7} \text{ ₹} = \text{₹}6.48 \text{ crore} $$ **Step 2 — Cooling water cost:** $$ C_{cw} = 50{,}000 \times 7200 \times 0.04 = 1.44 \times 10^{7} \text{ ₹} = \text{₹}1.44 \text{ crore} $$ **Step 3 — Electricity cost:** $$ C_{elec} = 500 \times 7200 \times 8 = 2.88 \times 10^{7} \text{ ₹} = \text{₹}2.88 \text{ crore} $$ **Step 4 — Total annual utility cost:** $$ C_{total} = 6.48 + 1.44 + 2.88 = \text{₹}10.80 \text{ crore/year} $$ **Step 5 — Cost per kg of steam equivalent:** Total steam equivalent per year = $3000 \times 7200 = 2.16 \times 10^{7}$ kg $$ \text{Cost per kg steam equivalent} = \frac{10.80 \times 10^{7}}{2.16 \times 10^{7}} = ₹5.00/\text{kg} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall (Valuation Warning):** Students often forget to **convert days to hours** before multiplying. Writing "300 × 3 = ₹900" without the 24-hr conversion loses **3 marks**. Always show the units explicitly in *every* step. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` > **(a)** Discuss the **importance of process utilities** under the following heads: (i) Economic, (ii) Operational, and (iii) Safety & Environmental. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the **utility network of a typical chemical plant**, clearly identifying the generation, distribution, and consumption zones. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q-B(a): **(i) Economic Importance (2.5 marks):** - Utilities account for **10–30%** of the total manufacturing cost. - Energy savings of even 5–10% translate to **crore-level annual savings** in large plants. - Optimum utility selection (e.g., using waste-heat recovery steam) directly improves product margin. **(ii) Operational Importance (2.5 marks):** - Continuous, uninterrupted supply of utilities is **non-negotiable** for plant operation. - Plant availability targets of **> 95%** can only be achieved with **redundant utility systems** (e.g., N+1 pump design, dual boiler operation). - Utilities enable **process control** — without instrument air, control valves cannot modulate. **(iii) Safety & Environmental Importance (2 marks):** - **Steam explosions** from utility failures are major hazard sources → proper insulation and relief valves mandatory. - **Inert gas blanketing** prevents flammable atmospheres in storage tanks. - **Effluent treatment utilities** ensure compliance with **Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB)** norms, avoiding penalties and shutdowns. #### Model Solution to Q-B(b): (Refer to the **Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.1** of these notes.) The block diagram should clearly show **four functional zones**: 1. **Input Zone:** Grid power, fuel, raw water, air. 2. **Generation Zone:** Boiler, cooling tower, compressor, captive power plant, refrigeration unit, DM plant. 3. **Distribution Zone:** Steam headers (LPS/HPS), CW headers, instrument air header, N₂ line. 4. **Consumption Zone:** Reactors, exchangers, control valves, pumps, storage tanks. 5. **Recycle/Return Zone:** Condensate return, CW return header feeding back to cooling tower. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Trap:** Students frequently draw only **generation → consumption** and **miss the return/recycle loop**. KTU examiners award **1 mark** specifically for showing the *closed-loop* nature of utility systems. **Always include the condensate return arrow and CW return line.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Importance of Process Utilities:** - ✅ **Process utilities** = ancillaries that support the *main* chemical reaction. - ✅ **Six pillars:** Water, Steam, Air, Electricity, Refrigeration, Inert Gases. - ✅ **Water is the highest-volume utility**; **steam is the highest-energy utility**. - ✅ **Instrument air must be oil-free and dry** (–40 °C dew point) — distinct from plant air. - ✅ **Utility cost = 10–30%** of total manufacturing cost (remember the range!). - ✅ **Plant availability target** = **> 95%** → requires redundancy (N+1 design). - ✅ **Criticality ranking:** Electricity > Cooling water > Steam > Instrument air > Chilled water > N₂. - ✅ **Steam pressure grades:** LPS (3–5 bar), MPS (10–15 bar), HPS (40–100 bar). - ✅ **Latent heat of steam at 1 atm:** $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg — use this constant in *all* steam energy calculations. - ✅ **Always show:** Generation → Distribution → Consumption → **Return/Recycle** loop in block diagrams. - ✅ **Safety utilities:** N₂ blanketing, steam traps, PSVs, flare systems. - ✅ **NEP-2020 link:** Utility efficiency maps directly to **SDG 7 (Affordable & Clean Energy)** and **SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption)**. - ✅ **Most-marked KTU question pattern:** *"Discuss the importance of process utilities"* (14 marks) — answer must cover **all three tiers** (Economic + Operational + Safety). - ✅ **Most common calculation:** Annual utility cost = $\dot{m} \times H_{plant} \times P_{unit}$ — *memorize this one equation*. ---

topics.title: Water as a Utility in Process Industries: Sources of Water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Water as a Utility in Process Industries: Sources of Water

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Water as a Process Utility — Formal Definition

In chemical and process engineering, a **utility** refers to any ancillary service, resource, or commodity that supports the main production process but is not part of the final product. Common utilities include **steam, cooling water, electricity, compressed air, fuel, and refrigerants**.

**Water, in the context of process industries, is defined as the most universally consumed and versatile utility fluid** that is harnessed for a wide spectrum of functions — ranging from being a direct reaction medium to serving as a heating/cooling agent, a solvent, a cleaning fluid, and a feedstock for steam/utility generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OECHT611):**
> Water is the single largest volume raw material used in process industries. It is classified as a **primary utility** because nearly every plant — whether it is a petroleum refinery, fertilizer plant, pharmaceutical unit, pulp & paper mill, or food processing facility — depends on a continuous, reliable supply of water of prescribed quality.

### 1.1.1 Why Water Dominates as a Utility

Water is irreplaceable in process plants because of a unique combination of physical, chemical, and economic properties:

| Property | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| High specific heat (4.18 kJ/kg·K) | Excellent cooling medium |
| High latent heat of vaporization (2260 kJ/kg) | Efficient heat-transfer agent in steam generation |
| Non-toxic, non-flammable | Safe for direct product contact |
| Universally available | Low raw-material cost |
| Excellent solvent for polar substances | Reacts/dissolves a wide range of chemicals |
| Wide liquid range (0–100 °C) at atmospheric pressure | Operationally convenient |
| Chemically stable in most conditions | Compatible with most materials of construction |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Water is the Blood of a Process Plant"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
> Think of a process industry as a living human body. Just as **blood** circulates through every organ, supplying nutrients, carrying away waste, and regulating temperature, **water flows through every unit operation** of a plant — supplying heat or removing it, carrying reagents into reactors, washing products, and flushing impurities out. If the blood supply stops or becomes contaminated, the body collapses. Similarly, if the water utility fails or its quality drops, the entire plant shuts down. This is why process engineers often say: *"Water is to a chemical plant what blood is to a living organism."*

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics in Industrial Water Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industrial Water Benchmarks (must memorize for KTU):**
> - **Specific heat capacity of water (cₚ):** **4.18 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - **Latent heat of vaporization at 100 °C:** **2260 kJ/kg**
> - **Density at 4 °C:** **1000 kg/m³**
> - **Boiling point at 1 atm:** **100 °C**
> - **Freezing point at 1 atm:** **0 °C**
> - **Surface tension at 20 °C:** **72.8 mN/m**
> - **Universal solvent** — dissolves more substances than any other liquid
> - **Typical industrial water consumption:** Refineries — **0.5 to 4 m³ per tonne of crude processed**; Power plants — **~150,000 m³/day for a 1000 MW unit**

## 1.4 Classification of Industrial Water Uses

Water in a process plant is broadly classified into the following categories:

1. **Process Water** — Direct ingredient in the chemical reaction or formulation (e.g., dilution, dissolution, hydrolysis).
2. **Boiler Feed Water (BFW)** — High-purity water converted into steam for heating and power generation.
3. **Cooling Water** — Recirculated water used to remove process heat via cooling towers and heat exchangers.
4. **Demi (DM) / Deionized Water** — Ultra-pure water for pharmaceutical, electronics, and specialty chemical use.
5. **General Service Water** — Wash water, floor cleaning, fire-fighting, and sanitation.
6. **Effluent Treatment Water** — Treated wastewater reused for gardening, ash handling, or low-grade applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** In a typical petroleum refinery, **cooling water + boiler feed water together account for ~85–90 % of total water consumption**. This is a frequent Part-A (3-mark) question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

The study of water as a utility is approached in **three logical layers**:

### Layer 1 → Why water is needed (the demand side)
### Layer 2 → Where water comes from (the supply side — *the focus of this module*)
### Layer 3 → How water quality is matched to end-use (the treatment side)

This module concentrates on **Layer 2 — Sources of Water**.

## 2.2 Sources of Water — Engineering Classification

Water available to a process industry originates from one of the following natural or engineered sources:

### 2.2.1 Surface Water (Fresh)
Water collected from above-ground natural reservoirs.

**Sub-categories:**
- **Rivers and Streams** — Running water, continuously replenished by rainfall and snowmelt. Generally low in dissolved salts but often high in suspended solids (turbidity) and microbiological load.
- **Lakes and Ponds** — Standing water bodies. Tend to have higher dissolved mineral content than rivers and support algal growth (which can clog treatment systems).
- **Reservoirs and Dams** — Artificially impounded surface water used for municipal and industrial supply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality signature of surface water:** Low total dissolved solids (TDS), **high turbidity (10–500 NTU)**, **moderate to high microbial content**, pH typically **6.5–8.5**, color from humic substances.

### 2.2.2 Ground Water (Sub-surface)
Water stored in aquifers below the earth's surface, accessed via wells, boreholes, or natural springs.

**Sub-categories:**
- **Shallow Wells** — Tap water from the upper aquifer; moderate purity, may be affected by surface contamination.
- **Deep Wells / Borewells** — Tap deeper, well-filtered aquifers. Very low turbidity, **high TDS and hardness** due to prolonged contact with rock strata (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Fe²⁺ leaching).
- **Springs** — Natural outlets of ground water where the water table meets the surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality signature of ground water:** Very low turbidity (< 1 NTU), **high hardness (100–500 mg/L as CaCO₃)**, **high TDS (200–1000+ mg/L)**, low microbial count, often contains dissolved iron and manganese.

### 2.2.3 Sea Water (Brackish / Saline)
Water from oceans, seas, or coastal estuaries. Characterized by extremely high salinity (**TDS ≈ 35,000 mg/L**) and is generally unsuitable for direct industrial use. Used only in coastal plants after **desalination (RO, multi-effect evaporation)** or in applications that tolerate salinity (e.g., once-through cooling, fire-water in some designs).

### 2.2.4 Rain Water
Direct precipitation collected from rooftops or catchment systems. Very low TDS and is essentially soft, but is intermittent, low-volume, and increasingly used only for **make-up to cooling towers, gardening, and as an emergency/secondary source** in large plants.

### 2.2.5 Reclaimed / Recycled Water
Treated wastewater from the plant's own effluent treatment plant (ETP) that is recycled for non-critical uses such as ash quenching, gardening, toilet flushing, or cooling-tower make-up after tertiary treatment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently asked:** "Compare surface water and ground water as sources of industrial water." — Memorize the comparison table below.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Water Quality & Quantity Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are routinely tested in KTU university examinations for OECHT611.

| Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TDS (Total Dissolved Solids)** | TDS = sum of all ionic species in mg/L | mg/L or ppm | Equivalent to salinity in dilute waters |
| **Total Hardness (TH)** | TH = 2.5[Ca²⁺] + 4.12[Mg²⁺] | mg/L as CaCO₃ | Multiply Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ion concentrations by their respective equivalent-weight factors |
| **Equivalent weight of CaCO₃** | Eq. wt. = 100/2 = 50 | g/eq | Used as a reference for expressing hardness |
| **Conversion: mg/L ↔ ppm** | 1 mg/L = 1 ppm (for aqueous solutions of density ≈ 1) | mg/L or ppm | Valid for dilute aqueous systems |
| **Water requirement (Q)** | Q = m · cₚ · ΔT / (ρ · cₚ · ΔT) per cooling duty | m³/h | Used in cooling-tower sizing |
| **Cooling water flow (mₙ)** | mₙ = Q_heat / (cₚ · ΔT) | kg/h | Derived from Q = m·cₚ·ΔT |
| **Concentration Cycle (Cycles of Concentration, N)** | N = C_makeup / C_blowdown | dimensionless | Key for cooling-tower water economy |
| **Evaporation loss (E)** | E = 0.00085 · ΔT · mₙ | kg/h | Empirical rule of thumb in cooling towers |
| **Drift loss (D)** | D ≈ 0.005–0.01 % of mₙ | kg/h | Mechanical carry-over from cooling tower |
| **Blowdown (B)** | B = E / (N − 1) − D | kg/h | Mass balance around cooling tower |
| **Make-up water (M)** | M = E + B + D | kg/h | Total water input to cooling system |
| **Hardness in °Clarke** | 1 °Clarke = 14.25 mg/L as CaCO₃ | — | British unit, occasionally asked |
| **Hardness in °French** | 1 °French = 10 mg/L as CaCO₃ | — | European unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Caution for KTU exam:** In the formula table above, do not confuse **Total Hardness (TH)** with **Temporary Hardness** (carbonate hardness, removable by boiling) and **Permanent Hardness** (non-carbonate hardness, removable only by chemical treatment). The relation **TH = Temporary + Permanent** is a frequently tested concept.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of This Framework

- **Plant siting decisions** — A plant built near a river will need extensive filtration; a plant on a coastal site must invest in desalination.
- **Operating cost optimization** — Choosing ground water (free, but high hardness) vs. surface water (cheaper to treat) directly affects the chemical dosing cost in the pre-treatment plant.
- **Regulatory compliance** — Effluent discharge norms (set by CPCB / State Pollution Control Boards) depend on the source water characteristics.
- **Sustainability metrics** — Modern KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **water conservation**; the *Cycles of Concentration* and *Make-up water* formulas are central to Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) plant design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hardness Conversion from Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ Ion Concentrations to "mg/L as CaCO₃"

The hardness of water is universally expressed as an **equivalent concentration of calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)**, even when the actual hardness is caused by other ions. The derivation is a classic KTU problem (often worth 3–7 marks).

### 3.1.1 General Principle

The equivalent weight of a salt is:

$$E = \frac{\text{Molecular weight of the salt}}{\text{Valency factor} \cdot n}$$

where **n** is the number of positive (or negative) charges per molecule.

For a given ion concentration in mg/L, the equivalent concentration as CaCO₃ is:

$$\text{Concentration as CaCO}_3 = \text{Concentration of ion} \times \frac{\text{Equivalent weight of CaCO}_3}{\text{Equivalent weight of the ion}}$$

### 3.1.2 Equivalent Weight of CaCO₃ (Reference Compound)

$$E_{\text{CaCO}_3} = \frac{40 + 12 + 3 \times 16}{2} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ g/eq}$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation for Ca²⁺ (Calcium Ion)

- Molecular weight of Ca²⁺ = **40 g/mol**
- Valency of Ca²⁺ = **2**
- Equivalent weight of Ca²⁺ = 40/2 = **20 g/eq**

Substituting into the general formula:

$$\text{Ca}^{2+} \text{ as CaCO}_3 = [\text{Ca}^{2+}] \times \frac{50}{20} = 2.5 \cdot [\text{Ca}^{2+}]$$

### 3.1.4 Derivation for Mg²⁺ (Magnesium Ion)

- Molecular weight of Mg²⁺ = **24 g/mol**
- Valency of Mg²⁺ = **2**
- Equivalent weight of Mg²⁺ = 24/2 = **12 g/eq**

Substituting into the general formula:

$$\text{Mg}^{2+} \text{ as CaCO}_3 = [\text{Mg}^{2+}] \times \frac{50}{12} = 4.12 \cdot [\text{Mg}^{2+}]$$

### 3.1.5 Combined Total Hardness Expression

$$\text{TH} = 2.5 \cdot [\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.12 \cdot [\text{Mg}^{2+}] \quad \text{(mg/L as CaCO}_3\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory trick for KTU:** Remember the constants **2.5** (Ca) and **4.12** (Mg). These two numbers alone will solve ~70 % of the hardness problems in your exam.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Total Hardness Calculation (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> **Problem:** A water sample contains the following dissolved ions:
> - Ca²⁺ = 60 mg/L
> - Mg²⁺ = 24 mg/L
> - HCO₃⁻ = 150 mg/L
>
> Calculate the **Total Hardness (TH)**, **Temporary Hardness (carbonate)**, and **Permanent Hardness (non-carbonate)** of the water in **mg/L as CaCO₃**. Comment on the suitability of this water for industrial use.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Calculate Ca²⁺ Hardness as CaCO₃

$$[\text{Ca}^{2+}] \text{ as CaCO}_3 = 60 \times \frac{50}{20} = 60 \times 2.5 = 150 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Calculate Mg²⁺ Hardness as CaCO₃

$$[\text{Mg}^{2+}] \text{ as CaCO}_3 = 24 \times \frac{50}{12} = 24 \times 4.12 = 98.88 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Total Hardness (TH)

$$\text{TH} = 150 + 98.88 = 248.88 \approx 249 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Temporary (Carbonate) Hardness

Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) is the principal source of carbonate hardness. The temporary hardness is limited by the **lower** of the two values: (a) Total Hardness, or (b) Alkalinity expressed as CaCO₃.

First, convert HCO₃⁻ to CaCO₃ equivalent:

$$[\text{HCO}_3^-] \text{ as CaCO}_3 = 150 \times \frac{50}{61} = 150 \times 0.82 = 123 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

Since **Alkalinity (123 mg/L) < Total Hardness (249 mg/L)**, the temporary hardness equals the alkalinity:

$$\text{Temporary Hardness} = 123 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Permanent (Non-Carbonate) Hardness

$$\text{Permanent Hardness} = \text{TH} - \text{Temporary} = 249 - 123 = 126 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

### 3.2.6 Step 6 — Industrial Suitability Comment

> [!NOTE]
> With a Total Hardness of **249 mg/L as CaCO₃**, this water is classified as **"hard to very hard"** (the U.S. Geological Survey scale: > 180 mg/L is "very hard"). It is **unsuitable for direct boiler feed**, **unacceptable for high-pressure cooling circuits** without softening, and **must be treated** (lime-soda, ion exchange, or RO) before use in process industries.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Water Sources

| Parameter | Rain Water | Surface Water (River) | Ground Water | Sea Water | Reclaimed Water |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| TDS (mg/L) | 5–20 | 50–500 | 200–1000+ | 35,000 | 500–2000 |
| Turbidity (NTU) | < 1 | 10–500 | < 1 | 5–50 | < 5 (post-tertiary) |
| Total Hardness (mg/L as CaCO₃) | 0–10 | 30–150 | 100–500 | 6000+ | 50–200 |
| Microbial count | Very low | High | Low | Very high | Variable |
| Color | Colorless | Yellowish-brown | Colorless | — | Variable |
| Major ions | Trace | Ca²⁺, HCO₃⁻, SiO₂ | Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, HCO₃⁻, Fe²⁺ | Na⁺, Cl⁻ | Mixed |
| pH | 5.5–6.5 | 6.5–8.5 | 7.0–8.5 | 7.5–8.4 | 6.5–8.0 |
| Typical industrial use | Make-up, gardening | Raw supply, cooling (with treatment) | Source water for many plants | Desalinated for BFW; once-through cooling | Cooling-tower make-up, gardening |
| Major treatment required | Disinfection, filtration | Coagulation, filtration, softening | Softening, iron removal, disinfection | Desalination (RO/MSF) | Tertiary treatment + disinfection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison matrix is **examination gold**. KTU has asked this as a full 14-mark question (with sub-parts) at least twice in recent years.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Classification of Industrial Water Sources

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Industrial Water Sources] --> pathB[Natural Sources]
    startA --> pathC[Engineered Sources]

    pathB --> pathD[Surface Water]
    pathB --> pathE[Ground Water]
    pathB --> pathF[Sea Water]
    pathB --> pathG[Rain Water]

    pathD --> nodeD1[Rivers and Streams]
    pathD --> nodeD2[Lakes and Ponds]
    pathD --> nodeD3[Reservoirs and Dams]

    pathE --> nodeE1[Shallow Wells]
    pathE --> nodeE2[Deep Wells and Borewells]
    pathE --> nodeE3[Natural Springs]

    pathF --> nodeF1[Open Sea]
    pathF --> nodeF2[Coastal Estuaries]
    pathF --> nodeF3[Brackish Water]

    pathG --> nodeG1[Roof Top Harvesting]
    pathG --> nodeG2[Catchment Basin Collection]

    pathC --> pathH[Reclaimed Recycled Water]
    pathC --> pathI[Treated Effluent]
    pathC --> pathJ[Purchased Municipal Supply]

    pathH --> nodeH1[Plant ETP Output]
    pathH --> nodeH2[Tertiary Treated for Reuse]

    pathI --> nodeI1[Neutralized Wastewater]
    pathI --> nodeI2[RO Reject Recovery]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every industrial water source can be traced to either a **Natural Source** (rain, surface, ground, sea) or an **Engineered Source** (recycled, municipal). The right-hand column indicates the typical sub-categories used in plant design.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Typical Water Flow in a Process Industry

```mermaid
graph LR
    src1[Source: River or Borewell] --> intakeA[Intake and Screening]
    intakeA --> clarifA[Clarification and Sedimentation]
    clarifA --> filtA[Filtration]
    filtA --> softA[Softening or DM Plant]
    softA --> splitA{Distribution Header}

    splitA --> coolA[Cooling Tower Make-up]
    splitA --> boilA[Boiler Feed Water]
    splitA --> procA[Process Water Tank]
    splitA --> servA[General Service Water]

    coolA --> coolUseA[Cooling Water Users]
    coolUseA --> coolRetA[Return to Cooling Tower]
    coolRetA --> coolA

    boilA --> boilUseA[Steam Generation]
    boilUseA --> boilCondA[Condensate Return]
    boilCondA --> softA

    procA --> procUseA[Process Reactors and Equipment]
    procUseA --> effA[Effluent Stream]
    effA --> etpA[Effluent Treatment Plant]
    etpA --> reuseA[Reclaimed Water for Reuse]
    reuseA --> splitA
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the **canonical block-level flow** of water through a process plant. Raw water is drawn from the **source**, conditioned through **pre-treatment units** (clarifier, filter, softener), split at a **distribution header** to its various **end-uses** (cooling, boiler, process, service), and the **spent water** is routed to the **ETP** for treatment and possible **recycle** back to the header — closing the water economy loop.

---

## 4.3 Concept Map — Quality Variation Across Sources

```mermaid
graph TD
    qualStart[Water Quality Gradient] --> qual1[Rain Water: Softest]
    qual1 --> qual2[Surface Water: Soft to Moderate]
    qual2 --> qual3[Ground Water: Hard to Very Hard]
    qual3 --> qual4[Sea Water: Extremely Saline]

    qualStart -.-> qualTrend[Quality Decreases: TDS, Hardness, Salinity Increase]
    qualTrend -.-> qual1
    qualTrend -.-> qual2
    qualTrend -.-> qual3
    qualTrend -.-> qual4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** As we move from rain → surface → ground → sea water, both **TDS and total hardness rise monotonically**, while **cost of treatment also rises**. This trade-off dictates source selection in plant design.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**"Why is water considered the most important utility in process industries? List any four major industrial uses of water."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Water is the most important utility in process industries because it is **universally available, cheap, non-toxic, non-flammable**, and possesses an exceptional combination of physical properties such as **high specific heat (4.18 kJ/kg·K)** and **high latent heat of vaporization (2260 kJ/kg)**.

Four major industrial uses of water:

1. **Cooling medium** — to remove process heat in heat exchangers and condensers.
2. **Boiler feed water** — for generating steam used in heating and power.
3. **Process water** — as a direct reaction medium, solvent, or diluent.
4. **General service water** — for washing, sanitation, and fire-fighting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only one or two uses and lose marks. Mention **at least four distinct uses** with a one-line justification each.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**"Differentiate between surface water and ground water as sources of industrial water supply."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — concise comparison):**

| Aspect | Surface Water | Ground Water |
|---|---|---|
| Source location | Rivers, lakes, reservoirs | Wells, borewells, springs |
| Turbidity | High (10–500 NTU) | Negligible (< 1 NTU) |
| Total Dissolved Solids | Low to moderate | High |
| Hardness | Low to moderate | High (often > 200 mg/L as CaCO₃) |
| Microbial content | High | Low (natural filtration) |
| Pre-treatment cost | High (clarification needed) | Lower for filtration, but softening needed |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write vague statements like *"surface water is dirtier."* The KTU examiner expects **quantitative** statements — quote **TDS, turbidity, or hardness ranges**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**"Discuss in detail the various sources of water used in process industries. Compare their typical quality characteristics (TDS, hardness, turbidity) and state the major treatment required for each."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Detailed Description of Sources

**Model Answer:**

The various sources of water used in process industries are classified into **natural** and **engineered** sources.

**1. Surface Water (Rivers, Lakes, Reservoirs):**
Surface water is the most common source for large plants situated near rivers or lakes. It is **continuously replenished** by rainfall and snowmelt. However, it carries **suspended solids, organic matter, and microbial contamination**. Lakes and reservoirs tend to support **algal blooms**, which can produce taste, odor, and toxicity issues.

**2. Ground Water (Wells, Borewells, Springs):**
Ground water has been **naturally filtered** through porous rock strata, giving it **low turbidity and low microbial count**. It is rich in **dissolved minerals** (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Fe²⁺, HCO₃⁻) due to prolonged rock–water contact, making it **hard and sometimes high in iron/manganese**. It is widely used where surface water is scarce or unreliable.

**3. Sea Water:**
Used only in **coastal plants** for applications that tolerate high salinity (e.g., once-through cooling in some designs). It is **completely unsuitable** for boilers, cooling-tower make-up, or process use without **desalination** (RO, multi-stage flash, multi-effect distillation).

**4. Rain Water:**
Intermittent and low-volume. It is the **softest** natural water but is unreliable for continuous industrial use. Often used as **supplementary make-up** to cooling towers or for gardening in ZLD plants.

**5. Reclaimed / Recycled Water:**
Treated wastewater from the plant's own **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)**. After **tertiary treatment** (filtration, RO, disinfection), it is reused for **cooling-tower make-up, ash handling, and gardening**. This is the cornerstone of **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** plant design — a key 2024-scheme sustainability theme.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Quality Comparison and Treatment Requirements

**Model Answer:**

| Source | TDS (mg/L) | Hardness (mg/L as CaCO₃) | Turbidity (NTU) | Major Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rain water | 5–20 | 0–10 | < 1 | Disinfection, fine filtration |
| Surface water | 50–500 | 30–150 | 10–500 | Coagulation, sedimentation, filtration, disinfection |
| Ground water | 200–1000+ | 100–500 | < 1 | Softening, iron removal, disinfection |
| Sea water | 35,000 | 6000+ | 5–50 | Desalination (RO, MSF, MED) |
| Reclaimed water | 500–2000 | 50–200 | < 5 | Tertiary treatment (RO + UV) |

**Concluding remark:** Source selection depends on **availability, quality, treatment cost, and regulatory norms**. Modern process industries aim for a **hybrid supply** combining surface/ground water with **recycled streams** to minimize fresh-water draw and achieve ZLD compliance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only descriptive paragraphs without a **comparison table**. KTU examiners award up to **2–3 marks** specifically for the **tabular comparison** of TDS/hardness/turbidity. Skipping the table = guaranteed mark loss.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**"A water sample has the following analysis: Ca²⁺ = 80 mg/L, Mg²⁺ = 36 mg/L, HCO₃⁻ = 122 mg/L. Calculate the Total Hardness, Temporary Hardness, and Permanent Hardness of the water. Comment on its industrial suitability."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Step-by-Step Hardness Calculation

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calcium hardness as CaCO₃:**

$$[\text{Ca}^{2+}] \text{ as CaCO}_3 = 80 \times \frac{50}{20} = 80 \times 2.5 = 200 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 2 — Magnesium hardness as CaCO₃:**

$$[\text{Mg}^{2+}] \text{ as CaCO}_3 = 36 \times \frac{50}{12} = 36 \times 4.12 = 148.3 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 3 — Total Hardness:**

$$\text{TH} = 200 + 148.3 = 348.3 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 4 — Bicarbonate alkalinity as CaCO₃:**

$$[\text{HCO}_3^-] \text{ as CaCO}_3 = 122 \times \frac{50}{61} = 122 \times 0.82 = 100 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 5 — Temporary Hardness (the lesser of TH and alkalinity):**

Since Alkalinity (100) < TH (348.3):

$$\text{Temporary Hardness} = 100 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 6 — Permanent Hardness:**

$$\text{Permanent Hardness} = \text{TH} - \text{Temporary} = 348.3 - 100 = 248.3 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Industrial Suitability Discussion

**Model Answer:**

| Hardness Range (mg/L as CaCO₃) | Classification | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|
| 0–60 | Soft | Suitable for most uses |
| 61–120 | Moderately hard | Needs softening for boilers |
| 121–180 | Hard | Unsuitable for high-pressure boilers |
| > 180 | Very hard | **Requires mandatory treatment** |

With **TH = 348.3 mg/L as CaCO₃**, the sample is classified as **"very hard."** It is:
- **Unsuitable for high-pressure boilers** — will cause scale formation on tubes, leading to tube failure.
- **Unsuitable for cooling-tower make-up** without softening — will cause CaCO₃ scaling in heat exchangers.
- **Acceptable for general service and washing** after basic filtration.
- **Recommended treatment:** Lime–soda softening, ion exchange, or reverse osmosis (RO) before industrial use.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** The most common mistake in this type of problem is the **molecular weight of HCO₃⁻** — students frequently take it as **61, but some incorrectly use 60 or 53**. The correct value is **Molecular weight of HCO₃⁻ = 1 + 12 + 48 = 61 g/mol**. Use **61**, not 60, or you will lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Water is the largest-volume utility** in almost every process industry; cooling + boiler feed = ~85–90 % of total water use.
- **Key physical properties to memorize:** cₚ = **4.18 kJ/kg·K**, latent heat = **2260 kJ/kg**, density = **1000 kg/m³**.
- **Sources:** Surface (rivers, lakes), Ground (wells, springs), Sea, Rain, Reclaimed. Each has a **distinct quality signature**.
- **Surface water:** High turbidity, low TDS, high microbial count.
- **Ground water:** Low turbidity, **high TDS and high hardness**, low microbial count.
- **Sea water:** TDS ≈ 35,000 mg/L; requires **desalination** for industrial use.
- **Rain water:** Softest natural water; unreliable for continuous supply.
- **Reclaimed water:** Tertiary-treated ETP output; key to **ZLD** plants.
- **Hardness constants:** Ca²⁺ → **2.5**, Mg²⁺ → **4.12** (multipliers to express as CaCO₃).
- **TH = Temporary (Carbonate) + Permanent (Non-carbonate) Hardness.**
- **Temporary hardness** = lesser of (Total Hardness, Bicarbonate Alkalinity as CaCO₃).
- **HCO₃⁻ molecular weight = 61 g/mol**; CaCO₃ equivalent weight = **50 g/eq**.
- **Hardness > 180 mg/L as CaCO₃** = "very hard" → **mandatory treatment** for industrial use.
- **Cooling tower formulas:** E = 0.00085·ΔT·m; B = E/(N−1) − D; M = E + B + D.
- **KTU 2024 focus:** Sustainability, water conservation, recycled water, ZLD — expect a question on **reclaimed water** in every model paper.
- **Common exam trap:** Confusing **mg/L with ppm** in dilute aqueous systems (they are equal — but be ready to state the assumption of density = 1).
- **Diagram tip:** Always draw the **block flow diagram** of water in a process plant when asked to "discuss sources" — it scores easy full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Advice:** A 14-mark question on this topic will almost always combine a **descriptive part** (sources, classification) with a **numerical part** (hardness calculation). Practice both styles — and you will comfortably secure 12+ out of 14.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hard and Soft Water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hard and Soft Water

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Hard and Soft Water — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardness of Water** is defined as the characteristic property of water that prevents the lathering of soap due to the presence of dissolved **bivalent metallic cations** (predominantly Calcium and Magnesium) along with their associated anions.

Mathematically expressed as the **sum of concentrations of all multivalent (mainly divalent) cations** present in water, expressed in terms of equivalent calcium carbonate ($\text{CaCO}_3$).

$$H_{total} = [\text{Ca}^{2+}] + [\text{Mg}^{2+}] + [\text{Fe}^{2+}] + [\text{Mn}^{2+}] + [\text{Sr}^{2+}] + \cdots \quad \text{(all as CaCO}_3\text{ equivalents)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In industrial water chemistry, hardness is conventionally reported as **equivalent $\text{CaCO}_3$**, even when the actual hardness is caused by other ions. This standardization allows uniform comparison across water samples from different geological origins.

**Soft Water** is water that is free from dissolved salts of Calcium and Magnesium, and produces a rich lather with soap immediately upon contact.

**Threshold of Hardness Classification (as per KTU/IS Standards):**

| Classification | Hardness as $\text{CaCO}_3$ (mg/L) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Soft Water | 0 – 60 |
| Moderately Hard | 61 – 120 |
| Hard Water | 121 – 180 |
| Very Hard Water | > 180 |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making Sense of the Chemistry

Imagine washing your hands in a bathtub:

- **Soft water** behaves like a freshly opened bar of soap in a clean sink — it dissolves instantly, producing a thick, creamy lather with just a few rubs. The surfactants (soap molecules) are free to do their job of trapping dirt.

- **Hard water** is like trying to lather that same soap in a sink that already has a thin film of oily grime coating it. The soap molecules, instead of binding with dirt, rush to grab the dissolved $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ ions floating in the water, forming an insoluble **scum** (calcium/magnesium stearate). Only *after* all those metallic ions are "neutralized" does the remaining soap finally produce a lather.

> [!TIP]
