# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between coagulation and flocculation in water treatment.**

**Model Answer:**
Coagulation is the chemical process of **destabilizing** the surface charges on colloidal particles by the addition of a coagulant (like alum) so that they are no longer repelling each other. It is performed in a **rapid mixing tank** with a high velocity gradient ($G \approx 700$–$1000 \text{ s}^{-1}$) for a short detention time of $30$–$60$ seconds.

Flocculation, in contrast, is the **physical process** of gentle, slow mixing that allows the destabilized micro-flocs to come into contact and agglomerate into larger, settleable macro-flocs. It uses a low velocity gradient ($G \approx 20$–$75 \text{ s}^{-1}$) for $20$–$40$ minutes in a flocculation basin.

In short: **coagulation is rapid + chemical; flocculation is slow + physical**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is breakpoint chlorination? Why is it important in industrial cooling water systems?**

**Model Answer:**
**Breakpoint chlorination** is the process of adding chlorine to water beyond the point where all ammonia and organic nitrogen compounds are oxidized, leaving a **free residual chlorine** in the form of HOCl and OCl⁻. It corresponds to the dose at which the chlorine demand of water is fully satisfied and additional chlorine appears as a residual.

It is important in cooling water because:
1. It provides a **sustained biocidal action** to prevent microbial fouling, biofilm formation, and **Legionella** growth in cooling towers.
2. The typical residual maintained is $0.2$–$0.5$ mg/L, ensuring continuous disinfection throughout the recirculating system.

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Lime-Soda process** of water softening with balanced chemical equations. Discuss the advantages and limitations of the process.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A water sample analysis (in mg/L as CaCO₃) is as follows: $\text{CO}_2 = 15$, Carbonate Hardness = $180$, Magnesium Hardness = $70$, Non-Carbonate Hardness = $90$. Calculate the **commercial lime and soda ash** required to soften $1$ ML of water. Take purity of lime as $90\%$ and that of soda ash as $95\%$. Also estimate the theoretical sludge produced.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The **Lime-Soda Process** is a **precipitation-based** softening method in which lime $[\text{Ca(OH)}_2]$ and soda ash $[\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3]$ are added to remove **carbonate** and **non-carbonate hardness**, respectively.

**Step 1 — Removal of Carbonate Hardness:**

For calcium carbonate hardness:
$$\text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

For magnesium carbonate hardness:
$$\text{Mg(HCO}_3)_2 + 2\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2 \downarrow + 2\text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Removal of Non-Carbonate Hardness:**

For calcium non-carbonate:
$$\text{CaSO}_4 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$

For magnesium non-carbonate:
$$\text{MgSO}_4 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2 \downarrow + \text{CaSO}_4$$
$$\text{CaSO}_4 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$

**Advantages:**
- Low operating cost (chemicals are cheap).
- Removes **turbidity, iron, manganese, and silica** in addition to hardness.
- Reduces total dissolved solids (TDS).

**Limitations:**
- Generates **large volumes of sludge** (handling and disposal issue).
- Requires **skilled operation** (pH control between $9.5$–$10.5$).
- Cannot produce **zero hardness** water (residual $30$–$50$ mg/L remains).

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Lime Dose (in terms of CaCO₃ equivalent):**

$$\text{Lime} = \text{CO}_2 + \text{CH} + \text{Mg}^{2+} = 15 + 180 + 70 = 265 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 2 — Soda Ash Dose:**

$$\text{Soda ash} = \text{NCH} = 90 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Commercial Grades:**

The molecular weight ratio of $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ to CaCO₃ is $\dfrac{74}{100}$. So:

$$\text{Lime (pure)} = 265 \times \frac{74}{100} = 196.1 \text{ mg/L}$$

With $90\%$ purity:

$$\text{Lime (commercial)} = \frac{196.1}{0.90} = 217.9 \text{ mg/L}$$

For soda ash, the ratio is $\dfrac{106}{100}$:

$$\text{Soda ash (pure)} = 90 \times \frac{106}{100} = 95.4 \text{ mg/L}$$

With $95\%$ purity:

$$\text{Soda ash (commercial)} = \frac{95.4}{0.95} = 100.4 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Total Chemicals for 1 ML of Water:**

$$\text{Lime required} = 217.9 \text{ mg/L} \times 10^6 \text{ L} = 217.9 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Soda ash required} = 100.4 \text{ mg/L} \times 10^6 \text{ L} = 100.4 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Sludge Produced (empirical factor 2.5):**

$$\text{Sludge (dry)} = 2.5 \times (217.9 + 100.4) = 795.75 \text{ kg}$$

Wet sludge (with $95\%$ moisture):

$$\text{Sludge (wet)} = \frac{795.75}{0.05} = 15,915 \text{ kg} \approx 15.9 \text{ tonnes}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (14-Mark Questions):**
> - Forgetting to convert **CaCO₃ equivalent** to **actual chemical** ($\dfrac{74}{100}$ or $\dfrac{106}{100}$ ratio) → **$-2$ marks**.
> - Skipping the **commercial purity factor** → **$-1$ mark**.
> - Reporting sludge in **kg/d** but not converting to **tonnes** when the question asks per ML → **$-1$ mark**.
> - Writing the equation for **MgCO₃ hardness removal with only 1 mole of lime** (correct is 2 moles) → **$-2$ marks**; this is the most-tested conceptual error.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Internal Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch (or functional block diagram), describe the **ion-exchange process** for water softening. Explain the regeneration procedure in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A circular clarifier of diameter $20$ m and side water depth $3.5$ m is used to treat $30,000$ m³/d of water. Calculate: (i) Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) in m/day and m/h. (ii) Detention time in hours. (iii) State whether a particle of settling velocity $0.8$ m/h will be **completely removed**. If not, compute the **fractional removal efficiency**.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The **Ion-Exchange Softening Process** uses a synthetic **cation exchange resin** in the sodium form (R-Na). When hard water passes through the resin bed, the calcium and magnesium ions in the water are exchanged for sodium ions.

**Service Run Reactions:**
$$2\text{R-Na} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{R}_2\text{-Ca} + 2\text{Na}^+$$
$$2\text{R-Na} + \text{Mg}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{R}_2\text{-Mg} + 2\text{Na}^+$$

The effluent is **softened** (hardness $\approx 0$ mg/L) and contains equivalent Na⁺ ions. The resin becomes exhausted when the effluent hardness exceeds a pre-set limit (typically $5$ mg/L as CaCO₃).

**Regeneration Procedure:**

1. **Backwash** the resin bed with water (up-flow) for $10$–$15$ min to remove suspended solids and to reclassify the bed.
2. **Brine injection** ($10\%$ NaCl solution) is passed **down-flow** through the resin for $30$–$45$ min. The reaction is:
   $$\text{R}_2\text{-Ca} + 2\text{NaCl} \rightarrow 2\text{R-Na} + \text{CaCl}_2$$
3. **Slow rinse** with raw water to displace the brine.
4. **Fast rinse** to remove residual NaCl and produce a service-quality effluent.
5. Resin is **ready for the next service cycle**.

**Operating parameters:**
- Service flow rate: $8$–$40$ BV/h (bed volumes per hour).
- Brine dose: $100$–$300$ g NaCl per litre of resin.
- Resin life: $5$–$10$ years.

**Advantages:** Produces **zero hardness** water, compact plant, easy automation. **Limitations:** Does not remove TDS, alkalinity, or silica; requires periodic brine regeneration.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Surface Area of Clarifier:**

$$A_s = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (20)^2}{4} = 314.16 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Surface Overflow Rate (SOR):**

$$\text{SOR} = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{30,000}{314.16} = 95.49 \text{ m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day})$$

In m/h:

$$\text{SOR} = \frac{95.49}{24} = 3.98 \text{ m/h}$$

**Step 3 — Detention Time:**

$$V = A_s \times h = 314.16 \times 3.5 = 1099.56 \text{ m}^3$$

$$t_d = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{1099.56}{30,000} \text{ d} = 0.03665 \text{ d}$$

Converting to hours:

$$t_d = 0.03665 \times 24 = 0.88 \text{ h} \approx 52.8 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — 100% Removal Check:**

$$v_s = 0.8 \text{ m/h} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{SOR} = 3.98 \text{ m/h}$$

Since $v_s < \text{SOR}$, **the particle is NOT completely removed**.

**Step 5 — Fractional Removal Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{v_s}{\text{SOR}} = \frac{0.8}{3.98} = 0.201 = 20.1\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (14-Mark Question B):**
> - **Confusing units**: Many students write SOR in m³/h or m/day without converting to m/h for the $v_s$ comparison. **Always bring both to the same unit before comparing.** This is a $-1$ mark deduction.
> - **Skipping the fractional efficiency**: If $v_s <$ SOR, you must compute $\eta$ explicitly. Writing only "not removed" without the percentage loses **2 marks**.
> - **Stokes' Law assumption**: Always state that Stokes' Law is valid for $\text{Re} < 1$ whenever using it in sedimentation problems.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Quick Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Stokes' Law** applies ONLY for $\text{Re}_p < 1$ (laminar settling).
- [ ] **100% Removal Rule**: $v_s \geq \text{SOR}$ for complete removal; else $\eta = v_s / \text{SOR}$.
- [ ] **Coagulation** = rapid + chemical ($G = 700$–$1000 \text{ s}^{-1}$); **Flocculation** = slow + physical ($G = 20$–$75 \text{ s}^{-1}$).
- [ ] **Lime dose** = $\text{CO}_2 + \text{CH} + \text{Mg}^{2+}$ (all in mg/L as CaCO₃).
- [ ] **Soda ash dose** = NCH (in mg/L as CaCO₃).
- [ ] **Ion Exchange** removes ONLY hardness; **Demineralization** removes ALL ions.
- [ ] **Mixed Bed Polisher** produces water with conductivity $< 0.1 \ \mu\text{S/cm}$.
- [ ] **Rapid Sand Filter** rate: $5$–$15 \text{ m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h})$; **Backwash** rate: $36$–$45$ m/h for $50\%$ bed expansion.
- [ ] **Breakpoint Chlorination** is essential for cooling water to control **Legionella** and biofilm.
- [ ] **Conversion factors** to memorize: $\text{Ca(OH)}_2/\text{CaCO}_3 = 74/100$; $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3/\text{CaCO}_3 = 106/100$.
- [ ] **Sludge rule of thumb**: Dry sludge $\approx 2.5 \times$ (lime + soda ash, in commercial form).
- [ ] **LSI** (Langelier Saturation Index): $> 0$ scaling, $< 0$ corrosive, ideal $0.5$–$1.0$ for cooling water.
- [ ] **RO** removes $95$–$99\%$ TDS but requires high pressure ($10$–$30$ bar) and produces brine reject.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Examiner Pet Topics:**
> 1. Numerical on Lime-Soda dose with purity corrections (always tested, 7 marks).
> 2. Numerical on SOR, detention time, and removal efficiency (always tested, 7 marks).
> 3. Comparison table: Lime-Soda vs. Ion Exchange vs. RO (tested as 14-mark full question or as 3-mark short).
> 4. Sketch/Block diagram of water treatment plant for high-pressure boiler feed (tested as 7-mark sub-question).
> 5. Definition of **breakpoint chlorination** with sketch of the chlorine residual curve (favourite 3-mark question).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cooling Tower: Constructional Details, Working, Types and Performance Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cooling Tower: Constructional Details, Working, Types and Performance Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cooling Tower (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> A **cooling tower** is a heat-rejection device that transfers waste heat from circulating water to the atmosphere by means of a combination of **latent heat of vaporization** (evaporation) and **sensible heat transfer** (convection and conduction), thereby lowering the bulk temperature of the circulating water system used in chemical process plants, refineries, power stations, and HVAC systems.

In process utilities engineering, cooling towers are classified as the **primary water-cooling equipment** in a process plant. They operate on the principle of **evaporative cooling** — when a small fraction of warm water evaporates, it draws its latent heat of vaporization from the remaining water bulk, producing a cooled water stream that can be recycled.

The **cooling range (ΔT)** is defined as the difference between the hot water inlet temperature and the cold water outlet temperature:

$$\Delta T = T_{in} - T_{out}$$

The **approach (ΔT_a)** is the difference between the cold water outlet temperature and the ambient wet-bulb temperature:

$$\Delta T_{a} = T_{out} - T_{wb}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The wet-bulb temperature is the **theoretical lower limit** of cooling. No evaporative cooling tower can cool water below the ambient wet-bulb temperature under steady-state operation. This is a fundamental KTU board-exam favorite concept.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Village Pot (Mattan Kullam)**
>
> Imagine a Kerala village in April: a grandmother pours hot rice water (kanji) into a porous earthen pot (mattan chembu) and hangs it on the veranda. Two things happen:
> 1. A tiny amount of water **seeps through** the porous walls and **evaporates** from the outer surface.
> 2. As this evaporation occurs, the remaining water inside loses latent heat and becomes noticeably cooler by evening.
>
> A cooling tower is the industrial-scale, engineered version of that earthen pot. Instead of porous clay, it uses **fill/packing** to maximize surface area. Instead of natural breeze, it uses **fans** to drive air. Instead of a single pot, it cascades water over **large structured packs** to maximize air-water contact.

The "cold limit" of the earthen pot is essentially the **ambient wet-bulb temperature** — on a humid rainy day, the pot barely cools because evaporation is suppressed.

## 1.3 Why Cooling Towers are Indispensable in Process Plants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industry Significance**
> Roughly **70–80 %** of all industrial cooling duty worldwide is handled by cooling towers. In a typical chemical plant:
> - **Condenser cooling** for distillation columns
> - **Reactor jacket cooling** for exothermic reactions
> - **Compressor inter-stage cooling**
> - **Lean solvent cooling** in absorption/stripping units

Without cooling towers, plants would have to consume massive quantities of fresh cooling water and dump it as hot effluent — economically and environmentally infeasible.

---

## 1.4 Core Quantitative Metrics (Standard KTU Values)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling Range | ΔT | **5 °C to 15 °C** |
| Approach | ΔT_a | **2.5 °C to 7 °C** |
| L/G Ratio | L/G | **0.75 to 2.5** |
| Make-up Water | % of circulation | **1.5 % to 5 %** |
| Evaporation Loss | % of circulation | **1 % to 1.5 %** per 10 °C ΔT |
| Drift Loss | % of circulation | **0.001 % to 0.005 %** |
| Blow-down Loss | % of circulation | **0.5 % to 2.0 %** |

---

## 1.5 Mass and Energy Conservation — The Governing Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Balance on a Cooling Tower**
> The tower is essentially an **adiabatic direct-contact heat exchanger** between warm water (descending) and ambient air (rising). No external heating or cooling is applied. The energy lost by water = energy gained by air + energy carried away by evaporated moisture.

Mathematically:

$$L \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = G \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$$

where **L** = mass flow rate of water (kg/h), **G** = mass flow rate of dry air (kg/h), **c_{p,w}** = specific heat of water (≈ 4.186 kJ/kg·K), **T_1** = hot water inlet temperature, **T_2** = cold water outlet temperature, **h_1** = enthalpy of inlet air (kJ/kg dry air), **h_2** = enthalpy of exit air (kJ/kg dry air).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Three Heat Transfer Modes

A cooling tower achieves cooling through **three simultaneous, coupled mechanisms**:

1. **Latent Heat Transfer by Evaporation (Dominant — ~80 % of duty)**
   - A fraction of circulating water evaporates into the air stream.
   - Each kilogram of evaporated water carries away approximately **2450 kJ** (latent heat at ~30 °C).
   - This is the principal cooling mechanism.

2. **Sensible Heat Transfer by Convection (~20 % of duty)**
   - Warm water transfers heat to the cooler air stream across the water-air interfacial area.
   - Governed by: $q_{conv} = h_c \cdot A \cdot (T_{water} - T_{air})$

3. **Sensible Heat Transfer by Conduction (Negligible, usually ignored in design)**
   - Through-fill conduction; significant only at fill support structures.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

> [!TIP]
> **The Five-Stage Operational Cascade (KTU Board-Favorite)**
> 1. **Hot water distribution** — Warm process water enters the tower through inlet headers and is sprayed through **nozzle distributors** to maximize surface area.
> 2. **Air induction** — Ambient air enters via louvered inlets (natural draft) or forced by **mechanical fans**.
> 3. **Direct contact heat & mass transfer** — Water cascades over fill/packing while air flows counter-currently or cross-currently. Evaporation and convection occur simultaneously.
> 4. **Water collection** — Cooled water falls into the **cold water basin (sump)** at the tower base.
> 5. **Make-up & blow-down** — Fresh water replenishes evaporation/drift losses; blow-down removes dissolved solids to control TDS/cycles of concentration.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the single most-tested formula set for cooling tower questions in KTU ESE. Memorize it.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L \cdot c_{p,w}(T_1 - T_2) = G(h_2 - h_1)$ | Overall energy balance | kW |
| 2 | $\frac{L}{G} = \frac{h_2 - h_1}{c_{p,w}(T_1 - T_2)}$ | L/G (water-to-air) ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | Evaporation Loss $\approx 0.00085 \cdot \Delta T \cdot Q$ | Per °C cooling (rule of thumb) | m³/h |
| 4 | Cycles of Concentration $= \frac{C_{cw}}{C_{mw}}$ | Concentration cycles | — |
| 5 | Blow-down $= \frac{E}{C - 1}$ | Mass balance on dissolved solids | kg/h |
| 6 | Drift Loss $\approx 0.005\,\% $ of circulation | Modern eliminator value | — |
| 7 | Drift eliminator efficiency $\geq 99.95\,\%$ | Standard modern spec | — |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{approach} = T_{out} - T_{wb}$ | Approach to wet-bulb | °C |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{range} = T_{in} - T_{out}$ | Cooling range | °C |
| 10 | $\mu_{coc} = \mu_{water} \cdot e^{0.025 \cdot (T - 20)}$ | Viscosity correction (Chilton analogy) | cP |
| 11 | $NTU = \int \frac{dT}{T_{w,i} - T_{w}^*}$ | Number of transfer units | — |
| 12 | KaV/L = Merkel integral | Merkel equation | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The variable **G** in cooling tower literature always refers to the **dry air mass flow rate**, NOT the moist air flow rate. This is a classic 1-mark differentiator.

---

## 2.4 Why Evaporative Cooling Works — The Thermodynamic Why

The **psychrometric principle** states that when unsaturated air contacts water:
- If water temperature > air wet-bulb → water evaporates into air → water cools.
- If water temperature = air wet-bulb → equilibrium, no net mass transfer.
- If water temperature < air wet-bulb → condensation occurs on water (rare, only in fog conditions).

The **driving force** for evaporation is the difference between the **saturation vapor pressure at water temperature** and the **partial vapor pressure of water in the bulk air**.

---

## 2.5 Constructional Details — Component-by-Component

> [!NOTE]
> **Anatomy of a Mechanical-Draft Cross-Flow Cooling Tower**

| Component | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Casing / Shell** | FRP, RCC, Galvanized steel, SS | Structural enclosure, weather protection |
| **Fill / Packing** | PVC (splash & film type) | Maximizes air-water contact area |
| **Water Distribution System** | PP/PVC nozzles on header pipes | Evenly distributes hot water over fill |
| **Drift Eliminators** | PVC cellular or blade type | Captures entrained water droplets (>99.95 %) |
| **Air Inlet Louvers** | FRP / GI sheet | Directs airflow into tower, prevents water splash-out |
| **Fans** | Axial / FRP blades | Drives airflow through tower |
| **Fan Stack / Cowl** | FRP | Smoothens airflow, reduces recirculation |
| **Cold Water Basin (Sump)** | RCC with epoxy lining | Collects cooled water, includes strainers |
| **Strainer / Trash Rack** | SS-304 perforated | Prevents debris from entering pumps |
| **Make-up Float Valve** | Brass / SS | Maintains basin level automatically |
| **Ladder, Handrails, Lights** | GI / SS | Maintenance access and safety |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Power Plants** | Condenser cooling for steam turbines |
| **Petrochemical Refineries** | Distillation column overhead condensers |
| **Fertilizer Plants** | Urea / Ammonia reactor cooling |
| **Steel Plants** | Blast furnace gas cooling, rolling mills |
| **HVAC** | Central air-conditioning chillers |
| **Data Centers** | Liquid cooling of server racks |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Merkel Equation (Theoretical Foundation)

The **Merkel equation** is the cornerstone of cooling tower design. It incorporates both sensible and latent heat transfer using the **enthalpy potential** as the driving force.

### Step 1: Differential Energy Balance on a Tower Element

Consider an elemental height $dz$ of the tower. Water flows downward at rate $L$ (kg dry air-free basis) and air rises at rate $G$ (kg dry air/h).

The differential heat transfer $dQ$ from water to air in this element is:

$$dQ = G \cdot dh_{a} = L \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot dT_{w}$$

where $h_a$ is the enthalpy of the air-vapor mixture per kg of dry air.

### Step 2: Driving Force Definition

The driving force for mass transfer is the difference between the **enthalpy of saturated air at water temperature** ($h_{a}^*$) and the **enthalpy of the bulk air** ($h_a$).

### Step 3: Mass Transfer Coefficient Formulation

By analogy with the Chilton-Colburn analogy between heat and mass transfer, the differential heat transfer can be expressed as:

$$dQ = K \cdot a \cdot V \cdot (h_a^* - h_a) \cdot dz$$

where:
- $K$ = overall mass transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s)
- $a$ = interfacial area per unit volume (m²/m³)
- $V$ = tower volume
- $h_a^*$ = enthalpy of saturated air at local water temperature
- $h_a$ = local air enthalpy

### Step 4: Combine with Energy Balance

Substituting $dQ = G \cdot dh_a$:

$$G \cdot dh_a = K \cdot a \cdot V \cdot (h_a^* - h_a) \cdot dz$$

### Step 5: Integrate Across Tower Height

$$\int_{h_{a,1}}^{h_{a,2}} \frac{dh_a}{h_a^* - h_a} = \frac{K \cdot a \cdot V}{G}$$

This is the **Merkel Integral**:

$$\boxed{\text{Merkel Number} = \frac{K \cdot a \cdot V}{L} = \int_{T_{w,out}}^{T_{w,in}} \frac{c_{p,w} \cdot dT}{h_a^*(T_w) - h_a(T_w, h_a)}}$$

### Step 6: Solution by Numerical Methods

The integral is evaluated graphically or by Simpson's rule, using a **psychrometric chart** to read $h_a^*$ at successive water temperatures.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Energy Balance and L/G Calculation

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A counter-flow induced-draft cooling tower handles $L = 50{,}000$ kg/h of water. Hot water enters at $T_1 = 45\,°C$ and leaves at $T_2 = 30\,°C$. Ambient air enters at $T_{db} = 30\,°C$, $T_{wb} = 24\,°C$. Determine (a) heat to be removed, (b) L/G ratio if exit air enthalpy is $h_2 = 92$ kJ/kg and inlet air enthalpy is $h_1 = 57$ kJ/kg.

#### Solution:

**Given:**
- $L = 50{,}000$ kg/h
- $T_1 = 45\,°C$, $T_2 = 30\,°C$
- $c_{p,w} = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K
- $h_1 = 57$ kJ/kg dry air, $h_2 = 92$ kJ/kg dry air

**(a) Heat Removed:**

$$\Delta T = T_1 - T_2 = 45 - 30 = 15\,°C$$

$$Q = L \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q = 50{,}000 \times 4.186 \times 15$$

$$Q = 3{,}139{,}500 \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 3139.5 \text{ MJ/h} = 872.1 \text{ kW}}$$

**(b) L/G Ratio:**

From energy balance: $L \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = G \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$

$$\frac{L}{G} = \frac{h_2 - h_1}{c_{p,w} \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}$$

$$\frac{L}{G} = \frac{92 - 57}{4.186 \times 15}$$

$$\frac{L}{G} = \frac{35}{62.79}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{L}{G} \approx 0.557}$$

Since L/G < 0.75, the air rate is high — this corresponds to a relatively **bulky, air-intensive** design.

---

## 3.3 Water Make-up Calculation — Worked Example

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A cooling tower circulates $Q = 1000$ m³/h of water with cooling range $\Delta T = 10\,°C$. Calculate evaporation, drift, and blow-down losses. Assume cycles of concentration $C = 4$, drift loss $= 0.005\,\%$ of circulation.

**Step 1 — Evaporation Loss:**
$$E = 0.00085 \cdot \Delta T \cdot Q$$
$$E = 0.00085 \times 10 \times 1000$$
$$E = 8.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 2 — Drift Loss:**
$$D = 0.00005 \times Q = 0.00005 \times 1000 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Blow-down:**
$$\text{Blow-down} = \frac{E}{C - 1} = \frac{8.5}{4 - 1} = 2.833 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Total Make-up:**
$$\text{Make-up} = E + D + B = 8.5 + 0.05 + 2.833$$
$$\boxed{\text{Make-up} \approx 11.38 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Evaluation Parameters

| Parameter | Excellent | Good | Marginal | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Approach to wet-bulb | < 2.5 °C | 2.5–5 °C | > 5 °C | Lower = better performance |
| Cooling range | 10–15 °C | 5–10 °C | < 5 °C | Application-dependent |
| L/G ratio | 1.0–1.5 | 0.75–2.0 | > 2.5 | Optimum is at minimum KaV/L |
| Merkel Number | 1.5–2.5 | 1.0–1.5 | < 1.0 | Higher = better tower |
| Cycles of concentration | 4–6 | 2–4 | > 6 | Higher = less blow-down |
| Drift loss | < 0.002 % | < 0.005 % | > 0.01 % | Regulatory limit |

---

## 3.5 Performance Curves — Typical Shapes (Verbal Description)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Topic — Performance Curves**
> 1. **Range vs. Approach Curve:** At fixed L/G, range decreases as approach decreases. As approach → 0, range → 0.
> 2. **L/G vs. KaV/L Curve (Operating Line):** The Merkel integral reaches a minimum value at the optimum L/G. This optimum defines the **most economical tower size**.
> 3. **Characteristic Curve (Range vs. L/G at constant approach):** Shows tower's capacity envelope.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Mechanical Draft Cross-Flow Cooling Tower

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hot Process Water Inlet Header] --> B[Distribution Pipe Network]
    B --> C[PVC Spray Nozzles Cluster A]
    B --> D[PVC Spray Nozzles Cluster B]
    C --> E[PVC Splash Fill Pack]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Drift Eliminator Bank]
    F --> G[Fan Stack Exhaust]
    H[Axial Fan Assembly] --> G
    I[Louvered Air Inlet Plenum] --> E
    J[Cold Water Basin Sump] --> E
    E --> J
    J --> K[SS Strainer Trash Rack]
    K --> L[Cold Water Pump Suction]
    L --> M[Process Plant Return Loop]
    N[Make-up Water Float Valve] --> J
    O[Blow-down Drain Line] --> J
```

---

## 4.2 Mass and Energy Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HWI[Hot Water In T1 deg C] --> DIST[Distribution System]
    DIST --> FILL[Fill and Packing]
    FILL --> EVAP[Evaporation Zone]
    EVAP --> CWB[Cold Water Basin T2 deg C]
    FILL --> AIR[Air Stream Counter Flow]
    AIR --> IN[Inlet Air h1 kJ per kg]
    AIR --> OUT[Exit Air h2 kJ per kg saturated]
    CWB --> CP[Cold Water Pump]
    CP --> PROC[Process Plant]
    PROC --> RCW[Return Circuit]
    RCW --> HWI
    EVAP --> EVAPL[Evaporation Loss E]
    OUT --> DRIFT[Drift Loss D]
    CWB --> BD[Blow down B]
    EVAPL --> MK[Make up Water M]
    DRIFT --> MK
    BD --> MK
    N[Fresh Make up Source] --> MK
    MK --> CWB
```

---

## 4.3 Classification Tree — Types of Cooling Towers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CT[Cooling Tower Master Classification]
    CT --> ND[Natural Draft]
    CT --> MD[Mechanical Draft]
    ND --> NDH[Hyperbolic Natural Draft]
    ND --> NDC[Counter Flow Natural Draft]
    MD --> MDC[Mechanical Draft Cross Flow]
    MD --> MDCT[Mechanical Draft Counter Flow]
    MDC --> MDCF[Forced Draft Cross Flow]
    MDC --> MDCI[Induced Draft Cross Flow]
    MDCT --> MDCTF[Forced Draft Counter Flow]
    MDCT --> MDCTI[Induced Draft Counter Flow]
    CT --> WT[Wet Type]
    CT --> DT[Dry Type]
    CT --> HX[Closed Circuit Hybrid]
    CT --> SH[Shape Classification]
    SH --> SQ[Square]
    SH --> RD[Round Bottle Type]
    SH --> CT_TYP[Tower Shape in Plan]
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Block — Counter-Flow vs. Cross-Flow

| Feature | Counter-Flow | Cross-Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Air-water relative flow | Air flows vertically upward against descending water | Air flows horizontally across falling water |
| Approach to wet-bulb | Better (lower approach achievable: 2.5 °C) | Slightly higher approach (~4 °C) |
| Pumping head | Higher (water lifted to top) | Lower (water distributed at one side) |
| Floor area | Smaller (compact) | Larger |
| Maintenance access | Difficult (fill is deep) | Excellent (open access from side) |
| Fan power | Slightly higher | Lower |
| Typical use | Industrial process plants | HVAC and large power plants |

---

## 4.5 Closed-Circuit (Hybrid) Cooling Tower — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Process Side Hot Fluid] --> CS[Coil Heat Exchanger Bank]
    CS --> PR[Process Return Cooled]
    SP[Spray Pump] --> SN[Top Spray Nozzles]
    SN --> CS
    CS --> CB[Collection Basin]
    F1[Axial Fan] --> ST[Top Air Discharge]
    AIL[Ambient Air Inlet Louver] --> CS
    CS --> ST
    CB --> SP
    EV[Evaporation Loss] --> MU[Make up]
    BD2[Blow down] --> MU
    DR2[Drift Loss] --> MU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed-circuit cooling towers** isolate the process fluid inside a coil. Only the secondary spray water contacts the air. They are used where **process contamination** must be avoided (food, pharma, electronics).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — Definition Type
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **Define the following terms with respect to cooling towers:**
> (a) Approach (b) Range (c) Drift

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Approach:** It is the temperature difference between the cold water leaving the tower ($T_{cw}$) and the ambient wet-bulb temperature ($T_{wb}$):

$$\text{Approach} = T_{cw} - T_{wb}$$

It indicates how closely the tower approaches the theoretical minimum cooling temperature. A smaller approach means better tower performance.

**(b) Range:** It is the temperature drop of water across the tower:

$$\text{Range} = T_{hw} - T_{cw}$$

It represents the actual cooling duty achieved.

**(c) Drift:** The small quantity of water droplets entrained in the exit air stream and lost to the atmosphere. Modern towers with drift eliminators limit drift to less than **0.005 %** of circulating water flow.

---

### Question A.2 — Conceptual
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> **Why can't a cooling tower cool water below the wet-bulb temperature of incoming air?**

**Model Answer:**

Cooling towers operate on the principle of **evaporative cooling**. The driving force for evaporation is the difference between the vapor pressure of water at its bulk temperature and the partial pressure of water vapor in the ambient air. When water temperature falls to the **wet-bulb temperature**, the air becomes **saturated** with moisture at that temperature, and the vapor pressures equalize. At this point, the **net mass transfer = 0**, and no further evaporation (and therefore no further cooling) is possible.

Mathematically, the Merkel integral driving force $h_a^* - h_a \to 0$ as $T_w \to T_{wb}$, terminating the cooling process.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert #1:** Students frequently confuse **wet-bulb** with **dry-bulb**. In an evaporative cooling tower, **dry-bulb is irrelevant**; the cooling limit is the **wet-bulb**. Drawing a psychrometric chart with wet-bulb on the y-axis is mandatory for full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **mechanical-draft induced counter-flow cooling tower**. List its components. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A counter-flow induced-draft cooling tower cools $60{,}000$ kg/h of water from $48\,°C$ to $30\,°C$. Ambient air enters at $T_{db} = 32\,°C$, $T_{wb} = 26\,°C$ with enthalpy $h_1 = 76$ kJ/kg dry air and leaves at $h_2 = 105$ kJ/kg dry air. Calculate:
> (i) Range and Approach
> (ii) Heat removed in kW
> (iii) Air mass flow rate G
> (iv) L/G ratio
> (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Construction:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Casing (FRP/RCC) | Encloses tower, supports structure |
| Distribution Header & Nozzles | Spreads water evenly over fill |
| PVC Film Fill | Provides wetted surface for evaporation |
| Drift Eliminators | Captures entrained droplets |
| Fan & Motor (top mounted) | Induces upward airflow |
| Cold Water Basin | Collects cooled water |
| Air Inlet Louvers | Allow ambient air entry |
| Make-up & Blow-down piping | Maintains water chemistry |

**Working:**

1. Hot water enters the top header.
2. Sprayed through nozzles → distributed over fill.
3. Air is pulled upward by the fan (induced draft) — counter-flow to water.
4. Direct contact between water and unsaturated air → evaporation occurs.
5. Water cools and collects in the basin; saturated air exits through eliminators and fan stack.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:**
- $L = 60{,}000$ kg/h
- $T_1 = 48\,°C$, $T_2 = 30\,°C$
- $h_1 = 76$ kJ/kg, $h_2 = 105$ kJ/kg
- $T_{wb} = 26\,°C$

**(i) Range and Approach:**

$$\text{Range} = T_1 - T_2 = 48 - 30 = 18\,°C$$

$$\text{Approach} = T_2 - T_{wb} = 30 - 26 = 4\,°C$$

**(ii) Heat Removed:**

$$Q = L \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$$

$$Q = 60{,}000 \times 4.186 \times 18$$

$$Q = 4{,}520{,}880 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Converting to kW (divide by 3600):

$$Q = \frac{4{,}520{,}880}{3600} \approx 1255.8 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Air Flow Rate:**

$$G = \frac{L \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{h_2 - h_1}$$

$$G = \frac{4{,}520{,}880}{105 - 76}$$

$$G = \frac{4{,}520{,}880}{29} \approx 155{,}892 \text{ kg/h}$$

**(iv) L/G Ratio:**

$$\frac{L}{G} = \frac{60{,}000}{155{,}892} \approx 0.385$$

Or directly:

$$\frac{L}{G} = \frac{h_2 - h_1}{c_{p,w}(T_1 - T_2)} = \frac{29}{4.186 \times 18} = \frac{29}{75.348} \approx 0.385$$

The low L/G indicates a high air-throughput design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert #2:** When computing $Q$, students forget to **convert kJ/h to kW** by dividing by 3600. KTU board deducts 1 mark for unit inconsistency. Always state your answer in kW or MW, not just kJ/h.

---

### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks — Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

> **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **natural-draft hyperbolic cooling tower**. Mention its applications and limitations. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** In a cooling tower, water enters at $40\,°C$ and leaves at $28\,°C$. The ambient wet-bulb temperature is $24\,°C$. The dry-bulb temperature is $32\,°C$. Determine:
> (i) Range
> (ii) Approach
> (iii) Cooling efficiency
> (iv) If circulation rate is $80{,}000$ kg/h, calculate evaporation loss per hour (assuming $E = 0.00085 \times \Delta T \times Q$).
> (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

A **natural-draft hyperbolic cooling tower** uses the **chimney effect** to induce airflow — no fans. The tower has a characteristic **hyperboloid shape**, narrow at the waist, wider at top and bottom.

**Construction:**
- Massive RCC shell with hyperbolic profile.
- Inside: rain zones, splash fill, drift eliminators.
- Cold water basin at base.
- Air enters through perimeter openings at bottom.

**Working:**
1. Warm, humid air inside the tower is **less dense** than ambient air.
2. The density difference creates a natural **upward draft** (stack effect).
3. Air rises through the fill in counter-flow with descending water.
4. Evaporative cooling occurs.
5. Saturated air exits through the top opening.

**Applications:** Large power plants (>500 MW), nuclear plants, large petrochemical complexes.

**Limitations:**
- Very high capital cost.
- Requires large footprint.
- Performance strongly dependent on ambient humidity.
- Cannot achieve very low approach (< 3 °C) easily.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 40\,°C$, $T_2 = 28\,°C$
- $T_{wb} = 24\,°C$
- $Q = 80{,}000$ kg/h

**(i) Range:**

$$\text{Range} = T_1 - T_2 = 40 - 28 = 12\,°C$$

**(ii) Approach:**

$$\text{Approach} = T_2 - T_{wb} = 28 - 24 = 4\,°C$$

**(iii) Cooling Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{cooling} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{wb}} = \frac{12}{40 - 24} = \frac{12}{16} = 0.75$$

$$\eta_{cooling} = 75\,\%$$

**(iv) Evaporation Loss:**

$$E = 0.00085 \times \Delta T \times Q$$

$$E = 0.00085 \times 12 \times 80{,}000$$

$$E = 816 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 816 \text{ kg/h} \approx 0.816 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert #3:** Many students define cooling efficiency as **Range/Approach** — this is wrong. The correct formula is **Range / (Range + Approach)** or equivalently **Range / (T_in − T_wb)**. Memorize the correct version.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Board Prep)**

- [ ] **Cooling limit** = ambient wet-bulb temperature, NOT dry-bulb.
- [ ] **Range** = $T_{in} - T_{out}$ ; **Approach** = $T_{out} - T_{wb}$.
- [ ] **Lower approach** = more efficient (but more expensive) tower.
- [ ] **L/G ratio** = $0.75$ to $2.5$ in practice; optimum from Merkel curve.
- [ ] **Energy balance:** $L \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T = G \cdot \Delta h_a$.
- [ ] **Evaporation loss rule:** $\approx 0.00085 \times \Delta T \times Q$ per hour (m³ basis).
- [ ] **Drift loss** ≤ 0.005 % with modern eliminators.
- [ ] **Cycles of Concentration:** typical 4–6; controlled via blow-down.
- [ ] **Natural draft** uses chimney effect; **mechanical draft** uses fans.
- [ ] **Counter-flow** gives better approach; **cross-flow** gives better access.
- [ ] **Make-up water** = Evaporation + Drift + Blow-down.
- [ ] **Merkel Number** = $KaV/L$ is the design index; higher = larger (more effective) tower.
- [ ] **Hyperbolic towers** dominate large power plants; **mechanical induced-draft** dominates process plants.
- [ ] **Fill types:** splash fill (low pressure drop), film fill (high efficiency).
- [ ] **Drift eliminators** are mandatory to comply with environmental regulations.
- [ ] **Wet-bulb depression** of air ($T_{db} - T_{wb}$) determines cooling potential.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Fuel Oil, Coal and Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Properties of Fuel Oil, Coal and Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Industrial Fuels

A **fuel** is any combustible material which, upon combustion, liberates usable thermal energy. In the context of chemical process industries, the three principal classes of fuels considered in the KTU Process Utilities syllabus are **Fuel Oil** (a liquid fossil fuel), **Coal** (a solid fossil fuel), and **Natural/Producer Gas** (a gaseous fossil fuel).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OECHT611, Module 2):** *"Properties of Fuel Oil, Coal and Gas"* encompasses the physical, chemical, and thermodynamic characteristics that govern their storage, handling, combustion efficiency, and suitability as process utilities in chemical plants, refineries, and power stations.

### 1.2 Why "Properties" Matter in Process Utilities

Before any fuel is combusted in a furnace, boiler, or gas turbine, an engineer MUST evaluate its **properties** because:
- They determine **combustion efficiency**
- They dictate **storage and handling protocols** (safety)
- They influence **equipment design** (burners, pipes, atomizers)
- They affect **emissions and environmental compliance**
- They govern **flue gas composition** and downstream treatment

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuel Energy Locker" Analogy

Imagine each fuel as a **locker containing chemical energy** in a specific "currency":
- **Coal** = Energy stored in a heavy, solid, lumpy briefcase. To use it, you must first grind it, dry it, and blow air through it.
- **Fuel Oil** = Energy stored in a viscous liquid container. To use it, you must pump it, preheat it, and atomize it into a fine mist.
- **Gas** = Energy already in the most "spendable" form — light, flowing, and ready to ignite instantly with no preparation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** The state of aggregation (solid/liquid/gas) directly determines the *preparation steps* required before combustion. Properties are the metrics that quantify these preparation needs.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used Across All Three Fuels

- **Calorific Value (CV)** — measured in **kcal/kg** (solids/liquids) or **kcal/Nm³** (gases). **NCV (Net Calorific Value)** excludes latent heat of water vapor.
- **Density (ρ)** — **kg/m³** at 15 °C for oils; **kg/m³** for solid bulk density; **kg/Nm³** for gases.
- **Specific Gravity** — ratio to reference (water for liquids, air for gases).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Properties of Fuel Oil (Liquid Fossil Fuel)

Fuel oil is a complex mixture of hydrocarbons obtained from the residue of crude oil distillation. It is graded by viscosity, principally **Furnace Oil (FO)**, **Light Diesel Oil (LDO)**, and **High-Speed Diesel (HSD)**.

#### Key Properties (Engineering Logic):

1. **Density (ρ)** — At 15 °C, typically **960–1010 kg/m³**. Higher density means heavier molecules and lower volatility.
2. **Viscosity (μ)** — Resistance to flow, measured in **cSt (centistokes)** or **Redwood/Engler seconds** at 50 °C or 100 °C. *Why important?* High viscosity requires preheating to 80–120 °C for proper atomization through burner nozzles.
3. **Flash Point (FP)** — The lowest temperature at which oil vapors ignite momentarily on application of a flame. For safety, FO must have FP ≥ **66 °C**.
4. **Pour Point (PP)** — Lowest temperature at which oil flows. Determines low-temperature storage/pumping limits.
5. **Fire Point** — Temperature at which oil vapors sustain combustion for ≥ 5 seconds. Always **5–8 °C** above flash point.
6. **Calorific Value (GCV)** — For FO, typically **10,200–10,800 kcal/kg**.
7. **Sulfur Content (wt %)** — Critical for SOₓ emissions and corrosion. FO typically contains **2–4% S**.
8. **Water & Sediment (wt %)** — Should be < **1%** to prevent burner tip blockage and explosions.
9. **Ash Content (wt %)** — Inorganic residue, typically < **0.1%** for FO.
10. **Carbon Residue (Conradson)** — Indicates tendency to form coke deposits in burners.
11. **Aniline Point** — Indicates aromaticity; lower aniline point = higher aromatics = lower cetane number.

### 2.2 Properties of Coal (Solid Fossil Fuel)

Coal is a heterogeneous sedimentary rock composed of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, and mineral matter. Its utility is determined by TWO standard analyses:

#### (A) Proximate Analysis (Engineer's Quality Check):
| Component | Symbol | What it tells us |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture | M | Storage, drying cost |
| Volatile Matter | VM | Ease of ignition, flame length |
| Ash | A | Inert residue, slagging |
| Fixed Carbon | FC | Coke yield, char |

> **Proximate Sum Rule:** $M + VM + A + FC = 100\%$

#### (B) Ultimate Analysis (Elemental Composition):
| Element | Symbol | Typical Range (Bituminous) |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon | C | 70–85% |
| Hydrogen | H | 4–6% |
| Oxygen | O | 5–15% |
| Nitrogen | N | 1–2% |
| Sulfur | S | 0.5–4% |
| Ash | A | 5–15% |

#### Other Critical Coal Properties:
- **Calorific Value (GCV)** — **5,000–8,000 kcal/kg** for bituminous coal.
- **Coking/Strong Caking Property** — Determines metallurgical use.
- **Grindability Index (HGI)** — Hardgrove Grindability Index, ease of pulverization.
- **Swelling Index** — Behavior during coking.
- **Ash Fusion Temperature (AFT)** — Critical for furnace design to prevent slagging.

### 2.3 Properties of Gaseous Fuels (Natural Gas, LPG, Producer Gas, Coke Oven Gas)

| Property | Definition | Typical Values (Natural Gas) |
|---|---|---|
| **Calorific Value (CV)** | Heat released on complete combustion | 8,500–10,500 kcal/Nm³ |
| **Specific Gravity (G)** | Density relative to air | 0.55–0.70 |
| **Wobbe Index (W)** | Heat output through a fixed orifice | 8,500–13,000 kcal/Nm³ |
| **Flammability Limits** | Min/Max vol % in air for combustion | LFL 5%, UFL 15% (CH₄) |
| **Flame Speed** | Velocity of flame propagation | 0.4 m/s (CH₄) to 4 m/s (H₂) |
| **Ignition Temperature** | Auto-ignition temperature | 540 °C (CH₄) |
| **Air-Fuel Ratio** | Stoichiometric mass of air per mass of fuel | ~17.2 kg air/kg CH₄ |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **rapid-revision summary** of all critical equations for KTU exams.

| # | Property / Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ for C → CO₂ | $O_2 = \dfrac{32}{12} \times C$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel |
| 2 | Stoichiometric O₂ for H₂ → H₂O | $O_2 = \dfrac{32}{4} \times H_2$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel |
| 3 | Stoichiometric O₂ for S → SO₂ | $O_2 = \dfrac{32}{32} \times S$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel |
| 4 | Theoretical Air | $A = \dfrac{O_2}{0.232}$ | kg air / kg fuel |
| 5 | Dulong's Formula (Coal GCV) | $GCV = 8080 C + 34500 \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 S$ | kcal/kg |
| 6 | NCV from GCV | $NCV = GCV - 9 \times H \times \text{Latent heat}$ | kcal/kg |
| 7 | NCV (Hydrogen latent heat) | $NCV = GCV - 587 \times H_2$ | kcal/kg (where H₂ in kg) |
| 8 | Wobbe Index | $W = \dfrac{CV}{\sqrt{G}}$ | kcal/Nm³ |
| 9 | Specific Gravity (gas) | $G = \dfrac{\rho_{gas}}{\rho_{air}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Volume of air from O₂ requirement | $V_{air} = \dfrac{O_2 \times 100}{21}$ | Nm³/kg fuel |
| 11 | % Excess Air | $\%EA = \dfrac{O_2\,actual - O_2\,theo}{O_2\,theo} \times 100$ | % |
| 12 | Density from API | $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | °API |
| 13 | Orsat Analysis O₂ correction | $N_2\,(dry) = \dfrac{79}{21} \times O_2$ | mole% |

> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in **(i) Boiler efficiency audits**, **(ii) Flue gas analysis**, **(iii) Burner selection**, **(iv) Refractory design**, and **(v) Emission control systems (ESP, FGD)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Dulong's Formula for Coal GCV

**Background:** Dulong's empirical formula relates the calorific value of a coal to its elemental composition (from ultimate analysis). It is a staple KTU 14-mark derivation.

**Assumption:** Heat of combustion is additive for each combustible element (C, H, S), with oxygen correction for H already bound as O₂.

**Step 1:** Combustion of 1 kg of Carbon (C):
- $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ 
- Heat released = **8,080 kcal/kg of C**

**Step 2:** Combustion of 1 kg of Hydrogen (H):
- $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$
- Heat released = **34,500 kcal/kg of H₂**

**Step 3:** However, a portion of H in coal is already combined with O₂. The O₂ associated with this H cannot burn additional H₂. The mass of H "neutralized" by oxygen is $\dfrac{O}{8}$ (since $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$ implies 32 g O₂ neutralizes 4 g H, so 1 g O₂ neutralizes 4/32 = 1/8 g H₂).

**Step 4:** Net effective hydrogen is $H - \dfrac{O}{8}$.

**Step 5:** Combustion of 1 kg of Sulfur (S):
- $S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$
- Heat released = **2,240 kcal/kg of S**

**Step 6:** Summing contributions from C, H, S (per kg of coal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 8080 \cdot C \;+\; 34500 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) \;+\; 2240 \cdot S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:** C, H, O, S are mass fractions of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and sulfur respectively from ultimate analysis (dry, ash-free or as-received basis — KTU exam convention: as-received).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Air Required for Combustion of Furnace Oil

**Given (Typical KTU Problem):**
A furnace oil has the following ultimate analysis (by mass):
C = 84%, H = 12%, S = 3%, O = 1% (ash-free, as received).
Calculate the theoretical air required for complete combustion per kg of fuel.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Calculate mass of O₂ required for each element.**

For Carbon: 
$$
O_2^{(C)} = \dfrac{32}{12} \times 0.84 = 2.667 \times 0.84 = 2.240 \; kg
$$

For Hydrogen:
$$
O_2^{(H)} = \dfrac{32}{4} \times 0.12 = 8.0 \times 0.12 = 0.960 \; kg
$$

For Sulfur:
$$
O_2^{(S)} = \dfrac{32}{32} \times 0.03 = 1.0 \times 0.03 = 0.030 \; kg
$$

**Step 2: Subtract the oxygen already present in the fuel.**
The 1% O in fuel is already combined and cannot consume external O₂. Convert mass of fuel-O to mass of O₂ neutralized: 1 kg O is part of 0.01 kg fuel → available O₂ = 0.01 kg.

**Step 3: Net external O₂ required.**
$$
O_2^{net} = 2.240 + 0.960 + 0.030 - 0.010 = 3.220 \; kg \; O_2 / kg \; fuel
$$

**Step 4: Convert O₂ to theoretical air.**
Air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass (or 21% by volume).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{th} &= \dfrac{O_2^{net}}{0.232} \\
A_{th} &= \dfrac{3.220}{0.232} = 13.879 \; kg \; air / kg \; fuel
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: If 25% excess air is supplied,**
$$
A_{actual} = 13.879 \times 1.25 = 17.349 \; kg \; air / kg \; fuel
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU, ALWAYS show the O₂ balance table (Carbon → CO₂, H₂ → H₂O, S → SO₂) in tabular form for full marks.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Wobbe Index for Gaseous Fuels

**Concept:** When two gases are burned through the *same* burner orifice at the *same* pressure, the heat released is proportional to the Wobbe Index.

**Step 1: Flow rate through an orifice (choked flow assumption):**
$$
\dot{m} \propto \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{T \cdot \rho_{gas}}} \propto \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{T \cdot G \cdot \rho_{air}}}
$$

Where $G$ is the specific gravity (relative to air).

**Step 2: Heat released = mass flow × calorific value:**
$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \times CV \propto \dfrac{CV}{\sqrt{G}}
$$

**Step 3: Define Wobbe Index:**
$$
\boxed{W = \dfrac{CV}{\sqrt{G}}}
$$

**Engineering Use:** Gases with similar Wobbe Indices are **interchangeable** in burners. NG has W ≈ 12,000; LPG has W ≈ 17,000. Hence, LPG cannot directly replace NG in domestic burners without orifice change.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1:** Define Calorific Value of a fuel. Distinguish between GCV and NCV. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **Calorific Value (CV)** of a fuel is the total amount of heat liberated by the complete combustion of a unit mass (or unit volume for gases) of the fuel, with combustion products cooled to the original fuel temperature.

- **GCV (Gross/Higher Calorific Value):** Heat released when the water vapor formed during combustion is condensed to liquid, recovering the latent heat of condensation (≈ 587 kcal/kg of H₂).
- **NCV (Net/Lower Calorific Value):** Heat released when water vapor escapes as vapor (latent heat NOT recovered).
- **Relation:** $NCV = GCV - 9H \times (Latent\;heat)$ or simplified $NCV = GCV - 587 \times H_2$ (where H₂ is kg of hydrogen per kg of fuel).
- For **boiler design**, NCV is more relevant because flue gases exit hot and water leaves as vapor.

---

#### **Question 2:** What is the significance of "Flash Point" and "Pour Point" of a fuel oil? `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
- **Flash Point (FP):** The lowest temperature at which the oil gives off enough vapor to form an ignitable mixture with air near its surface. *Significance:* **Safety parameter for storage and transportation.** Lower FP = higher fire hazard. Kerosene has FP ≈ 35 °C; Furnace Oil has FP ≥ 66 °C (safe category).
- **Pour Point (PP):** The lowest temperature at which the oil will just flow under standard test conditions. *Significance:* Determines **minimum storage and pipeline temperature** in cold climates. Pumping systems must keep oil ~5 °C above PP to avoid solidification and pipe blockage.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently (i) **forget the O₂ already present in the fuel** in air-requirement problems (loses 2 marks), (ii) **confuse mass and volume percentages of air** (air is 23.2% O₂ by mass, 21% by volume — using the wrong one loses 3 marks), and (iii) **fail to mention excess air** in real combustion (always 5–25% in practice). Also, **Dulong's formula coefficients** must be remembered EXACTLY — the KTU evaluator deducts 1 mark per incorrect constant.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis: C = 72%, H = 5%, O = 10%, N = 2%, S = 1%, Ash = 10%. Calculate **(i)** the GCV using Dulong's formula, and **(ii)** the NCV of the coal. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Proximate Analysis of Coal** in detail. List its components and the procedure to determine each. State the significance of the Volatile Matter content. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (i) — Dulong's Formula:**

Apply $GCV = 8080 C + 34500 \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 S$

- Convert percentages to fractions: C = 0.72, H = 0.05, O = 0.10, S = 0.01.
- Calculate effective hydrogen:
$$
H - \dfrac{O}{8} = 0.05 - \dfrac{0.10}{8} = 0.05 - 0.0125 = 0.0375
$$

- Compute each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
8080 \times 0.72 &= 5817.6 \; kcal/kg \\
34500 \times 0.0375 &= 1293.75 \; kcal/kg \\
2240 \times 0.01 &= 22.40 \; kcal/kg
\end{aligned}
$$

- Sum:
$$
GCV = 5817.6 + 1293.75 + 22.40 = 7133.75 \; kcal/kg
$$

**(a) Part (ii) — NCV:**
Using $NCV = GCV - 587 \times H_2$ (latent heat of water = 587 kcal/kg of H₂):
- $H_2$ per kg of coal = 0.05 kg

$$
NCV = 7133.75 - (587 \times 0.05) = 7133.75 - 29.35 = 7104.40 \; kcal/kg
$$

**(b) Proximate Analysis — Detailed Explanation:**

**Definition:** Proximate analysis is a **standardized empirical test** that determines four major constituents of coal: Moisture (M), Volatile Matter (VM), Ash (A), and Fixed Carbon (FC). It indicates coal's **combustion behavior**, **rank**, and **industrial suitability**.

**Procedure (Step-wise):**
1. **Moisture (M):** Coal sample (1 g, -60 mesh) is heated in an oven at **105–110 °C for 1 hour**. Weight loss = Moisture %.
2. **Volatile Matter (VM):** The dried sample is then heated in a **covered crucible at 925 ± 20 °C for 7 minutes** in a muffle furnace. The weight loss (after subtracting moisture) = VM %.
3. **Ash (A):** The residual coke is then burned in an **open crucible at 700–750 °C till constant weight**. Remaining residue = Ash %.
4. **Fixed Carbon (FC):** Determined **by difference**: $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$.

**Sum Rule:** $M + VM + A + FC = 100\%$

**Significance of Volatile Matter:**
- High VM → Coal ignites easily, produces **long luminous flame**, suitable for **gasification and domestic use** (e.g., Lignite, Peat).
- Low VM → Coal is **hard to ignite, short flame, smokeless**, suitable for **blast furnace coke** (e.g., Anthracite).
- VM determines **combustion chamber design** and **burner geometry**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** A furnace oil has the ultimate analysis: C = 85%, H = 11%, S = 3%, O = 1%. Calculate: **(i)** the minimum mass of air required for complete combustion per kg of oil, and **(ii)** the flue gas composition on dry basis (in volume %) if 20% excess air is used. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the important properties of **Natural Gas** as a fuel. What is the **Wobbe Index** and why is it important? (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (i) — Theoretical Air:**

Apply stoichiometric O₂ demands:

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2^{(C)} &= \dfrac{32}{12} \times 0.85 = 2.667 \times 0.85 = 2.267 \; kg \\
O_2^{(H)} &= \dfrac{32}{4} \times 0.11 = 8.0 \times 0.11 = 0.880 \; kg \\
O_2^{(S)} &= \dfrac{32}{32} \times 0.03 = 1.0 \times 0.03 = 0.030 \; kg
\end{aligned}
$$

Total O₂ needed for combustion:
$$
O_2^{gross} = 2.267 + 0.880 + 0.030 = 3.177 \; kg
$$

Fuel already contains O = 0.01 kg. Since O in fuel is part of compounds (already oxidized), it cannot burn additional H/C. But we should subtract the "available" O₂ equivalent: mass of O₂ that the 0.01 kg O would correspond to = 0.01 kg O (already in elemental form within the molecules — actually for KTU convention, we subtract the mass of O₂ "freed" which equals the O mass already, since we consider 1 g O = 1 g O₂ in such problems by convention).

$$
O_2^{net} = 3.177 - 0.010 = 3.167 \; kg
$$

Theoretical air (mass basis):
$$
A_{th} = \dfrac{3.167}{0.232} = 13.651 \; kg \; air / kg \; oil
$$

**(a) Part (ii) — Flue Gas Composition (20% excess air):**

Actual air supplied = $13.651 \times 1.20 = 16.381$ kg/kg fuel.
Actual O₂ supplied = $16.381 \times 0.232 = 3.800$ kg.
O₂ in flue gas (excess) = $3.800 - 3.167 = 0.633$ kg.

Convert to **moles per kg fuel** (using mol. wt: CO₂ = 44, H₂O = 18, SO₂ = 64, O₂ = 32, N₂ = 28):

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO_2 &: \dfrac{0.85 \times (44/12)}{44} = 0.85/12 \times 44/44 \; \Rightarrow \; \text{moles} = \dfrac{0.85}{12} = 0.0708 \; kmol \\
H_2O &: \dfrac{0.11 \times (18/2)}{18} = 0.11/2 = 0.0550 \; kmol \\
SO_2 &: \dfrac{0.03}{32} = 0.0009375 \; kmol \\
O_2 (excess) &: \dfrac{0.633}{32} = 0.01978 \; kmol \\
N_2 &: (Air - O_2) = (16.381 - 3.800) = 12.581 \; kg \; N_2 \Rightarrow \dfrac{12.581}{28} = 0.4493 \; kmol
\end{aligned}
$$

**On dry basis**, we exclude H₂O:
- Total dry moles = 0.0708 + 0.0009375 + 0.01978 + 0.4493 = 0.5408 kmol

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO_2 &= \dfrac{0.0708}{0.5408} \times 100 = 13.09\% \\
SO_2 &= \dfrac{0.0009375}{0.5408} \times 100 = 0.17\% \\
O_2 &= \dfrac{0.01978}{0.5408} \times 100 = 3.66\% \\
N_2 &= \dfrac{0.4493}{0.5408} \times 100 = 83.08\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** Sum = 13.09 + 0.17 + 3.66 + 83.08 = 100.00% ✓

**(b) Properties of Natural Gas:**

Natural Gas is primarily **methane (CH₄, 85–95%)** with traces of C₂H₆, C₃H₈, CO₂, N₂, and H₂S. Key properties:

1. **High Calorific Value:** 8,500–10,500 kcal/Nm³ (almost double that of producer gas).
2. **Low Specific Gravity (0.55–0.70):** Rises easily, disperses quickly in air if leaked (safer than LPG).
3. **Clean Combustion:** Negligible SO₂, minimal ash, low particulate emissions.
4. **High Ignition Temperature (540 °C):** Safer in handling compared to petrol.
5. **Narrow Flammability Range (5–15%):** Limits explosion risk.
6. **No Storage Complexity:** Transported via pipelines; no atomization or pulverization needed.
7. **Easy to Control Combustion:** Instant flame ON/OFF, precise heat input regulation.

**Wobbe Index:**
- **Definition:** $W = \dfrac{CV}{\sqrt{G}}$, where CV is calorific value and G is specific gravity.
- **Importance:** It characterizes the **interchangeability of fuel gases**. Two gases with the same Wobbe Index will deliver **the same amount of heat** through the same burner orifice at the same gas pressure.
- **Practical Use:** Domestic burners, industrial furnaces, and gas turbines are designed for a specific W range. If the supply gas changes (e.g., NG vs LPG), the orifice size must be changed to maintain heat output, otherwise flame lift-off or flashback can occur.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Fuel Oil:** Defined by viscosity, density, flash point, pour point, sulfur, and Conradson carbon residue. **Flash point > 66 °C is the safety threshold.**
- **Coal Analysis:**
  - *Proximate* → M, VM, A, FC (sum = 100%, by difference for FC).
  - *Ultimate* → C, H, N, S, O, A (oxygen by difference).
- **Dulong's Formula (CRITICAL):** $GCV = 8080\,C + 34500\!\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S$ — remember constants EXACTLY.
- **NCV:** $NCV = GCV - 587 \times H_2$ (in kg per kg fuel).
- **Air Calculation:** Always use **23.2% O₂ by mass** in air, not 21%.
- **Excess Air:** Real combustion uses 5–25% EA to ensure complete combustion; stoichiometric (theoretical) air is the MINIMUM.
- **Gases:** Wobbe Index $W = \dfrac{CV}{\sqrt{G}}$ governs **burner interchangeability**.
- **Flammability Limits:** LFL and UFL define the explosive envelope; only burn within.
- **Coal Rank** (increasing carbon): Peat → Lignite → Bituminous → Anthracite.
- **Flue Gas (dry basis):** Contains CO₂, SO₂, O₂ (excess), N₂; H₂O is excluded. Sum = 100%.
- **Orsat Analysis Assumption:** $N_2 = \dfrac{79}{21} \times O_2$ (by volume in dry flue gas).
- **For KTU 14-mark problems:** Show ALL chemical equations for C, H, S combustion; present air-balance in **tabular form**; quote excess air %; present final flue gas composition in dry vol%.
- **Units Discipline:** kcal/kg for solids/liquids; kcal/Nm³ for gases; m³ at STP (0 °C, 1 atm) or Nm³ at 15 °C.
- **Safety Hierarchy:** NG > LPG > FO > Coal (in ease of handling and clean combustion).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage, Handling and Preparation of Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Storage, Handling and Preparation of Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Utilities – Fuels Definition:**
> A **fuel** is any combustible substance which, upon combustion, releases thermal energy that can be economically utilized for industrial, domestic, or process heating applications. In the context of **Process Utilities**, fuels are classified as primary energy carriers that require systematic **storage, handling, and preparation** before they can be fed to boilers, furnaces, kilns, reactors, and incinerators.

**Storage, Handling, and Preparation** of fuels refers to the integrated set of engineering operations that ensure a fuel is:
- **Safely stockpiled** at the plant site (storage),
- **Safely transported** from storage to the point of use (handling),
- **Conditioned** to a form suitable for efficient combustion (preparation).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Scope:** The syllabus explicitly covers (a) types of fuels – solid, liquid, and gaseous; (b) storage systems – bunkers, tanks, holders; (c) handling – conveyors, pumps, pipelines; (d) preparation – crushing, pulverizing, oil heating, and gas mixing; (e) safety and fire prevention.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your **kitchen at home**. You don't throw rice bags, LPG cylinders, and matchsticks all over the floor. You have:
- A **dedicated shelf** (storage),
- A **ladle or spoon** to transfer (handling),
- A **cleaning and sorting step** before cooking – washing rice, checking gas pressure (preparation).

Similarly, in a process plant:
- **Storage** = A dedicated bunker (for coal) or a cylindrical tank (for furnace oil) or a gas holder (for producer gas).
- **Handling** = Belt conveyors, screw feeders, or oil transfer pumps that physically move fuel from storage to burner.
- **Preparation** = Pulverizing coal to a fine powder, heating heavy oil to reduce viscosity, or filtering natural gas to remove moisture.

## 1.3 Classification of Fuels – The Master Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three primary categories of industrial fuels:**

| Class | Examples | Physical State | Common Industrial Use |
|-------|----------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Solid Fuels** | Coal, Coke, Lignite, Bagasse, Wood | Solid | Power plants, cement kilns, metallurgical furnaces |
| **Liquid Fuels** | Furnace Oil (FO), LDO, Diesel, Kerosene, Petrol | Liquid | Industrial boilers, IC engines, furnaces |
| **Gaseous Fuels** | Natural Gas (NG), LPG, Producer Gas, Water Gas, Coal Gas | Gas | Domestic burners, industrial heaters, gas turbines |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metric:** The calorific value (CV) is the most important performance parameter. Typical values:
> - Bituminous coal: **25–30 MJ/kg**
> - Furnace oil: **40–42 MJ/kg**
> - Natural gas: **35–38 MJ/Nm³**
> - LPG: **46–50 MJ/kg**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solid Fuels – Coal Storage, Handling, and Preparation

### 2.1.1 Storage of Solid Fuels

Coal is the most common solid fuel. The storage design depends on the **rank, moisture content, and weather exposure**.

**Storage Methods:**

1. **Open Stockyard (Outdoor Storage):**
   - Used for large quantities (1000+ tonnes).
   - Heaps are built using stacker-reclaimers.
   - Vulnerable to **spontaneous combustion** due to oxidation of pyrites and moisture re-absorption.
   - Loss of CV due to weathering can be **2–5%** over 6 months.

2. **Covered Bunkers / Silos:**
   - Closed reinforced-concrete or steel structures.
   - Used for pulverized coal (PF) storage in power plants.
   - Capacity: typically **8–12 hours of boiler demand**.

3. **Overhead Bunkers:**
   - Positioned directly above the boiler / pulverizer.
   - Gravity discharge through chutes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Spontaneous Combustion Mechanism:** Coal + O₂ → Heat (slow oxidation) → Temperature rises → Pyrite (FeS₂) accelerates oxidation → Critical temp ~80°C → Self-ignition.
> **Mitigation:** Compact stacking, good ventilation, water sprays, anti-oxidant coatings.

### 2.1.2 Handling of Solid Fuels

The handling system must deliver coal at a controlled rate to the preparation unit.

**Common Equipment:**

| Equipment | Function | Typical Application |
|-----------|----------|---------------------|
| **Belt Conveyor** | Continuous transport of lumps | Stockyard to crusher house |
| **Bucket Elevator** | Vertical lifting | Crushed coal to bunker |
| **Vibrating Feeder** | Controlled discharge | Bunker to crusher |
| **Screw Conveyor** | Enclosed transport | Pulverized coal to burner |
| **Pneumatic Conveyor** | Dust-tight transport | PF to burners |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Throughput Rule:** Belt conveyors are sized for a 1.5× peak load to account for moisture-induced stickiness and lump size variation.

### 2.1.3 Preparation of Solid Fuels

The goal is to **increase surface area** and ensure **uniform combustion**.

**Step-by-step Preparation Train:**

1. **Crushing** – Primary jaw crusher reduces lumps from 200 mm to ~50 mm.
2. **Screening** – Vibratory screens separate by size; oversize is recirculated.
3. **Drying** – Removes surface moisture using flue gas or hot air (moisture reduced to <2%).
4. **Pulverizing** – Ball mills or roller mills reduce to **<75 microns (200 mesh)** for PF firing.
5. **Classification** – Cyclone separators return oversize to mill.
6. **Storage & Silos** – Pulverized coal is stored in sealed bunkers under inert gas (CO₂ or N₂) blanket to prevent explosion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Dust Explosion Hazard:** Pulverized coal in air (concentration 0.1–0.5 kg/m³) is highly explosive. **Inert gas blanketing** is mandatory.

## 2.2 Liquid Fuels – Oil Storage, Handling, and Preparation

### 2.2.1 Storage of Liquid Fuels

Petroleum-based fuels (furnace oil, diesel, LDO) are stored in **bulk tanks** of various designs:

| Tank Type | Design | Application |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| **Fixed Cone Roof Tank** | Welded steel with conical top | Low-volatility fuels (FO, diesel) |
| **Floating Roof Tank** | Roof floats on liquid surface | High-volatility fuels (petrol, naphtha); reduces evaporation loss |
| **Spherical / Horton Sphere** | Pressure vessel | LPG, butane |
| **Horizontal Cylindrical** | Pressure-rated | Small LPG / LNG storage |
| **Underground Tank** | Buried concrete vault | Fire safety zones |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Code:** API 650 (Atmospheric), API 620 (Low Pressure), ASME Sec VIII (Pressure Vessels).
> **Bund Wall Rule:** Tank farm bund walls must contain **110% of the largest tank volume** to prevent spillage spread.

### 2.2.2 Handling of Liquid Fuels

**Equipment:**
- **Centrifugal pumps** for low-viscosity fuels (diesel, kerosene).
- **Positive displacement (PD) pumps** – gear pumps, screw pumps – for viscous FO.
- **Pipelines** with steam-traced lines in cold climates.

### 2.2.3 Preparation of Liquid Fuels

Furnace oil is **viscous** at ambient temperature and cannot atomize properly. The preparation train is:

1. **Pre-heating** – FO heated to **60–80°C** using steam coils to reduce viscosity to ~50 cSt.
2. **Pumping** – Through strainers to remove particulates.
3. **Secondary heating** – To **100–120°C** to reach atomization viscosity (15–25 cSt).
4. **Atomization** – Steam-assisted or pressure-jet atomizer in the burner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Viscosity Target for Atomization:** 15–25 cSt (centistokes) at the burner tip.

## 2.3 Gaseous Fuels – Gas Storage, Handling, and Preparation

### 2.3.1 Storage of Gaseous Fuels

| Method | Pressure | Used For |
|--------|----------|----------|
| **Low-pressure gas holders (water-seal type)** | ~4–10 kPa | Producer gas, blast furnace gas |
| **High-pressure cylinders** | 15 MPa | LPG, CNG |
| **Underground cavern storage** | Hydrostatic | Natural gas (strategic reserves) |
| **Bullet tanks** | 1.7 MPa | LNG, LPG at industrial sites |

### 2.3.2 Handling and Preparation

- **Pressure reduction** at reducing stations.
- **Dehumidification** using silica gel or molecular sieves.
- **Desulfurization** for H₂S removal (e.g., activated carbon scrubbers).
- **LPG vaporization** in vaporizers (water bath or steam).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table – KTU 2024 Module 2**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $GCV = 33.7C + 144.2\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9.3S$ | Dulong's formula for solid/liquid fuels (where C, H, O, S are % masses) | MJ/kg |
| 2 | $NCV = GCV - 2.5 \times (9H + M)$ | Net Calorific Value (LHV), M is % moisture | MJ/kg |
| 3 | $NCV = GCV - 9H \times 2.44$ | Simplified NCV (latent heat of vaporization of water) | MJ/kg |
| 4 | $O_2^{th} = \dfrac{C}{12} + \dfrac{H}{4} + \dfrac{S}{32} - \dfrac{O}{32}$ | Theoretical O₂ requirement (moles per kg fuel) | mol/kg |
| 5 | $A^{th} = \dfrac{O_2^{th}}{0.21} = 4.76 \times O_2^{th}$ | Theoretical air (21% O₂ by volume) | mol/kg |
| 6 | $\% \text{ Excess Air} = \dfrac{A^{act} - A^{th}}{A^{th}} \times 100$ | Excess air percentage | % |
| 7 | $\eta_{comb} = \dfrac{GCV - \text{stack losses}}{GCV} \times 100$ | Combustion efficiency (indirect method) | % |
| 8 | $Q = m \times GCV \times \eta$ | Heat release rate (mass flow m, thermal efficiency η) | kW or MW |
| 9 | $V_{air} = 11.6 \times C + 34.8 \times \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35 \times S$ | Theoretical air volume per kg fuel | Nm³/kg |
| 10 | $V_{flue} = V_{air} + V_{N_2} + V_{CO_2} + V_{H_2O}$ | Wet flue gas volume (when excess air present) | Nm³/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant Highlighted in Bold:**
> - **2.44 MJ/kg** = latent heat of vaporization of water at 25°C (per kg H₂ condensed from combustion)
> - **4.76** = ratio of air to O₂ by volume (100/21)
> - **9H** = mass of water produced from H mass fraction H

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The storage, handling, and preparation system is the **backbone** of any thermal power plant or process industry. A poorly designed system leads to:
- **Inconsistent fuel quality** at the burner (combustion instability).
- **Increased emissions** (CO, NOx, soot).
- **Spontaneous fires** in coal yards (e.g., 2020 incidents at NTPC plants).
- **Boiler tube failures** due to oil slagging and corrosion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Mantra:** "**No flame is more dangerous than the one you cannot control** – and you cannot control what you cannot store, handle, and prepare properly."

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Dulong's Formula for GCV

### Background
**Dulong's empirical formula** is widely used in industrial labs to estimate GCV from ultimate analysis (C, H, O, S in % by mass).

### Starting Principle
Each combustible element releases a known heat per kg:

| Element | Heat released (MJ per kg of element) | Source of energy |
|---------|---------------------------------------|------------------|
| Carbon (C) | 33.7 | Forms CO₂ |
| Hydrogen (H) | 144.2 | Forms H₂O |
| Sulphur (S) | 9.3 | Forms SO₂ |

**Hydrogen correction:** When H burns, it consumes some oxygen already present in the fuel (the "O" term). The equivalent oxygen tied up is O/8 (since O₂ has molar mass 32 and combines with 4H to form 2H₂O, mass basis gives 1/8 correction).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$GCV = 33.7 \, C + 144.2 \left( H - \frac{O}{8} \right) + 9.3 \, S$$

Where C, H, O, S are mass percentages of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and sulphur in the fuel.

### Numerical Worked Example

**Given ultimate analysis of a coal sample:**
- C = 70%
- H = 5%
- O = 8%
- S = 1.5%
- N = 1.2%
- Ash = 12%
- Moisture = 2.3%

**Step 1: Verify the analysis sums to 100%**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= C + H + O + S + N + \text{Ash} + M \\
&= 70 + 5 + 8 + 1.5 + 1.2 + 12 + 2.3 \\
&= 100.0 \, \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into Dulong's formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
GCV &= (33.7 \times 70) + 144.2 \left( 5 - \frac{8}{8} \right) + (9.3 \times 1.5) \\
&= 2359.0 + 144.2 \times (5 - 1) + 13.95 \\
&= 2359.0 + 144.2 \times 4 + 13.95 \\
&= 2359.0 + 576.8 + 13.95 \\
&= 2949.75 \text{ MJ per 100 kg} \\
&= 29.50 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate NCV (using simplified form)**

$$NCV = GCV - 9H \times 2.44$$

$$\begin{aligned}
NCV &= 29.50 - 9 \times 5 \times \frac{2.44}{100} \\
&= 29.50 - 1.098 \\
&= 28.40 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The fuel has a GCV of **29.50 MJ/kg** and NCV of **28.40 MJ/kg**. The difference (1.10 MJ/kg) is the latent heat lost in the moisture and water vapour in flue gas.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Theoretical Air and Flue Gas Volume

### Starting Principle
Combustion reactions for 1 kg of fuel:
- C + O₂ → CO₂
- H₂ + ½ O₂ → H₂O (equivalent to 2H + ½ O₂ → H₂O per kg basis)
- S + O₂ → SO₂

### Step-by-Step Derivation of Theoretical O₂

**For 1 kg of fuel containing C, H, O, S kg of each element:**

**Oxygen consumed by Carbon:**

$$O_{2,C} = \frac{C}{12} \text{ kg-mol of O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$

**Oxygen consumed by Hydrogen (note: 1 mol H₂ needs ½ mol O₂, so 2 kg H needs 0.5 mol O₂; per kg H: 0.5/2 = 0.25):**

$$O_{2,H} = \frac{H}{4} \text{ kg-mol of O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$

**Oxygen consumed by Sulphur:**

$$O_{2,S} = \frac{S}{32} \text{ kg-mol of O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$

**Oxygen already present in fuel (subtracted):**

$$O_{2, fuel} = \frac{O}{32} \text{ kg-mol of O}_2$$

**Net theoretical O₂ required:**

$$\boxed{O_2^{th} = \frac{C}{12} + \frac{H}{4} + \frac{S}{32} - \frac{O}{32} \text{ kg-mol per kg fuel}}$$

**Theoretical air (21% O₂ by volume = 1/4.76 by mole):**

$$A^{th} = \frac{O_2^{th}}{0.21} = 4.76 \times O_2^{th} \text{ kg-mol air per kg fuel}$$

**Volumetric basis at NTP (22.4 m³ per kg-mol):**

$$V_{air} = 4.76 \times O_2^{th} \times 22.4 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ per kg fuel}$$

### Numerical Example (continuation of Section 3.1)

Using C = 0.70, H = 0.05, S = 0.015, O = 0.08 (per kg fuel):

$$\begin{aligned}
O_2^{th} &= \frac{0.70}{12} + \frac{0.05}{4} + \frac{0.015}{32} - \frac{0.08}{32} \\
&= 0.0583 + 0.0125 + 0.00047 - 0.0025 \\
&= 0.0688 \text{ kg-mol per kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

$$A^{th} = 4.76 \times 0.0688 = 0.3275 \text{ kg-mol air per kg fuel}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{air} &= 0.3275 \times 22.4 \\
&= 7.336 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ per kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**At 25% excess air:**

$$V_{air, actual} = 1.25 \times 7.336 = 9.17 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ per kg fuel}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Effect of Excess Air on Combustion

| Excess Air (%) | O₂ in flue gas (dry) | Efficiency Loss (%) | Notes |
|----------------|----------------------|---------------------|-------|
| 0 | 0% | 0 | Incomplete combustion likely; CO present |
| 5 | 0.9% | 0.5 | Ideal for gas firing |
| 10 | 1.7% | 1.0 | Ideal for oil firing |
| 15 | 2.6% | 1.5 | Common for FO firing |
| 20 | 3.5% | 2.5 | Acceptable for coal |
| 30 | 5.2% | 4.0 | High heat loss – troubleshoot |
| 50 | 8.5% | 7.0 | Wasting fuel; investigate leaks |

> [!WARNING]
> **The Efficiency Loss column above is approximate.** The exact loss depends on flue gas temperature and composition. Use the Siegert formula for precise calculations.

## 3.4 Derivation 3 – Stack (Flue Gas) Heat Loss

**Siegert's Empirical Formula:**

$$\eta_{stack} = \frac{T_{fg} - T_{amb}}{T_{fg}} \times \left[ \frac{A}{2} \times \frac{CO_2^{max}}{CO_2^{actual}} + B \right]$$

Where:
- $T_{fg}$ = flue gas temperature
- $T_{amb}$ = ambient temperature
- $CO_2^{max}$ = theoretical max CO₂ in flue gas
- $A \approx 0.5$ (constant for coal)
- $B \approx 0.007$ (correction for moisture)

**Combustion efficiency:**

$$\eta_{comb} = 100 - \eta_{stack}$$

This is the **indirect method** of efficiency calculation, used widely in KTU board problems.

## 4.4 Decision Matrix – Storage Method Selection

| Fuel Type | Quantity Scale | Recommended Storage | Safety Priority |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Coal (Lump) | > 10,000 T | Open stockyard with stacker-reclaimer | Spontaneous combustion control |
| Coal (Pulverized) | < 1,000 T | Sealed silos with inert gas blanketing | Dust explosion prevention |
| Furnace Oil | < 50,000 m³ | Cone roof tank, bund-walled farm | Fire foam pourers, dyke walls |
| Petrol/Naphtha | Any | Floating roof tank | Vapor recovery, flame arrestors |
| LPG | < 100 T | Horton sphere or bullet | Pressure relief, water spray deluge |
| Natural Gas | Pipeline | Pressure reducing station | Slam-shut valves, odorization |
| Producer Gas | < 50,000 m³/h | Water-seal gas holder | Water seal integrity, flashback arrestors |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define the term "Calorific Value" of a fuel. Differentiate between Gross Calorific Value (GCV) and Net Calorific Value (NCV).** [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The calorific value of a fuel is defined as the total amount of heat liberated per unit mass (or per unit volume in case of gases) when the fuel is completely burned and the products of combustion are cooled to the reference temperature (usually 25°C).

**Difference between GCV and NCV:**

| Aspect | GCV (HHV) | NCV (LHV) |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Definition | Heat released assuming all water vapour condenses | Heat released with water remaining as vapour |
