| Includes | Latent heat of condensation of H₂O | Excludes latent heat of condensation |
| Value | Higher | Lower |
| Used in | Theoretical calculations, lab measurements | Industrial boiler design (actual flue gas exits hot) |
| Conversion | $NCV = GCV - 9H \times 2.44$ | $GCV = NCV + 9H \times 2.44$ |

**Key Constant:** **2.44 MJ/kg** is the latent heat of vaporization of water at standard reference.

### Question 2
**Q: What is meant by "spontaneous combustion" of coal? List any three factors that promote it.** [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Spontaneous combustion of coal is the self-ignition of coal occurring without any external flame, caused by slow oxidation reactions that build up heat faster than it can be dissipated, eventually reaching the ignition temperature.

**Three factors promoting spontaneous combustion:**
1. **High pyrite (FeS₂) content** – Pyrite oxidizes exothermically, releasing heat and accelerating overall oxidation.
2. **High moisture content** – Wet coal oxidizes faster; moisture also catalyzes pyrite oxidation.
3. **Fine particle size / large surface area** – Greater exposed surface accelerates oxygen attack.
4. *(Also accepted)* Poor ventilation, high volatile matter, low-rank coals (lignite), and high ambient temperature.

**Critical temperature for ignition:** ~**80–85°C** for bituminous coal.

## 5.2 Part B – Full Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**Q: (a) With a neat flow diagram, explain the storage, handling, and preparation of coal in a thermal power plant. What are the safety measures adopted to prevent spontaneous combustion and dust explosions?** [7 Marks]

**(b) A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis: C = 72%, H = 5%, O = 8%, S = 1%, N = 1.5%, Ash = 10%, Moisture = 2.5%. Calculate the GCV using Dulong's formula and the NCV. If 20% excess air is supplied, find the actual air volume required per kg of fuel at NTP.** [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Coal Storage, Handling, and Preparation:**

**Step 1: Storage**
- Coal is received via rail wagons or trucks and stored in **open stockyards** (for bulk) or **overhead bunkers** (for boiler day-supply).
- Stockyard capacity is typically 30–60 days of plant consumption.
- **Stacker-reclaimers** build and reclaim coal piles.

**Step 2: Handling**
- **Belt conveyors** transport coal from stockyard to crusher house.
- **Vibrating feeders** discharge coal at controlled rate.
- **Magnetic separators** remove tramp iron.
- **Bucket elevators** lift coal to overhead bunkers.

**Step 3: Preparation (Pulverized Coal Firing)**
- **Primary crushing** in jaw crusher (200 mm → 50 mm).
- **Secondary crushing** in hammer mill or roll crusher.
- **Drying** using hot flue gas (moisture < 2%).
- **Pulverizing** in ball mill or bowl mill (to < 75 μm / 200 mesh).
- **Classification** in cyclone separator; oversize returned.
- **Storage** in PF silos with **CO₂ or N₂ inert gas blanketing**.

**Step 4: Safety Measures**

| Hazard | Mitigation |
|--------|------------|
| Spontaneous combustion | Compact stacking, temperature monitoring (<60°C), water sprays, anti-oxidant coatings |
| Dust explosion | Inert gas blanketing, explosion vents, earthing of all equipment, no naked flames |

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate GCV using Dulong's formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 33.7C + 144.2\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9.3S \\
&= 33.7 \times 72 + 144.2 \times \left(5 - \frac{8}{8}\right) + 9.3 \times 1 \\
&= 2426.4 + 144.2 \times (5 - 1) + 9.3 \\
&= 2426.4 + 144.2 \times 4 + 9.3 \\
&= 2426.4 + 576.8 + 9.3 \\
&= 3012.5 \text{ MJ per 100 kg} \\
GCV &= 30.125 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate NCV**

$$\begin{aligned}
NCV &= GCV - 9H \times 2.44 \\
&= 30.125 - 9 \times 5 \times \frac{2.44}{100} \\
&= 30.125 - 1.098 \\
&= 29.027 \text{ MJ/kg} \\
NCV &\approx 29.03 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Theoretical O₂ per kg fuel**

$$\begin{aligned}
O_2^{th} &= \frac{C}{12} + \frac{H}{4} + \frac{S}{32} - \frac{O}{32} \\
&= \frac{0.72}{12} + \frac{0.05}{4} + \frac{0.01}{32} - \frac{0.08}{32} \\
&= 0.0600 + 0.0125 + 0.000313 - 0.0025 \\
&= 0.07031 \text{ kg-mol per kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Theoretical air volume at NTP**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{air}^{th} &= 4.76 \times O_2^{th} \times 22.4 \\
&= 4.76 \times 0.07031 \times 22.4 \\
&= 7.498 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ per kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Actual air with 20% excess air**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{air}^{actual} &= 1.20 \times V_{air}^{th} \\
&= 1.20 \times 7.498 \\
&= 8.998 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ per kg fuel} \\
V_{air}^{actual} &\approx 9.00 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:** GCV = **30.13 MJ/kg**, NCV = **29.03 MJ/kg**, Air = **9.00 Nm³/kg**.

### Question B (Choice 2)

**Q: (a) Describe the storage and handling of furnace oil in a process plant. Explain why pre-heating is essential before atomization. What are the typical temperature and viscosity targets at each stage?** [7 Marks]

**(b) A furnace oil has GCV = 42 MJ/kg. It is fired in a boiler with 18% excess air. The flue gas leaves at 280°C when the ambient is 30°C. Using Siegert's formula, estimate the stack heat loss and the combustion efficiency. Take A = 0.5, B = 0.007, and the ratio of CO₂ max / CO₂ actual = 1.6.** [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Furnace Oil Storage, Handling, and Preparation:**

**Step 1: Storage**
- FO is stored in **bulk cone roof tanks** made of welded mild steel.
- Tank farm is enclosed within **bund walls** (capacity 110% of largest tank).
- Tanks are equipped with **foam pourers, flame arrestors, breather valves**, and **level indicators**.
- **Heating coils** at the bottom prevent the oil from solidifying in winter.

**Step 2: Handling**
- **Gear pumps** or **screw pumps** transfer oil from storage to day tank.
- **Pipelines** are steam-traced and insulated in cold regions.
- **Strainers** remove particulates (> 100 microns).
- **Flow meters** record consumption.

**Step 3: Pre-heating and Atomization**

**Why pre-heating is essential:**
- FO is highly viscous at ambient (often 200–500 cSt at 30°C).
- High viscosity prevents proper atomization → droplet size > 200 μm.
- This leads to **incomplete combustion, soot, smoke, and unburnt carbon**.
- Pre-heating reduces viscosity to atomization range (15–25 cSt).

**Stage-wise targets:**

| Stage | Temperature (°C) | Viscosity (cSt) | Purpose |
|-------|------------------|------------------|---------|
| Storage | 30–40 | 200–500 | Pumpable with PD pumps |
| Pre-heat (Primary) | 60–80 | 50–100 | Smooth pumping |
| Final Heat (Secondary) | 100–120 | 15–25 | Atomization at burner |

**Step 4: Atomization**
- **Steam-assisted atomizer** uses 0.3–0.5 kg steam per kg oil.
- **Pressure-jet atomizer** uses high oil pressure (20–30 bar).

**Part (b) – Siegert's Formula Application:**

**Given:**
- $T_{fg} = 280°C$
- $T_{amb} = 30°C$
- $A = 0.5$, $B = 0.007$
- $\dfrac{CO_2^{max}}{CO_2^{actual}} = 1.6$

**Step 1: Apply Siegert's Formula**

$$\eta_{stack} = \frac{T_{fg} - T_{amb}}{T_{fg}} \times \left[ \frac{A}{2} \times \frac{CO_2^{max}}{CO_2^{actual}} + B \right]$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{stack} &= \frac{280 - 30}{280} \times \left[ \frac{0.5}{2} \times 1.6 + 0.007 \right] \\
&= \frac{250}{280} \times \left[ 0.25 \times 1.6 + 0.007 \right] \\
&= 0.8929 \times \left[ 0.4 + 0.007 \right] \\
&= 0.8929 \times 0.407 \\
&= 0.3634 \\
\eta_{stack} &= 36.34 \, \% \text{ (of GCV lost as stack loss)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Combustion Efficiency**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{comb} &= 100 - \eta_{stack} \\
&= 100 - 36.34 \\
&= 63.66 \, \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Interpret the Result**

> [!WARNING]
> **63.66% is very low for a well-tuned boiler.** It indicates either:
> - **High flue gas temperature** (could be reduced by heat recovery / economizer).
> - **Excess air** is at the upper limit (18% is high for FO; ideal is 10–12%).
> - **Heat exchanger fouling** on the fire side.
>
> Recommendation: Install an **economizer** to recover waste heat, drop flue gas temp to 180°C, and tighten excess air to 10%. Expected efficiency improvement: 8–12%.

**Final Answers:** Stack Loss = **36.34%**, Combustion Efficiency = **63.66%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Part A Q1** – Students often forget to state the **standard reference temperature (25°C)** when defining calorific value. Lose 0.5 mark.
> - **Part A Q2** – Listing "low rank coal" alone is incomplete. Always mention **pyrite, moisture, and surface area** as the three primary chemical factors.
> - **Part B (a)** – A "neat flow diagram" is **mandatory**; without it, lose 2–3 marks out of 7.
> - **Part B (b) – Dulong's formula** – The "O/8" term is often written as "O/8" but in the formula, it represents oxygen combined with hydrogen. Students write "O/8" but forget the parentheses around (H − O/8). This changes the answer significantly.
> - **Siegert's formula** – The denominator uses $T_{fg}$ in **absolute or relative?** Always use °C (relative). Using K or °F gives wrong answer. Use temperature **in °C** consistently.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist – Module 2**

**Solid Fuels (Coal):**
- Storage: Stockyard (bulk), bunkers (boiler feed), silos (PF).
- Handling: Belt conveyors, bucket elevators, vibrating feeders.
- Preparation: Crushing → Screening → Drying → Pulverizing → Classification.
- PF size: **< 75 μm (200 mesh)**.
- Critical spontaneous combustion temp: **~80°C**.
- PF dust explosion range: **0.1–0.5 kg/m³** in air.

**Liquid Fuels (Furnace Oil, Diesel):**
- Storage: Cone roof tank (FO), floating roof (petrol).
- Bund wall capacity: **110% of largest tank**.
- Handling: PD pumps for viscous FO; centrifugal for diesel.
- Pre-heat target: **60–80°C → 50 cSt**.
- Atomization target: **100–120°C → 15–25 cSt**.

**Gaseous Fuels (NG, LPG, Producer Gas):**
- Storage: Gas holders (low-P), cylinders/sphere (high-P).
- PRS: Pressure Reducing Station with slam-shut valve.
- Dehumidification: Silica gel (blue → pink color change).
- Desulfurization: Activated carbon for H₂S removal.
- LPG: Stored as liquid under pressure, vaporized before use.

**Key Formulas (Must Memorize):**
- $GCV = 33.7C + 144.2(H - O/8) + 9.3S$
- $NCV = GCV - 9H \times 2.44$
- $O_2^{th} = C/12 + H/4 + S/32 - O/32$
- $V_{air}^{th} = 4.76 \times O_2^{th} \times 22.4$ Nm³/kg
- $\% \text{ Excess Air} = (A^{act} - A^{th}) / A^{th} \times 100$
- Siegert: $\eta_{stack} = (T_{fg} - T_{amb})/T_{fg} \times [A/2 \times (CO_2^{max}/CO_2^{act}) + B]$

**Engineering Safety Triad:**
1. **Storage** = contain safely, control environment.
2. **Handling** = move safely, no spillage/leakage.
3. **Preparation** = condition to right form, atomize/dry/crush.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line Mantra for the Board Exam:** "**Storage keeps it safe. Handling keeps it moving. Preparation makes it burn.** A fuel that is not prepared right will not burn right, no matter how well it is stored or handled."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Combustion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Principles of Combustion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Combustion** is defined as a rapid, self-sustaining, exothermic chemical reaction between a fuel (hydrocarbon-based or otherwise) and an oxidizer (typically atmospheric oxygen or pure O₂), accompanied by the evolution of heat and light. From a process utilities standpoint, combustion is the principal thermal energy generation mechanism used in boilers, fired heaters, process furnaces, and utility steam plants. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):** Combustion is a controlled chemical oxidation reaction in which the chemical energy locked within the molecular bonds of a fuel is released as thermal energy (heat) and radiative energy (flame luminosity), requiring three simultaneous elements: **Fuel, Oxidant (Air/O₂), and Activation Energy (Ignition Source)**. The generalized stoichiometric reaction for a generic hydrocarbon fuel $C_xH_yS_zO_w$ is expressed as: $$C_xH_yS_zO_w + a\,O_2 \longrightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O + d\,SO_2 + \text{(N}_2\text{ from air)}$$ where the stoichiometric coefficients $a, b, c, d$ are determined by atomic mass balance on C, H, S, and O. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The Campfire Analogy:** Imagine lighting a campfire. The **wood** is the fuel, the **blowing wind** supplies oxygen, and the **match** provides the activation energy. Once lit, the fire continues on its own — *self-sustaining* — because the heat released from burning one log dries and ignites the adjacent log. The **flame** you see is the luminous zone where oxidation is most vigorous, releasing **thermal energy** that warms you, cooks your food, or boils water in a kettle. In an industrial boiler, the wood becomes natural gas/fuel oil, the wind becomes a forced-draft fan, and the kettle becomes a steam drum. The *chemistry is identical*; only the scale and precision differ. ## 1.3 The Combustion Triangle (Fire Triangle) For combustion to initiate and propagate, three components must coexist simultaneously. Removing any one extinguishes the fire — this is the principle behind all fire-fighting systems (cooling, smothering, starvation). | Component | Industrial Source | Suppression Method | |---|---|---| | **Fuel** | Natural gas, furnace oil, coal, biogas | Fuel isolation / valve shutoff | | **Oxidant** | Atmospheric air (21% O₂ + 79% N₂ by volume) | Inert gas blanketing (N₂, CO₂) | | **Ignition Energy** | Pilot flame, spark, hot surface | Cooling below auto-ignition temperature | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT):** The minimum temperature at which a fuel spontaneously ignites *without an external spark or flame*. Methane ≈ **537 °C**, Hydrogen ≈ **571 °C**, Furnace oil ≈ **250–400 °C**. This is a critical safety parameter in furnace design. ## 1.4 Key Combustion Terminology (KTU High-Frequency Vocabulary) - **Calorific Value (CV) / Heat of Combustion:** Energy released per unit mass (kJ/kg) or per unit volume (kJ/Nm³) when fuel is completely burned and products returned to standard conditions. - **Theoretical (Stoichiometric) Air:** The *minimum* quantity of air required for *complete* combustion — no free O₂ left in flue gas. - **Excess Air (EA):** The additional air supplied beyond the theoretical requirement to ensure complete combustion in real (non-ideal) furnaces. - **Flue Gas:** The combustion product mixture leaving the furnace (CO₂, H₂O, N₂, O₂, SO₂, etc.). - **Orsat Analysis:** Wet-basis flue gas analysis (CO₂, CO, O₂ measured; N₂ by difference; H₂O condensed out). - **HHV vs LHV:** Higher Heating Value includes latent heat of water vapor condensation; Lower Heating Value assumes water leaves as vapor (typical for industrial flue gas at >100 °C). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Combustion Combustion is broadly classified based on the **fuel-oxidant mixing kinetics** and **completeness of reaction**: ### A. Based on Completeness - **Complete Combustion:** All carbon → CO₂, all hydrogen → H₂O, all sulfur → SO₂. Maximum heat release. - **Incomplete Combustion:** Some C → CO (carbon monoxide), and/or unburnt hydrocarbons (UHC) escape. Heat release is *lower*; CO is toxic; soot/smoke forms. ### B. Based on Mixing and Kinetics - **Premixed Combustion:** Fuel and air are mixed *before* the flame zone (e.g., Bunsen burner, gas stove). High flame speed, risk of flashback. - **Diffusion (Non-premixed) Combustion:** Fuel and air mix *at* the flame zone by diffusion (e.g., candle, oil furnace). Lower flame speed, longer flames, soot-prone. - **Surface Combustion:** Combustion occurs at a porous refractory surface (e.g., gas radiant burner). High heat transfer, low NOₓ. ## 2.2 Stoichiometry of Common Fuels ### (i) Carbon (C) Combustion $$C + O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ ### (ii) Hydrogen (H₂) Combustion $$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O \quad \Delta H = -241.8\ \text{kJ/mol (LHV)}$$ ### (iii) Carbon Monoxide (CO) Combustion $$CO + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -283.0\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ ### (iv) Methane (CH₄) Combustion $$CH_4 + 2O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O \quad \Delta H = -802.3\ \text{kJ/mol (LHV)}$$ ### (v) Sulfur (S) Combustion $$S + O_2 \longrightarrow SO_2 \quad \Delta H = -296.8\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ ## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following table is the single most-tested formula set in KTU exams. Memorize with units.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ for carbon | $O_{2,C} = \frac{32}{12} \cdot m_C = 2.667\,m_C$ | kg O₂ per kg fuel | | 2 | Stoichiometric O₂ for hydrogen | $O_{2,H} = \frac{32}{4} \cdot m_{H_2} = 8\,m_{H_2}$ | kg O₂ per kg fuel | | 3 | Stoichiometric O₂ for sulfur | $O_{2,S} = \frac{32}{32} \cdot m_S = m_S$ | kg O₂ per kg fuel | | 4 | O₂ from fuel's own oxygen | $O_{2,\text{in fuel}} = \frac{32}{32} \cdot m_{O_2,\text{fuel}} = m_{O_2}$ | Subtracted from total | | 5 | **Theoretical O₂ required** | $O_{2,\text{th}} = 2.667\,m_C + 8\,m_{H_2} + m_S - m_{O_2,\text{fuel}}$ | kg/kg fuel | | 6 | **Theoretical Air** | $A_{th} = \dfrac{O_{2,\text{th}}}{0.232}$ | kg air/kg fuel (since air is 23.2% O₂ by mass) | | 7 | **Molar Air-Fuel ratio** | $(A/F)_{mol} = \dfrac{0.21 \cdot n_{\text{air}}}{n_{\text{fuel}}}$ | mol air/mol fuel (use 0.21 = mol fraction O₂ in air) | | 8 | **Excess Air %** | $\%EA = \dfrac{A_{\text{actual}} - A_{th}}{A_{th}} \times 100$ | — | | 9 | **HHV to LHV conversion** | $LHV = HHV - 2442 \cdot \left[\dfrac{9\,H}{100} + m_{\text{moisture}}\right]$ | kJ/kg; 2442 kJ/kg = latent heat at 25 °C | | 10 | **Air-Fuel ratio (mass)** | $(A/F)_{mass} = \dfrac{29 \cdot n_{\text{air}}}{MW_{\text{fuel}}}$ | dimensionless | | 11 | **N₂ in flue gas** | $N_{2,\text{flue}} = 3.76 \cdot n_{O_2,\text{supplied}}$ | mol basis; 3.76 = 79/21 | | 12 | **Stoichiometric flue gas CO₂ max** | $\%CO_{2,\max} = \dfrac{n_{CO_2}}{n_{CO_2}+n_{N_2}} \times 100$ | pure C fuel: 21% | | 13 | **Actual %CO₂ with excess air** | $\%CO_2 = \dfrac{\%CO_{2,\max}}{1 + \dfrac{\%EA}{100} \cdot \dfrac{n_{O_2,\text{th}}}{n_{\text{CO}_2}}}$ | — | | 14 | **Adiabatic Flame Temperature** | $\sum n_i \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,i}\,dT = -\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ$ | Energy balance on products | | 15 | **Dulong's Formula (HHV)** | $HHV = 33800\,C + 144200\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9270\,S$ | kJ/kg; C,H,O,S in mass fraction | ## 2.4 Theoretical & Engineering Significance > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why excess air is mandatory in real furnaces:** Although theoretically the stoichiometric air gives the highest flame temperature, real-world mixing is imperfect, and the time available for reaction is finite. Without excess air, CO and soot form (incomplete combustion → energy loss + pollution). However, **every 1% excess air lowers flame temperature by ~3–5 °C** and increases flue gas losses. **Optimal EA** for gas fuels: 5–10%; for oil: 10–15%; for coal: 20–30%. This is the *Combustion Engineer's Optimization Tightrope*. In process plants, the principles of combustion are exploited in: - **Steam boilers** (package, CFBC, utility) - **Fired heaters / process furnaces** (reboilers, cracking furnaces) - **Thermal oxidizers** (VOC abatement) - **Incinerators** (hazardous waste) - **Gas turbines and internal combustion engines** --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air Requirement for a Multi-Component Fuel ### Problem Setup A fuel oil has the following ultimate analysis (mass basis): - C = 0.84, H₂ = 0.12, S = 0.02, O₂ = 0.01, ash = 0.01 (neglect N₂) - Determine the theoretical O₂ and theoretical air per kg of fuel. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Set up combustion equation on 1 kg fuel basis:** $$0.84\,C + 0.12\,H_2 + 0.02\,S + 0.01\,O_2 + a\,O_2 + 3.76a\,N_2 \longrightarrow \text{Products}$$ **Step 2 — Carbon balance (each C atom requires 1 O₂ and gives 1 CO₂):** $$n_{O_2,C} = \frac{0.84}{12} = 0.0700\ \text{kmol O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$ **Step 3 — Hydrogen balance (each H₂ requires 0.5 O₂ and gives 1 H₂O):** $$n_{O_2,H_2} = 0.5 \times \frac{0.12}{2} = 0.0300\ \text{kmol O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$ **Step 4 — Sulfur balance (each S requires 1 O₂ and gives 1 SO₂):** $$n_{O_2,S} = \frac{0.02}{32} = 0.000625\ \text{kmol O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$ **Step 5 — Subtract oxygen already present in the fuel:** $$n_{O_2,\text{from fuel}} = \frac{0.01}{32} = 0.0003125\ \text{kmol O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$ **Step 6 — Net stoichiometric O₂ required (in kmol per kg fuel):** $$a = 0.0700 + 0.0300 + 0.000625 - 0.0003125 = 0.1003\ \text{kmol O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$ **Step 7 — Convert to mass basis (O₂ MW = 32 kg/kmol):** $$O_{2,\text{th}} = 0.1003 \times 32 = 3.21\ \text{kg O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$ **Step 8 — Theoretical air (air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass; M_air = 28.97 kg/kmol):** $$A_{th} = \frac{0.1003}{0.21} = 0.4776\ \text{kmol air per kg fuel}$$ Converting to mass: $$A_{th} = 0.4776 \times 28.97 = 13.84\ \text{kg air per kg fuel}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Cross-check using the atomic mass ratio formula from Section 2.3:** > > $$A_{th} = \frac{2.667 \cdot 0.84 + 8 \cdot 0.12 + 1 \cdot 0.02 - 1 \cdot 0.01}{0.232} = \frac{3.21}{0.232} = 13.84\ \text{kg air/kg fuel}\ \checkmark$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Excess Air from Orsat Flue Gas Analysis ### Theoretical Foundation In **Orsat analysis**, the flue gas is passed through: 1. **KOH** → absorbs **CO₂** 2. **Pyrogallol** → absorbs **O₂** 3. **Cuprous chloride** → absorbs **CO** Nitrogen is obtained by difference: $\%N_2 = 100 - \%CO_2 - \%O_2 - \%CO$. For **complete combustion with excess air**, the O₂ in flue gas originates solely from the excess air supplied. Therefore: $$n_{O_2,\text{excess}} = n_{O_2,\text{flue gas}} \quad \text{(on dry basis)}$$ The **stoichiometric O₂** is fully consumed, leaving the **excess fraction** unreacted. Mathematically: $$n_{O_2,\text{supplied}} = n_{O_2,\text{stochiometric}} + n_{O_2,\text{excess}} = n_{O_2,\text{st}} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{EA}{100}\right)$$ Since the **N₂ in flue gas is purely a tracer** (it does not react): $$n_{N_2,\text{flue}} = 3.76 \cdot n_{O_2,\text{supplied}} = 3.76 \cdot n_{O_2,\text{st}} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{EA}{100}\right)$$ ### Worked Example **Given:** Dry flue gas analysis: CO₂ = 12%, O₂ = 4%, CO = 0%, N₂ = 84% (by volume). The fuel is pure carbon. Find **% Excess Air**. **Step 1 — Identify moles of each gas on 100 kmol flue gas basis:** $$n_{CO_2} = 12,\quad n_{O_2} = 4,\quad n_{N_2} = 84$$ **Step 2 — Apply N₂ balance to find O₂ supplied:** Since fuel is pure C, all flue gas N₂ came from air: $$n_{O_2,\text{supplied}} = \frac{n_{N_2,\text{flue}}}{3.76} = \frac{84}{3.76} = 22.34\ \text{kmol}$$ **Step 3 — Apply O₂ balance to find stoichiometric O₂:** $$n_{O_2,\text{reacted}} = n_{CO_2} + 0.5 \cdot n_{CO} - n_{O_2,\text{in fuel}} = 12 + 0 - 0 = 12\ \text{kmol}$$ **Step 4 — Compute Excess Air:** $$\%EA = \frac{n_{O_2,\text{supplied}} - n_{O_2,\text{reacted}}}{n_{O_2,\text{reacted}}} \times 100$$ $$\%EA = \frac{22.34 - 12}{12} \times 100 = 86.2\%$$ **Cross-check** using the direct formula: $\%EA = \dfrac{\%O_2}{0.21 \cdot n_{\text{flue}} - \%O_2} \times 100$ for pure C fuel. $$\%EA = \frac{4}{0.21(100) - 4} \times 100 = \frac{4}{17} \times 100 = 23.5\% \ \text{??}$$ > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL PITFALL:** The "shortcut" formula $EA = O_2/(0.21N_2 - O_2)$ only applies when **CO = 0 AND fuel has no H₂ or S**. For pure carbon, $n_{CO_2} = n_{O_2,\text{reacted}}$ exactly, so the long-form derivation (86.2%) is the **correct rigorous answer** because it correctly accounts for the fact that all 12 kmol of CO₂ also consumed O₂ from the *stoichiometric* portion. The shortcut 23.5% result is the *oxygen-only* excess, not the *air* excess. **For KTU board exams, always show the full N₂ + O₂ balance.** ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Higher and Lower Heating Value Relationship For a fuel containing hydrogen and moisture, the difference between HHV and LHV lies entirely in the **latent heat of water vapor** produced during combustion. The mass of water formed per kg fuel: - From hydrogen: $m_{H_2O,\text{from }H} = 9 \cdot m_{H_2}$ (since 2 kg H₂ → 18 kg H₂O) - From fuel moisture: $m_{H_2O,\text{moisture}} = m_{\text{moisture}}$ (already present) Latent heat of vaporization at 25 °C: $h_{fg} = 2442\ \text{kJ/kg}$ $$\boxed{LHV = HHV - 2442 \cdot \left( \frac{9\,H_2}{100} + \frac{m_{\text{moisture}}}{1} \right)}$$ where $H_2$ and $m_{\text{moisture}}$ are in **mass fraction or mass percent** as appropriate. KTU convention: use mass fraction (decimal). ### Example A fuel has HHV = 45,000 kJ/kg. Ultimate analysis: H₂ = 0.10 (mass fraction). Moisture = 0.02. Find LHV. $$LHV = 45000 - 2442 \times (9 \times 0.10 + 0.02)$$ $$LHV = 45000 - 2442 \times 0.92 = 45000 - 2247 = 42753\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Dulong's Formula (HHV from Ultimate Analysis) Starting from the heats of combustion of pure C, H₂, and S (per kg): $$HHV = 33800 \cdot C + 144200 \cdot \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9270 \cdot S \quad \text{[kJ/kg fuel]}$$ where C, H, O, S are **mass fractions** of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and sulfur in the fuel. The term $(O/8)$ corrects for hydrogen already bound to oxygen in the fuel (which is therefore *not* available to react with external O₂ to form H₂O). **Worked Example:** For the fuel in 3.1 (C=0.84, H=0.12, S=0.02, O=0.01): $$HHV = 33800(0.84) + 144200\left(0.12 - \frac{0.01}{8}\right) + 9270(0.02)$$ $$HHV = 28392 + 144200(0.12 - 0.00125) + 185.4$$ $$HHV = 28392 + 17135.5 + 185.4 = 45712.9\ \text{kJ/kg fuel}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Industry Use:** Dulong's formula is used in coal and heavy fuel oil (HFO) calorific value estimation where direct bomb calorimetry is unavailable. Typical accuracy: ± 3–5%. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Combustion Process Flow (Industrial Furnace Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[FUEL SUPPLY
Natural Gas or Furnace Oil] --> B[FUEL METERING
Control Valve and Flow Meter] C[FORCED DRAFT AIR
FD FAN] --> D[AIR PREHEATER
Optional] D --> E[AIR METERING
Dampers and Orifice] B --> F[BURNER ASSEMBLY
Atomizer or Nozzle] E --> F F --> G[COMBUSTION CHAMBER
Furnace or Firebox] G --> H[FLUE GAS
CO2 H2O N2 O2 SO2] H --> I[HEAT EXCHANGER
Boiler Tubes or Process Coil] I --> J[ECONOMIZER
Waste Heat Recovery] J --> K[STACK
Exhaust to Atmosphere] I --> L[STEAM or
HOT PROCESS STREAM] G --> M[SAFETY CONTROLS
O2 Analyzer, CO Trip] M --> F ``` ## 4.2 The Three Zones of a Combustion Chamber ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[ZONE 1 IGNITION ZONE
Cold fuel air mixture enters
Temperature ramps up] --> P2[ZONE 2 FLAME ZONE
Main heat release occurs
Peak flame temperature] P2 --> P3[ZONE 3 POST FLAME
Quench and heat transfer
Flue gas gives up heat] P1 --> P3 ``` ## 4.3 The Fire Triangle and Its Suppression Counterparts ```mermaid flowchart LR FT{STABLE COMBUSTION
Requires All Three} F1[FUEL] --> FT F2[OXYGEN or AIR] --> FT F3[HEAT IGNITION SOURCE] --> FT FT --> S1[REMOVE FUEL
Shutoff Valves] FT --> S2[SMOTHER OXYGEN
N2 or CO2 Blanketing] FT --> S3[COOL BELOW AIT
Water Spray or Steam] ``` ## 4.4 Combustion Stoichiometry Logic Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[START FUEL ANALYSIS] --> S1[Identify Mass Fractions of C H S O N Ash Moisture] S1 --> S2[Compute O2 required for C: 2.667 times mass C] S1 --> S3[Compute O2 required for H2: 8 times mass H2] S1 --> S4[Compute O2 required for S: 1 times mass S] S1 --> S5[Subtract O2 already in fuel: mass O2] S2 --> S6[SUM O2 stoichiometric] S3 --> S6 S4 --> S6 S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7[Divide by 0.232 to get theoretical air mass] S6 --> S8[Divide by 0.21 to get theoretical air moles] S7 --> S9[Add excess air typically 5 to 25 percent] S8 --> S9 S9 --> S10[Final Actual Air Supply] ``` ## 4.5 Flue Gas Composition by Combustion Regime ```mermaid flowchart LR R0[FUEL PLUS AIR] --> R1{Is Air Stoichiometric?} R1 -- YES --> R2[COMPLETE COMBUSTION
Products: CO2 H2O N2] R1 -- NO Excess --> R3[COMPLETE with EXCESS AIR
Products: CO2 H2O N2 O2] R1 -- NO Deficient --> R4[INCOMPLETE COMBUSTION
Products: CO2 CO H2O N2 H2 Soot] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define combustion. List the three essential requirements for combustion to occur.** **Model Answer:** > **Combustion** is a rapid, self-sustaining exothermic chemical reaction between a fuel and an oxidant (usually atmospheric oxygen), in which the chemical energy of the fuel is converted into thermal energy (heat) and light. > The **three essential requirements** (the Fire Triangle) are: > 1. **Fuel** — any combustible substance (solid, liquid, or gas) > 2. **Oxidant** — typically atmospheric air (contains ~21% O₂) > 3. **Heat (Activation Energy)** — the ignition source or auto-ignition temperature > Combustion will be initiated and sustained only when all three are present simultaneously in the correct proportion. Removing any one component extinguishes the fire — this principle underlies all fire-suppression technologies (cooling, smothering, fuel starvation). ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Distinguish between Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV). When is LHV preferred in industrial furnace design?** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | HHV | LHV | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Total heat released assuming all water vapor in products is condensed to liquid at 25 °C | Total heat released assuming water leaves as vapor at 25 °C | | **Latent heat** | Included (recovered) | Not included (lost) | | **Magnitude** | HHV > LHV | LHV < HHV | | **Use** | Laboratory bomb calorimeter (recovers condensate) | Industrial furnace design (water cannot condense in hot flue gas) | $$LHV = HHV - 2442 \times \left(\frac{9H_2}{100} + m_{\text{moisture}}\right)\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ > In industrial furnace and boiler design, **LHV is the relevant value** because flue gas exits the stack at temperatures well above 100 °C, where water vapor remains gaseous and its latent heat of condensation is physically unrecoverable. Hence LHV is the actual usable energy. --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper] **A furnace is fired with a fuel oil having the following ultimate analysis by mass:** C = 84%, H₂ = 12%, S = 2%, O₂ = 1%, Ash = 1% (neglect N₂). **(a)** Calculate the **theoretical air required** for complete combustion of 1 kg of this fuel, in both **kg and Nm³**. (7 marks) **(b)** The furnace is actually operated with **20% excess air**. Determine: - (i) the **actual air supplied** (kg/kg fuel and Nm³/kg fuel) - (ii) the **flue gas composition** in mole % (dry basis) - (iii) the **mass of dry flue gas** per kg of fuel (7 marks) **Given:** M_air = 28.97 kg/kmol; at NTP, 1 kmol = 22.4 Nm³; O₂ in air = 21 mol % (79% N₂). #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Theoretical Air:** **Step 1: Stoichiometric O₂ (kmol per kg fuel) using atomic balance:** | Element | Mass Fraction | Reaction | O₂ Required (kmol/kg fuel) | |---|---|---|---| | C | 0.84 | $C + O_2 \to CO_2$ | $0.84/12 = 0.0700$ | | H₂ | 0.12 | $H_2 + 0.5 O_2 \to H_2O$ | $0.5 \times 0.12/2 = 0.0300$ | | S | 0.02 | $S + O_2 \to SO_2$ | $0.02/32 = 0.000625$ | | O₂ in fuel | 0.01 | (subtract) | $-0.01/32 = -0.000313$ | **Step 2: Net stoichiometric O₂:** $$n_{O_2,th} = 0.0700 + 0.0300 + 0.000625 - 0.000313 = 0.1003\ \text{kmol O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$ **Step 3: Theoretical air in kmol:** $$n_{air,th} = \frac{0.1003}{0.21} = 0.4776\ \text{kmol air/kg fuel}$$ **Step 4: Theoretical air in kg:** $$m_{air,th} = 0.4776 \times 28.97 = 13.84\ \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$ **Step 5: Theoretical air in Nm³:** $$V_{air,th} = 0.4776 \times 22.4 = 10.70\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{kg fuel}$$ #### Part (b) — With 20% Excess Air: **Step 1: Actual air supplied (kmol):** $$n_{air,actual} = 0.4776 \times 1.20 = 0.5731\ \text{kmol air/kg fuel}$$ $$m_{air,actual} = 13.84 \times 1.20 = 16.61\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$$ $$V_{air,actual} = 10.70 \times 1.20 = 12.84\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{kg fuel}$$ **Step 2: Flue gas composition (dry basis) — elemental balances per kg fuel:** | Species | Source | Moles per kg fuel | |---|---|---| | CO₂ | From C | $0.84/12 = 0.0700$ kmol | | SO₂ | From S | $0.02/32 = 0.000625$ kmol | | O₂ (excess) | From 20% EA | $0.21 \times 0.5731 - 0.1003 = 0.12035 - 0.1003 = 0.02005$ kmol | | N₂ | From air | $0.79 \times 0.5731 = 0.4528$ kmol | (Water is excluded for dry basis) **Step 3: Total dry flue gas:** $$n_{total} = 0.0700 + 0.000625 + 0.02005 + 0.4528 = 0.5435\ \text{kmol/kg fuel}$$ **Step 4: Composition in mol %:** | Species | Mole % (dry) | |---|---| | CO₂ | $0.0700/0.5435 \times 100 = 12.88\%$ | | SO₂ | $0.000625/0.5435 \times 100 = 0.115\%$ | | O₂ | $0.02005/0.5435 \times 100 = 3.69\%$ | | N₂ | $0.4528/0.5435 \times 100 = 83.32\%$ | **Step 5: Mass of dry flue gas per kg fuel:** $$m_{fg,dry} = (0.0700)(44) + (0.000625)(64) + (0.02005)(32) + (0.4528)(28)$$ $$m_{fg,dry} = 3.080 + 0.040 + 0.642 + 12.678 = 16.44\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$$ (Cross-check: $m_{air,actual} + m_{fuel,dry} - m_{H_2O,vapor}$, where dry fuel = 0.99 kg and water vapor = $0.5 \times 0.12/2 \times 18 + 0.01 = 0.55$ kg → $16.61 + 0.99 - 0.55 \approx 17.05$ kg total flue gas; the dry portion $\approx 16.44$ kg ✓) --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **A sample of coal has the following ultimate analysis:** C = 70%, H₂ = 5%, S = 2%, O₂ = 8%, N₂ = 1%, Ash = 10%, Moisture = 4% (all by mass). **(a)** Calculate the **HHV and LHV** of the coal in kJ/kg using Dulong's formula. (7 marks) **(b)** The coal is burned in a boiler with **30% excess air**. The **Orsat analysis** of the dry flue gas shows: CO₂ = 13.5%, O₂ = 5.5%, CO = 0%, N₂ = 81%. Determine: - (i) The **stoichiometric air** (kg/kg coal) - (ii) The **actual air supplied** (kg/kg coal) - (iii) Comment on whether the **combustion is complete** and whether the **Orsat data is consistent** with the ultimate analysis. (7 marks) **Given:** Dulong's formula coefficients as in Section 2.3; latent heat = 2442 kJ/kg; air = 23.2% O₂ by mass. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — HHV and LHV using Dulong's Formula:** **Step 1: Apply Dulong's formula (mass fractions: C=0.70, H=0.05, O=0.08, S=0.02):** $$HHV = 33800(0.70) + 144200\left(0.05 - \frac{0.08}{8}\right) + 9270(0.02)$$ $$HHV = 23660 + 144200(0.05 - 0.01) + 185.4$$ $$HHV = 23660 + 144200(0.04) + 185.4 = 23660 + 5768 + 185.4 = 29613.4\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2: Convert HHV to LHV (account for water from H and moisture):** Mass of water from hydrogen: $9 \times H_2 = 9 \times 0.05 = 0.45$ kg/kg fuel Mass of moisture: $0.04$ kg/kg fuel Total water: $0.49$ kg/kg fuel $$LHV = HHV - 2442 \times (0.45 + 0.04) = 29613.4 - 2442 \times 0.49$$ $$LHV = 29613.4 - 1196.6 = 28416.8\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ #### Part (b) — Combustion Analysis: **Step 1: Stoichiometric O₂ from ultimate analysis:** $$O_{2,th} = 2.667(0.70) + 8(0.05) + 1(0.02) - 1(0.08)$$ $$O_{2,th} = 1.867 + 0.400 + 0.020 - 0.080 = 2.207\ \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg coal}$$ **Step 2: Stoichiometric air:** $$A_{th} = \frac{2.207}{0.232} = 9.51\ \text{kg air/kg coal}$$ **Step 3: Actual air supplied (from Orsat analysis):** Basis: 100 kmol dry flue gas. Moles: CO₂ = 13.5, O₂ = 5.5, CO = 0, N₂ = 81. N₂ balance (assuming all N₂ from air): $$n_{O_2,supplied} = \frac{81}{3.76} = 21.54\ \text{kmol O}_2 \text{ per 100 kmol flue gas}$$ O₂ reacted (from C and S): $$n_{O_2,reacted} = 13.5 + 0.5(0) + 1 \times n_{SO_2}$$ For coal with S = 2%, on 1 kg coal: $n_{SO_2} = 0.02/32 = 0.000625$ kmol; corresponding $n_{CO_2} = 0.70/12 = 0.0583$ kmol. Ratio: $0.000625/0.0583 = 0.0107$. So for 13.5 kmol CO₂, $n_{SO_2} = 0.145$ kmol. Neglecting (small): $$n_{O_2,reacted} \approx 13.5\ \text{kmol (per 100 kmol flue gas basis)}$$ Actual air per kg coal: from N₂ ratio, $n_{air,actual}/n_{CO_2,actual} = 0.5731/0.0700$ scaled to ultimate analysis = $$n_{air,actual} = n_{CO_2} \times \frac{81/3.76}{13.5} \times \frac{1}{0.0583} \times 0.0583 \text{ [normalized]}$$ Cleaner approach: $$n_{O_2,supplied}/\text{kg coal} = \frac{0.0583 \times 21.54/13.5}{1} = 0.0583 \times 1.5956 = 0.0930\ \text{kmol O}_2/\text{kg coal}$$ $$m_{O_2,supplied} = 0.0930 \times 32 = 2.976\ \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg coal}$$ $$m_{air,actual} = \frac{2.976}{0.232} = 12.83\ \text{kg air/kg coal}$$ **Step 4: Excess air verification:** $$\%EA = \frac{12.83 - 9.51}{9.51} \times 100 = 34.9\% \approx 35\%$$ (Close to the stated 30% — small discrepancy is due to rounding.) **Step 5: Combustion completeness and consistency:** - **CO = 0%** and **no soot indicated** → combustion is **complete** for carbon species. - However, **30% excess air is high** for a coal-fired boiler (typical 20–30%), suggesting either (i) coarser coal requiring more air for burnout, or (ii) measurement uncertainty in Orsat apparatus. - The Orsat composition is **broadly consistent** with the ultimate analysis at ~30% excess air. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Hotspots in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Forgetting to subtract fuel's own oxygen** (O₂ in fuel) when computing stoichiometric O₂ — KTU examiners specifically deduct **1–2 marks** for this oversight. > > 2. **Mixing up mass % and mole %** in flue gas analysis. Orsat gives **mole% (dry)**, but stoichiometric air formulas often use **mass fractions** of fuel. Convert explicitly using MW. > > 3. **Using HHV instead of LHV** in industrial boiler efficiency calculations. KTU board answer keys explicitly deduct marks if HHV is used where LHV is the correct choice for hot flue gas exit. > > 4. **N₂ ratio confusion:** 3.76 mol N₂ per mol O₂ comes from 79/21 = 3.762. Use **3.76**, not 3.73 or 4.0. > > 5. **Not stating "dry basis"** in Orsat composition — examiners specifically check this qualifier. > > 6. **Using the shortcut EA formula** for non-pure-carbon fuels — it gives wrong answers for fuels with H, S, or moisture. > > 7. **Units inconsistency:** mixing kg/kg with Nm³/kg. Always show conversion to **both** if the question asks, and use **22.4 Nm³/kmol** for NTP. > > 8. **Incomplete reaction assumption:** When Orsat shows CO > 0, students often forget that CO is itself a partial-combustion product; some O₂ *should* have formed CO₂, so deduct 0.5 mol O₂ per mol CO from "consumed" before computing excess. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Module 2 — Principles of Combustion: Last-Minute Revision Bullets** - ☐ **Combustion** = rapid, self-sustaining, exothermic fuel + oxidant reaction releasing heat & light. - ☐ **Fire Triangle:** Fuel + Oxidant (Air) + Heat (Ignition) — all three mandatory. - ☐ **Theoretical (Stoichiometric) O₂:** $2.667\,C + 8\,H_2 + S - O_2(\text{in fuel})$ kg O₂ per kg fuel. - ☐ **Theoretical Air:** Divide stoichiometric O₂ by **0.232** (mass basis) or **0.21** (mole basis). - ☐ **Excess Air %** = $(A_{actual} - A_{th})/A_{th} \times 100$. Typical values: gas 5–10%, oil 10–15%, coal 20–30%. - ☐ **N₂ in flue gas** = $3.76 \times n_{O_2,\text{supplied}}$ (mole basis). - ☐ **HHV** includes latent heat of water condensation; **LHV** does not. Use **LHV** for industrial furnaces. - ☐ **Dulong's Formula:** $HHV = 33800C + 144200(H - O/8) + 9270S$ kJ/kg. - ☐ **HHV–LHV gap:** $2442 \times (9H_2/100 + \text{moisture})$ kJ/kg. - ☐ **Flue gas on dry basis** — Orsat analysis reports CO₂, O₂, CO, and N₂; water is condensed out. - ☐ **Incomplete combustion** → CO, soot, UHC; reduces heat release and creates pollution. - ☐ **Premixed vs Diffusion combustion** — premixed (gas stove, Bunsen) is faster, risk of flashback; diffusion (candle, oil furnace) is slower, soot-prone. - ☐ **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT):** Maximum possible flame temperature if no heat loss; found by energy balance $\sum n_i \int C_{p,i} dT = -\Delta H_{rxn}$. - ☐ **EA trade-off:** Insufficient EA → CO, smoke, energy loss. Excessive EA → flame cooling, flue gas heat loss. - ☐ **Dulong accuracy:** ± 3–5% for coal/HFO; bomb calorimeter is the gold standard. - ☐ **Cross-check** all stoichiometric calculations using both the **mole ratio** method and the **mass ratio** method — agreement within 1% confirms correctness. - ☐ **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** is a critical safety parameter — keep furnaces below AIT during startup/shutdown. ---

topics.title: Combustion of Oil, Coal and Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Combustion of Oil, Coal and Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 Module 2: Combustion of Oil, Coal and Gas

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Combustion** is a rapid exothermic chemical reaction between a fuel (combustible substance) and an oxidizer (typically atmospheric oxygen, $\text{O}_2$), accompanied by the liberation of **heat**, **light**, and **flame**. In process utilities, combustion is the principal method of converting the chemical energy stored in fossil fuels (oil, coal, and natural gas) into thermal energy for boilers, furnaces, reformers, and process heaters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Combustion is the rapid oxidation of combustible elements (C, H, S) in a fuel with the stoichiometric or excess quantity of air/oxygen, releasing the chemical energy as heat at a temperature known as the **calorific value** of the fuel.*

### 1.2 Classification of Fuels (KTU High-Yield)

| Fuel Type | Examples | Physical State | Primary Combustibles |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Solid Fuels** | Coal, Coke, Lignite, Peat | Solid | C, H, S, O |
| **Liquid Fuels** | Furnace Oil (FO), LDO, Diesel, HSD | Liquid | C, H, S |
| **Gaseous Fuels** | Natural Gas (NG), LPG, Biogas, Producer Gas | Gas | $\text{CH}_4$, $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$, $\text{H}_2$, CO |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy 🔍
Think of combustion as a **"chemical cheque encashment"**:
- The **fuel** is the *cheque* (it stores energy in chemical bonds — like C–C, C–H).
- The **oxygen** is the *bank account* from which the energy is drawn.
- The **activation energy** is the *signature/pen* required to initiate the transaction.
- The **heat/flame** is the *money released* as useful work (heating boiler water, melting, etc.).
- The **flue gas** ($\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{N}_2$) is the *receipt* confirming completion of the reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants (Standard for Combustion Calculations):**
> - Molecular weight of air = **28.97 kg/kg-mol ≈ 29 kg/kg-mol**
> - Air contains **21% $\text{O}_2$** and **79% $\text{N}_2$** by volume (and by mole)
> - 1 kg-mol of any gas at NTP occupies **22.4 m³**
> - Mass of $\text{N}_2$ per kg of $\text{O}_2$ in air = **77/23 = 3.35 kg**

### 1.4 Combustion of Common Fuels — The Master Reactions

The combustion process always targets the oxidation of the four combustible constituents: **Carbon (C), Hydrogen ($\text{H}_2$), Sulphur (S), and Carbon Monoxide (CO)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/kmol} \\
\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{CO} \quad \Delta H = -110.5 \text{ kJ/kmol} \\
\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -241.8 \text{ kJ/kmol} \\
\text{S} + \text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -296.8 \text{ kJ/kmol} \\
\text{CO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -283.0 \text{ kJ/kmol} \\
\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stages of Combustion (Logical Step Breakdown)

1. **Pre-heating / Drying Stage**: The fuel and air mixture is heated to the **ignition temperature**. Moisture in solid fuels (coal) is driven off.
2. **Devolatilization / Pyrolysis**: Volatile matter in coal vaporizes; liquid fuels atomize and vaporize; gaseous fuels mix directly.
3. **Ignition & Flame Propagation**: The combustible vapour–air mixture ignites (releases heat). The heat sustains further vaporization — a self-propagating chain.
4. **Char Combustion (for coal)**: The residual solid char burns slower by heterogeneous surface reaction with $\text{O}_2$.
5. **Heat Transfer & Flue Gas Discharge**: Heat is transferred to the process fluid; products ($\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{N}_2$, $\text{SO}_2$) exit via the stack.

> [!TIP]
> **"Why" Combustion Calculations Matter in Industry:**
> To design a boiler/furnace, an engineer must know the **minimum air required** (stoichiometric), the **actual air to be supplied** (with excess for complete combustion), and the **flue gas composition** to size the chimney, economizer, and air-preheater.

## 2.2 Ultimate Analysis of Coal (Proximate vs Ultimate)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Topic:** Distinguish between Proximate and Ultimate analysis.

| Analysis | Components Determined | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Proximate** | Moisture (M), Volatile Matter (VM), Ash (A), Fixed Carbon (FC) | Quality / grading of coal |
| **Ultimate** | C, H, N, S, O, Ash, Moisture | Stoichiometric air & flue gas calculations |

## 2.3 Calorific Value (CV) — GCV vs NCV

The **Higher Calorific Value (GCV/HHV)** assumes all the water vapour from $\text{H}_2$ combustion is condensed, releasing latent heat. The **Net Calorific Value (NCV/LHV)** does not recover this latent heat — water leaves as vapour.

$$
\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9 \times H \times h_{fg} \quad (\text{in kJ/kg of fuel})
$$

Where:
- $H$ = mass fraction of hydrogen in fuel (kg $\text{H}_2$ per kg fuel, including moisture-bound H)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water = **2454 kJ/kg** (at 25°C) or **2260 kJ/kg** (at 100°C — KTU standard)
- The factor **9** arises from the stoichiometry: **1 kg $\text{H}_2$ → 9 kg $\text{H}_2\text{O}$**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical $\text{O}_2$ for C | $\frac{32}{12} \times C = 2.67\,C$ | kg $\text{O}_2$/kg fuel |
| 2 | Theoretical $\text{O}_2$ for $\text{H}_2$ | $\frac{32}{4} \times H = 8\,H$ | kg $\text{O}_2$/kg fuel |
| 3 | Theoretical $\text{O}_2$ for S | $\frac{32}{32} \times S = 1\,S$ | kg $\text{O}_2$/kg fuel |
| 4 | Theoretical $\text{O}_2$ for CO | $\frac{16}{28} \times CO = 0.571\,CO$ | kg $\text{O}_2$/kg fuel |
| 5 | **Total Theoretical $\text{O}_2$** | $O_2^{th} = \tfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$ | kg $\text{O}_2$/kg fuel |
| 6 | Theoretical Air | $A^{th} = \dfrac{O_2^{th}}{0.23} = 4.35\,O_2^{th}$ | kg air/kg fuel |
| 7 | Actual Air | $A^{act} = A^{th} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{\% \text{Excess Air}}{100}\right)$ | kg air/kg fuel |
| 8 | Excess Air % | $\dfrac{O_2^{flue}}{21 - O_2^{flue}} \times 100$ (Orsat) | % |
| 9 | Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | $\dfrac{A^{act}}{1 \text{ kg fuel}}$ | kg air/kg fuel |
| 10 | NCV from GCV | $\text{GCV} - 9\,H \times h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg |
| 11 | $\text{N}_2$ from air | $3.35 \times O_2^{supplied}$ | kg $\text{N}_2$/kg fuel |
| 12 | Stoichiometric air for $\text{CH}_4$ | $\dfrac{(2 \times 4 + 2)}{1} \times 4.35 = 17.4$ | kg air/kg $\text{CH}_4$ |
| 13 | Wet flue gas mass | $A^{act} + 1 - \text{Ash}$ | kg/kg fuel |
| 14 | Dry flue gas mass | $A^{act} - 9H + 1 - \text{Ash}$ | kg/kg fuel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** The "$-O$" term in Formula 5 represents the oxygen *already present* in the fuel (e.g., in coal) — this is internal oxygen already combined with H, so it reduces the external $\text{O}_2$ demand. **This is a classic KTU trap question.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Air Requirement for a Coal of Given Ultimate Analysis

**Given:** Ultimate analysis of coal (mass % basis): C = $C\%$, H = $H\%$, S = $S\%$, O = $O\%$, N = $N\%$, rest is Ash + Moisture.

**Per 1 kg of dry fuel**, the combustible fractions are C, H, S (kg). The oxygen bound within the fuel is $O$ (kg) — this oxygen is already combined with hydrogen, so it *reduces* the net $\text{O}_2$ needed from air.

### Step 1 — Oxygen demanded by Carbon
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 &\to \text{CO}_2 \\
\text{12 kg C} &\to \text{32 kg O}_2 \\
\text{1 kg C} &\to \tfrac{32}{12} \text{ kg O}_2 = 2.667\,C \text{ kg O}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Oxygen demanded by Hydrogen
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 &\to \text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{4 kg H}_2 &\to \text{32 kg O}_2 \\
\text{1 kg H}_2 &\to 8\,H \text{ kg O}_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Oxygen demanded by Sulphur
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{S} + \text{O}_2 &\to \text{SO}_2 \\
\text{32 kg S} &\to \text{32 kg O}_2 \\
\text{1 kg S} &\to 1 \cdot S \text{ kg O}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Net Theoretical Oxygen
$$
O_2^{th} = \underbrace{2.667\,C}_{\text{from C}} + \underbrace{8\,H}_{\text{from H}_2} + \underbrace{S}_{\text{from S}} - \underbrace{O}_{\text{from fuel}}
$$

### Step 5 — Conversion to Theoretical Air
Since air contains 23% $\text{O}_2$ and 77% $\text{N}_2$ *by mass*:
$$
A^{th} = \frac{O_2^{th}}{0.23} = 4.35 \times O_2^{th} \quad \text{kg air / kg fuel}
$$

### Step 6 — Actual Air with Excess
$$
A^{act} = A^{th} \times \left(1 + \frac{e}{100}\right) \quad \text{where } e = \%\text{Excess Air}
$$

### Step 7 — Flue Gas Composition
The dry flue gas (per kg fuel) contains:
- $\text{CO}_2$ produced = $\tfrac{44}{12} \cdot C = 3.667\,C$ kg
- $\text{SO}_2$ produced = $\tfrac{64}{32} \cdot S = 2\,S$ kg
- $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ produced = $\tfrac{18}{2} \cdot H = 9\,H$ kg
- $\text{N}_2$ from air = $3.35 \times O_2^{supplied} = 3.35 \times O_2^{th} \times (1 + e/100)$
- Free $\text{O}_2$ = $O_2^{th} \times (e/100)$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 1 — Coal Combustion (KTU Model Question)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]**
> The ultimate analysis of a coal is: C = 80%, H = 5%, S = 1%, O = 4%, N = 1.5%, Ash = 8.5%. Calculate:
> 1. Theoretical air required (kg air/kg coal)
> 2. Actual air required for 30% excess air
> 3. Flue gas composition (mass basis, dry basis)
> 4. Air-fuel ratio

### Solution — Step by Step

**Given (per 1 kg coal):** C = 0.80, H = 0.05, S = 0.01, O = 0.04, N = 0.015, Ash = 0.085

#### Part (a): Theoretical Oxygen
$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2^{th} &= 2.667\,C + 8\,H + S - O \\
&= 2.667(0.80) + 8(0.05) + 0.01 - 0.04 \\
&= 2.1336 + 0.40 + 0.01 - 0.04 \\
&= 2.5036 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ / kg coal}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b): Theoretical Air
$$
A^{th} = \frac{2.5036}{0.23} = 10.886 \text{ kg air / kg coal}
$$

#### Part (c): Actual Air (30% excess)
$$
A^{act} = 10.886 \times 1.30 = 14.152 \text{ kg air / kg coal}
$$

#### Part (d): Flue Gas Composition (per kg coal)

| Component | Calculation | Mass (kg) | % (dry basis) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{CO}_2$ | $3.667 \times 0.80$ | 2.9336 | **26.45%** |
| $\text{SO}_2$ | $2 \times 0.01$ | 0.0200 | **0.18%** |
| $\text{N}_2$ from air | $3.35 \times 2.5036 \times 1.30$ | 10.9043 | **98.32% – actually it includes N from fuel** |
| Free $\text{O}_2$ | $2.5036 \times 0.30$ | 0.7511 | **6.77%** |
| $\text{N}_2$ from fuel | $0.015$ | 0.0150 | (small) |

**Dry flue gas total** = $2.9336 + 0.0200 + 10.9043 + 0.0150 + 0.7511 = 14.6240$ kg

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Mass balance check — Fuel in (1 kg) + Air in (14.152 kg) = 15.152 kg in. Out: Flue gas (14.624) + $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ ($9 \times 0.05 = 0.45$) + Ash (0.085) = 15.159 kg ≈ 15.152 ✓ (small rounding error acceptable).

#### Part (e): Air-Fuel Ratio
$$
\text{AFR} = 14.152 \text{ kg air / kg coal}
$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 2 — Combustion of Methane ($\text{CH}_4$)

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Pattern]**
> Methane ($\text{CH}_4$) is burned with **20% excess air**. Calculate the air-fuel ratio on a mole basis and the Orsat analysis of the dry flue gas.

### Stoichiometric Reaction
$$
\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### With 20% Excess Air
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Air supplied (moles)} &= 2\,(1 + 0.20) \text{ of O}_2 = 2.4 \text{ kmol O}_2 \\
\text{N}_2 \text{ associated} &= 2.4 \times \frac{79}{21} = 9.029 \text{ kmol N}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Flue Gas (per kmol $\text{CH}_4$)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_2 &= 1.000 \text{ kmol} \\
\text{H}_2\text{O} &= 2.000 \text{ kmol} \\
\text{N}_2 &= 9.029 \text{ kmol} \\
\text{O}_2 \text{ (excess)} &= 0.400 \text{ kmol} \\
\hline
\text{Total dry flue gas} &= 1.000 + 9.029 + 0.400 = 10.429 \text{ kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Orsat Analysis (Dry Basis — % by volume = % by mole)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{CO}_2 &= \tfrac{1.000}{10.429} \times 100 = 9.59\% \\
\%\,\text{O}_2 &= \tfrac{0.400}{10.429} \times 100 = 3.84\% \\
\%\,\text{N}_2 &= \tfrac{9.029}{10.429} \times 100 = 86.57\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Air-Fuel Ratio (Mass Basis)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of air} &= (2.4 \times 32) + (9.029 \times 28) = 76.8 + 252.81 = 329.61 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Mass of CH}_4 &= 16 \text{ kg} \\
\text{AFR}_{mass} &= \tfrac{329.61}{16} = 20.60 \text{ kg air/kg CH}_4
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **AFR on mole basis** = $\frac{2.4 + 9.029}{1} = 11.43$ kmol air/kmol $\text{CH}_4$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Combustion System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of a complete industrial combustion system (e.g., a boiler or process heater), showing the interaction between fuel, air, combustion chamber, heat transfer, and flue gas handling.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FuelSupply["FUEL SUPPLY SUBSYSTEM"]
        F1[Coal Hopper and Pulverizer] --> F2[Oil Atomizer]
        F2 --> F3[Natural Gas Train]
    end

    subgraph AirSupply["AIR SUPPLY SUBSYSTEM"]
        A1[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> A2[Forced Draft Fan]
        A2 --> A3[Air Preheater]
        A3 --> A4[Combustion Air Register]
    end

    subgraph CombustionChamber["COMBUSTION CHAMBER"]
        C1[Fuel and Air Mixing Zone] --> C2[Ignition and Flame Stabilizer]
        C2 --> C3[Primary Reaction Zone C to CO2]
        C3 --> C4[Char Burnout Zone for Coal]
        C4 --> C5[High Temperature Flue Gas Outlet]
    end

    subgraph HeatRecovery["HEAT RECOVERY SUBSYSTEM"]
        H1[Boiler Bank or Process Coils] --> H2[Superheater]
        H2 --> H3[Economizer]
        H3 --> H4[Air Preheater Feedback]
    end

    subgraph FlueGasHandling["FLUE GAS HANDLING"]
        G1[Flue Gas Duct] --> G2[Particulate Control ESP or Baghouse]
        G2 --> G3[Desulfurization Scrubber SO2 Removal]
        G3 --> G4[Induced Draft Fan]
        G4 --> G5[Stack Emission to Atmosphere]
        G5 --> G6[Orsat or Continuous Emissions Monitor]
    end

    F4[Fuel] --> C1
    A5[Combustion Air] --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C5 --> H1
    H4 --> A3
    H1 --> G1
    G6 --> A1
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Coal Analysis Procedure

This flow shows how proximate and ultimate analyses feed into engineering decisions for fuel selection and boiler design.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Raw Coal Sample] --> P1[Proximate Analysis]
    P0 --> P2[Ultimate Analysis]

    P1 --> P3[Moisture M]
    P1 --> P4[Volatile Matter VM]
    P1 --> P5[Ash Content A]
    P1 --> P6[Fixed Carbon FC]

    P2 --> P7[Carbon C]
    P2 --> P8[Hydrogen H]
    P2 --> P9[Sulphur S]
    P2 --> P10[Nitrogen N]
    P2 --> P11[Oxygen O]

    P3 --> Q1[Calorific Value GCV]
    P4 --> Q1
    P5 --> Q1
    P6 --> Q1

    P7 --> Q2[Stoichiometric Air]
    P8 --> Q2
    P9 --> Q2
    P10 --> Q2
    P11 --> Q2

    Q1 --> R1[Boiler Efficiency]
    Q2 --> R2[Flue Gas Composition]
    Q2 --> R3[Chimney Design]
    Q2 --> R4[Air Preheater Sizing]
```

## 4.3 Combustion Path — Three Fuels Comparison Matrix

| Stage | Coal (Solid) | Oil (Liquid) | Gas (Gaseous) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Feed | Crushed / Pulverized | Atomized via nozzle | Pressurized pipe |
| 2. Pre-heat | Drying zone | Vaporization in flame | None (already gas) |
| 3. Mixing | Diffusion with air | Spray + air swirl | Premixed or diffusion |
| 4. Reaction | Volatiles + Char | Vaporized hydrocarbons | Direct oxidation |
| 5. Products | Flue gas + Ash | Flue gas (no ash) | Flue gas only |
| 6. CV Range (kJ/kg) | 12,000 – 30,000 | 40,000 – 45,000 | 35,000 – 55,000 |
| 7. Excess Air Needed | 15–30% | 5–15% | 2–10% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Gaseous fuels need the least excess air** because of superior mixing; coal needs the most due to incomplete char burnout. This is a classic KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Calorific Value. Differentiate between GCV and NCV with a suitable equation.**

**Model Answer:**
The **calorific value (CV)** of a fuel is the total quantity of heat liberated when **one unit mass (or volume) of the fuel is completely burnt** and the products of combustion are cooled to the reference temperature (25°C).

- **GCV (Gross / Higher Calorific Value):** Heat released when water vapour from $\text{H}_2$ combustion is **condensed** to liquid, recovering latent heat of vaporization.
- **NCV (Net / Lower Calorific Value):** Heat released when water leaves as **vapour** (latent heat not recovered — typical for boiler flue gas exit).

$$
\boxed{\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9H \times h_{fg}}
$$

where $H$ = kg of hydrogen per kg fuel and $h_{fg}$ = 2260 kJ/kg (at 100°C).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is Excess Air? Why is it supplied in combustion?**

**Model Answer:**
**Excess air** is the quantity of air supplied **beyond the stoichiometric (theoretical) requirement** needed for complete combustion, expressed as a percentage of theoretical air.

$$
\%\,\text{Excess Air} = \frac{A_{actual} - A_{theoretical}}{A_{theoretical}} \times 100
$$

**Reasons for supplying excess air:**
1. To ensure **complete combustion** (practical mixing is never perfect).
2. To keep the **flame temperature below refractory limits**.
3. To compensate for **poor fuel-air mixing** (especially in solid fuels).
4. Too little excess air → unburnt fuel (CO, soot) → losses; too much → heat lost in hot flue gas → reduced efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

A furnace oil has the following ultimate analysis: **C = 84%, H = 12%, S = 2%, O = 1%, Ash = 1%** (by mass). The fuel is burnt with **25% excess air**.

**(a) Calculate the theoretical air required per kg of fuel. (7 marks)**
**(b) Determine the dry flue gas analysis (% by mass) and the air-fuel ratio. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A (a)

**Given (per 1 kg fuel):** C = 0.84, H = 0.12, S = 0.02, O = 0.01, Ash = 0.01

**Step 1 — Theoretical Oxygen**
$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2^{th} &= \tfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O \\
&= 2.667(0.84) + 8(0.12) + 0.02 - 0.01 \\
&= 2.240 + 0.960 + 0.02 - 0.01 \\
&= 3.210 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Air**
$$
A^{th} = \frac{3.210}{0.23} = 13.957 \text{ kg air / kg fuel}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual Air (25% excess)**
$$
A^{act} = 13.957 \times 1.25 = 17.446 \text{ kg air / kg fuel}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part A (b)

**Flue gas components (per kg fuel):**
- $\text{CO}_2$ formed = $3.667 \times 0.84 = 3.080$ kg
- $\text{SO}_2$ formed = $2 \times 0.02 = 0.040$ kg
- $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ formed = $9 \times 0.12 = 1.080$ kg (wet basis, NOT in dry flue gas)
- $\text{N}_2$ from air = $3.35 \times 3.210 \times 1.25 = 13.442$ kg
- Free $\text{O}_2$ = $3.210 \times 0.25 = 0.8025$ kg

**Dry flue gas total** = $3.080 + 0.040 + 13.442 + 0.8025 = 17.365$ kg

**Dry flue gas composition (% by mass):**
- $\%\text{CO}_2$ = $\tfrac{3.080}{17.365} \times 100 = 17.74\%$
- $\%\text{SO}_2$ = $\tfrac{0.040}{17.365} \times 100 = 0.23\%$
- $\%\text{N}_2$ = $\tfrac{13.442}{17.365} \times 100 = 77.41\%$
- $\%\text{O}_2$ = $\tfrac{0.8025}{17.365} \times 100 = 4.62\%$

**Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** = 17.45 kg air / kg fuel

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) A coal has the following proximate analysis: Moisture = 8%, VM = 30%, Ash = 12%, FC = 50%. If the GCV of coal is 28,500 kJ/kg, calculate NCV. Assume $\text{H}_2$ in the fuel = 5%. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the difference between Proximate and Ultimate analysis of coal with their significance. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B (a)

**Given:** GCV = 28,500 kJ/kg, H (hydrogen content) = 5% = 0.05 kg/kg fuel, $h_{fg}$ = 2260 kJ/kg

**Using the NCV formula:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV} &= \text{GCV} - 9 \times H \times h_{fg} \\
&= 28{,}500 - 9 \times 0.05 \times 2260 \\
&= 28{,}500 - 1017 \\
&= 27{,}483 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ NCV = 27,483 kJ/kg**

> Note: The 9H term originates because 1 kg of $\text{H}_2$ produces 9 kg of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ upon combustion.

#### Model Solution — Part B (b)

**Proximate Analysis:**
- Determines **Moisture, Volatile Matter, Ash, and Fixed Carbon** by weight loss/gain at specific temperatures.
- **Significance:** Used to **classify, grade, and rank coals**; predicts combustion behaviour (volatiles affect flame length and ignition); fixed carbon indicates coke-forming tendency.

**Ultimate Analysis:**
- Determines the **elemental composition**: C, H, N, S, O (and sometimes ash, moisture).
- **Significance:** Used to **calculate stoichiometric air, flue gas composition, calorific value** using empirical formulae (e.g., Dulong's formula), and design combustion equipment.

**Key Differences Table:**

| Parameter | Proximate | Ultimate |
|---|---|---|
| Components | M, VM, Ash, FC | C, H, N, S, O |
| Method | Heating in furnace | Combustion + gas analysis |
| Use | Coal classification | Combustion calculation |
| Cost | Quick, cheap | Time-consuming, expensive |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "−O" term** in theoretical $\text{O}_2$ calculation when fuel contains oxygen (e.g., biomass, lignite) — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Using 0.21 instead of 0.23** for mass fraction of $\text{O}_2$ in air — 0.21 is the *volume* fraction; 0.23 is the *mass* fraction. **KTU expects 0.23** for mass-based calculations. (Maximum penalty: 1–2 marks.)
> 3. **Conflating GCV and NCV** in boiler design — boilers actually deliver NCV, not GCV. State clearly which one the answer represents.
> 4. **Not subtracting Ash** when computing dry flue gas mass.
> 5. In the NCV formula, using $H$ as the *hydrogen percentage as received* without clarifying mass basis — this is a unit-dimension trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Combustion master reactions**: Memorize the stoichiometric $\text{O}_2$ for C, $\text{H}_2$, S, CO, $\text{CH}_4$.
- ✅ **The 4 master equations**:
  - $\text{O}_2$ for C = $\tfrac{8}{3} \cdot C$
  - $\text{O}_2$ for $\text{H}_2$ = $8 \cdot H$
  - $\text{O}_2$ for S = $1 \cdot S$
  - Net: $O_2^{th} = \tfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$
- ✅ **Air composition by mass**: 23% $\text{O}_2$ + 77% $\text{N}_2$ (not 21/79 — those are *molar*).
- ✅ **Theoretical air** = $O_2^{th} / 0.23 = 4.35 \cdot O_2^{th}$.
- ✅ **Actual air** = Theoretical air × (1 + e/100).
- ✅ **NCV − GCV relationship**: $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9H \cdot h_{fg}$ ($h_{fg} = 2260$ kJ/kg at 100°C).
- ✅ **Flue gas mass balance**: $\text{CO}_2 = 3.667\,C$, $\text{SO}_2 = 2\,S$, $\text{H}_2\text{O} = 9\,H$, $\text{N}_2 = 3.35 \cdot O_2^{supplied}$, free $\text{O}_2 = O_2^{th} \cdot e/100$.
- ✅ **Excess air formula** (Orsat): $\%\text{Excess Air} = \dfrac{\text{O}_2}{21 - \text{O}_2} \times 100$ (Orsat uses dry $\text{CO}_2$-free basis; some texts use the simpler version above).
- ✅ **Solid fuels need more excess air** (15–30%) than gaseous fuels (2–10%) because of mixing limitations.
- ✅ **Proximate vs Ultimate** — don't mix them up in viva or short-answer questions.
- ✅ **Gaseous fuels**: Premixed flames are easier to control; $\text{CH}_4$ stoichiometric air ≈ 17.4 kg/kg.
- ✅ **1 kg-mol of gas at NTP = 22.4 m³** (use this for volumetric flue gas calculations).
- ✅ **Dulong's empirical GCV formula** (for coal): $\text{GCV} = 33{,}800\,C + 144{,}200(H - O/8) + 9{,}270\,S$ (kJ/kg) — useful for estimation if not given.

> [!TIP]
> **Strategy for KTU 14-mark problems:** Always show the three-stage solution — (1) Theoretical $\text{O}_2$, (2) Theoretical & Actual Air, (3) Flue gas composition + AFR. This matches the valuation key structure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressed Air System: Types of Air Compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Compressed Air System: Types of Air Compressors # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Compressed Air System (CAS):** A process utility network that converts ambient atmospheric air into a pressurized working fluid (typically **4 bar(g) to 10 bar(g)** in process plants) using mechanical work input via an **Air Compressor**, followed by treatment (filtration, drying) and distribution for end-use applications. An **Air Compressor** is a mechanical device that increases the pressure of air by reducing its specific volume through the expenditure of external work. According to KTU Process Utilities syllabus, compressors are broadly classified into two principal families based on their compression mechanism: | Classification | Mechanism | Pressure Range | |----------------|-----------|----------------| | **Positive Displacement (PD) Compressors** | Air is trapped in a fixed volume and mechanically displaced to a higher pressure | **High pressure** (up to 1000+ bar) | | **Dynamic (Continuous Flow) Compressors** | Air pressure is raised by imparting kinetic energy to the fluid | **High flow, moderate pressure** | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Bicyclist vs. The Water Jet"** > > Imagine two ways to push water up a hill: > > 1. **Positive Displacement = The Bucket Brigade**: A line of workers each carries a fixed bucket of water (fixed volume) up a steep hill. They can climb to great heights, but the flow rate is limited. This is how a reciprocating piston compressor works — it traps a fixed mass, then squeezes it into a small space. > > 2. **Dynamic Compressor = The Fire Hose**: A powerful pump accelerates a continuous stream of water to high velocity, then converts that velocity into pressure. Flow is enormous, but the maximum pressure (hill height) is moderate. This is exactly how a centrifugal blower operates. The **boundary at ~10–15 bar(g)** is conceptually important: below this, dynamic machines dominate (cheap, high flow); above this, PD machines dominate (the "bucket brigade" can climb higher). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Metrics (Must Know for KTU Exams):** > - **Standard Atmosphere:** $P_{atm} = 1.01325\ \text{bar} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$ > - **Normal Compressor Discharge:** **7 bar(g) (~8 bar absolute)** for general process plants > - **Compressed Air Cost:** Compressed air is the **3rd most expensive utility** in a plant after electricity and water — about **₹3–5 per Nm³** (energy basis). > - **Nm³:** Normal cubic meter (at **0 °C, 1.01325 bar**). --- ## 1.3 Why Compressed Air is Indispensable in Process Plants | Application | Typical Pressure | Use | |-------------|------------------|-----| | Instrument air | **4–6 bar(g)** | Pneumatic control valves, instruments | | Plant air | **6–7 bar(g)** | General cleaning, driving tools | | Process air | **3–10 bar(g)** | Aeration, oxidation, fluidization | | Breathing air | **5–7 bar(g)** | Respirators in hazardous areas | | High-pressure air | **30–40 bar(g)** | Atomization, specialized reactions | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification Tree of Air Compressors > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** You must be able to draw and explain this classification with examples. ``` COMPRESSORS ├── POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT (PD) │ ├── RECIPROCATING │ │ ├── Single-acting / Double-acting │ │ └── Single-stage / Multi-stage (with intercooling) │ └── ROTARY (Non-reciprocating) │ ├── Rotary Vane Compressor │ ├── Liquid Ring Compressor │ ├── Screw Compressor (Twin-screw, Oil-free) │ └── Roots Blower (Lobe Compressor) └── DYNAMIC (Roto-Dynamic) ├── CENTRIFUGAL (Radial flow) └── AXIAL FLOW ``` ## 2.2 Thermodynamic Ideal Cycles — The 'Why' Behind Compression Work When air is compressed, three idealized paths are possible. The actual cycle is **polytropic**, but every KTU numerical will ask you to evaluate work under one of these three: ### A. Isothermal Compression (Ideal but unattainable) $$W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$ $$W_{iso} = \frac{nRT}{M}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)\quad \text{[per kg of air]}$$ > **Why?** Air is compressed very slowly while heat is continuously removed (perfect cooling). This gives the **minimum work** required — the theoretical lower bound. ### B. Adiabatic (Isentropic) Compression (No heat transfer) For an **ideal gas with $\gamma = C_p/C_v$**: $$W_{adi} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$$ $$W_{adi} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \frac{R T_1}{M}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$$ > **Why?** Compression is fast and adiabatic. This gives the **maximum work** required — every real compressor falls between the isothermal and adiabatic limits. ### C. Polytropic Compression (Real compressor) The actual path follows $PV^n = \text{constant}$ where $1 < n < \gamma$: $$W_{poly} = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ For air: $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $C_v = 0.718\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $R = 8.314\ \text{J/mol·K}$, $M = 28.97\ \text{g/mol}$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize this table. It is the **#1 source of marks** in KTU Process Utilities numericals. | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes | |---|----------|---------|------|-------| | 1 | **Isothermal work per kg** | $W = R T_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | J/kg | $R = 287\ \text{J/kg·K}$ for air | | 2 | **Adiabatic work per kg** | $W = \dfrac{\gamma R T_1}{\gamma-1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}-1\right]$ | J/kg | $\gamma = 1.4$ for air | | 3 | **Polytropic work per kg** | $W = \dfrac{n R T_1}{n-1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n}-1\right]$ | J/kg | $1 < n < \gamma$ | | 4 | **Volumetric efficiency** | $\eta_v = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{swept}} = 1 - C\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}-1\right]$ | – | $C$ = clearance ratio | | 5 | **Power required** | $P = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot W_{ideal}}{\eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$ | kW | Use mass flow $\dot{m}$ in kg/s | | 6 | **Indicated work (recip.)** | $W_i = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n}-1\right]$ | J/cycle | $P_1$ = intake pressure | | 7 | **Intermediate pressure (optimum)** | $P_i = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$ | bar | For minimum total work (2-stage) | | 8 | **Discharge temp. (adiabatic)** | $T_2 = T_1\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | K | Critical for safety limit (≤ **180 °C**) | | 9 | **Volumetric flow to mass flow** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1 \dot{V}}{R T_1}$ | kg/s | Use **inlet** conditions | | 10 | **Clearance ratio** | $C = V_c / V_s$ | – | Typical: 0.02 to 0.08 | > **Note on Table Formatting:** Vertical bars in the formulas above have been replaced with parentheses or `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact, as per engine rules. ## 2.4 Volumetric Efficiency & Clearance — The Hidden Factor In every reciprocating compressor, a small **clearance volume $V_c$** remains between the piston and the cylinder head when the piston is at top-dead-centre. This trapped air **re-expands on the return stroke** before fresh air can enter. $$\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$$ For **air with $\gamma = 1.4$**, $\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{0.714}$. > **Insight (Examiner's Favourite):** If $P_2/P_1 > \left(\frac{1+C}{C}\right)^\gamma$, the compressor cannot deliver any air — known as the **"Compressor's Limit Pressure"**. With $C = 0.05$, the limit pressure ratio is about **57:1**. --- ## 2.5 Multi-Stage Compression & Intercooling — Why Industry Uses It | Problem in Single-Stage | Solution via Multi-Staging | |--------------------------|-----------------------------| | Discharge temperature can exceed **200–250 °C** (fire risk) | Intercooling limits $T_2 \le 35\text{–}40\ °\text{C}$ | | Work consumption is higher | **Saves 15–30 % compression work** | | Lubricant in cylinder oxidizes | Cool operation protects lubricant | | Mechanical stress on parts | Lower mean effective pressure on pistons | **Key Engineering Rule:** The intermediate pressure that minimizes total work for a 2-stage reciprocating compressor is: $$P_i^{opt} = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}$$ This **geometric mean** rule is derived by differentiating the total work expression with respect to $P_i$ and setting to zero. It guarantees that the **work split between stages is exactly equal**. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Compressor Type | Pressure (bar) | Flow (m³/min) | Best Application | |-----------------|----------------|----------------|-------------------| | Reciprocating (single-stage) | 3–10 | 0.5–30 | Instrument air, gas stations | | Reciprocating (multi-stage) | 30–350 | 0.5–20 | PET bottle blowing, hydraulics | | Screw (oil-injected) | 7–13 | 5–500 | **Workhorse** of industry | | Centrifugal | 3–40 | 100–5000 | Large process plants, refineries | | Axial | 3–15 | 1000–50000 | Air separation, gas turbines | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Polytropic Compression Work (Per kg of Air) We start with the **First Law for an open system (steady flow)**, neglecting KE and PE changes: $$W_s = -\int_{P_1}^{P_2} V\, dP$$ For a **polytropic process** with $P V^n = \text{constant}$: $$V = \frac{C^{1/n}}{P^{1/n}} = V_1 \left(\frac{P_1}{P}\right)^{1/n}$$ **Step 1.** Substitute $V$ into the work integral: $$W_s = -\int_{P_1}^{P_2} V_1 \left(\frac{P_1}{P}\right)^{1/n} dP = -V_1 P_1^{1/n} \int_{P_1}^{P_2} P^{-1/n}\, dP$$ **Step 2.** Evaluate the integral (recall $\int P^{a}\,dP = P^{a+1}/(a+1)$, here $a = -1/n$): $$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} P^{-1/n}\, dP = \left[\frac{P^{(n-1)/n}}{(n-1)/n}\right]_{P_1}^{P_2} = \frac{n}{n-1}\left(P_2^{(n-1)/n} - P_1^{(n-1)/n}\right)$$ **Step 3.** Assemble: $$W_s = -V_1 P_1^{1/n} \cdot \frac{n}{n-1}\left(P_2^{(n-1)/n} - P_1^{(n-1)/n}\right)$$ **Step 4.** Factor out $P_1^{(n-1)/n}$ and use $P_1^{1/n} \cdot P_1^{(n-1)/n} = P_1$: $$W_s = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[1 - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n}\right]$$ **Step 5.** Since compression work is done **on the gas** (system receives work, sign convention), the work input is positive: $$\boxed{\,W_{poly} = \frac{n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\!\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]\,}$$ **Step 6.** Convert to per-kg basis using $P_1 V_1 = R T_1 / M$ (or simply $R_{specific} T_1$ for air, $R = 287$ J/kg·K): $$W_{poly} = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] \quad \text{[J/kg]}$$ **Verification of special cases:** | If | $n \to$ | Result | Interpretation | |----|---------|--------|----------------| | $n = 1$ (isothermal, take limit) | $W_{iso} = R T_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | ✓ | Minimum work | | $n = \gamma = 1.4$ | $W_{adi}$ expression | ✓ | Maximum work (no cooling) | | $1 < n < 1.4$ | Realistic value | – | Real compressor with partial cooling | --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Optimum Intermediate Pressure for 2-Stage Compression Total work = work in stage 1 + work in stage 2 (assuming perfect intercooling returns $T$ to $T_1$): $$W_{total} = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_i}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] + \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_i}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ **Step 1.** Differentiate with respect to $P_i$ (treating $P_1, P_2$ as constants): $$\frac{dW_{total}}{dP_i} = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[\frac{n-1}{n} P_1^{1/n} \cdot P_i^{-1/n} \cdot \frac{1}{n-1} - \frac{n-1}{n} P_2^{1/n} \cdot P_i^{-1/n} \cdot \frac{1}{n-1}\right]$$ (Using $\frac{d}{dP_i}[P_i^{(n-1)/n}] = \frac{n-1}{n}\, P_i^{-1/n}$) **Step 2.** Simplify (factor $P_i^{-1/n}/n$): $$\frac{dW_{total}}{dP_i} = R T_1 \, P_i^{-1/n} \left[P_1^{1/n}\, P_i^{-1/n} - P_2^{1/n}\, P_i^{-1/n}\right]$$ After algebraic simplification: $$\frac{dW_{total}}{dP_i} = \frac{R T_1}{n} \left[\frac{P_1^{1/n}}{P_i^{1/n}} - \frac{P_2^{1/n}}{P_i^{2/n}}\right]$$ **Step 3.** Set derivative to zero for extremum: $$\frac{P_1^{1/n}}{P_i^{1/n}} = \frac{P_2^{1/n}}{P_i^{2/n}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad P_1^{1/n}\, P_i^{1/n} = P_2^{1/n}\, P_i^{0}$$ $$P_i^{1/n} = \frac{P_2^{1/n}}{P_1^{1/n}} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$ **Step 4.** Solve: $$\boxed{\,P_i^{opt} = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}\,}$$ **Physical meaning:** Each stage does **equal work** when $P_i = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$. This is a beautiful result and is asked in almost every KTU University exam. --- ## 3.3 Derivative-Free Numerical: Discharge Temperature Limit To find the **maximum safe pressure ratio** such that discharge temperature $T_2 \le T_{max}$ (say **180 °C = 453.15 K**), start with intake at $T_1 = 30\ °\text{C} = 303.15\ \text{K}$: $$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ $$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = \left(\frac{453.15}{303.15}\right)^{1.4/0.4} = (1.495)^{3.5}$$ $$\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)_{max} \approx 3.46$$ > **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Single-stage compression should not exceed a pressure ratio of **~4:1** to keep the discharge temperature below the lubricant's auto-ignition and to stay within safe mechanical limits. --- ## 3.4 Master Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) > **Problem:** A single-stage, single-acting reciprocating compressor compresses **0.06 m³/s of air at 1 bar, 27 °C** to **6 bar**. The clearance ratio is **0.04**, and the polytropic index is **n = 1.32**. Mechanical efficiency is 85 %, motor efficiency 92 %.
