T_s)$ | Enthalpy of superheated steam | kJ/kg | | 4 | $V = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | Specific volume of wet steam | m³/kg | | 5 | $S = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | Specific entropy of wet steam | kJ/(kg·K) | | 6 | $\dot{m}_{steam} = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda}$ | Steam generation rate | kg/s | | 7 | $W = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H$ | Heat duty transferred by steam | kW | | 8 | $S = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{steam}}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot \text{CV}}$ | Steam-to-fuel ratio (Boiler efficiency indicator) | kg steam/kg fuel | | 9 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{steam} (H_s - H_{fw})}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot \text{CV}}$ | Boiler thermal efficiency | dimensionless | | 10 | $P_{sat}(T) \approx P_c \cdot e^{\left(\dfrac{L}{R}\right)\left(\dfrac{1}{T_c} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)}$ | Clausius-Clapeyron approx. for saturation pressure | Pa | **Boundary Conditions & Notes:** - For **saturated liquid** → $x = 0$ → $H = h_f$ - For **saturated vapor** → $x = 1$ → $H = h_g = h_f + h_{fg}$ - At **critical point** (22.064 MPa, 373.946 °C) → $h_{fg} = 0$ - Always refer to **steam tables** (saturated + superheated) for accurate $h_f, h_g, h_{fg}, v, s$ values. ## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility The thermodynamic properties of steam are exploited in: - **Evaporators** (sugar, dairy, paper industries): Latent heat release at constant temperature. - **Steam Turbines** (power plants): High-pressure superheated steam expands to generate electricity. - **Steam Distillation** (essential oils, petroleum refining): Co-distillation of immiscible components. - **Steam Stripping** (gas treating, ammonia recovery): Desorption of volatiles from a liquid stream. - **Steam Tracing** (pipelines): Maintaining viscosity of heavy oils in cold environments. - **Cogeneration Systems**: Combined Heat and Power (CHP) using exhaust steam for process heating. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Connection (CO2 & CO3):** A 14-mark question often asks to size a boiler, determine steam quality, or compute steam economy for a given process duty. Memorize the formula sheet above for instant recall. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Total Heat of Wet Steam from First Principles ### Problem Statement Derive an expression for the total enthalpy of **1 kg of wet steam** with dryness fraction $x$ at pressure $P$, given that the liquid phase carries enthalpy $h_f$ and the vapor phase carries enthalpy $h_g$. ### Derivation **Step 1**: Consider 1 kg of wet steam. It contains a mixture of liquid water and water vapor. Mass of vapor in 1 kg of wet steam: $m_v = x$ kg Mass of liquid in 1 kg of wet steam: $m_l = (1 - x)$ kg **Step 2**: The total enthalpy $H$ of this mixture is the sum of enthalpies of its constituents: $$H = m_l \cdot h_f + m_v \cdot h_g$$ **Step 3**: Substitute the mass expressions: $$H = (1 - x) \cdot h_f + x \cdot h_g$$ **Step 4**: Expand the expression: $$H = h_f - x \cdot h_f + x \cdot h_g$$ **Step 5**: Factor out $x$: $$H = h_f + x \cdot (h_g - h_f)$$ **Step 6**: Recognize that $(h_g - h_f) = h_{fg}$ is the **latent heat of vaporization**: $$\boxed{H = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}}$$ > This is the **fundamental KTU formula** for wet steam enthalpy (in kJ/kg). ## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Volume of Wet Steam ### Problem Statement Show that the specific volume of wet steam is given by $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$. ### Derivation **Step 1**: In a wet steam mixture, total volume $V$ is the sum of volumes of liquid and vapor phases: $$V = V_{liquid} + V_{vapor}$$ **Step 2**: Express each volume in terms of mass and specific volume: $$V = m_l \cdot v_f + m_v \cdot v_g$$ **Step 3**: For 1 kg of wet steam: $m_l = (1-x)$ and $m_v = x$: $$V = (1 - x) \cdot v_f + x \cdot v_g$$ **Step 4**: Expand: $$V = v_f - x \cdot v_f + x \cdot v_g = v_f + x \cdot (v_g - v_f)$$ **Step 5**: Define $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ and recognize $V = v$ (specific volume for 1 kg): $$\boxed{v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}}$$ ## 3.3 Solved Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern) ### Problem A boiler operates at 10 bar pressure and produces wet steam with a dryness fraction of 0.92. Calculate: 1. The total enthalpy of the wet steam. 2. The specific volume. 3. The total heat to be supplied per kg of steam, given feed water enters at 60 °C. **Given Data (from Steam Tables, 10 bar):** - Saturation temperature: $T_s = 179.91$ °C - $h_f = 762.81$ kJ/kg - $h_{fg} = 2015.3$ kJ/kg - $v_f = 0.001157$ m³/kg - $v_{fg} = 0.19436$ m³/kg ### Solution **Part (a): Total Enthalpy of Wet Steam** $$H = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$$ Substituting values: $$H = 762.81 + (0.92) \cdot (2015.3)$$ $$H = 762.81 + 1854.076$$ $$\boxed{H = 2616.886 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Part (b): Specific Volume of Wet Steam** $$v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$$ $$v = 0.001157 + (0.92) \cdot (0.19436)$$ $$v = 0.001157 + 0.178811$$ $$\boxed{v = 0.179968 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}}$$ **Part (c): Total Heat Supplied per kg of Steam** Feed water enters at 60 °C. Sensible heat from 60 °C to 179.91 °C: $$q_{fw} = C_{p,\text{water}} \cdot (T_s - T_{fw}) = 4.18 \cdot (179.91 - 60)$$ $$q_{fw} = 4.18 \cdot 119.91 = 501.22 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Total heat supplied to convert feed water (at 60 °C) to wet steam (at 10 bar, $x = 0.92$): $$Q_{total} = q_{fw} + x \cdot h_{fg}$$ $$Q_{total} = 501.22 + 0.92 \cdot 2015.3$$ $$Q_{total} = 501.22 + 1854.076$$ $$\boxed{Q_{total} = 2355.30 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Saturated Steam Properties vs. Pressure | Pressure (bar) | $T_s$ (°C) | $h_f$ (kJ/kg) | $h_{fg}$ (kJ/kg) | $h_g$ (kJ/kg) | $v_g$ (m³/kg) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | 99.63 | 417.46 | 2258.0 | 2675.5 | 1.6940 | | 2 | 120.23 | 504.70 | 2201.9 | 2706.7 | 0.8857 | | 4 | 143.63 | 604.74 | 2133.8 | 2738.6 | 0.4625 | | 7 | 164.97 | 697.22 | 2066.3 | 2763.5 | 0.2729 | | 10 | 179.91 | 762.81 | 2015.3 | 2778.1 | 0.1944 | | 15 | 198.32 | 844.84 | 1947.3 | 2792.2 | 0.1317 | | 20 | 212.42 | 908.79 | 1890.7 | 2799.5 | 0.0996 | **Observed Trends (Exam-Worthy Inferences):** - As pressure increases, $T_s$ **rises** (clausius-clapeyron principle). - $h_f$ **increases** with pressure (more energy stored in compressed liquid). - $h_{fg}$ **decreases** with pressure (latent heat vanishing at critical point). - $v_g$ **decreases** sharply (steam becomes denser at high pressure). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 P-T Phase Diagram of Water (Schematic Block Architecture) The following block diagram explains the **Pressure–Temperature (P–T) relationship** for water/steam and indicates the regions of solid, liquid, vapor, and supercritical fluid. ```mermaid flowchart TB axisA["Pressure P (MPa)"] --> axisB subgraph axisB [" "] direction TB regionC["Supercritical Region (P > 22.064 MPa, T > 373.95 C)"] regionA["Solid Phase Ice Region (Low T, High P)"] regionD["Liquid Water Region (P-T sub-critical, T < Ts)"] regionE["Saturated Liquid-Vapor Curve Line (Ts at given P)"] regionF["Vapor Steam Region (P < Psat at given T)"] end triplePt["Triple Point (0.000611 MPa, 0.01 C) - All 3 phases coexist"] criticalPt["Critical Point (22.064 MPa, 373.95 C) - Latent heat becomes zero"] fusionLine["Fusion Curve - separates solid and liquid"] vapLine["Vaporization Curve - separates liquid and vapor"] sublimLine["Sublimation Curve - separates solid and vapor"] axisA --> triplePt axisA --> criticalPt axisB -.Fusion Curve.-> regionA axisB -.Vaporization Curve.-> regionE axisB -.Sublimation Curve.-> regionF ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** The **vaporization curve** is the working curve of every boiler engineer. It is the locus of (P, T) points where liquid water and steam coexist in equilibrium. ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Steam Generation, Distribution, and Utilization System ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Feed Water Tank
Deaerated Water at 60 C"] --> A2["Feed Water Pump
P = 1.5 x Operating P"] A2 --> A3["Economizer
Preheats by Flue Gas"] A3 --> A4["Boiler Steam Drum
P = 7 to 40 bar typical"] A4 --> A5["Steam Header
Saturated Steam Collection"] A5 --> B1["Process Heat Exchanger 1
Latent Heat Release"] A5 --> B2["Process Heat Exchanger 2
Latent Heat Release"] A5 --> B3["Steam Turbine
Mechanical Work Extraction"] A5 --> B4["Steam Ejector / Vacuum System"] B1 --> C1["Condensate Return Header
Hot Liquid"] B2 --> C1 B3 --> C1 C1 --> D1["Condensate Receiver"] D1 --> D2["Condensate Pump"] D2 --> A1 E1["Fuel + Air Combustion"] --> A4 A4 --> F1["Flue Gas to Economizer"] F1 --> F2["Flue Gas to Stack"] ``` **System Description:** 1. **Feed Water Loop**: Deaerated water at ~60 °C is pressurized, preheated in the economizer, and fed to the boiler drum. 2. **Steam Generation**: In the boiler, combustion gases transfer heat to convert water into saturated or superheated steam. 3. **Distribution**: Steam is collected in a header and distributed to **process heaters**, **turbines**, and **ejectors**. 4. **Recovery**: After heat/energy release, steam condenses to **hot liquid condensate**, which is recovered, treated, and recycled to the feed water tank. ## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Steam Quality vs. State ```mermaid flowchart TB s1["Water at 25 C, 1 atm
Sub-cooled Liquid"] --> s2["Water at 100 C, 1 atm
Saturated Liquid x=0"] s2 --> s3["Wet Steam 100 C, 1 atm
x = 0.4, 0.5, 0.6 etc"] s3 --> s4["Dry Saturated Steam
x = 1, T = Ts"] s4 --> s5["Superheated Steam
T > Ts at given P"] s5 --> s6["Turbine Exhaust
Lower P, Lower T"] p1["Process A: Heating Coil
Requires Saturated Steam"] -.uses.-> s4 p2["Process B: Power Generation
Requires Superheated Steam"] -.uses.-> s5 p3["Process C: Heating Pad
Allows Wet Steam"] -.uses.-> s3 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Concept:** Different processes demand different steam qualities. **Turbines** need superheated (to avoid blade erosion), **heat exchangers** work best with dry saturated (maximum temperature gradient), and **direct injection heating** can use wet steam for cost savings. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define steam. List any four industrial applications of steam.** **Model Answer:** Steam is the gaseous form of water produced when water absorbs its **latent heat of vaporization** at or above its saturation temperature corresponding to a given pressure. Four industrial applications: 1. **Heating medium** in reboilers, evaporators, and jacketed reactors (latent heat release). 2. **Mechanical work driver** in steam turbines (Cogeneration / Combined Heat and Power). 3. **Process reactant** in steam reforming, steam cracking, and water-gas shift reactions. 4. **Atomization medium** in oil-fired burners and steam ejectors for vacuum generation. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Tip (3-mark question):** 1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for applications. Use bullet points for clarity. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is dryness fraction? How is it related to the total enthalpy of wet steam?** **Model Answer:** **Dryness fraction ($x$)** is the ratio of the mass of actual dry vapor to the total mass of wet steam mixture. It is dimensionless and lies in the range $0 \leq x \leq 1$. $$x = \frac{m_{vapor}}{m_{vapor} + m_{liquid}}$$ **Relationship to Total Enthalpy:** The total enthalpy of 1 kg of wet steam is given by: $$H = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$$ where $h_f$ is the enthalpy of saturated liquid and $h_{fg}$ is the latent heat of vaporization at the given pressure. As $x$ increases from 0 to 1, the enthalpy rises linearly from $h_f$ to $h_g$. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Tip:** Always mention the range of $x$ and define it as a mass-based ratio (not volume-based). A common mistake is using vapor volume — this is WRONG. ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **classification of steam** with the help of a P-T diagram. State the **properties of saturated steam** at 10 bar with reference to steam tables. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A boiler generates **5000 kg/h of wet steam** at **15 bar pressure** with a dryness fraction of **0.95**. The feed water enters the boiler at **45 °C**. Calculate: 1. Total enthalpy of the wet steam generated. 2. Total heat load on the boiler per hour. 3. The mass of fuel oil required per hour if the calorific value is **42,000 kJ/kg** and boiler efficiency is **85%**. **(7 Marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks) **Classification of Steam (4 Marks):** 1. **Saturated Liquid Steam (x = 0):** Water at its boiling point corresponding to a given pressure, with no vapor content. 2. **Wet Steam (0 < x < 1):** A two-phase mixture of liquid and vapor at the saturation temperature. 3. **Dry Saturated Steam (x = 1):** Pure saturated vapor at the saturation temperature, free of any entrained liquid. 4. **Superheated Steam (T > T_s):** Steam heated above its saturation temperature, with no moisture, used in turbines to avoid blade erosion. **P-T Diagram Explanation (2 Marks):** - Plot P (y-axis, log scale) vs. T (x-axis). - The **vaporization curve** separates liquid and vapor regions. - The **critical point** (22.064 MPa, 373.95 °C) marks the end of the curve, where $h_{fg} = 0$. - Above the critical point → **supercritical fluid region**. **Steam Table Values at 10 bar (1 Mark):** - $T_s = 179.91$ °C - $h_f = 762.81$ kJ/kg - $h_{fg} = 2015.3$ kJ/kg - $h_g = 2778.1$ kJ/kg - $v_g = 0.1944$ m³/kg --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** - $\dot{m}_{steam} = 5000$ kg/h - $P = 15$ bar - $x = 0.95$ - $T_{fw} = 45$ °C - CV of fuel = 42,000 kJ/kg - $\eta_{boiler} = 0.85$ **Steam Table values at 15 bar:** - $T_s = 198.32$ °C - $h_f = 844.84$ kJ/kg - $h_{fg} = 1947.3$ kJ/kg **Step 1: Sensible heat gained by feed water (1 Mark)** $$q_{fw} = C_p \cdot (T_s - T_{fw}) = 4.18 \cdot (198.32 - 45)$$ $$q_{fw} = 4.18 \cdot 153.32 = 640.88 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2: Total enthalpy of wet steam (2 Marks)** $$H = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$$ $$H = 844.84 + (0.95) \cdot (1947.3) = 844.84 + 1849.94$$ $$\boxed{H = 2694.78 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Step 3: Total heat load on the boiler (2 Marks)** $$Q_{total} = q_{fw} + x \cdot h_{fg} = 640.88 + 1849.94$$ $$\boxed{Q_{total} = 2490.82 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ Hourly heat load: $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot Q_{total} = 5000 \cdot 2490.82$$ $$\boxed{\dot{Q} = 12,454,100 \text{ kJ/h} = 3,459.47 \text{ kW}}$$ **Step 4: Fuel oil required per hour (2 Marks)** $$\eta_{boiler} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot \text{CV}}$$ $$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\eta_{boiler} \cdot \text{CV}} = \frac{12,454,100}{0.85 \cdot 42,000}$$ $$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{12,454,100}{35,700} = 348.86 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m}_{fuel} \approx 349 \text{ kg/h}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls (Examiner's Warning):** > 1. Many students **forget to include $q_{fw}$** in the total heat calculation. The boiler heats water from $T_{fw}$ to $T_s$, then vaporizes it — both contribute. > 2. Students often confuse **$h_g$** with **$H$** for wet steam. $h_g$ corresponds to **x = 1** (saturated vapor); wet steam has $H = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$. > 3. Do not use $\Delta T$ in **K** when $C_p$ is given in **kJ/(kg·K)** — the value is numerically the same as °C, so the calculation is correct, but write units carefully to avoid losing a mark. > 4. Steam table values must be read at the **operating pressure**, not the feed water pressure. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — *Internal Choice Alternative* **(a)** Discuss the **importance of steam** as a process utility in the chemical industry. Explain why **saturated steam is preferred** over superheated steam for process heating applications. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A process heat exchanger requires **2000 kg/h of dry saturated steam** at **7 bar** to deliver a heat duty of **1200 kW**. The feed water enters at **30 °C**. 1. Calculate the **steam economy** (kg steam / kJ heat transferred). 2. Determine the **boiler steam generation rate** if the dryness fraction of the generated steam is **0.93**. 3. If the available fuel has a calorific value of **38,000 kJ/kg**, calculate the **fuel consumption rate** assuming 80% boiler efficiency. **(7 Marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution Outline for Question B (For Reference) **Part (a) — Key Points to Cover (7 Marks):** - Steam as heating medium (1 Mark): Isothermal heat release at high latent heat (2257 kJ/kg at 1 atm). - Steam as mechanical driver (1 Mark): Turbines for pumps and compressors. - Steam as reactant (1 Mark): Steam reforming, cracking. - Steam as humidification agent (1 Mark): Air-conditioning, drying. - **Why saturated steam is preferred (3 Marks):** 1. Maximum temperature gradient (latent heat at constant $T_s$). 2. Self-regulating temperature (changes with pressure). 3. Higher heat transfer coefficient during condensation. 4. Superheated steam, being gas, has lower $h$ and poor heat transfer → not economical for heating. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution Outline:** 1. $q = H_g - H_{fw} = 2763.5 - (4.18 \cdot (164.97 - 30)) = 2763.5 - 564.0 = 2199.5$ kJ/kg - Steam economy = $1/2199.5 = 4.547 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/kJ 2. With $x = 0.93$ at 7 bar: $H_{wet} = 697.22 + 0.93 \cdot 2066.3 = 2618.89$ kJ/kg - Boiler steam rate = $\frac{\dot{Q}}{H_{wet} - H_{fw}} = \frac{2000 \cdot 2199.5}{2618.89 - 165.16} = \frac{4.399 \times 10^6}{2453.73} = 1793$ kg/h 3. Fuel rate = $\frac{1793 \cdot (2618.89 - 165.16)}{0.80 \cdot 38000} = \frac{1793 \cdot 2453.73}{30400} = 144.7$ kg/h ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin This for Last-Minute Review):** - **Steam** = gaseous water with stored latent and/or sensible heat. **Generated in a boiler.** - **Saturated steam** is in equilibrium with liquid water. **Superheated steam** is above $T_s$. - **Dryness fraction** $x = m_v / m_{total}$, range $0 \leq x \leq 1$, dimensionless. - **Master formula for wet steam enthalpy**: $H = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ ⭐ (Most Repeated KTU Formula). - **Total heat input to boiler** = $C_p (T_s - T_{fw}) + x \cdot h_{fg}$ per kg of steam. - **Boiler efficiency**: $\eta = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{steam}(H_s - H_{fw})}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot CV}$. - **As pressure increases**: - $T_s$ ↑, $h_f$ ↑, $h_{fg}$ ↓, $v_g$ ↓. - At critical point (22.064 MPa, 373.95 °C): $h_{fg} = 0$. - **Saturated steam > superheated steam** for process heating (isothermal, high latent heat). - **Superheated steam** is mandatory for turbines (avoids blade erosion from moisture). - Always use **steam tables** for accurate property values; never approximate from memory in numericals. - Standard values to remember: $\lambda_{100°C} = 2257$ kJ/kg, $C_{p,water} = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K), $C_{p,steam} = 2.01$ kJ/(kg·K). - **Industrial uses of steam**: reboilers, evaporators, turbines, ejectors, steam tracing, distillation, stripping, SMR. - **P-T diagram** is the **most-asked visual**; mark the vaporization curve, critical point, and triple point. - **Mollier diagram** (h-s chart) is used for turbine work calculation — relevant for Module 4 onwards.

topics.title: Properties of Steam: Wet Steam, Dry Saturated Steam, and Superheated Steam
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Properties of Steam: Wet Steam, Dry Saturated Steam, and Superheated Steam # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In process utilities and thermodynamics, **steam** is the gaseous phase of water (H₂O) used extensively as a working fluid for **heat transfer, power generation, and mechanical work** in chemical process industries. The three principal classifications of steam, based on the thermodynamic state of water during phase change, are: ### a) Wet Steam **Wet steam** is a mechanical mixture of **saturated liquid water (saturated water)** and **dry saturated steam (saturated vapor)** coexisting in thermal equilibrium at the same **pressure** and **saturation temperature (Tₛ)**. It is characterized quantitatively by the dimensionless parameter known as the **dryness fraction (x)**, also called the **quality of steam**, defined as: $$x = \frac{\text{Mass of dry saturated steam}}{\text{Total mass of wet steam}} \quad ; \quad 0 \leq x < 1$$ ### b) Dry Saturated Steam **Dry saturated steam** is steam that exists exactly at its **saturation temperature** for a given pressure, containing **no entrained liquid water** (i.e., x = 1, also called *saturated vapor*). It is essentially pure vapor at the dew point of water. ### c) Superheated Steam **Superheated steam** is steam that has been heated to a temperature **above its saturation temperature** at a given pressure. The difference between the actual temperature and the saturation temperature is termed the **degree of superheat**. $$\Delta T_{sup} = T_{sup} - T_{sat}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must master the ability to compute **specific enthalpy (h), specific internal energy (u), specific volume (v), and specific entropy (s)** of all three steam states using steam table data. These properties are the most frequently tested concepts in ESE questions. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a kettle boiling on a stove: - **Phase 1 – Subcooled (Sensible) Water:** Before the kettle whistles, the water inside is hot but still liquid. You can see it, touch it — it's water. - **Phase 2 – Wet Steam (Two-Phase Mixture):** As the kettle starts whistling vigorously, look closely near the spout. You will see a **cloud-like white plume**. That visible white mist is NOT pure steam — it is a **suspension of tiny liquid water droplets inside invisible vapor**. This is **wet steam** — a mixture of liquid and vapor in equilibrium. - **Phase 3 – Dry Saturated Steam:** Inside the kettle, *just above* the boiling water surface, the vapor is completely gaseous, at exactly 100 °C (at atmospheric pressure) — this is **dry saturated steam** (x = 1). - **Phase 4 – Superheated Steam:** Now take that dry steam and pass it through a coil heated further (as in a power plant superheater). The temperature rises *above* 100 °C even though pressure is constant. This is **superheated steam** — invisible, dry, and capable of transferring much more energy. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world visibility check:** Pure dry/superheated steam is **invisible**. The white "steam" you see from a kettle is actually wet steam (condensed microdroplets). This is a quick mental test for steam quality. --- ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants - **Latent Heat of Vaporization (h_fg)** at 100 °C: **2257 kJ/kg** (Steam's enormous latent heat makes it an exceptional heat-transfer medium) - **Specific Heat of Superheated Steam (Cₚ):** ≈ **2.01 kJ/(kg·K)** (approximately constant) - **Specific Heat of Liquid Water (C_pw):** **4.18 kJ/(kg·K)** - **Critical Point of Water:** P_c = **22.06 MPa**, T_c = **374 °C** - **Triple Point:** P_t = **0.006112 bar**, T_t = **0.01 °C** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Significance:** Steam's high latent heat (≈ 5× the energy required to heat water from 0 °C to 100 °C) is the fundamental reason it dominates industrial heat-transfer applications — **1 kg of steam releases ~2257 kJ on condensation**, far exceeding the energy stored in equal mass of hot water. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermodynamic Evolution of Water to Steam The complete transformation from subcooled liquid water at 0 °C to superheated steam can be visualized in **five distinct thermodynamic stages**: ### Stage 1: Sensible Heating of Liquid (Subcooled Region) Water is heated from 0 °C to the saturation temperature T_s at constant pressure. Energy absorbed is **sensible heat** (no phase change, temperature rises): $$q_1 = h_f = C_{pw} \cdot (T_s - 0)$$ ### Stage 2: Phase Change (Latent Heating, Wet Steam Region) At T_s, water begins to vaporize. The mixture of liquid + vapor is **wet steam**. The fraction that has vaporized is the dryness fraction *x*. Latent heat absorbed: $$q_2 = x \cdot h_{fg}$$ where $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ is the **latent heat of vaporization**. ### Stage 3: End of Vaporization (Dry Saturated Steam, x = 1) All liquid has converted to vapor. The steam is at its dew point (T = T_s) — **dry saturated steam**. ### Stage 4: Superheating Region Further heat is added at constant pressure, raising the temperature above T_s. The steam becomes **superheated**: $$q_3 = C_p \cdot (T_{sup} - T_s)$$ ### Stage 5: Total Enthalpy of Superheated Steam Summing all stages, the total enthalpy (per unit mass) of superheated steam measured from 0 °C liquid water is: $$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} + C_p \cdot (T_{sup} - T_s)$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Pattern:** This additive structure of enthalpy is the single most important equation for ESE problems. Examiners often provide steam-table values of $h_f$, $h_g$, and $C_p$, then ask for total enthalpy of a given state. --- ## 2.2 The Critical Parameter: Dryness Fraction (x) The dryness fraction quantifies how much of the wet steam mixture is actually vapor: $$x = \frac{m_{vapor}}{m_{liquid} + m_{vapor}} = \frac{m_g}{m_f + m_g}$$ If a tank contains 5 kg of liquid water and 3 kg of dry saturated vapor: $$x = \frac{3}{5+3} = 0.375 \;\; (37.5\%)$$ The complementary parameter, **wetness fraction (y)**, is: $$y = 1 - x$$ --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **#** | **Property** | **Wet Steam Formula** | **Dry Saturated Steam (x = 1)** | **Superheated Steam** | **Units** | |:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | Specific Volume | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | $v = v_g$ | $v_{sup}$ (from tables) | m³/kg | | 2 | Specific Enthalpy | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | $h = h_g$ | $h = h_g + C_p(T_{sup} - T_s)$ | kJ/kg | | 3 | Specific Internal Energy | $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ | $u = u_g$ | $u_{sup}$ (from tables) | kJ/kg | | 4 | Specific Entropy | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | $s = s_g$ | $s_{sup}$ (from tables) | kJ/(kg·K) | | 5 | Latent Heat | $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ | — | — | kJ/kg | | 6 | Internal Energy of Evap. | $u_{fg} = h_{fg} - P \cdot v_{fg}$ | — | — | kJ/kg | | 7 | Dryness Fraction | $x = \dfrac{m_g}{m_f + m_g}$ | x = 1 | Not applicable | — | | 8 | Degree of Superheat | — | — | $\Delta T = T_{sup} - T_s$ | K or °C | | 9 | Total Enthalpy (from 0 °C) | $H = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | $H = h_g$ | $H = h_f + h_{fg} + C_p(T_{sup}-T_s)$ | kJ/kg | | 10 | Specific Heat (approx.) | — | — | $C_p \approx 2.01$ kJ/(kg·K) | kJ/(kg·K) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These properties are foundational to designing **boilers, condensers, evaporators, distillation columns, heat exchangers, and steam turbines**. KTU students from Chemical, Mechanical, and Biotechnology streams must master this for process-equipment design and energy-balance calculations. --- ## 2.4 Engineering Significance | **Steam State** | **Industrial Use** | |:---|:---| | Wet steam (low x) | **NOT preferred** — entrained water causes turbine blade erosion | | Wet steam (high x) | Used in **process heating** via direct injection | | Dry saturated steam | Used in **sterilization, humidification, food processing** | | Superheated steam | Used in **power generation turbines, process reboilers** (high-temp duty) | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Enthalpy of Wet Steam (Rigorous) ### Setup Consider **1 kg of water** initially at 0 °C, contained in a piston-cylinder assembly at constant pressure P. Heat is supplied until a fraction *x* of the mass has vaporized into steam. ### Step 1 — Sensible Heating from 0 °C to T_s The water (mass = 1 kg) is heated from 0 °C to saturation temperature $T_s$ at constant pressure. Using the specific heat of liquid water $C_{pw}$: $$Q_1 = m \cdot C_{pw} \cdot (T_s - 0)$$ $$\boxed{Q_1 = 1 \cdot C_{pw} \cdot T_s = h_f}$$ By definition, $h_f$ is the **enthalpy of saturated liquid** at the given pressure, measured from 0 °C reference. So $Q_1 = h_f$. ### Step 2 — Latent Heating (Phase Change) Of the 1 kg total mass, a mass $x$ kg vaporizes. The latent heat absorbed is: $$Q_2 = m_{vaporized} \cdot h_{fg} = x \cdot h_{fg}$$ ### Step 3 — Total Enthalpy of Wet Steam Adding sensible and latent contributions: $$h_{wet} = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} \quad \text{(per kg of mixture)} \quad \blacksquare$$ ### Step 4 — Alternative Form Using h_g Since $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$, we can rewrite: $$h_{wet} = h_f + x \cdot (h_g - h_f)$$ $$h_{wet} = (1 - x) \cdot h_f + x \cdot h_g$$ This elegant form shows wet steam enthalpy is a **mass-weighted average** of saturated liquid and saturated vapor enthalpies — a direct consequence of the linear lever rule on the T-h diagram. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Internal Energy of Evaporation (u_fg) Starting from the first law for a constant-pressure phase change: $$h_{fg} = u_{fg} + P \cdot \Delta v$$ where $\Delta v = v_g - v_f = v_{fg}$. Rearranging: $$\boxed{u_{fg} = h_{fg} - P \cdot v_{fg}} \quad \blacksquare$$ **Physical meaning:** The latent heat $h_{fg}$ partly goes into (a) the **internal energy of vaporization** $u_{fg}$ (which does the molecular work of separating liquid molecules into vapor) and (b) the **flow work** $P \cdot v_{fg}$ (work done by the expanding vapor at constant pressure). --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Enthalpy of Superheated Steam ### Step 1 Start with dry saturated steam at $T_s$ with enthalpy $h_g$. ### Step 2 Heat the steam at constant pressure from $T_s$ to $T_{sup}$. The added sensible heat is: $$Q_{sup} = C_p \cdot (T_{sup} - T_s)$$ ### Step 3 Total enthalpy: $$h_{sup} = h_g + C_p \cdot (T_{sup} - T_s)$$ Since $h_g = h_f + h_{fg}$: $$\boxed{h_{sup} = h_f + h_{fg} + C_p \cdot (T_{sup} - T_s)} \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Trick for KTU exams:** If exact $C_p$ is not given, assume $C_p = 2.01$ kJ/(kg·K). Many solved problems in KTU textbooks use this approximation. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** Steam at 10 bar has a dryness fraction of 0.85. Calculate (a) the specific enthalpy, (b) the specific volume, and (c) the specific entropy of the wet steam. ### Given (read from standard steam tables at 10 bar = 1.0 MPa): - $T_s = 179.9 \; °C$ - $h_f = 762.81 \; kJ/kg$ - $h_{fg} = 2015.3 \; kJ/kg$ - $v_f = 0.001127 \; m^3/kg$ - $v_g = 0.1944 \; m^3/kg$ - $s_f = 2.1387 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ - $s_{fg} = 4.4474 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ - $x = 0.85$ ### (a) Specific Enthalpy $$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$$ $$h = 762.81 + (0.85)(2015.3)$$ $$h = 762.81 + 1713.005$$ $$\boxed{h = 2475.815 \; kJ/kg}$$ ### (b) Specific Volume $$v_{fg} = v_g - v_f = 0.1944 - 0.001127 = 0.193273 \; m^3/kg$$ $$v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg} = 0.001127 + (0.85)(0.193273)$$ $$v = 0.001127 + 0.164282$$ $$\boxed{v = 0.165409 \; m^3/kg}$$ ### (c) Specific Entropy $$s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg} = 2.1387 + (0.85)(4.4474)$$ $$s = 2.1387 + 3.78029$$ $$\boxed{s = 5.91899 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)}$$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Example: Superheated Steam Enthalpy **Problem:** Steam is generated at 20 bar with a superheat of 80 °C. Find its total enthalpy per kg, given that at 20 bar, $T_s = 212.4 \; °C$, $h_f = 908.79 \; kJ/kg$, $h_{fg} = 1890.7 \; kJ/kg$, and $C_p = 2.01 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$. ### Solution $$T_{sup} = T_s + \Delta T_{sup} = 212.4 + 80 = 292.4 \; °C$$ $$h_{sup} = h_f + h_{fg} + C_p \cdot (T_{sup} - T_s)$$ $$h_{sup} = 908.79 + 1890.7 + 2.01 \times 80$$ $$h_{sup} = 908.79 + 1890.7 + 160.8$$ $$\boxed{h_{sup} = 2960.29 \; kJ/kg}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Insight:** Many valuation scripts lose 1–2 marks because students forget the **$h_f$** term in superheated enthalpy — they only add $C_p \cdot \Delta T$ to $h_g$. Always write out the **full additive form** to be safe. --- ## 3.6 Comparison Matrix of the Three Steam States | **Parameter** | **Wet Steam** | **Dry Saturated Steam** | **Superheated Steam** | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | Dryness fraction *x* | $0 \leq x < 1$ | $x = 1$ | Not applicable (always vapor) | | Temperature relative to $T_s$ | $T = T_s$ | $T = T_s$ | $T > T_s$ | | Phase content | Liquid + vapor | Vapor only | Vapor only | | Specific volume *v* | Small (between $v_f$ and $v_g$) | $v_g$ (moderate) | Very large (≫ $v_g$) | | Specific enthalpy *h* | $h_f + x h_{fg}$ | $h_g$ | $h_g + C_p \Delta T$ | | Specific entropy *s* | $s_f + x s_{fg}$ | $s_g$ | $s_g + C_p \ln(T_{sup}/T_s)$ | | Energy release on condensation | $\leq h_{fg}$ | $h_{fg}$ (maximum) | $h_{fg} + C_p \Delta T$ (very large) | | Visibility | Visible white cloud | Invisible | Invisible | | Industrial use | Direct process heating | Sterilization, reboilers | Power turbines, high-temp heating | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Steam Formation Flow (Process Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Subcooled Liquid Water
T less than T_sat
0 degree C reference"] -->|Sensible heat C_pw dT| B["Saturated Liquid Water
T = T_sat
h equals h_f"] B -->|Latent heat x times h_fg| C["Wet Steam Mixture
T = T_sat
0 less than x less than 1
h equals h_f plus x h_fg"] C -->|Continue heating x reaches 1| D["Dry Saturated Steam
T = T_sat
x equals 1
h equals h_g"] D -->|Superheat C_p dT| E["Superheated Steam
T greater than T_sat
h equals h_g plus C_p dT"] E -->|Reject heat in condenser| C C -->|Reject heat further| B B -->|Reject heat| A ``` ## 4.2 Classification Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S0["Steam Sample at Pressure P"] --> S1{"Compare actual T
with T_sat at P"} S1 -->|T less than T_sat| S2["Compressed Liquid
or Subcooled Water"] S1 -->|T equals T_sat| S3{"Single phase or
two phase?"} S3 -->|Two phase mixture| S4["Wet Steam
0 less than x less than 1"] S3 -->|Pure vapor| S5["Dry Saturated Steam
x equals 1"] S1 -->|T greater than T_sat| S6["Superheated Steam
Degree of superheat
equals T minus T_sat"] S4 --> S4A["Compute properties using
h equals h_f plus x h_fg"] S5 --> S5A["Use saturation values
h equals h_g"] S6 --> S6A["Use superheat tables or
h equals h_g plus C_p dT"] ``` ## 4.3 Process Industry Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph LR subgraph BoilerIsland["Boiler Island Generation"] FW["Feed Water Tank
subcooled"] --> ECO["Economiser
preheats to T_sat"] ECO --> EVAP["Evaporator Drums
wet steam region"] EVAP --> SH1["Primary Superheater
stage 1"] SH1 --> SH2["Final Superheater
stage 2 T greater than T_sat"] end subgraph ProcessConsumers["Process Consumers"] SH2 --> ST["Steam Turbine
power generation"] SH2 --> RB["Reboilers Distillation
high temp duty"] SH2 --> HX["Process Heat Exchangers
heating duties"] SH2 --> STR["Steriliser Autoclave
food pharma"] end subgraph ReturnLoop["Condensate Recovery"] ST --> COND["Condenser
wet steam to liquid"] RB --> COND HX --> COND STR --> COND COND --> FW end ``` ## 4.4 State-Property Block Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["Pressure P bar"] --> P2["Saturation Temp T_s from
steam table lookup"] P2 --> P3{"Three Zones
at given P"} P3 --> Z1["Z1 Wet
x in 0 to 1
T fixed at T_s"] P3 --> Z2["Z2 Dry Sat
x equals 1
T fixed at T_s"] P3 --> Z3["Z3 Superheated
T greater than T_s
degree of superheat"] Z1 --> R1["Read h_f h_g v_f v_g
s_f s_g from tables"] Z2 --> R1 Z3 --> R2["Read superheated
steam table at P T"] R1 --> F1["Apply weighted formulas
h equals h_f plus x h_fg"] R2 --> F2["Direct read or
h equals h_g plus C_p dT"] F1 --> OUT["Required Property h v s u"] F2 --> OUT ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define the term "dryness fraction" of steam. Why is it an important parameter in steam engineering?** **Model Answer:** The **dryness fraction (x)** of wet steam is defined as the ratio of the mass of dry saturated steam to the total mass of the wet steam mixture: $$x = \frac{m_{dry\;saturated\;steam}}{m_{total\;wet\;steam}} = \frac{m_g}{m_f + m_g}$$ **Importance:** 1. Determines the **amount of latent heat** actually available in wet steam: $h_{fg,available} = x \cdot h_{fg}$ 2. Affects **specific volume** and **specific enthalpy** of the steam 3. **Quality control indicator** — low *x* means more entrained water, which can damage **steam turbine blades** via erosion 4. Critical for **process efficiency** calculations in chemical plants 5. Determines **steam quality** in headers supplying process equipment *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for importance)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Differentiate between dry saturated steam and superheated steam.** **Model Answer:** | **Basis** | **Dry Saturated Steam** | **Superheated Steam** | |:---|:---|:---| | Temperature | $T = T_{saturation}$ at given P | $T > T_{saturation}$ at given P | | Phase | Pure vapor at dew point | Pure vapor above dew point | | Heat content | $h = h_g$ (only latent + sensible to T_s) | $h = h_g + C_p(T - T_s)$ (additional sensible) | | Specific volume | $v = v_g$ (moderate) | Much larger than $v_g$ | | Entropy | $s = s_g$ | $s > s_g$ | | Visibility | Invisible (if truly dry) | Invisible | | Use | Sterilizers, low-temp heaters | Power turbines, high-temp process duty | *(3 marks: 1 for each difference listed with at least 4 distinct points)* --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (MODULE 3 PITFALLS):** > - **Mark Loss #1 (–2 marks):** Students often compute wet-steam enthalpy using $h_g$ only instead of $h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$. Always check whether the steam is wet, dry, or superheated **first**. > - **Mark Loss #2 (–2 marks):** Confusing **specific volume $v_g$** with **specific volume of superheated steam**. For superheated steam, $v_{sup}$ must be read from the superheated steam table, not from the saturated table. > - **Mark Loss #3 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to **convert units** (e.g., pressure in bar vs MPa, temperature in °C vs K). At 10 bar: $P = 1.0$ MPa. > - **Mark Loss #4 (–1 mark):** Writing latent heat $h_{fg}$ when the steam is **superheated** — there is no $h_{fg}$ component for superheated steam; the relevant addition is $C_p \cdot \Delta T$. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3] **(a)** A boiler generates steam at 15 bar with a dryness fraction of 0.9. Using steam table data at 15 bar ($T_s = 198.3 \; °C$, $h_f = 844.84 \; kJ/kg$, $h_{fg} = 1947.3 \; kJ/kg$, $v_f = 0.001154 \; m^3/kg$, $v_g = 0.1317 \; m^3/kg$, $s_f = 2.3150 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$, $s_{fg} = 4.1265 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$), calculate: **(i)** The specific enthalpy of the wet steam **(3 marks)** **(ii)** The specific volume of the wet steam **(2 marks)** **(iii)** The specific entropy of the wet steam **(2 marks)** **(b)** The above wet steam is then superheated by 70 °C at the same pressure. Assuming $C_p = 2.01 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$, determine: **(i)** The total enthalpy of the superheated steam per kg **(4 marks)** **(ii)** The total heat supplied per kg to take the steam from saturated liquid at 15 bar to the final superheated state **(3 marks)** --- #### MODEL SOLUTION **Part (a) — Wet Steam Property Calculations** **Given:** P = 15 bar, x = 0.9, and the steam table values above. **(i) Specific Enthalpy (3 marks)** $$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$$ $$h = 844.84 + (0.9)(1947.3)$$ $$h = 844.84 + 1752.57$$ $$\boxed{h = 2597.41 \; kJ/kg}$$ **(ii) Specific Volume (2 marks)** $$v = v_f + x(v_g - v_f)$$ $$v = 0.001154 + 0.9 \cdot (0.1317 - 0.001154)$$ $$v = 0.001154 + 0.9 \cdot (0.130546)$$ $$v = 0.001154 + 0.117491$$ $$\boxed{v = 0.118645 \; m^3/kg}$$ **(iii) Specific Entropy (2 marks)** $$s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$$ $$s = 2.3150 + (0.9)(4.1265)$$ $$s = 2.3150 + 3.71385$$ $$\boxed{s = 6.02885 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)}$$ --- **Part (b) — Superheated Steam Calculations** **Given:** Superheat $\Delta T = 70 \; °C$, $C_p = 2.01 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$, $T_s = 198.3 \; °C$. $T_{sup} = T_s + \Delta T = 198.3 + 70 = 268.3 \; °C$. **(i) Total Enthalpy of Superheated Steam (4 marks)** Method 1 — Direct addition: $$h_{sup} = h_g + C_p \cdot (T_{sup} - T_s)$$ $$h_g = h_f + h_{fg} = 844.84 + 1947.3 = 2792.14 \; kJ/kg$$ $$h_{sup} = 2792.14 + 2.01 \cdot 70$$ $$h_{sup} = 2792.14 + 140.7$$ $$\boxed{h_{sup} = 2932.84 \; kJ/kg}$$ Method 2 — Full additive form (safer for partial marks): $$h_{sup} = h_f + h_{fg} + C_p \cdot (T_{sup} - T_s) = 844.84 + 1947.3 + 140.7 = 2932.84 \; kJ/kg$$ **(ii) Total Heat Supplied from Saturated Liquid (3 marks)** Heat supplied = (Final enthalpy) − (Initial enthalpy of saturated liquid) $$q_{total} = h_{sup} - h_f = 2932.84 - 844.84$$ $$\boxed{q_{total} = 2088.00 \; kJ/kg}$$ **Breakdown verification:** - Latent heat: $h_{fg} = 1947.3 \; kJ/kg$ - Superheat: $C_p \cdot \Delta T = 140.7 \; kJ/kg$ - Total: $1947.3 + 140.7 = 2088.0 \; kJ/kg$ ✓ --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3 Alternative] **(a)** Define the following terms with one relevant equation each: **(i)** Wet steam and dryness fraction **(2 marks)** **(ii)** Dry saturated steam **(1 mark)** **(iii)** Superheated steam and degree of superheat **(2 marks)** **(iv)** Latent heat of vaporization and internal energy of evaporation **(2 marks)** **(b)** A steam sample at 7 bar has an enthalpy of 2400 kJ/kg. Using the following data at 7 bar — $T_s = 165.0 \; °C$, $h_f = 697.22 \; kJ/kg$, $h_{fg} = 2066.3 \; kJ/kg$, $v_g = 0.2728 \; m^3/kg$, $s_g = 6.7075 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ — determine: **(i)** The dryness fraction of the steam **(3 marks)** **(ii)** The specific volume of the wet steam, assuming $v_f \approx 0$ **(2 marks)** **(iii)** The specific entropy of the wet steam, given $s_f = 1.9922 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ **(2 marks)** --- #### MODEL SOLUTION **Part (a) — Definitions (7 marks)** **(i) Wet steam & dryness fraction (2 marks):** Wet steam is a two-phase mixture of saturated water and dry saturated vapor at the same temperature and pressure. The dryness fraction is: $$x = \frac{m_g}{m_f + m_g}, \quad 0 \leq x < 1$$ **(ii) Dry saturated steam (1 mark):** Steam containing no liquid water, existing at the saturation temperature for the given pressure, i.e., $x = 1$ and $h = h_g$, $v = v_g$. **(iii) Superheated steam & degree of superheat (2 marks):** Steam heated to a temperature above its saturation temperature at the given pressure. The degree of superheat is: $$\Delta T_{sup} = T_{sup} - T_{sat}$$ **(iv) Latent heat & internal energy of evaporation (2 marks):** - Latent heat of vaporization: $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ — energy required to vaporize 1 kg of saturated liquid at constant P and T. - Internal energy of evaporation: $u_{fg} = h_{fg} - P \cdot v_{fg}$ — portion of latent heat that does internal molecular work. --- **Part (b) — Property Determination (7 marks)** **Given:** P = 7 bar, h = 2400 kJ/kg, $h_f = 697.22$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2066.3$ kJ/kg. **(i) Dryness Fraction (3 marks)** $$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$$ $$2400 = 697.22 + x \cdot (2066.3)$$ $$x \cdot 2066.3 = 2400 - 697.22 = 1702.78$$ $$x = \frac{1702.78}{2066.3}$$ $$\boxed{x = 0.824}$$ **(ii) Specific Volume (2 marks)** Given $v_f \approx 0$: $$v \approx x \cdot v_g = 0.824 \times 0.2728$$ $$\boxed{v = 0.2248 \; m^3/kg}$$ **(iii) Specific Entropy (2 marks)** First, $s_{fg} = s_g - s_f = 6.7075 - 1.9922 = 4.7153 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ $$s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$$ $$s = 1.9922 + (0.824)(4.7153)$$ $$s = 1.9922 + 3.8854$$ $$\boxed{s = 5.8776 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)}$$ --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)** - ✅ **Three states of steam:** Wet (two-phase), Dry Saturated (pure vapor at T_s), Superheated (vapor above T_s). - ✅ **Dryness fraction (x)** is the single most important parameter for wet steam; range $0 \leq x < 1$, with $x = 1$ defining dry saturated. - ✅ **Core formula set (memorize):** - $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ - $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ - $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ - ✅ **For superheated steam:** $h = h_g + C_p \cdot (T_{sup} - T_s)$; never use $h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ for superheated state. - ✅ **Reference for enthalpy:** All enthalpies in steam tables are measured from **0 °C saturated liquid water** as the datum. - ✅ **Latent heat relationship:** $h_{fg} = u_{fg} + P \cdot v_{fg}$. - ✅ **At 100 °C (atmospheric):** $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg — useful for sanity-checking textbook numbers. - ✅ **Critical point:** 22.06 MPa, 374 °C — beyond this, no distinction between liquid and vapor. - ✅ **Always verify the state of steam** (wet/dry/superheated) **before** picking the right formula — this is the #1 valuation pitfall. - ✅ **Specific heat approximation for superheated steam:** $C_p \approx 2.01 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ unless otherwise specified. - ✅ **Wet steam is the only state that uses dryness fraction**; dry saturated and superheated do not have an *x* value (x = 1 for dry sat, undefined for superheat). - ✅ **Industrial rule of thumb:** Superheated steam is preferred for **turbines** (avoids blade erosion); wet steam is used cautiously for **direct process heating** where condensation heat transfer is desired.

topics.title: Boilers: Types of Steam Generators/Boilers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Boilers: Types of Steam Generators/Boilers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Steam Generator (Boiler)** is a closed pressure vessel in which a liquid (most commonly water) is converted into saturated or superheated steam by the application of heat energy from a combustion of fuel (solid, liquid, or gaseous) or from a waste-heat source. The device operates on the **Rankine cycle** principle and is one of the most critical unit operations in any process plant, providing the working fluid for turbines, heating media for heat exchangers, and process steam for chemical reactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> A boiler is a *pressure vessel designed to transfer heat produced by combustion to water until it becomes steam or to a fluid that is circulated for the purpose of transferring heat to other equipment*. The heat can be supplied electrically, via fossil-fuel combustion, or via process waste heat streams.

**Key Operating Metrics (KTU Standard Units):**

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Steam Generation Capacity | $W_s$ | **kg/hr** or **TPD** (Tonnes Per Day) | 100 – 500,000 kg/hr |
| Working Pressure | $P_w$ | **bar (g)** or **MPa** | 10 – 200 bar |
| Steam Temperature | $T_s$ | **°C** | 180 – 540 °C |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th}$ | **%** | 65 – 92 % |
| Heat Release Rate | $HRR$ | **kW/m³** | 300 – 1500 kW/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU examinations, students are frequently expected to define a boiler and classify it based on (i) the **tube contents** (fire-tube vs. water-tube), (ii) the **axis of the shell** (horizontal vs. vertical), (iii) the **fire position** (internally or externally fired), and (iv) the **circulation method** (natural vs. forced).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker with a Furnace"

Imagine a **pressure cooker** sitting on top of a very hot, controlled campfire. The fire's flames are guided through pipes (or around the water), transferring heat. The sealed lid prevents the water from boiling away, so the temperature climbs **above 100 °C**, producing high-pressure steam. A safety valve on top (like the whistle on a kettle) is the only way out for the steam.

- The **water** is your "feed stock."
- The **fire/furnace** is your "heat source."
- The **pipes carrying fire** or **shell holding water** defines the boiler type.
- The **safety valve** is your "control mechanism."

This is exactly how a boiler works — only it's engineered to industrial scale, with precise fuel-air ratios, water treatment, and ASME-standard pressure containment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Steam Generators

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates a clear understanding of the following hierarchical classification. The two most fundamental divisions are **Fire-Tube** and **Water-Tube** boilers.

### A. Classification Based on Tube Contents

**(i) Fire-Tube Boilers (Smoke-Tube or Shell Boilers)**

- Hot gases of combustion pass through the tubes that are surrounded by water.
- The **shell** contains water; the **tubes** carry the fire.
- Operate at **low-to-medium pressures** (≤ 20 bar).
- High thermal storage capacity (slow response).

> [!NOTE]
> **Sub-types under Fire-Tube:**
> 1. **Cochran Boiler** — Vertical, multi-tubular, internal fire-box.
> 2. **Lancashire Boiler** — Horizontal, two internal fire-tubes, stationary.
> 3. **Cornish Boiler** — Horizontal, single internal fire-tube, longer shell.
> 4. **Locomotive Boiler** — Horizontal, multi-tubular, mobile application.
> 5. **Babcock & Wilcox (Fire-Tube Variant)** — Earlier 1867 design.
> 6. **Scotch Marine Boiler** — Compact, marine applications, fire-tubes surrounded by water.

**(ii) Water-Tube Boilers**

- Water circulates inside the tubes; hot gases flow outside the tubes.
- Tubes are exposed to radiant and convective heat.
- Operate at **high pressures** (40 – 250 bar) and **high capacities** (> 100,000 kg/hr).
- Small water inventory → rapid response to load changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sub-types under Water-Tube:**
> 1. **Babcock & Wilcox (B&W) Boiler** — Horizontal, inclined, bent tubes, natural circulation.
> 2. **Stirling Boiler** — Three-drum, bent tubes, improved B&W.
> 3. **La-Mont Boiler** — Forced circulation, single/double drum, external pump.
> 4. **Benson Boiler** — Once-through, no drum, ultra-supercritical.
> 5. **Loeffler Boiler** — Uses part of the steam for circulation, no feed pump at high pressure.
> 6. **Cochran (Water-Tube Variant)** — Vertical, multi-tubular, internal furnace.
> 7. **Velox Boiler** — Forced circulation, supercharged by gas turbine.

### B. Other Classification Criteria (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Classification Basis | Categories | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Axis of Shell** | Horizontal / Vertical | Lancashire (H), Cochran (V) |
| **Fire Position** | Internally Fired / Externally Fired | Lancashire (Internal), Scotch (External) |
| **Circulation Method** | Natural / Forced / Once-through | Lancashire (Natural), La-Mont (Forced), Benson (Once) |
| **Pressure Range** | Low (< 20 bar) / Medium (20–50 bar) / High (50–100 bar) / Supercritical (> 221 bar) | All B&W types |
| **Mobility** | Stationary / Portable / Mobile | Lancashire (Stationary), Locomotive (Mobile) |
| **Heat Source** | Fossil-fuel / Waste-heat / Electric / Nuclear | Most process plants |

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Fire-Tube vs. Water-Tube Design

The **fundamental engineering trade-off** is between **pressure containment** and **heat-transfer surface area per unit volume**.

- **Fire-Tube logic:** A large cylindrical shell, easy to fabricate and roll, can hold water and resist pressure economically. Tubes carrying gas have low pressure, so they can be made of simple iron.
- **Water-Tube logic:** Tubes are small, easily rolled, and can be strengthened to handle high internal pressure. The shell (steam drum) is just a separator, so it does not need to be a giant pressure vessel. You can pack thousands of tubes to achieve massive heat-transfer area in a compact footprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Gold Rule (Memorize This!):**
> - Want **low pressure, simple plant, low capital**? → **Fire-Tube**.
> - Want **high pressure, high capacity, fast response, superheat, reheat**? → **Water-Tube**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **exam-locked**: appearing in nearly every KTU ESE and internal assessment on this module. Master the units, the meaning of each term, and the standard heat values.

| # | Formula | Description | Units (SI) |
| :--: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = m_s \times (h_s - h_{fw})$ | Total heat transferred to steam | kW (or kJ/s) |
| 2 | $W_s = \dfrac{Q}{h_g - h_f}$ | Steam generation rate (from liquid water at saturation) | kg/s |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{m_s}{H_{f,c}}$ | Evaporation Ratio / Factor (steam produced per kg fuel) | kg steam / kg fuel |
| 4 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{m_s (h_s - h_{fw})}{m_f \times GCV} \times 100$ | Thermal Efficiency (from & above GCV) | % |
| 5 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{m_s (h_s - h_{fw})}{m_f \times NCV} \times 100$ | Thermal Efficiency (from & above NCV) | % |
| 6 | $H_{eq} = m_s \times (h_s - h_{fw})$ | Equivalent Evaporation (basis: from & at 100 °C, $h_g = 2257$ kJ/kg) | kg of equiv. steam |
| 7 | $\% = \dfrac{\text{Measured Evap}}{\text{Equiv. Evap}} \times 100$ | Boiler Efficiency Rating | % |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference (heat exchanger) | K or °C |
| 9 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}$ | Overall Heat Transfer Equation | kW |
| 10 | $\text{Fuel Consumption} = \dfrac{m_s (h_s - h_{fw})}{GCV \times \eta_{th}}$ | Hourly fuel requirement | kg/hr |

**Where:**
- $m_s$ = mass flow rate of steam (kg/s)
- $h_s$ = specific enthalpy of steam at operating condition (kJ/kg)
- $h_{fw}$ = specific enthalpy of feedwater (kJ/kg)
- $m_f$ = mass flow rate of fuel (kg/s)
- $GCV$ = Gross Calorific Value (kJ/kg)
- $NCV$ = Net Calorific Value (kJ/kg)
- $H_{f,c}$ = Heat of fuel combustion (kJ/kg)
- $h_g$ = enthalpy of saturated steam at 100 °C = **2257 kJ/kg** (standard datum)
- $h_f$ = enthalpy of saturated water at 100 °C = **419 kJ/kg** (standard datum)
- $U$ = Overall heat transfer coefficient (kW/m²·K)
- $A$ = Heat transfer area (m²)
- $\Delta T_1, \Delta T_2$ = Temperature differences at the two ends of the heat exchanger

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Latent Heat of Vaporization at 100 °C** = $h_{fg} = 2257 - 419 = \mathbf{1838 \text{ kJ/kg}}$. This is the standard "from and at 100 °C" datum referenced in all equivalent-evaporation problems in KTU exams.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Boilers are the **backbone of process industries**:
- **Power Generation:** Thermal power plants use water-tube boilers (B&W, Benson) to feed Rankine-cycle turbines.
- **Chemical Process Industry:** Low-pressure fire-tube boilers provide heating steam for reboilers, jacketed reactors, and distillation columns.
- **Pharmaceuticals & Food:** Clean-steam boilers (often electric) provide sterilizing steam.
- **Pulp & Paper:** Recovery boilers convert black liquor into steam and recover chemicals.
- **Petrochemical:** Waste-heat boilers recover heat from FCC regenerator flue gases.
- **Marine:** Scotch marine boilers dominate due to compactness and robustness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Boiler Thermal Efficiency from First Principles

**Statement:**
A boiler consumes fuel at a mass flow rate $m_f$ (kg/hr) and produces steam at a rate $m_s$ (kg/hr). The enthalpy of the steam leaving the boiler is $h_s$ (kJ/kg) and the enthalpy of the feedwater entering is $h_{fw}$ (kJ/kg). The fuel has a Gross Calorific Value of $GCV$ (kJ/kg). Derive the expression for thermal efficiency.

### Step 1: Energy In — Heat Released by Fuel Combustion

The chemical energy released per unit time by the fuel is:

$$
E_{in} = m_f \times GCV
$$

The units check: $[\text{kg/hr}] \times [\text{kJ/kg}] = [\text{kJ/hr}]$.

### Step 2: Energy Out — Heat Absorbed by Steam

The heat picked up by the water in converting it from feedwater to steam is:

$$
E_{out} = m_s \times (h_s - h_{fw})
$$

Units: $[\text{kg/hr}] \times [\text{kJ/kg}] = [\text{kJ/hr}]$.

### Step 3: Define Thermal Efficiency

By definition, thermal efficiency is the ratio of useful heat output to heat input:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100
$$

### Step 4: Substitute and Simplify

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{m_s \times (h_s - h_{fw})}{m_f \times GCV} \times 100
$$

Units check:
$$
\frac{[\text{kg/hr}] \times [\text{kJ/kg}]}{[\text{kg/hr}] \times [\text{kJ/kg}]} = \text{dimensionless} \Rightarrow \text{Result in } \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The term $(h_s - h_{fw})$ is the **actual heat absorbed per kg of steam**. For saturated steam at 100 °C, this equals 2257 kJ/kg. For superheated steam at higher temperatures/pressures, this value increases significantly, which is why superheat cycles achieve higher plant efficiencies.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Equivalent Evaporation

**Statement:**
The actual operating conditions of a boiler are: steam pressure $P$ bar, steam temperature $T$ °C, feedwater temperature $T_{fw}$ °C, and steam generation $m_s$ kg/hr. Prove that the **Equivalent Evaporation from and at 100 °C** is given by:

$$
m_{eq} = m_s \times \frac{(h_s - h_{fw})}{(h_g - h_f)_{100^\circ C}}
$$

### Step 1: Define Equivalent Evaporation

By IBR (Indian Boiler Regulations) and IS standards, equivalent evaporation is the amount of dry saturated steam that would be produced per hour from feedwater at 100 °C, if the latent heat of vaporization at 100 °C were the only heat source — which is the standard datum $h_{fg} = 2257 - 419 = 1838$ kJ/kg.

### Step 2: Total Heat Output at Actual Conditions

At actual operating pressure and temperature, the total heat delivered to steam per hour is:

$$
Q_{actual} = m_s \times (h_s - h_{fw})
$$

### Step 3: Heat Delivered at Standard (100 °C) Conditions

If this same heat $Q_{actual}$ were used to produce dry saturated steam at 100 °C from water at 100 °C, the mass of such steam would be:

$$
m_{eq} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{(h_g - h_f)_{100^\circ C}}
$$

### Step 4: Combine

$$
m_{eq} = \frac{m_s \times (h_s - h_{fw})}{2257 - 419}
$$

$$
m_{eq} = \frac{m_s \times (h_s - h_{fw})}{1838}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Logic:** When a problem says "a boiler produces 5000 kg/hr of steam at 10 bar from feedwater at 50 °C," you must first look up $h_s$ at 10 bar saturated (≈ 2778 kJ/kg) and $h_{fw}$ at 50 °C (≈ 210 kJ/kg), then compute $m_{eq}$ to compare across boilers of different designs.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution)

**Problem:**
A Lancashire boiler produces **4000 kg/hr** of dry saturated steam at a pressure of **10 bar**. The feedwater enters at **45 °C**. The coal used has a GCV of **30,000 kJ/kg**, and the boiler operates at a thermal efficiency of **70 %**. Calculate:
1. The heat absorbed by the steam per hour.
2. The coal consumption per hour.
3. The equivalent evaporation from and at 100 °C.

**Step 1: Enthalpy Look-up (from steam tables)**
- $h_s$ (dry saturated steam at 10 bar) = **2778.1 kJ/kg**
- $h_{fw}$ (water at 45 °C) = **188.4 kJ/kg**
- $h_g$ at 100 °C = **2257.0 kJ/kg**
- $h_f$ at 100 °C = **419.0 kJ/kg**

**Step 2: Heat absorbed per hour**

$$
Q = m_s \times (h_s - h_{fw})
$$

$$
Q = 4000 \times (2778.1 - 188.4)
$$

$$
Q = 4000 \times 2589.7 = 1.0359 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/hr}
$$

**Step 3: Coal consumption per hour**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{Q}{m_f \times GCV} \times 100
$$

Rearranging:

$$
m_f = \frac{Q}{\eta_{th} \times GCV} = \frac{1.0359 \times 10^7}{0.70 \times 30000}
$$

$$
m_f = \frac{1.0359 \times 10^7}{21000} \approx 493.3 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Step 4: Equivalent Evaporation**

$$
m_{eq} = \frac{m_s \times (h_s - h_{fw})}{2257 - 419} = \frac{4000 \times 2589.7}{1838}
$$

$$
m_{eq} = \frac{1.0359 \times 10^7}{1838} \approx 5636 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** A boiler that actually produces 4000 kg/hr is "equivalent" to one producing 5636 kg/hr under idealized 100 °C conditions. This metric allows direct comparison of boilers of completely different sizes, pressures, and feedwater temperatures.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->



## 4.3 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Working of a Typical Lancashire Boiler (KTU Standard Sketch)

```mermaid
    p1["FEEDWATER INLET"] --> e1["ECONOMISER PREHEAT"]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Tip:** When asked to "sketch" in the KTU exam, draw the shell, label the water level, mark the two internal fire-tubes side by side, and indicate the direction of flue gas flow with arrows. Showing the economiser and superheater (if asked) adds bonus marks.

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Matrix: Fire-Tube vs. Water-Tube

```mermaid

    Q2 --> R2W["WATER TUBE: LARGE OVER 100000 KG PER HR"]

    Q3 --> R3W["WATER TUBE: FAST DUE TO LOW WATER INVENTORY"]
```
