# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part A question carries **3 marks**. Answers should be **precise, 3–4 sentence definitions with at least one numerical or schematic reference**. Avoid vague or descriptive fluff.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a steam generator. List any four applications of steam in a process plant.**

**Model Answer:**
A steam generator (or boiler) is a closed pressure vessel in which water is converted into steam by the application of heat from combustion of fuel or a waste-heat source. It transfers the heat of combustion to water while containing the resulting pressure.
Applications in a process plant include:
1. Process heating in **distillation columns** (reboilers).
2. Driving **steam turbines** for mechanical/electrical power.
3. Heating **jacket reactors** in batch chemical processes.
4. **Cogeneration** in CHP plants for combined heat and power.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a fire-tube and a water-tube boiler on the basis of (i) pressure, (ii) capacity, and (iii) application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Fire-Tube Boiler | Water-Tube Boiler |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pressure** | Low to medium, ≤ 20 bar | High, up to 250 bar (and supercritical) |
| **Capacity** | Small, ≤ 10,000 kg/hr | Large, ≥ 100,000 kg/hr |
| **Application** | Small process plants, hospitals, laundries, marine (Scotch) | Thermal power stations, large chemical, and petrochemical industries |
| **Water Treatment** | Simple | High-purity feedwater mandatory |
| **Response to Load** | Slow (high thermal inertia) | Fast (low water inventory) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as the two model options. Each sub-part is 7 marks, demanding a structured, diagram-supported answer.

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a Lancashire boiler. Discuss its advantages and limitations.**

#### **Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks)**

**Construction:**
- A Lancashire boiler is a **horizontal, stationary, internally fired, multi-tubular fire-tube boiler**.
- It consists of a **cylindrical shell** of large diameter (1.5 to 3 m) and length (6 to 9 m).
- The shell contains water, with the water level typically at half the shell height.
- It has **two internal fire-tubes (or flues)** of large diameter (0.4 to 0.8 m), running longitudinally.
- Each fire-tube is lined internally with firebricks and contains a **grate** at the front.
- A **bridge** (firebrick wall) is built at the rear to deflect hot gases and improve heat transfer.
- A **combustion chamber** (or smoke box) sits at the back, followed by a chimney.
- Mounted on top are **mountings**: safety valve, pressure gauge, water level indicator, steam stop valve, feed check valve, blow-off cock, manhole, and fusible plug.

**Working:**
1. **Coal is fed** onto the grate inside each fire-tube.
2. **Hot flue gases** rise along the tubes, heat the surrounding water by conduction, and are deflected by the bridge.
3. The deflected gases then **travel back along the bottom of the shell** to the front, heating the water by convection.
4. Finally, gases pass through a **smoke box** and escape via the **chimney**.
5. **Feedwater** enters through the check valve, and the level is monitored continuously.
6. **Steam accumulates** in the upper space of the shell and exits via the stop valve when the pressure reaches the design value.

#### **Part (b) — Advantages and Limitations (7 Marks)**

**Advantages:**
1. **Simple construction** and ease of operation; ideal for small industries.
2. **Low capital cost** compared to water-tube boilers.
3. **High steam space** and large water capacity ensure steady steam output.
4. **Clean steam** output (water does not contact combustion products directly).
5. Can use **low-grade coal** effectively.

**Limitations:**
1. **Low pressure** operation (limited to about 16 bar).
2. **Low capacity** — not suitable for large power plants.
3. **Slow response** to fluctuating steam demand.
4. **High risk of explosion** — large water inventory under pressure.
5. **Floor space** requirements are significant.
6. **Long downtime** for tube cleaning and maintenance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

**Explain the construction, working, and salient features of a Babcock and Wilcox (B&W) water-tube boiler. Compare its performance with a modern Benson boiler.**

#### **Part (a) — B&W Boiler Construction and Working (7 Marks)**

**Construction:**
- A B&W boiler is a **horizontal, stationary, externally fired, water-tube boiler with natural circulation**.
- It has a **steam drum** (water-and-steam drum) at the top and a **mud drum** at the bottom, connected by **numerous inclined bent water-tubes**.
- The bent tubes are expanded into the headers and drums, providing a large heat-transfer area.
- Tubes are typically inclined at **15° to 20°** to the horizontal.
- A **water-walled furnace** surrounds the lower portion of the boiler.
- **Superheaters, economisers, and air preheaters** are integrated for efficiency.

**Working:**
1. **Feedwater** enters the economiser (cold side) and is preheated by flue gas.
2. It then enters the **steam drum** through tubes.
3. Water flows **downward through the rear header** into the inclined tubes.
4. In the tubes, water is heated and partially vaporized. The **steam-water mixture rises** due to the density difference — this is **natural circulation**.
5. The mixture enters the **steam drum**, where steam separates from water.
6. The dry steam may pass through a **superheater** for higher temperature.
7. Hot flue gases pass through the **superheater → economiser → air preheater → chimney**, transferring residual heat at each stage.

#### **Part (b) — Comparison with Benson Boiler (7 Marks)**

| Feature | B&W Boiler | Benson Boiler |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Drum** | Present (steam + mud drum) | No drum (once-through) |
| **Circulation** | Natural circulation | Forced / once-through, no circulation loop |
| **Pressure Range** | 40–100 bar | 180–600 bar (supercritical) |
| **Steam Quality** | Lower | Very high (supercritical) |
| **Response Time** | Slower (high inventory) | Fast (low inventory) |
| **Water Treatment** | High purity needed | Ultra-high purity needed (no drum to absorb impurities) |
| **Application** | Conventional thermal power | Ultra-supercritical modern power plants |
| **Maintenance** | Drum cleaning required | Lower inventory → less scale buildup |
| **Capital Cost** | Lower | Higher (high-pressure materials) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Common Mark Deductions):**
> 1. **Confusing fire-tube and water-tube circulation** — students frequently write "water circulates in fire-tube boilers" (false: it's hot gas). Marks lost: 2–3.
> 2. **Forgetting units in efficiency formula** — if you write $\eta = m_s (h_s - h_{fw}) / m_f GCV$ without the percentage, you lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up Lancashire and Cornish** — Cornish has **one** fire-tube; Lancashire has **two**. Examiners check this in the sketch.
> 4. **Missing the "from and at 100 °C" datum** when computing equivalent evaporation — you must explicitly use $h_{fg} = 1838$ kJ/kg.
> 5. **Not labelling the diagram** — always label: water level, fire-tubes, grate, chimney, steam outlet, safety valve. A box without labels loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Omitting mud drum/steam drum distinction in B&W** — this is the single most tested component in water-tube questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision — Read This the Night Before the Exam!**

- **Boiler** = closed pressure vessel that converts water into steam using heat from combustion or waste heat.
- **Two master classes:** Fire-tube (low P, low capacity) and Water-tube (high P, high capacity).
- **Lancashire** = horizontal, 2 fire-tubes, internal furnace, low pressure.
- **Cochran** = vertical, multi-tubular, internal furnace, compact.
- **Babcock & Wilcox** = water-tube, 2 drums, bent tubes, natural circulation, medium-high pressure.
- **Benson** = water-tube, no drum, once-through, supercritical, ultra-high pressure.
- **La-Mont** = water-tube, forced circulation, external pump.
- **Equivalent Evaporation Datum** = $h_{fg} = 1838$ kJ/kg (from and at 100 °C).
- **Thermal Efficiency** $\eta_{th} = m_s (h_s - h_{fw}) / (m_f \cdot GCV) \times 100$.
- **Fire-tube advantage:** simple, low cost, clean steam.
- **Water-tube advantage:** high pressure, high capacity, fast response, superheat-ready.
- **Fire-tube disadvantage:** explosion risk, low pressure, slow response.
- **Water-tube disadvantage:** complex, expensive, requires high-purity feedwater.
- **IBR standard** = Indian Boiler Regulations — important for KTU Indian-context problems.
- **Steam tables** are always required: know how to look up $h_s$ at given pressure and $h_{fw}$ at given temperature.
- **Don't forget** the economiser, superheater, and air preheater when describing a modern boiler — they are integral to the system.
- **Key intuition:** Fire-tube → water surrounds fire; Water-tube → water travels through fire (i.e., the tubes). The phrase "tube contents" is your key to classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combustion in Boilers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Combustion in Boilers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combustion** is a rapid, exothermic chemical reaction between a **fuel** (hydrocarbonaceous material) and an **oxidizer** (usually atmospheric oxygen, O₂), accompanied by the evolution of **heat**, **light (flame)**, and the production of **combustion products** (CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, N₂, O₂, etc.). In the context of **boilers**, combustion refers to the controlled oxidation of fuel inside a **furnace / fire-box** to liberate the chemical energy bound in the fuel, which is then transferred to water/steam via the heating surfaces.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Process Utilities parlance, combustion in boilers is treated as a **steady-flow chemical reaction** governed by:

- **Stoichiometry** (mass balance of C, H, S, O, N atoms),
- **Thermochemistry** (enthalpy of reaction → calorific value),
- **Combustion kinetics** (ignition, flame stability, residence time).

> [!NOTE]
> The three Ts of combustion that every KTU paper expects a student to recall:
> 1. **Temperature** — must exceed the *ignition temperature* of the fuel.
> 2. **Turbulence** — proper mixing of fuel and air.
> 3. **Time** — adequate *residence time* inside the furnace.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Controlled Bonfire"

Imagine lighting a damp log on an open field (an **uncontrolled** bonfire). You get:
- Lots of **smoke** (unburnt C and CO — *incomplete combustion*),
- Low useful heat because wind carries flames away (poor *turbulence*),
- A smell that disappears as soon as the wind changes (no *time control*).

Now imagine the **same log burned inside a closed cylindrical drum** with:
- Pre-heated air blown from below at a measured rate (*controlled excess air*),
- A tall chimney providing natural draft (*controlled residence time*),
- Bricks surrounding the drum retaining heat (*temperature maintained above ignition point*).

That drum is, in essence, a **primitive fire-tube boiler furnace**. The transition from "bonfire" to "boiler furnace" is the essence of **combustion engineering** — turning a *wild exothermic event* into a *measurable, efficient, and safe heat source*.

## 1.3 Fuel Classification (Syllabus Snapshot)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 expects familiarity with the three fuel classes used in industrial boilers.

| Class | Examples | Storage | Typical GCV (kcal/kg or kcal/Nm³) |
|-------|----------|---------|-----------------------------------|
| **Solid** | Coal, lignite, bagasse, biomass | Heap / bunker | 3 000 – 8 000 kcal/kg |
| **Liquid** | Furnace oil (FO), LDO, HSD, naphtha | Storage tanks | 9 500 – 10 500 kcal/kg |
| **Gaseous** | Natural gas (NG), LPG, biogas, coke-oven gas | Cylinders / bullets | 8 000 – 12 000 kcal/Nm³ |

## 1.4 Key Combustion Reactions (Foundation)

For a generic fuel containing C, H₂, S and ash, the principal oxidation reactions are:

$$
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -94.05 \text{ kcal/mol} \; (=-393.5 \text{ kJ/mol})
$$

$$
\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \quad \Delta H = -68.32 \text{ kcal/mol}
$$

$$
\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -70.96 \text{ kcal/mol}
$$

The negative sign convention indicates **heat release (exothermic)**. The magnitude of $\Delta H$ per mole of fuel atom defines the **calorific value** — the cornerstone quantity of every boiler problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stoichiometric Air Requirement (Theoretical Air)

**Stoichiometric (theoretical) air** is the *minimum* quantity of air required for **complete combustion** — i.e., to convert all C → CO₂, all H → H₂O, and all S → SO₂, with **zero free O₂** remaining in the flue gas.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-step Logic for a Solid Fuel

Consider **1 kg of fuel** with ultimate analysis by mass: **C, H, O, S, N, ash** (in fractional form, summing to 1).

**Step 1 — Oxygen demanded by each combustible element:**

- Carbon: $\;\dfrac{\text{C}}{12}\; \text{kmol of C} \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{\text{C}}{12}\; \text{kmol O}_2$
- Hydrogen: $\;\dfrac{\text{H}}{2}\; \text{kmol of H}_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{\text{H}}{4}\; \text{kmol O}_2$
- Sulphur: $\;\dfrac{\text{S}}{32}\; \text{kmol of S} \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{\text{S}}{32}\; \text{kmol O}_2$

**Step 2 — Subtract the oxygen already present in the fuel:**

$$
\text{O}_2^{(\text{net required})} \;=\; \left[\dfrac{\text{C}}{12} + \dfrac{\text{H}}{4} + \dfrac{\text{S}}{32}\right] \;-\; \dfrac{\text{O}}{32} \quad \big[\text{kmol O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}\big]
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass of air using the standard molar mass of air = 28.97 kg/kmol and O₂ fraction in air ≈ 0.21 (or more precisely 0.231):**

$$
\boxed{\;A_0 \;=\; \dfrac{28.97}{0.21} \times \left[\dfrac{\text{C}}{12} + \dfrac{\text{H}}{4} + \dfrac{\text{S}}{32} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{32}\right] \quad \text{kg air / kg fuel}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **gaseous fuels**, the same logic applies in **Nm³ of air per Nm³ of fuel** rather than per kg. Each combustible component (CH₄, C₂H₆, H₂, CO) carries its own stoichiometric oxygen demand.

## 2.2 Excess Air (EA) and Equivalence Ratio

Real boilers cannot achieve perfect mixing → a *practical excess* of air is supplied to ensure all fuel particles find oxygen before they leave the high-temperature zone.

$$
\%\,\text{Excess Air (EA)} \;=\; \dfrac{A_{\text{actual}} - A_0}{A_0} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Equivalence Ratio } (\phi) \;=\; \dfrac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}
$$

- $\phi < 1$ → **lean mixture** (excess air, oxidizing flame — preferred in boilers for safety).
- $\phi > 1$ → **rich mixture** (deficient air — risk of CO, soot, explosion).

> [!TIP]
> Typical excess-air levels used in industry: **pulverised coal 15–25 %**, **stoker-fired 30–50 %**, **oil-fired 10–20 %**, **gas-fired 5–10 %**.

## 2.3 Calorific Values (GCV vs NCV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite KTU question.** Be precise with the terms.

- **GCV** = Gross (or Higher) Calorific Value = **HHV** — heat released assuming all H₂O product condenses to liquid (latent heat recovered).
- **NCV** = Net (or Lower) Calorific Value = **LHV** — heat released when H₂O exits as **vapour** (typical boiler stack condition).

### 2.3.1 Dulong's Formula (Solid & Liquid Fuels)

For a fuel of ultimate analysis C, H, O, S, N (mass %, dry basis):

$$
\boxed{\;\text{GCV} \;=\; 8080\,\text{C} \;+\; 34500\!\left(\text{H} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{8}\right) \;+\; 2240\,\text{S} \quad [\text{kcal/kg}]\;}
$$

and the latent-heat correction at STP (water of condensation = 9 × H₂ formed = 9 × H × 9 ≈ 587 kcal per kg of H):

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NCV} \;=\; \text{GCV} \;-\; 587 \cdot \dfrac{9\,\text{H}}{100} \;\approx\; \text{GCV} - 52.83\,\text{H} \quad [\text{kcal/kg}]\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> When the fuel is **wetted** (contains moisture M%), further deduct $\;0.09\,M\,(100 - T_{\text{flue}})/100$. For KTU-level problems, the simplified form **NCV = GCV − 587 × H₂O formed** is sufficient.

## 2.4 Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) and Combustion Efficiency

$$
\text{AFR} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Mass of air supplied}}{\text{Mass of fuel fired}} \;=\; A_{\text{actual}}
$$

**Combustion efficiency** (Dulong-style heat-loss method):

$$
\eta_{\text{comb}} \;=\; 100 \;-\; \big[\,\%\,\text{heat lost in dry flue gas} \;+\; \%\,\text{heat lost by H}_2\text{O} \;+\; \%\,\text{heat lost by CO, soot, radiation}\,\big]
$$

The dry-flue-gas loss (Siegert's formula) is the dominant term:

$$
\boxed{\;L_{\text{DFG}} \;=\; 0.65\,\big(T_{\text{fg}} - T_{\text{air}}\big)\,\dfrac{\%\,\text{CO}_2}{100} \quad [\%]\;}
$$

with $T_{\text{fg}}$ the flue-gas temperature (°C) and $T_{\text{air}}$ the combustion-air temperature (°C).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master this table — at least **one numerical in every KTU ESE** requires one of these formulas.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Theoretical O₂ per kg fuel | $\dfrac{\text{C}}{12} + \dfrac{\text{H}}{4} + \dfrac{\text{S}}{32} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{32}$ | kmol O₂/kg | For solid fuels |
| 2 | Theoretical air A₀ | $\dfrac{28.97}{0.21}\!\left[\dfrac{\text{C}}{12} + \dfrac{\text{H}}{4} + \dfrac{\text{S}}{32} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{32}\right]$ | kg air/kg fuel | Molar mass air = 28.97 |
| 3 | % Excess Air | $\dfrac{A_{\text{act}} - A_0}{A_0} \times 100$ | % | Operating variable |
| 4 | GCV (Dulong) | $8080\,\text{C} + 34500(\text{H} - \text{O}/8) + 2240\,\text{S}$ | kcal/kg | Mass-percent basis |
| 5 | NCV | $\text{GCV} - 52.83\,\text{H}$ | kcal/kg | At constant pressure, water as vapour |
| 6 | AFR (actual) | $(1 + \text{EA}/100)\,A_0$ | kg/kg | Practical operating value |
| 7 | Dry flue-gas loss | $0.65\,(T_{\text{fg}} - T_{\text{air}})\,\dfrac{\%\,\text{CO}_2}{100}$ | % | Siegert approximation |
| 8 | Stoichiometric O₂ (gases) | $2\,\text{CH}_4 + 3.5\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 + 0.5\,\text{H}_2 + 0.5\,\text{CO} + \ldots$ | Nm³ O₂/Nm³ fuel | For gas analysis in vol % |
| 9 | Theoretical air (gases) | $\dfrac{100}{21}\times \text{O}_2 \text{ required (Nm³)}$ | Nm³ air/Nm³ fuel | Since air is 21 % O₂ by vol |
| 10 | Combustion efficiency | $100 - L_{\text{DFG}} - L_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} - L_{\text{CO,rad}}$ | % | Energy balance |

> [!NOTE]
> For gas fuels, the volume-basis stoichiometric oxygen is obtained by summing each component's individual demand: **CH₄ → 2 O₂, C₂H₆ → 3.5 O₂, H₂ → 0.5 O₂, CO → 0.5 O₂**, then air = (100/21) × O₂ required.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boiler designers** use A₀ to size FD/ID fans and pre-heater ducts.
- **Plant operators** use the Orsat-CO₂ reading + stack temperature to compute $L_{\text{DFG}}$ *online* and trim excess air for optimum efficiency (every 1 % excess-air reduction ≈ **0.5 % fuel saving** in oil-fired boilers).
- **Energy auditors** rely on the Dulong GCV formula when an online calorimeter is unavailable — typical accuracy ± 2 %.
- **Emission engineers** use excess-air trends to control NOₓ formation (lower EA → higher flame temperature → more thermal NOₓ — a deliberate trade-off).

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Dulong's Formula from First Principles

Dulong's formula is **not empirical** — it is the *sum of the heats of combustion of C, H and S in the fuel*, corrected for the O already combined as O₂.

### Step 1 — Heat of combustion of each element per kg

- Carbon: $8080$ kcal per kg C.
- Hydrogen: $34\,500$ kcal per kg H₂ (HHV, water as liquid).
- Sulphur: $2240$ kcal per kg S.

### Step 2 — Oxygen already in the fuel "steals" hydrogen equivalent

H₂ that is already oxidized to H₂O inside the fuel cannot release heat. The fraction $\text{O}/8$ (by mass) represents H₂ that is "pre-combusted". Thus, **net effective hydrogen** is:

$$
\text{H}_{\text{effective}} \;=\; \text{H} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{8} \quad [\text{kg H per kg fuel}]
$$

### Step 3 — Add the contributions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= 8080\,\text{C} \;+\; 34\,500\!\left(\text{H} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{8}\right) \;+\; 2240\,\text{S} \\
\text{GCV} &= 8080\,\text{C} \;+\; 34\,500\,\text{H} \;-\; 4312.5\,\text{O} \;+\; 2240\,\text{S} \quad \big[\text{kcal/kg}\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the classical form.

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks use $34500$ or $34200$ for H₂ depending on whether the reference temperature is 18 °C or 25 °C. KTU accepts both with appropriate assumption statement.

### Step 4 — Convert GCV to NCV

Every kg of H in the fuel produces **9 kg of water**. To evaporate that water at 25 °C (latent heat ≈ 587 kcal/kg) costs:

$$
\text{Latent heat lost} \;=\; 9 \times 587 \times \dfrac{\text{H}}{100} \;=\; 52.83\,\text{H} \quad [\text{kcal/kg fuel}]
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NCV} \;=\; 8080\,\text{C} \;+\; 34\,500\!\left(\text{H} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{8}\right) \;+\; 2240\,\text{S} \;-\; 52.83\,\text{H}\;}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Air for a Solid Fuel

Let **1 kg of fuel** contain C, H, O, S, N, A kg of each (A = ash, total = 1).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{kmol of C} &= \dfrac{\text{C}}{12} \\
\text{kmol of H}_2 &= \dfrac{\text{H}}{2} \\
\text{kmol of S} &= \dfrac{\text{S}}{32}
\end{aligned}
$$

Stoichiometric O₂ demanded:

$$
n_{\text{O}_2} \;=\; \underbrace{\dfrac{\text{C}}{12}}_{\text{for C→CO}_2} \;+\; \underbrace{\dfrac{\text{H}}{4}}_{\text{for H}_2\!\to\!\text{H}_2\text{O}} \;+\; \underbrace{\dfrac{\text{S}}{32}}_{\text{for S→SO}_2} \quad [\text{kmol}]
$$

Subtract O₂ already inside the fuel (one O atom per O atom in fuel, so $\text{O}/32$ kmol of O₂):

$$
n_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{net}} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{C}}{12} \;+\; \dfrac{\text{H}}{4} \;+\; \dfrac{\text{S}}{32} \;-\; \dfrac{\text{O}}{32}
$$

Mass of air required:

$$
\boxed{\;A_0 \;=\; 28.97 \times \dfrac{n_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{net}}}{0.21} \;=\; \dfrac{28.97}{0.21}\!\left[\dfrac{\text{C}}{12} + \dfrac{\text{H}}{4} + \dfrac{\text{S}}{32} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{32}\right]\quad \text{kg/kg}\;}
$$

Equivalent numerical shortcut using mass-basis factors (kg O₂ per kg element):

$$
\boxed{\;A_0 \;=\; 11.6\,\text{C} \;+\; 34.8\!\left(\text{H} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{8}\right) \;+\; 4.35\,\text{S} \quad [\text{kg air/kg fuel}]\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This **numerical form** is the one you should use inside a 14-mark numerical — it saves a page of kmol calculations.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

> **Problem.** A coal has the following ultimate analysis (mass %, dry basis):
> C = 72 %, H = 5.2 %, O = 9.0 %, S = 1.3 %, N = 1.5 %, Ash = 11.0 %.
> The boiler is fired with **30 % excess air**. Calculate:
> 1. **GCV and NCV** of the coal (kcal/kg).
> 2. **Theoretical air** $A_0$ and **actual air–fuel ratio**.
> 3. **Orsat analysis** (% CO₂, % O₂ in dry flue gas).
> 4. **Mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel**.

### Part (a) — Calorific Values (Dulong)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= 8080(0.72) \;+\; 34\,500\!\left(0.052 - \dfrac{0.090}{8}\right) \;+\; 2240(0.013) \\
&= 5817.6 \;+\; 34\,500(0.052 - 0.01125) \;+\; 29.12 \\
&= 5817.6 \;+\; 34\,500(0.04075) \;+\; 29.12 \\
&= 5817.6 \;+\; 1405.875 \;+\; 29.12 \\
&= 7252.595 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rounding:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{GCV} \;\approx\; 7253 \text{ kcal/kg}\;}
$$

Now NCV:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV} &= \text{GCV} - 52.83 \times \text{H} \\
&= 7252.595 - 52.83(5.2) \\
&= 7252.595 - 274.716 \\
&= 6977.879 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NCV} \;\approx\; 6978 \text{ kcal/kg}\;}
$$

### Part (b) — Theoretical Air $A_0$ (numerical form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= 11.6\,\text{C} \;+\; 34.8\!\left(\text{H} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{8}\right) \;+\; 4.35\,\text{S} \\
&= 11.6(0.72) \;+\; 34.8\!\left(0.052 - \dfrac{0.090}{8}\right) \;+\; 4.35(0.013) \\
&= 8.352 \;+\; 34.8(0.04075) \;+\; 0.05655 \\
&= 8.352 \;+\; 1.41810 \;+\; 0.05655 \\
&= 9.82665 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;A_0 \;\approx\; 9.83 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}\;}
$$

Actual air–fuel ratio with 30 % excess:

$$
A_{\text{act}} \;=\; 1.30 \times 9.82665 \;=\; 12.775 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{AFR} \;\approx\; 12.78 \text{ kg/kg}\;}
$$

### Part (c) — Orsat Analysis of Dry Flue Gas

The dry flue gas composition (by volume, ignoring water vapour) per kg fuel:

| Component | Source | Moles (kmol) |
|-----------|--------|--------------|
| CO₂ | from C | $\dfrac{\text{C}}{12} = 0.06$ |
| SO₂ | from S | $\dfrac{\text{S}}{32} = 0.000406$ |
| O₂ | excess | $0.21 \times A_{\text{act}}/28.97 - \text{O}_2$ consumed |
| N₂ | from air | $0.79 \times A_{\text{act}}/28.97$ |

O₂ supplied in actual air:

$$
\text{O}_2^{(\text{supplied})} \;=\; \dfrac{0.21 \times 12.775}{28.97} \;=\; \dfrac{2.6828}{28.97} \;=\; 0.09260 \text{ kmol}
$$

O₂ consumed (stoichiometric for complete combustion):

$$
\text{O}_2^{(\text{consumed})} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{C}}{12} + \dfrac{\text{H}}{4} + \dfrac{\text{S}}{32} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{32}
$$

Using the analysis values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_2^{(\text{consumed})} &= \dfrac{0.72}{12} \;+\; \dfrac{0.052}{4} \;+\; \dfrac{0.013}{32} \;-\; \dfrac{0.090}{32} \\
&= 0.0600 \;+\; 0.0130 \;+\; 0.000406 \;-\; 0.002813 \\
&= 0.0706 \text{ kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Excess O₂ in flue gas:

$$
\text{O}_2^{(\text{excess})} \;=\; 0.09260 - 0.0706 \;=\; 0.0220 \text{ kmol}
$$

N₂ in flue gas:

$$
\text{N}_2 \;=\; \dfrac{0.79 \times 12.775}{28.97} \;=\; \dfrac{10.0923}{28.97} \;=\; 0.3484 \text{ kmol}
$$

Total dry flue gas moles:

$$
n_{\text{total}} \;=\; 0.0600 + 0.000406 + 0.0220 + 0.3484 \;=\; 0.4308 \text{ kmol}
$$

Volume percentages:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{CO}_2 &= \dfrac{0.0600}{0.4308}\times 100 = 13.93 \% \\
\%\,\text{SO}_2 &= \dfrac{0.000406}{0.4308}\times 100 \approx 0.09 \% \\
\%\,\text{O}_2 &= \dfrac{0.0220}{0.4308}\times 100 = 5.11 \% \\
\%\,\text{N}_2 &= \dfrac{0.3484}{0.4308}\times 100 = 80.87 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Orsat (dry)} \;\approx\; \text{CO}_2 \; 13.93\%, \; \text{O}_2 \; 5.11\%, \; \text{N}_2 \; 80.87\%, \; \text{SO}_2 \; 0.09\%\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical Orsat burette **does not detect SO₂ separately** — it absorbs along with CO₂ as "acidic gases". In real plant practice SO₂ is lumped with CO₂. KTU numericals often **neglect SO₂** for simplicity; we have included it here for completeness.

### Part (d) — Mass of Dry Flue Gas per kg Fuel

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{DFG}} &= 44(0.0600) \;+\; 64(0.000406) \;+\; 32(0.0220) \;+\; 28(0.3484) \\
&= 2.640 \;+\; 0.026 \;+\; 0.704 \;+\; 9.755 \\
&= 13.125 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;m_{\text{DFG}} \;\approx\; 13.13 \text{ kg dry flue gas / kg fuel}\;}
$$

This value is critical for computing the **dry flue-gas heat loss** in the boiler efficiency formula.

## 3.4 Validation Checks

1. **Carbon balance:** C-in = 0.72 kg → C-out as CO₂ = 0.72 × (12/44) × (CO₂ kmol × 44) = 0.72 kg ✓.
2. **Hydrogen balance:** H-in = 0.052 kg → H₂O formed = 0.052 × 9 = 0.468 kg (not in dry gas — confirms "dry" basis).
3. **N₂ balance:** N₂ from air (≈ 0.79 × 12.775) = 10.09 kg → all appears in dry gas ✓.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Combustion Architecture in a Boiler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Storage Bunker or Tank] --> B[Fuel Preparation Mill or Atomizer]
    B --> C[Furnace Fire-Box]
    D[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> E[Air Pre-Heater FD Fan]
    E --> C
    C -->|Combustion Reaction Exothermic| F[Hot Flue Gas]
    F --> G[Boiler Bank Water-Tube Heating Surface]
    G --> H[Superheater and Economizer]
    H --> I[Air Pre-Heater Secondary]
    I --> J[ID Fan Draft]
    J --> K[Electrostatic Precipitator ESP]
    K --> L[Stack Chimney]
    L --> M[Atmosphere]
    C -->|Water Inlet| N[Feedwater Pump]
    N --> G
    G --> O[Steam Outlet to Turbine or Process]

    style C fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style F fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style O fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **furnace (C)** is the heart — the only block where chemistry happens.
- **Air path** is pressurised by the **FD (forced-draft) fan** through the air pre-heater before meeting the fuel.
- **Flue-gas path** crosses **water/steam surfaces** in counter-flow (G, H, I) — heat transfer, not chemistry.
- The **ID (induced-draft) fan (J)** maintains negative furnace pressure, preventing flame puff-out.
- **ESP (K)** is the emission-control device; not part of combustion chemistry but inseparable from real installations.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define (i) Gross Calorific Value (GCV) and (ii) Net Calorific Value (NCV) of a fuel. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**
- **GCV (HHV):** Total heat liberated by **complete combustion of unit mass (or volume)** of fuel, with the products of combustion cooled back to the original fuel temperature **and the water vapour condensed to liquid**.
- **NCV (LHV):** Same as GCV **except the water vapour is NOT condensed** — it leaves as vapour carrying away latent heat.
- **Relation:** $\;\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9 \times \dfrac{\text{H}}{100} \times h_{\text{fg}}\;\approx\;\text{GCV} - 52.83\,\text{H}\;$ (kcal/kg).
- **Boiler relevance:** NCV is the correct basis for efficiency calculations because flue gases exit the stack at temperatures well above 100 °C (water remains vapour).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is meant by "excess air" in boiler combustion? Why is it necessary and what is its typical range?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Excess air** = actual air supplied *beyond* the stoichiometric minimum, expressed as a **percentage** of the theoretical air.
- **Necessity:** Real combustion is **kinetically limited** — fuel particles need *time* and *turbulence* to find O₂. Without excess air, pockets of unburnt C and CO escape (smoke, soot, heat loss, CO pollution).
- **Typical values:**
  - **Pulverised coal:** 15 – 25 %
  - **Stoker coal:** 30 – 50 %
  - **Oil-fired:** 10 – 20 %
  - **Gas-fired (NG/LPG):** 5 – 10 %
- **Trade-off:** Too little EA → incomplete combustion + smoke; Too much EA → high stack heat loss + lower efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`

A coal sample has the following **ultimate analysis** (mass %, dry basis):

**C = 70 %, H = 5.0 %, O = 8.0 %, S = 1.5 %, N = 1.5 %, Ash = 14.0 %**

The boiler is operated with **25 % excess air**. Calculate:

**(a)** [7 marks] — **Theoretical air requirement per kg of fuel** and **actual air-fuel ratio**.

**(b)** [7 marks] — **Orsat analysis** (% CO₂, O₂, N₂ on dry basis) and the **mass of dry flue gas per kg fuel**.

---

#### Solution (a) — Theoretical and Actual Air

**Given (mass basis, per kg fuel):**
- C = 0.70, H = 0.05, O = 0.08, S = 0.015, N = 0.015, Ash = 0.14

**Stoichiometric O₂ per kg fuel** (numerical shortcut):

$$
\text{O}_2^{(\text{min})} = 11.6\,\text{C} \;+\; 34.8\!\left(\text{H} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{8}\right) \;+\; 4.35\,\text{S}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_2^{(\text{min})} &= 11.6(0.70) \;+\; 34.8\!\left(0.05 - \dfrac{0.08}{8}\right) \;+\; 4.35(0.015) \\
&= 8.12 \;+\; 34.8(0.05 - 0.010) \;+\; 0.0653 \\
&= 8.12 \;+\; 34.8(0.040) \;+\; 0.0653 \\
&= 8.12 \;+\; 1.392 \;+\; 0.0653 \\
&= 9.5773 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to **theoretical air** (1 kg O₂ comes with $\frac{0.79}{0.21} = 3.762$ kg N₂, total 4.762 kg air):

$$
A_0 \;=\; \dfrac{9.5773}{0.21} \;\approx\; 45.61 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative method** using the kmol form (preferred by examiners to demonstrate understanding):
> $\;n_{\text{O}_2} = \dfrac{0.70}{12} + \dfrac{0.05}{4} + \dfrac{0.015}{32} - \dfrac{0.08}{32} = 0.05833 + 0.0125 + 0.000469 - 0.0025 = 0.06880\;$ kmol.
> $\;A_0 = 0.06880 \times \dfrac{28.97}{0.21} = 0.06880 \times 137.95 = 9.491\;$ kg/kg. (Small difference due to N₂ mass fraction assumed 0.79 vs the precise 0.77.)

**Actual air-fuel ratio:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{AFR} \;=\; 1.25 \times A_0 \;\approx\; 57.01 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}\;}
$$

---

#### Solution (b) — Orsat Analysis & Dry Flue Gas Mass

**Step 1 — Flue gas constituents (kmol per kg fuel):**

- $\text{CO}_2$ from C: $\;n_{\text{CO}_2} = \dfrac{0.70}{12} = 0.05833\;$ kmol
- $\text{SO}_2$ from S: $\;n_{\text{SO}_2} = \dfrac{0.015}{32} = 0.000469\;$ kmol
- $\text{O}_2$ supplied (actual): $\;\dfrac{0.21 \times 57.01}{28.97} = \dfrac{11.972}{28.97} = 0.4133\;$ kmol O₂
- $\text{O}_2$ consumed (stoichiometric): $\;n_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{cons}} = \dfrac{\text{C}}{12} + \dfrac{\text{H}}{4} + \dfrac{\text{S}}{32} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{32}$

$$
n_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{cons}} = 0.05833 + 0.0125 + 0.000469 - 0.0025 = 0.06880 \text{ kmol}
$$

- $\text{O}_2$ excess in flue gas: $\;0.4133 - 0.06880 = 0.3445\;$ kmol

- $\text{N}_2$ from air: $\;\dfrac{0.79 \times 57.01}{28.97} = \dfrac{45.04}{28.97} = 1.555\;$ kmol

**Step 2 — Total dry flue gas:**

$$
n_{\text{DFG}} = 0.05833 + 0.000469 + 0.3445 + 1.555 = 1.958 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Step 3 — Volume percentages (Orsat):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{CO}_2 &= \dfrac{0.05833}{1.958} \times 100 = 2.98 \% \\
\%\,\text{SO}_2 &= \dfrac{0.000469}{1.958} \times 100 = 0.024 \% \;\;(\text{often absorbed with CO}_2)\\
\%\,\text{O}_2 &= \dfrac{0.3445}{1.958} \times 100 = 17.59 \% \\
\%\,\text{N}_2 &= \dfrac{1.555}{1.958} \times 100 = 79.41 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Orsat (dry)} \;\approx\; \text{CO}_2 \; 2.98\%, \; \text{O}_2 \; 17.59\%, \; \text{N}_2 \; 79.41\%, \; \text{SO}_2 \; \text{trace}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of dry flue gas (kg per kg fuel):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{DFG}} &= 44 \times 0.05833 \;+\; 64 \times 0.000469 \;+\; 32 \times 0.3445 \;+\; 28 \times 1.555 \\
&= 2.5665 \;+\; 0.0300 \;+\; 11.024 \;+\; 43.540 \\
&= 57.16 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;m_{\text{DFG}} \;\approx\; 57.16 \text{ kg / kg fuel}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall / Mark-Loss Warning:**
> 1. **Do NOT forget to subtract O₂ already present in the fuel** in the O₂-demand term. Many students write $\text{O}_2 = \text{C}/12 + \text{H}/4 + \text{S}/32$ and **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Orsat analysis must be on a DRY basis** — water vapour must be removed from the percentages. Showing %H₂O is **wrong** in an Orsat answer.
> 3. The question asked for **mass** of dry flue gas, not volume — students often quote Nm³ and lose full 7 marks of part (b).
> 4. **Unit consistency** — always write kmol OR kg OR Nm³, never mix them in the same equation.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** [7 marks] — With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a fluidised-bed combustion (FBC) boiler**. List its **advantages over a conventional grate-fired boiler**.

**(b)** [7 marks] — A furnace oil has the following composition by mass: **C = 86 %, H = 12 %, S = 1.5 %, O = 0.5 %**. Calculate **(i) GCV by Dulong's formula**, **(ii) NCV**, and **(iii) the stoichiometric air required per kg of oil**.

---

#### Model Solution (a) — FBC Boiler

**Construction:**

```
       ┌──────────────────────────┐
       │      FURNACE FREEBOARD   │ ← Secondary air inlets
       │  ┌────────────────────┐  │
       │  │   BUBBLING BED     │  │
       │  │   (Sand + Fuel)    │  │ ← Inert bed material (silica sand)
       │  │   Fluidised by     │  │   particle size 0.5–1.5 mm
       │  │   primary air      │  │
       │  └─────┬───────┬──────┘  │
       │        │       │         │
       │     Air plenum   Bed      │
       │        │       cooler     │
       └────────┴───────┴──────────┘
                ↓
        Fly-ash cyclone → ESP → Stack
```

**Working principle:**

1. Crushed coal (6–10 mm) is fed onto a **bed of inert sand** resting on a perforated plate (distributor).
2. **Primary air** is forced upward through the distributor at a velocity (≈ 1–3 m/s) that suspends (fluidises) the sand-coal mixture — the bed behaves like a boiling liquid.
3. Coal particles disperse uniformly and burn at the **bed temperature of 850–950 °C** — well below the ash-fusion point (≈ 1200 °C).
4. **Secondary air** injected above the bed (freeboard) burns volatiles & CO released from the bed → ensures complete combustion.
5. Hot flue gases pass over the in-bed evaporator tubes (immersed coils) and then to the boiler bank.
6. Bed temperature is controlled by **in-bed heat-exchange tubes** + ash discharge.

**Advantages over grate-fired boilers:**

1. **Higher combustion efficiency** (98–99 %) due to intimate fuel-air mixing.
2. **Lower excess air** (20–30 %) → higher boiler efficiency.
3. **Lower flame temperature** (850–950 °C) → **reduced NOₓ formation** by ~70 %.
4. **Ability to burn low-grade, high-ash, high-moisture fuels** (lignite, rice husk, washery rejects) efficiently.
5. **Compact furnace** — high heat-release rate (≈ 1 MW/m² of bed).
6. **Sulphur capture in-bed** by adding limestone (CaCO₃ → CaSO₄), reducing SO₂ emission.
7. **Flexibility in load following** — quick response to steam demand swings.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Furnace Oil Combustion Calculations

**Given (per kg oil):** C = 0.86, H = 0.12, S = 0.015, O = 0.005.

**(i) GCV by Dulong's Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= 8080\,\text{C} \;+\; 34\,500\!\left(\text{H} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{8}\right) \;+\; 2240\,\text{S} \\
&= 8080(0.86) \;+\; 34\,500\!\left(0.12 - \dfrac{0.005}{8}\right) \;+\; 2240(0.015) \\
&= 6948.8 \;+\; 34\,500(0.12 - 0.000625) \;+\; 33.60 \\
&= 6948.8 \;+\; 34\,500(0.119375) \;+\; 33.60 \\
&= 6948.8 \;+\; 4118.4375 \;+\; 33.60 \\
&= 11\,100.84 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{GCV} \;\approx\; 11\,101 \text{ kcal/kg}\;}
$$

**(ii) NCV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV} &= \text{GCV} - 52.83\,\text{H} \\
&= 11\,100.84 - 52.83(12) \\
&= 11\,100.84 - 633.96 \\
&= 10\,466.88 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NCV} \;\approx\; 10\,467 \text{ kcal/kg}\;}
$$

**(iii) Stoichiometric Air:**

Using the numerical shortcut:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= 11.6\,\text{C} \;+\; 34.8\!\left(\text{H} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{8}\right) \;+\; 4.35\,\text{S} \\
&= 11.6(0.86) \;+\; 34.8(0.12 - 0.000625) \;+\; 4.35(0.015) \\
&= 9.976 \;+\; 34.8(0.119375) \;+\; 0.06525 \\
&= 9.976 \;+\; 4.1543 \;+\; 0.06525 \\
&= 14.195 \text{ kg air/kg oil}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;A_0 \;\approx\; 14.20 \text{ kg air/kg oil}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall / Mark-Loss Warning:**
> 1. **Mass-basis (kg/kg)** vs **volume-basis (Nm³/Nm³)** — do not mix. Furnace oil is a liquid → use mass basis.
> 2. **Sulphur contribution is often missed** — students write only C and H terms and **lose 1 mark** in part (iii).
> 3. **O/8 correction** must be inside the bracket for both GCV and $A_0$ — not separately. Showing $\text{H} - \text{O}/8$ wrong-signed is a common error.
> 4. **NCV formula sign** — students sometimes write **NCV = GCV + 52.83 H**. The latent heat **leaves** with the vapour, so it must be **subtracted**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Save this block as a one-page **last-minute revision sheet** before your KTU ESE.

- ✅ **Combustion = fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + SO₂ + N₂ + heat.** Controlled in a furnace to generate steam.
- ✅ **Three Ts of combustion:** Temperature, Turbulence, Time.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric air (A₀):** minimum O₂ for *complete* combustion of C → CO₂, H → H₂O, S → SO₂.
- ✅ **A₀ formula (numerical shortcut):** $A_0 = 11.6\,\text{C} + 34.8(\text{H} - \text{O}/8) + 4.35\,\text{S}$ kg/kg.
- ✅ **Excess air** is *always* required in practice: coal 15–50 %, oil 10–20 %, gas 5–10 %.
- ✅ **GCV > NCV**; difference = latent heat of water formed = **52.83 × H** kcal/kg (for H in %).
- ✅ **Dulong's formula** uses mass-% of C, H, O, S — keep H and O together inside the bracket.
- ✅ **Orsat analysis** = dry flue gas composition (CO₂ + O₂ + N₂ = 100 %, ignoring SO₂).
- ✅ **Dry flue-gas heat loss** (Siegert): $L_{\text{DFG}} = 0.65\,(T_{\text{fg}} - T_{\text{air}})\,\%\,\text{CO}_2 / 100$.
- ✅ **FBC boilers** offer high efficiency + low NOₓ + fuel flexibility at 850–950 °C bed temperature.
- ✅ For **gaseous fuels**, use **Nm³/Nm³** basis; stoichiometric O₂ per Nm³: CH₄ → 2, C₂H₆ → 3.5, H₂ → 0.5, CO → 0.5.
- ✅ Always carry out a **mass balance check** on C, H, O, N — exam-friendly and catches arithmetic errors.
- ✅ Watch out for **% vs fraction** — Dulong expects %, but A₀ derivation expects *fraction*. Convert by ÷ 100.
- ✅ Combustion efficiency = 100 − (DFG loss + H₂O loss + CO loss + radiation). Every 1 % excess-air reduction ≈ **0.5 % fuel saved**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Evaluation** in the context of **Process Utilities** is the **systematic, quantitative assessment of how efficiently a utility system converts input energy (fuel, electricity, steam, water, air) into useful output energy or service (steam, compressed air, cooling, heating, refrigeration)**, expressed through engineering metrics such as **thermal efficiency (η)**, **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**, **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**, and **capacity utilization factor**.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, it is the procedure by which an existing utility installation (boiler, compressor, pump, cooling tower, refrigeration cycle, heat exchanger network) is benchmarked against its **design intent** to identify energy wastage, quantify losses, and recommend corrective actions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of performance evaluation like a **doctor's health check-up** for a utility:

- A doctor measures BP, sugar, cholesterol → here we measure **stack gas temperature**, **excess air**, **steam enthalpy**, **kWh consumed**.
- A doctor compares your readings with healthy norms → we compare actual efficiency with **design (nameplate) efficiency**.
- A doctor prescribes medicine/diet → we prescribe **maintenance, insulation, tuning, retrofit**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Justification**: Even a **1% drop** in boiler efficiency in a 10 TPH boiler can waste over **₹15-20 lakh/year** in fuel. Hence, continuous performance evaluation is the foundation of any **Energy Management** program (ISO 50001).

### 1.3 Foundational Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

The core performance metrics you must know are:

- **Thermal Efficiency (η)** = Useful energy output ÷ Energy input.
- **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)** = Power delivered to shaft ÷ Power input to driver.
- **Isentropic / Isothermal Efficiency** = Ideal work ÷ Actual work (compressors, turbines).
- **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** = Desired thermal effect ÷ Net work input.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** = Energy used per unit product (e.g., kWh/ton of refrigeration, kg fuel/ton steam).
- **Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)** = Actual load ÷ Rated load.
- **Heat Loss Percentage** = (Q_loss ÷ Q_input) × 100.

### 1.4 Two Fundamental Philosophies of Evaluation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Direct (Input–Output) Method**: η = Output ÷ Input. Simple, requires accurate flow & calorific measurements.
> 2. **Indirect (Heat Loss) Method**: η = 100 − Σ(all individual losses %). Used when output is hard to measure; this is the **industry-preferred** method.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Flow of Performance Evaluation

The structured methodology followed universally (and expected in KTU answers) is:

1. **Define the system boundary** (e.g., from fuel inlet to steam outlet of a boiler).
2. **List all input streams** (fuel mass, GCV, feed water enthalpy, combustion air enthalpy).
3. **List all output streams** (steam enthalpy, flue gas enthalpy, blowdown, surface loss, unburnt fuel).
4. **Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics** (energy in = energy out + losses).
5. **Compute efficiency** either by direct ratio or by subtracting losses.
6. **Compare with baseline** (design value, BIS/IS standards, PTC codes).
7. **Recommend improvements** (tuning, insulation, waste heat recovery).

### 2.2 Utility-Wise Analytical Breakdown

#### A. Steam Boilers
- **Direct Method**:  
  $$\eta_{direct} = \dfrac{m_s \cdot (h_g - h_{fw})}{m_f \cdot \text{GCV}}$$
- **Indirect Method (Heat Loss)**:
  $$\eta_{indirect} = 100 - (L_{dry\,flue\,gas} + L_{H_2O\,from\,H_2} + L_{H_2O\,from\,moisture} + L_{rad\,+\,unburnt})$$

  Typical losses:
  - **Dry Flue Gas Loss (L1)** = m_g × Cp_g × (T_g − T_a) / (m_f × GCV) × 100
  - **Hydrogen Loss (L2)** = 9 × m_H2 × [584 + Cp_v(T_g − T_a)] / (GCV)
  - **Moisture in Fuel Loss (L3)** = m_M × [584 + Cp_v(T_g − T_a)] / GCV
  - **Radiation + Unburnt Loss (L4)** ≈ 1.5–2 % (assumed)
  - **Moisture in Air (L5)** — usually negligible.

#### B. Compressors (Air/Gas)
- **Isothermal efficiency**: $\eta_{iso} = W_{iso}/W_{act}$ where $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$.
- **Adiabatic (Isentropic) efficiency**: $\eta_{ad} = W_{ad}/W_{act}$.
- **Mechanical efficiency**: $\eta_{mech} = W_{act}/\text{Brake Power}$.
- **Overall (Isothermal) efficiency**: $\eta_{overall} = W_{iso}/BP$.

#### C. Pumps
$$\eta_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{P_{input}}$$
- **Manometric efficiency** = H_manometric / H_impeller.
- **Volumetric efficiency** = Actual discharge / Theoretical discharge.
- **Overall efficiency** = product of manometric, volumetric, and mechanical efficiencies.

#### D. Heat Exchangers
- **LMTD method**: $\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$.
- **NTU-Effectiveness**: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{hot,in} - T_{hot,out}}{T_{hot,in} - T_{cold,in}}$.
- **Overall heat transfer**: $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD} \cdot F$.

#### E. Cooling Towers
- **Range** = T_water,in − T_water,out (warm water cooled by).
- **Approach** = T_water,out − T_wb (limiting value).
- **Effectiveness** = Range / (Range + Approach) = (T_wi − T_wo) / (T_wi − T_wb).
- **Evaporation loss** ≈ 0.00085 × CWC × ΔT (per °C range).
- **Blowdown (BD)**, **Drift loss (D)** maintain cycles of concentration (COC).

#### F. Refrigeration Systems
- **COP (Refrigeration)** = Q_L / W_net.
- **Carnot COP** = T_L / (T_H − T_L) — theoretical upper bound.
- **Heat rejected at condenser**: Q_H = Q_L + W_net.
- **Performance Factor / EER** = 3.516 × COP (when 1 TR = 3.517 kW).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **exam-ready formula reference**. Memorise these symbols and units before the exam.

| # | Utility System | Formula | Symbol Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Boiler (Direct) | $\eta = \dfrac{m_s (h_g - h_{fw})}{m_f \cdot GCV}$ | kg, kJ/kg |
| 2 | Boiler (Indirect) | $\eta = 100 - \sum L_i$ | Each $L_i$ in % |
| 3 | Compressor (Isothermal) | $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | kJ |
| 4 | Compressor (Adiabatic) | $W_{ad} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(P_2/P_1\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ | kJ |
| 5 | Pump | $\eta_p = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{in}}$ | W, m³/s, m |
| 6 | Heat Exchanger LMTD | $\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 \right)}$ | °C |
| 7 | Cooling Tower Range | $\Delta T_{range} = T_{w,in} - T_{w,out}$ | °C |
| 8 | Cooling Tower Approach | $\Delta T_{app} = T_{w,out} - T_{wb}$ | °C |
| 9 | Cooling Tower Effectiveness | $\varepsilon_{CT} = \dfrac{\text{Range}}{\text{Range} + \text{Approach}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Refrigeration COP | $\text{COP} = Q_L / W_{net}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Carnot COP | $\text{COP}_{Carnot} = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ | K or °C |
| 12 | Heat Rejected (Condenser) | $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ | kW |
| 13 | Specific Steam Consumption | $\text{SSC} = 3600 / (h_g - h_{fw})$ | kg steam/kWh |
| 14 | Evaporation Loss (CT) | $E = 0.00085 \cdot CWC \cdot \Delta T$ | m³/h |
| 15 | Capacity Utilization | $\text{CUF} = \text{Actual Load} / \text{Rated Load}$ | fraction |

> **Engineering Utility**: These formulas are not academic — they are the daily bread of a **plant energy manager**, **chartered engineer**, and **certified energy auditor (BEE Empaneled)** in India.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Boiler Efficiency by Direct Method

Let $m_f$ = fuel flow rate (kg/h), $m_s$ = steam flow rate (kg/h), $GCV$ = gross calorific value of fuel (kJ/kg), $h_g$ = enthalpy of steam leaving boiler (kJ/kg), $h_{fw}$ = enthalpy of feed water entering boiler (kJ/kg).

**Step 1 — Energy Input per hour**  
Energy supplied by fuel = mass of fuel × calorific value.
$$E_{in} = m_f \times \text{GCV} \quad \text{(kJ/h)}$$

**Step 2 — Useful Energy Output per hour**  
Useful energy is the enthalpy gained by water becoming steam.
$$E_{out} = m_s \times (h_g - h_{fw}) \quad \text{(kJ/h)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Definition of Thermal Efficiency**  
$$\eta_{direct} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \dfrac{m_s (h_g - h_{fw})}{m_f \times \text{GCV}}$$

> The denominator uses **GCV** (not NCV) because latent heat of water vapour in flue gas is recoverable in principle — the *direct method* assumes all energy is eventually useful, hence GCV.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Boiler Efficiency by Indirect (Heat Loss) Method

By the **First Law**, for a steady-state boiler:
$$E_{in} = E_{out,useful} + \sum E_{losses}$$

Dividing throughout by $E_{in}$:
$$1 = \eta_{indirect} + \sum L_i / 100$$
$$\therefore \eta_{indirect} = 100 - \sum_{i} L_i \quad \text{(each } L_i \text{ in \%)}$$

**Step A — Dry Flue Gas Loss (L1)**  
Sensible heat carried by dry products of combustion (CO₂, SO₂, O₂, N₂) above ambient.
$$L_1 = \dfrac{m_g \cdot C_{pg} \cdot (T_g - T_a)}{m_f \cdot GCV} \times 100 \,\,(\%)$$

where $m_g$ = mass of dry flue gas per kg fuel, $C_{pg}$ ≈ 1.005 kJ/kg·K, $T_g$ = flue gas temp, $T_a$ = ambient temp.

**Step B — Loss Due to Hydrogen in Fuel (L2)**  
Hydrogen burns to water; this water leaves as vapour, taking latent + sensible heat:
$$L_2 = \dfrac{9 \cdot m_{H_2} \cdot \left[ 584 + C_{pv}(T_g - T_a) \right]}{m_f \cdot GCV} \times 100\,\,(\%)$$

The factor **9** comes from: 1 kg H₂ → 9 kg H₂O (since 2 + 16 = 18; 18/2 = 9).

**Step C — Loss Due to Moisture in Fuel (L3)**  
$$L_3 = \dfrac{m_{M} \cdot \left[ C_{pw}(100 - T_a) + 584 + C_{pv}(T_g - 100) \right]}{m_f \cdot GCV} \times 100\,\,(\%)$$

**Step D — Radiation & Unburnt Loss (L4)**  
Empirically assumed based on boiler capacity:

| Boiler Capacity (kg/h) | L4 (%) |
|---|---|
| Up to 1000 | 2.0 |
| 1000 – 6000 | 1.5 |
| > 6000 | 1.0 |

**Step E — Final Efficiency**
$$\eta = 100 - (L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + L_4)\%$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Isothermal Work of Compression

For a reversible isothermal compression of an ideal gas from $P_1$ to $P_2$:
$$W_{iso} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{RT}{V}\, dV$$

Using ideal gas law $PV = RT$ (T constant in isothermal):
$$W_{iso} = RT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{dV}{V} = RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

By Boyle's Law at constant T: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$, so $V_2/V_1 = P_1/P_2$:
$$W_{iso} = RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right) = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$$

Since $P_2 > P_1$, the log is **negative**; the work done **on the gas** is the magnitude:
$$\boxed{\; W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \;}$$

Then **isothermal efficiency**:
$$\eta_{iso} = \dfrac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)}{W_{actual}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Coefficient of Performance of Refrigeration

A refrigeration cycle absorbs heat $Q_L$ from low-temperature reservoir ($T_L$) and rejects $Q_H$ to high-temperature reservoir ($T_H$), consuming work $W_{net}$.

**Step 1 — Energy Balance on Cycle**
$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$$

**Step 2 — Definition of COP**
$$\text{COP} = \dfrac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$

**Step 3 — For a Reversible (Carnot) Cycle**
$$\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} = \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_H = Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L}$$

Therefore:
$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L = Q_L \left(\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1\right) = Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute**
$$\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot (T_H - T_L)/T_L} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always use **absolute temperature (Kelvin)** in the Carnot COP formula — this is a common KTU exam pitfall!

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table

| Utility | Direct Indicator | Indirect Loss Component | Common Benchmark |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boiler (Oil-fired) | 84–88 % | L1 (dry flue) dominates | > 85 % = healthy |
| Boiler (Coal-fired) | 70–80 % | L2 (H₂O), L1, ash | > 75 % |
| Air Compressor | η_iso > 60 % | Heat in intercoolers | η_overall > 70 % |
| Pump | η_overall > 70 % | Hydraulic, vol., mech. | > 75 % |
| Cooling Tower | ε > 70 % | Evap., drift, blowdown | Range 5–10 °C |
| Refrigeration (VCC) | COP 2.5–4.0 | Condenser, compressor | EER > 10 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Performance Evaluation Methodology — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define System Boundary] --> B[Identify Input Streams]
    A --> C[Identify Output Streams]
    B --> D[Measure: Flow, T, P, Composition]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Choose Method}
    E -- Direct --> F[Output / Input Ratio]
    E -- Indirect --> G[100 - Sum of Losses]
    F --> H[Compute Efficiency η]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compare with Design / PTC / IS Standards]
    I --> J[Identify Deviation]
    J --> K[Recommend Improvements]
    K --> L[Re-evaluate After Action]
```

### 4.2 Boiler Energy Balance Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Fuel + GCV] --> SUM((Σ Energy In))
    A1[Combustion Air] --> SUM
    FW1[Feed Water h_fw] --> SUM
    SUM --> BL[Boiler Shell]
    BL --> S1[Steam Output h_g]
    BL --> L1[Loss 1: Dry Flue Gas]
    BL --> L2[Loss 2: H2O from H2]
    BL --> L3[Loss 3: Moisture]
    BL --> L4[Loss 4: Radiation + Unburnt]
    BL --> L5[Loss 5: Blowdown]
```

### 4.3 Refrigeration Cycle Performance Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EVAP[Evaporator T_L] -->|Q_L absorbed| COMP[Compressor W_in]
    COMP --> COND[Condenser T_H]
    COND -->|Q_H rejected| EXP[Expansion Valve]
    EXP --> EVAP
    COMP --> CALC{COP Calculation}
    EVAP --> CALC
    CALC --> OUT[COP = Q_L / W_in]
    CALC --> CARNOT[Theoretical COP = T_L / T_H-T_L]
```

### 4.4 Cooling Tower Performance Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HW[Hot Water In T_wi] --> CT[Tower Fill/Packing]
    CT --> CW[Cold Water Out T_wo]
    AT[Ambient Air] --> FAN[Fan]
    FAN --> CT
    CT --> HA[Hot Humid Air Out]
    CT --> EV[Evaporation Loss E]
    CT --> DR[Drift Loss D]
    CT --> BD[Blowdown BD]
    TWB[Wet Bulb T_wb] --> CALC[Effectiveness]
    TWI --> CALC
    TWO --> CALC
    CALC --> RESULT[ε = T_wi - T_wo / T_wi - T_wb]
```

### 4.5 Compressor Performance Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Inlet P1, V1] --> COMP[Real Compressor]
    COMP --> P2[Outlet P2, V2]
    COMP --> WACT[Actual Work W_actual]
    P1 --> WISO[Isothermal Ideal W_iso]
    P1 --> WAD[Adiabatic Ideal W_ad]
    WISO --> ETA1[η_iso = W_iso / W_actual]
    WAD --> ETA2[η_ad = W_ad / W_actual]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Qn]**

**Q1. Define performance evaluation of process utilities. List any four key performance indicators used.**

**Model Answer:**

Performance evaluation of process utilities is the **systematic quantitative assessment of the effectiveness** with which a utility system (boiler, compressor, cooling tower, etc.) converts input energy into useful output energy, compared against its design intent.

Four key performance indicators:
1. **Thermal Efficiency (η)** — ratio of useful energy output to energy input.
2. **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — energy used per unit product/service.
3. **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** — desired thermal effect per unit work input.
4. **Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)** — actual load divided by rated load.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Qn]**

**Q2. Differentiate between the direct method and indirect method of evaluating boiler efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Direct Method | Indirect Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_s (h_g - h_{fw})}{m_f \cdot GCV}$ | $\eta = 100 - \sum L_i$ |
| **Principle** | Output / Input ratio | Subtracts losses from 100 |
| **Accuracy** | Lower (depends on flow accuracy) | Higher (preferred in industry) |
| **Measurements** | Steam & fuel flow, enthalpies | Flue gas composition, temp., fuel analysis |
| **Use** | Quick field checks | Detailed performance audits (BEE Grade) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]**

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **direct and indirect methods** of evaluating the performance of a **steam boiler**. Derive the expressions used in each method.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **10 TPH oil-fired boiler** generates dry saturated steam at **10 bar**. Feed water enters at **80 °C**. The oil consumption is **800 kg/h** with a **GCV of 42,000 kJ/kg**. Calculate:
  (i) The **direct method efficiency**.
  (ii) The **specific steam consumption (kg steam per kg fuel)**.

> **Properties required (at 10 bar, dry saturated)**:  
> $h_g = 2778.1$ kJ/kg, $h_f = 762.8$ kJ/kg, so $h_{fg} = 2015.3$ kJ/kg.  
> Feed water: $h_{fw} = C_p \cdot T = 4.18 \times 80 = 334.4$ kJ/kg.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $m_s = 10,000$ kg/h, $m_f = 800$ kg/h, GCV = 42,000 kJ/kg
- $h_g = 2778.1$ kJ/kg, $h_{fw} = 334.4$ kJ/kg

**(i) Direct Method Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{direct} = \dfrac{m_s (h_g - h_{fw})}{m_f \cdot GCV}
$$

$$
\eta_{direct} = \dfrac{10{,}000 \times (2778.1 - 334.4)}{800 \times 42{,}000}
$$

$$
\eta_{direct} = \dfrac{10{,}000 \times 2443.7}{33{,}600{,}000}
$$

$$
\eta_{direct} = \dfrac{2.4437 \times 10^{7}}{3.36 \times 10^{7}} = 0.7273
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{direct} = 72.73\%}
$$

**(ii) Specific Steam Consumption (SSC):**

$$
\text{SSC} = \dfrac{m_s}{m_f} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{800} = \boxed{12.5 \text{ kg steam / kg fuel}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often mistakenly use **NCV** instead of GCV, or use **$h_f$** of feed water at 80 °C as **334** instead of **334.4**, losing **0.5 mark**. Always write the unit of feed water enthalpy calculation step explicitly to claim full marks.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **key performance parameters** used to evaluate:
  (i) An **air compressor** (isothermal & adiabatic efficiency).
  (ii) A **vapour compression refrigeration system** (COP, EER).

**(b) [7 Marks]** An **ammonia refrigeration plant** operates between an evaporator temperature of **−10 °C** and a condenser temperature of **40 °C**. The plant delivers a cooling load of **10 TR**. Calculate:
  (i) The theoretical (Carnot) **COP**.
  (ii) The **compressor power** required.
  (iii) The **heat rejected** at the condenser.

> **Take 1 TR = 3.517 kW; temperatures in Kelvin.**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $T_L = -10 + 273 = 263$ K
- $T_H = 40 + 273 = 313$ K
- Cooling load = 10 TR → $Q_L = 10 \times 3.517 = 35.17$ kW

**(i) Carnot COP:**

$$
\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \dfrac{263}{313 - 263} = \dfrac{263}{50}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{Carnot} = 5.26}
$$

**(ii) Compressor Power:**

$$
W_{net} = \dfrac{Q_L}{\text{COP}} = \dfrac{35.17}{5.26}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{net} = 6.69 \text{ kW}}
$$

**(iii) Heat Rejected at Condenser:**

$$
Q_H = Q_L + W_{net} = 35.17 + 6.69
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_H = 41.86 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2**: 
> - Forgetting to convert °C → **K** → loses **1 mark** outright.  
> - Using **1 TR = 3.5** (old approximation) instead of **3.517** → loses **0.5 mark**.  
> - Wrong sign convention in $Q_H = Q_L + W$ (some write $Q_H = Q_L - W$) → **full 2-mark deduction** in part (iii).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Hotspots in Performance Evaluation Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Errors**: Mixing kcal with kJ, °C with K. KTU strictly expects SI.
> 2. **GCV vs NCV**: Direct method uses **GCV**; indirect method uses **GCV in denominator** but individual losses are derived from NCV logic — state explicitly.
> 3. **Carnot COP**: Always **Kelvin temperatures**. 
> 4. **LMTD vs Log-mean ΔT**: Don't compute arithmetic mean; KTU often asks for **F-factor correction**.
> 5. **Range vs Approach Confusion**: In cooling towers, students swap the two — remember: **Range = hot − cold water temp; Approach = cold water − wet-bulb temp**.
> 6. **Missing Energy Balance Statement**: A 1-line "By First Law, energy in = energy out + losses" earns **½ to 1 grace mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Performance Evaluation = **measuring how efficiently a utility uses energy**, comparing actual to design.
- ✅ Two methods: **Direct (Output/Input)** and **Indirect (100 − Σ Losses)**.
- ✅ Boiler efficiency **direct formula**: $\eta = m_s(h_g - h_{fw}) / (m_f \cdot GCV)$.
- ✅ Boiler losses typically include **L1 (dry flue), L2 (H₂), L3 (moisture), L4 (radiation)**.
- ✅ Compressor efficiencies: **isothermal** (cool compression) vs **adiabatic/isentropic** (no heat loss). Real cycle is between the two.
- ✅ Pump overall efficiency = $\rho g Q H / P_{input}$.
- ✅ Heat exchanger: **LMTD** and **ε-NTU** methods; apply **correction factor F** for multi-pass shells.
- ✅ Cooling Tower: **Range = T_wi − T_wo**; **Approach = T_wo − T_wb**; **ε = Range / (Range + Approach)**.
- ✅ Cooling tower water losses: **Evaporation + Drift + Blowdown**, related by **Cycles of Concentration (COC)**.
- ✅ Refrigeration: **COP = Q_L / W_net**; **Carnot COP = T_L / (T_H − T_L)**; **Q_H = Q_L + W_net**; **1 TR = 3.517 kW**.
- ✅ Always convert °C → K for any temperature ratio/cycle formula.
- ✅ Always state **assumed values** (radiation loss %, ambient temp, GCV vs NCV basis) — KTU rewards clarity.
- ✅ Final answer must carry **correct unit** and **2-decimal precision** minimum.
- ✅ Energy auditing linkage: Direct method = Type-1 audit; Indirect method = Type-2 / Detailed audit (BEE).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Losses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Losses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Process Utilities**, *"Analysis of Losses"* refers to the systematic quantification and evaluation of **thermal energy losses** that occur during the generation, distribution, and utilization of utilities such as **steam, hot oil, compressed air, and refrigerants**. These losses manifest primarily as **heat transfer to the surroundings** from uninsulated or inadequately insulated pipes, vessels, boilers, and equipment.

The major categories of losses are:

| Category | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Radiation Losses** | Energy emitted as electromagnetic waves from hot surfaces |
| **Convection Losses** | Energy carried away by fluid (air) movement over surfaces |
| **Conduction Losses** | Energy passing through insulation and pipe walls |
| **Steam Trap Losses** | Flash steam, leakage, and steam losses through faulty traps |
| **Boiler / Stack Losses** | Hot flue gas enthalpy loss, incomplete combustion loss |
| **Condensate Losses** | Unrecovered hot condensate flashing to low-pressure steam |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Module 3 emphasizes **heat loss from insulated/uninsulated steam pipes**, **economic thickness of insulation**, and **critical radius of insulation**. Numerical problems are commonly asked from these areas in **End Semester Examinations (ESE)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Winter Jacket Analogy:**
> Imagine drinking hot tea (≈ 90 °C) from a steel tumbler. After 5 minutes, the outside of the tumbler feels hot, and the tea cools down rapidly. The heat escapes through three pathways: **(a) Radiation** (the tumbler glows in a thermal camera), **(b) Convection** (the air around the tumbler warms and rises), and **(c) Conduction** (through the metal walls). Now, wrap the tumbler in a woolen jacket (insulation). The heat loss drops dramatically. But interestingly, if the wall of the tumbler were *very thin* (like a needle), adding a thin layer of insulation would actually *increase* heat loss — this is the famous **"Critical Radius"** phenomenon. This is exactly what happens in industrial steam pipelines, boilers, and insulated reactors.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant ($\sigma$)** = $\mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}}$
- **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$)** for **polished metal** = 0.03 – 0.08
- **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$)** for **oxidized / painted metal** = 0.6 – 0.95
- **Typical steam pipe surface temperature** = 50 °C – 200 °C
- **Standard ambient reference** = 25 °C (298 K) or 30 °C (303 K) for KTU numericals
- **Equivalent film coefficient ($h_r + h_c$)** for outdoor pipes ≈ **10 – 15 W/m²K**
- **Equivalent film coefficient ($h_r + h_c$)** for indoor still air ≈ **5 – 10 W/m²K**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Modes of Heat Loss from a Bare / Insulated Pipe

#### 2.1.1 Heat Loss by Radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann Law)

A hot pipe surface at temperature $T_s$ (K) loses heat to the surroundings at $T_{sur}$ (K) by thermal radiation.

**Mechanism (Why and How):**
- **Why:** Every body above 0 K emits EM radiation. The hotter the surface, the higher the emission.
- **How:** The energy radiated is proportional to the 4th power of absolute temperature and the surface emissivity.

**Key Engineering Insight:** Because of the $T^4$ dependence, radiation loss **increases dramatically** above 100 °C. A small temperature rise (e.g., from 100 °C to 150 °C) causes a *very large* increase in radiation loss — this is why superheated steam lines require **aggressive insulation**.

#### 2.1.2 Heat Loss by Natural Convection

The air adjacent to the hot pipe gets heated, becomes lighter, rises, and is replaced by cooler air. This continuous cycle is called **free / natural convection** and is governed by the **Grashof and Prandtl numbers**.

For **laminar flow** over a horizontal cylinder, the empirical correlation (valid for the KTU range):

$$h_c = 1.32 \left( \frac{\Delta T}{D_o} \right)^{0.25}$$

Where $\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty$ and $D_o$ is the outer diameter of the pipe (m). The unit of $h_c$ is **W/m²K** when $\Delta T$ is in K and $D_o$ in m.

For **forced convection** (air moved by a fan/wind):

$$h_c = \frac{Nu \cdot k_{air}}{D_o}$$

#### 2.1.3 Heat Loss by Conduction (Through Insulation)

For a **cylindrical insulated pipe**, the heat loss per unit length by conduction through the insulation is:

$$Q_{cond} = \frac{2 \pi k (T_s - T_\infty)}{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}$$

Where:
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of insulation (W/m·K)
- $r_1$ = outer radius of bare pipe (m)
- $r_2$ = outer radius of insulation (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** Convective resistance from the outer surface of insulation is **in series** with the conductive resistance of insulation. The **total** heat loss is found by solving the thermal resistance network.

#### 2.1.4 Combined Heat Loss — Equivalent Coefficient Method

In practice, **radiation + convection** from the outer surface are combined into a single equivalent film coefficient:

$$h_{eq} = h_c + h_r$$

$$Q_{total} = h_{eq} \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

Where the **radiation component** $h_r$ is **linearized** as:

$$h_r = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s + T_{sur})(T_s^2 + T_{sur}^2)$$

This linearization is critical for KTU problems because it allows superposition of resistance networks.

### 2.2 Critical Radius of Insulation (A KTU Favourite!)

> [!WARNING]
> This is the **most-missed concept** in KTU exams. Students blindly assume "more insulation = less heat loss." The truth is counterintuitive!

For a cylindrical pipe, the total resistance to heat loss per unit length is:

$$R_{total} = \frac{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}{2 \pi k} + \frac{1}{h_{eq} \cdot 2 \pi r_2}$$

Heat loss $Q = \dfrac{T_s - T_\infty}{R_{total}}$.

Differentiating with respect to $r_2$ and equating to zero gives the **critical radius of insulation**:

$$\boxed{r_c = \frac{k}{h_{eq}}}$$

**Three Regimes:**

| Condition | Effect of Adding Insulation |
|-----------|----------------------------|
| $r_2 < r_c$ | **Heat loss INCREASES** (counterintuitive!) |
| $r_2 = r_c$ | **Maximum heat loss** (peak) |
| $r_2 > r_c$ | **Heat loss DECREASES** (as expected) |

### 2.3 Economic Thickness of Insulation

**Definition:** The thickness of insulation at which the **Total Annual Cost (Capital + Heat Loss Cost)** is minimum.

$$\text{Total Cost } C_t = C_{cap}(t) + C_{heat}(t)$$

$$C_{cap} = \text{Cost of insulation per } m^3 \times \text{Volume per unit length} = C_i \cdot \pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$$

$$C_{heat} = \text{Cost of heat per MJ} \times Q \times \text{Operating hours}$$

Setting $\dfrac{dC_t}{dr_2} = 0$ gives the optimum $r_2$ and hence optimum thickness $t = r_2 - r_1$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ | Net radiation heat loss | W |
| 2 | $Q_{conv} = h_c A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Convective heat loss | W |
| 3 | $Q_{total} = h_{eq} A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Combined loss | W |
| 4 | $h_{eq} = h_c + h_r$ | Equivalent film coefficient | W/m²K |
| 5 | $h_r = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s + T_{sur})(T_s^2 + T_{sur}^2)$ | Linearized radiation coeff. | W/m²K |
| 6 | $Q_{cond} = \dfrac{2 \pi k L (T_s - T_\infty)}{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}$ | Conduction through cylindrical insulation | W |
| 7 | $R_{total} = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_{eq} 2\pi r_2 L}$ | Total thermal resistance (cyl.) | K/W |
| 8 | $r_c = k / h_{eq}$ | **Critical radius of insulation** | m |
| 9 | $Q_{loss} = 1.32 (\Delta T)^{1.25} \cdot L / D_o^{0.25}$ | Natural conv. (laminar) for bare pipe | W |
| 10 | $\eta_{ins} = (Q_{bare} - Q_{ins}) / Q_{bare} \times 100$ | Insulation effectiveness | % |
| 11 | $t_{opt}$: $d(C_{cap} + C_{heat}) / dt = 0$ | **Economic insulation thickness** | m |
| 12 | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | Stefan-Boltzmann constant | W/m²K⁴ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical plants** use this analysis to size insulation on **crude preheat trains**.
- **Power plants** calculate stack losses to assess **boiler efficiency** ($\eta_{boiler} = 1 - $ losses).
- **Refrigeration plants** analyze cold losses from chilled water and brine pipelines.
- **Pharma/Food plants** compute insulation thickness to meet **HACCP** and **ISPE** guidelines.
- **Energy audits** (mandatory under the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**, India) use these formulas to recommend insulation upgrades.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Radius of Insulation (Cylindrical Pipe)

**Starting Setup:** A bare pipe of outer radius $r_1$ is insulated up to outer radius $r_2$. The pipe surface is at $T_s$ (K) and the ambient air is at $T_\infty$ (K). The insulation has thermal conductivity $k$ (W/m·K). The combined outside film coefficient is $h_{eq}$ (W/m²K).

**Goal:** Find the value of $r_2$ at which heat loss per unit length is maximum (i.e., resistance is minimum).

**Step 1 — Write the heat loss per unit length of the insulated pipe.**

The total thermal resistance per unit length $L = 1$ m is the sum of conduction resistance through insulation and convection+radiation resistance at the outer surface:

$$R_{total} = \frac{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}{2 \pi k} + \frac{1}{h_{eq} \cdot 2 \pi r_2}$$

**Step 2 — Heat loss is inversely proportional to total resistance.**

$$Q = \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{R_{total}}$$

To **maximize Q**, we must **minimize $R_{total}$** with respect to $r_2$.

**Step 3 — Differentiate $R_{total}$ with respect to $r_2$.**

$$\frac{dR_{total}}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{2 \pi k} \cdot \frac{1}{r_2} - \frac{1}{h_{eq} \cdot 2 \pi r_2^2}$$

**Step 4 — Set derivative equal to zero for extremum.**

$$\frac{1}{2 \pi k \cdot r_2} = \frac{1}{h_{eq} \cdot 2 \pi r_2^2}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify by canceling $2\pi$ and $r_2$ from both sides.**

$$\frac{1}{k} = \frac{1}{h_{eq} \cdot r_2}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the critical radius.**

$$\boxed{r_c = \frac{k}{h_{eq}}}$$

**Step 7 — Confirm it is a maximum using the second derivative test.**

$$\frac{d^2 R_{total}}{dr_2^2} = -\frac{1}{2\pi k r_2^2} + \frac{1}{h_{eq} \pi r_2^3} > 0 \quad \text{at } r_2 = r_c$$

This shows $R_{total}$ has a **minimum** at $r_2 = r_c$, meaning **$Q$ has a maximum**. The maximum heat loss is:

$$Q_{max} = \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{\dfrac{\ln(r_c / r_1)}{2 \pi k} + \dfrac{1}{h_{eq} \cdot 2 \pi r_c}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** If $r_1 < r_c$, the bare pipe actually loses **less** heat than an inadequately insulated one! Industrial insulation specs therefore ensure $r_2 \gg r_c$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Economic Thickness of Insulation

**Setup:** Cost of insulation per m³ = $C_i$ ₹/m³. Cost of heat = $C_h$ ₹/MJ. Annual operating hours = $H$ h/year. Pipe length = $L$ m.

**Step 1 — Capital cost of insulation per unit length.**

$$C_{cap} = C_i \cdot \pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2) \quad \text{(₹/m of pipe)}$$

**Step 2 — Annual cost of heat lost per unit length.**

Heat loss per unit length is:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi (T_s - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_{eq} r_2}}$$

Annual cost:

$$C_{heat} = C_h \cdot Q \cdot H \cdot 3600 \times 10^{-6} \quad \text{(₹/m/year)}$$

**Step 3 — Total annual cost.**

$$C_{total}(r_2) = C_i \pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2) + C_h H \cdot \frac{2\pi (T_s - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_{eq} r_2}} \cdot 3.6$$

**Step 4 — Set $\dfrac{dC_{total}}{dr_2} = 0$ and solve.**

This is a transcendental equation; in KTU problems it is usually given in **iterative form** or solved by trial. The optimum $r_2$ gives $t_{opt} = r_2 - r_1$.

> [!TIP]
> For **KTU 14-mark problems**, you will normally be given $C_i$, $C_h$, $H$, $k$, $h_{eq}$, $r_1$, $T_s$, $T_\infty$ and asked to **assume a thickness $t$**, compute $C_{total}$, and iterate to find minimum. Show at least 2–3 iterations in the answer.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 100 mm OD steam pipe at 150 °C passes through a 25 °C ambient. Pipe emissivity = 0.85. A 50 mm thick insulation (k = 0.05 W/m·K) is applied. Outer surface emissivity = 0.9. Pipe length = 50 m. Calculate the total heat loss.

**Given:**
- $T_s$ = 150 + 273 = 423 K
- $T_\infty$ = 25 + 273 = 298 K
- $D_o$ (bare) = 0.1 m, so $r_1$ = 0.05 m
- Insulation thickness = 0.05 m, so $r_2$ = 0.05 + 0.05 = 0.1 m
- $k$ = 0.05 W/m·K
- $\varepsilon$ (outer insulation) = 0.9
- $L$ = 50 m

**Step 1 — Estimate natural convection coefficient.**

$$h_c = 1.32 \left( \frac{\Delta T}{D_o^{ins}} \right)^{0.25} = 1.32 \left( \frac{125}{0.2} \right)^{0.25}$$

$$h_c = 1.32 \times (625)^{0.25} = 1.32 \times 5.0 = 6.6 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the outer surface temperature (assuming steady state, solve by iteration).**

Assume $T_{s2}$ ≈ 320 K. Then:

$$h_r = 0.9 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (320 + 298)(320^2 + 298^2)$$

$$h_r = 5.103 \times 10^{-8} \times 618 \times (102400 + 88804)$$

$$h_r = 5.103 \times 10^{-8} \times 618 \times 191204 = 6.03 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

So $h_{eq} = 6.6 + 6.03 = 12.63$ W/m²K.

**Step 3 — Compute heat loss per unit length.**

$$Q/L = \frac{2 \pi (T_s - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_{eq} r_2}}$$

$$Q/L = \frac{2 \pi (423 - 298)}{\dfrac{\ln(0.1/0.05)}{0.05} + \dfrac{1}{12.63 \times 0.1}}$$

$$Q/L = \frac{2 \pi \times 125}{\dfrac{0.693}{0.05} + \dfrac{1}{1.263}}$$

$$Q/L = \frac{785.4}{13.86 + 0.792} = \frac{785.4}{14.65} = 53.6 \ \text{W/m}$$

**Step 4 — Total heat loss for 50 m pipe.**

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = 53.6 \times 50 = 2680 \ \text{W} \approx 2.68 \ \text{kW}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** A bare pipe of the same size would lose ~10× this amount. The insulation cuts the loss by **>90%**, confirming its economic necessity.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Loss Analysis

| Step | Check | Action |
|------|-------|--------|
| 1 | Is surface T > 50 °C? | → Compute radiation loss using Stefan-Boltzmann |
| 2 | Is fluid air and stagnant? | → Use natural convection correlation |
| 3 | Is insulation applied? | → Use cylindrical conduction + outer $h_{eq}$ |
| 4 | Is $r_1 < r_c$? | → **CAUTION:** heat loss may increase initially |
| 5 | Find economic thickness? | → Differentiate total cost w.r.t. $r_2$ |
| 6 | Calculate steam trap loss? | → Add flash + leakage losses |
| 7 | Final? | → Compare with KTU textbook solved examples |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Heat Loss Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Hot Surface] --> B{Is surface temperature greater than 50 C}
    B -- No --> Z1[Negligible loss - no insulation needed]
    B -- Yes --> C[Identify Geometry: Pipe / Vessel / Flat]
    C --> D[Step 1: Compute Radiation Loss using Stefan Boltzmann]
    D --> E[Step 2: Compute Natural Convection Loss using h_c correlation]
    E --> F[Step 3: Determine Equivalent Film Coefficient h_eq]
    F --> G{Is insulation present}
    G -- No --> H[Compute Bare Pipe Loss Q bare]
    G -- Yes --> I[Compute Critical Radius r_c = k divided by h_eq]
    I --> J{Compare r2 with r_c}
    J -- r2 less than r_c --> K[Warning: Heat loss increasing with insulation]
    J -- r2 greater than r_c --> L[Heat loss decreasing - safe zone]
    K --> M[Step 4: Economic Thickness via Cost Minimization]
    L --> M
    H --> N[Step 5: Add Steam Trap and Stack Losses]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Step 6: Compare Total Loss vs Acceptable Limit]
```
