```mermaid
    O --> P[Final Report: Recommended Insulation Thickness and Annual Cost Savings]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Thermal Resistance Network (Cylindrical Insulation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Inner Pipe Surface T_s] -->|Conduction through pipe wall R1| nodeB[Pipe-Insulation Interface T_i]
    nodeB -->|Conduction through insulation R2 equals ln of r2 over r1 divided by 2 pi k L| nodeC[Outer Insulation Surface T_s2]
    nodeC -->|Combined Convection and Radiation Resistance R3 equals 1 divided by h_eq A| nodeD[Ambient Air T_infinity]
    nodeE[Heat Flow Q from T_s to T_infinity] --> nodeA
    nodeD --> nodeF[Q rejected to surroundings]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Heat Loss Composition (Stacked Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subA[Total Heat Loss Q total] --> subB[Convection Loss Q conv approx 30 to 50 percent]
    subA --> subC[Radiation Loss Q rad approx 50 to 70 percent]
    subA --> subD[Conduction Loss Q cond approx 0 to 5 percent negligible when insulated]
    subB --> subE[Reduced by wind shields and increased air velocity]
    subC --> subF[Reduced by low emissivity paints and aluminum cladding]
    subD --> subG[Controlled by insulation thickness and k value]
```

### 4.4 Logic Map — Economic Thickness Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Inputs: C_i, C_h, H, k, h_eq, r1, T_s, T_infinity] --> p2[Assume insulation thickness t1]
    p2 --> p3[Compute r2 equals r1 plus t1]
    p3 --> p4[Compute C capital and C heat]
    p4 --> p5[Compute C total equals C capital plus C heat]
    p5 --> p6{Is C total minimum}
    p6 -- No --> p7[Try new thickness t2]
    p7 --> p2
    p6 -- Yes --> p8[Report t opt and C total min]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Define "critical radius of insulation" and explain its significance with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

The **critical radius of insulation** is the outer radius of insulation at which the heat loss from a cylindrical pipe is **maximum** (i.e., total thermal resistance is minimum). It is given by:

$$r_c = \frac{k}{h_{eq}}$$

**Significance:**
- If the bare pipe radius $r_1 < r_c$, adding insulation initially **increases** heat loss until $r_2 = r_c$, after which heat loss decreases.
- **Example:** A thin electrical wire (small $r_1$, large $h_{eq}$ in air) wrapped with rubber insulation loses more heat than a bare wire. This is why electrical wires below $r_c$ are not insulated for thermal protection.
- **Industrial implication:** Always ensure $r_2 \gg r_c$ for steam pipes (e.g., choose $r_2 \geq 2r_c$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between radiation loss and convection loss from a hot steam pipe.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Radiation Loss | Convection Loss |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| Mechanism | EM wave emission | Fluid motion carrying heat |
| Driving force | $T^4$ difference | Density difference (buoyancy) |
| Depends on | Emissivity, surface condition | Geometry, $\Delta T$, fluid velocity |
| Temperature dependence | Very strong ($T^4$) | Moderate ($\Delta T^{1.25}$) |
| Reduction method | Low-$\varepsilon$ paint, aluminum foil | Wind shields, increased $h_c$ control |
| Formula | $Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ | $Q_{conv} = h_c A (T_s - T_\infty)$ |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

A steam pipeline of **75 mm OD** carries superheated steam at **300 °C** through an area where the ambient temperature is **30 °C**. The pipe is insulated with **40 mm thick lagging** of thermal conductivity **0.06 W/m·K**. The outer surface of insulation has emissivity **0.85**. Estimate the heat loss per metre length of pipe. Take the equivalent convection coefficient (including radiation) as **$h_c = 1.32 (\Delta T / D_o)^{0.25}$** and assume the outer surface temperature of insulation is **50 °C** for first iteration.

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks] — Compute heat loss per metre length using the given iteration assumption.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $D_o$ (bare) = 0.075 m → $r_1$ = 0.0375 m
- Insulation thickness = 0.040 m → $r_2$ = 0.0375 + 0.040 = 0.0775 m
- $T_s$ (pipe) = 300 + 273 = 573 K
- $T_\infty$ = 30 + 273 = 303 K
- $T_{s2}$ (assumed) = 50 + 273 = 323 K
- $k$ = 0.06 W/m·K
- $\varepsilon$ = 0.85
- $\sigma$ = $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴

**Step 1 — Convection coefficient at outer insulation surface.**

$$h_c = 1.32 \left( \frac{T_{s2} - T_\infty}{D_o^{ins}} \right)^{0.25} = 1.32 \left( \frac{323 - 303}{0.155} \right)^{0.25}$$

$$h_c = 1.32 \times (129.03)^{0.25} = 1.32 \times 3.376 = 4.46 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Linearized radiation coefficient.**

$$h_r = \varepsilon \sigma (T_{s2} + T_\infty)(T_{s2}^2 + T_\infty^2)$$

$$h_r = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (323 + 303) \times (323^2 + 303^2)$$

$$h_r = 4.82 \times 10^{-8} \times 626 \times (104329 + 91809)$$

$$h_r = 4.82 \times 10^{-8} \times 626 \times 196138 = 5.92 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent film coefficient.**

$$h_{eq} = h_c + h_r = 4.46 + 5.92 = 10.38 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Heat loss per metre length (cylindrical conduction + surface loss).**

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2 \pi (T_s - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_{eq} r_2}}$$

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2 \pi (573 - 303)}{\dfrac{\ln(0.0775/0.0375)}{0.06} + \dfrac{1}{10.38 \times 0.0775}}$$

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2 \pi \times 270}{\dfrac{0.7259}{0.06} + \dfrac{1}{0.804}}$$

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{1696.46}{12.10 + 1.244} = \frac{1696.46}{13.34} = 127.2 \ \text{W/m}$$

**Step 5 — Check critical radius.**

$$r_c = \frac{k}{h_{eq}} = \frac{0.06}{10.38} = 0.00578 \ \text{m} = 5.78 \ \text{mm}$$

Since $r_2 = 77.5$ mm $\gg r_c = 5.78$ mm, we are **well within the safe zone** of insulation.

$$\boxed{Q/L \approx 127 \ \text{W/m}}$$

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks] — Recalculate assuming a different insulation thickness of 60 mm and compare. Also calculate the percentage reduction in heat loss.**

**Model Solution:**

For $t = 60$ mm = 0.060 m, $r_2$ = 0.0375 + 0.060 = 0.0975 m.

**Step 1 — Assume $T_{s2} \approx 45$ °C = 318 K (lower because more insulation).**

$$h_c = 1.32 \left( \frac{318 - 303}{0.195} \right)^{0.25} = 1.32 \times (76.92)^{0.25} = 1.32 \times 2.965 = 3.91 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Radiation coefficient.**

$$h_r = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (318 + 303) \times (318^2 + 303^2)$$

$$h_r = 4.82 \times 10^{-8} \times 621 \times (101124 + 91809) = 4.82 \times 10^{-8} \times 621 \times 192933 = 5.78 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent film coefficient.**

$$h_{eq} = 3.91 + 5.78 = 9.69 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Heat loss per metre length.**

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2 \pi \times 270}{\dfrac{\ln(0.0975/0.0375)}{0.06} + \dfrac{1}{9.69 \times 0.0975}}$$

$$= \frac{1696.46}{\dfrac{0.9555}{0.06} + \dfrac{1}{0.945}}$$

$$= \frac{1696.46}{15.925 + 1.058} = \frac{1696.46}{16.98} = 99.9 \ \text{W/m}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage reduction compared to 40 mm insulation.**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{127.2 - 99.9}{127.2} \times 100 = 21.5\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:** Doubling the insulation thickness (40 → 80 mm) only reduces heat loss by ~30%. This is the **diminishing returns** phenomenon, justifying the use of **economic thickness** optimization in real plant design.

---

#### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Define **economic thickness of insulation**. Derive the expression for critical radius of insulation for a cylindrical pipe.

**(b)** A bare steam pipe of **100 mm OD** at **200 °C** loses heat to surroundings at **30 °C**. Pipe emissivity = 0.8. Calculate the heat loss per metre length of bare pipe. Take $h_c = 1.32 (\Delta T / D_o)^{0.25}$ and $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴.

**Model Solution:**

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks] — Theory & Derivation.**

Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the full derivation. Key points to write:

- **Definition (2 marks):** Economic thickness is the thickness at which the sum of (i) capitalized cost of insulation and (ii) annual cost of heat lost is minimum.
- **Derivation (5 marks):** Set up $R_{total}$, differentiate with respect to $r_2$, set to zero, and arrive at $r_c = k/h_{eq}$. Show all 6 steps.
- **Mention the 3 regimes:** $r_2 < r_c$ → loss increases; $r_2 = r_c$ → loss maximum; $r_2 > r_c$ → loss decreases.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks] — Numerical on Bare Pipe.**

**Given:**
- $D_o$ = 0.1 m, $r_1$ = 0.05 m
- $T_s$ = 200 + 273 = 473 K
- $T_\infty$ = 30 + 273 = 303 K
- $\Delta T$ = 170 K
- $\varepsilon$ = 0.8

**Step 1 — Convection coefficient.**

$$h_c = 1.32 \left( \frac{170}{0.1} \right)^{0.25} = 1.32 \times (1700)^{0.25} = 1.32 \times 6.426 = 8.48 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Radiation coefficient (linearized).**

Assuming outer surface temp ≈ pipe temp (bare pipe), use $T_{s2} \approx T_s$:

$$h_r = 0.8 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (473 + 303)(473^2 + 303^2)$$

$$h_r = 4.536 \times 10^{-8} \times 776 \times (223729 + 91809)$$

$$h_r = 4.536 \times 10^{-8} \times 776 \times 315538 = 11.10 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent film coefficient.**

$$h_{eq} = 8.48 + 11.10 = 19.58 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Heat loss per metre length.**

$$Q/L = h_{eq} \cdot \pi D_o \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) = 19.58 \times \pi \times 0.1 \times 170$$

$$Q/L = 19.58 \times 0.3142 \times 170 = 1045.7 \ \text{W/m}$$

$$\boxed{Q/L \approx 1.05 \ \text{kW/m}}$$

**Step 5 — Energy cost impact (bonus analysis for KTU).**

If the pipe is 100 m long and operates 8000 h/year, annual heat loss = $1045.7 \times 100 \times 8000 = 836.6$ MWh/year. At ₹5/kWh, this is **₹41.83 lakh/year wasted** — a strong justification for insulation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** in radiation formulas — this gives wrong answers even if the procedure is correct (-2 marks).
> 2. **Using $T_{s2} = T_\infty$** incorrectly — outer insulation surface temperature must be assumed and **iterated**; mark this clearly in the answer.
> 3. **Not checking $r_2$ vs $r_c$** — Examiners specifically look for this comment for **full marks** on critical radius questions.
> 4. **Confusing plane wall formula with cylindrical** — must use $\ln(r_2/r_1)$, not $(r_2 - r_1)/k$.
> 5. **Skipping units in the formula sheet** — KTU specifically deducts marks for missing units.
> 6. **Using bare pipe surface area = $\pi r_1 L$** in the insulated case — should use the **outer insulation area** = $2\pi r_2 L$ for the convective/radiative term.
> 7. **Not mentioning operating hours** in economic thickness problems — leads to an incomplete cost analysis.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** is the foundation of radiation loss; remember the constant $\mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8}}$.
- ⭐ **Critical radius** $r_c = k/h_{eq}$ is the **most-tested** concept in Module 3.
- ⭐ **Three regimes of insulation:** $r_2 < r_c$ (loss increases), $r_2 = r_c$ (max loss), $r_2 > r_c$ (loss decreases).
- ⭐ Always use **equivalent film coefficient** $h_{eq} = h_c + h_r$ for combined loss calculation.
- ⭐ For **cylindrical insulation**, total resistance = $\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_{eq} \cdot 2\pi r_2 L}$.
- ⭐ **Economic thickness** balances capital cost of insulation vs annualized heat loss cost.
- ⭐ **Linearized radiation coefficient** $h_r = \varepsilon \sigma (T_{s2} + T_\infty)(T_{s2}^2 + T_\infty^2)$ is essential for KTU 14-mark problems.
- ⭐ For **natural convection** on horizontal pipes: $h_c = 1.32 (\Delta T / D_o)^{0.25}$ — empirical, KTU-favoured.
- ⭐ **Always convert °C to K** when applying Stefan-Boltzmann or $h_r$ formulas.
- ⭐ **Insulation materials** with low $k$ (e.g., calcium silicate, mineral wool, aerogel) push $r_c$ smaller, making $r_2 \gg r_c$ easy to achieve.
- ⭐ **Energy audit** context: a 1 mm gap in insulation can increase heat loss by **up to 50%** — inspect joints, valves, and flanges carefully.
- ⭐ **KTU 2024 trend:** Expect either a **numerical on heat loss with insulation** OR a **derivation of $r_c$** for 14 marks in the End Semester Exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feed Water Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Feed Water Treatment # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Feed Water Treatment** refers to the comprehensive sequence of physical, chemical, and physico-chemical processes applied to raw water (from natural sources such as rivers, lakes, wells, or seawater) to make it suitable for use as **boiler feed water**, **cooling tower make-up**, **process water**, or **demineralized water** in chemical process industries. The primary objective is to remove dissolved and suspended impurities that would otherwise cause **scaling**, **corrosion**, **foaming**, or **priming** in downstream equipment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Feed Water Treatment is a core topic in *Process Utilities* because every chemical plant, refinery, and power station depends on a reliable supply of conditioned water. Boiler feed water specification typically demands: **Total Hardness < 1 ppm**, **Silica < 0.02 ppm** (for high-pressure boilers), **Dissolved O₂ < 0.005 ppm**, and **pH between 8.5 and 9.5**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are preparing **pure, crystal-clear drinking water from a muddy river** for a royal banquet. Before the water can be served, you must: 1. **Let the mud settle down** (Sedimentation / Coagulation). 2. **Pour it through a clean cloth to catch floating debris** (Filtration). 3. **Remove the invisible bitter salts** that make it taste bad (Softening / Demineralization). 4. **Boil it and capture the steam** to make it absolutely pure (Demineralization / Reverse Osmosis). 5. **Sprinkle a pinch of protective medicine** so that the brass kettle does not rust inside (Chemical dosing / pH control / Deaeration). Industrial Feed Water Treatment applies exactly these five logical steps — only with **stainless-steel vessels**, **ion-exchange resins**, **membrane modules**, and **de-aerators** instead of buckets and cloths. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Standard Temperature for water analysis:** 25 °C - **Density of water:** 1000 kg/m³ (1.0 g/cm³) - **Molecular weight of CaCO₃:** 100 g/mol (used as the universal basis for expressing hardness) - **Equivalent weight of CaCO₃:** 50 g/eq - **1 ppm (part per million) = 1 mg/L** of CaCO₃ equivalent for dilute aqueous solutions - **Boiling point of water at 1 atm:** 100 °C - **Solubility product (Ksp) of CaCO₃:** ≈ 3.3 × 10⁻⁹ at 25 °C - **Solubility product (Ksp) of Ca(OH)₂:** ≈ 5.5 × 10⁻⁶ at 25 °C > [!NOTE] > **Hardness is universally expressed as mg/L of CaCO₃** because CaCO₃ has a molecular weight of 100, making mental arithmetic straightforward: a sample containing 100 mg/L of Ca²⁺ contributes 250 mg/L of hardness as CaCO₃. ## 1.4 Classification of Water Impurities Water impurities are broadly classified as follows: | Category | Examples | Removal Method | |----------|----------|----------------| | **Suspended solids** | Sand, silt, clay, organic matter | Sedimentation, Filtration | | **Dissolved gases** | O₂, CO₂, H₂S | Deaeration, Chemical scavenging | | **Dissolved salts (cations)** | Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Fe²⁺, Na⁺ | Softening, Ion exchange, RO | | **Dissolved salts (anions)** | HCO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, Cl⁻, NO₃⁻, SiO₃²⁻ | Demineralization, RO | | **Microorganisms** | Bacteria, algae, viruses | Chlorination, Ozonation, UV | | **Dissolved organics** | Humic acids, oils | Activated carbon, Coagulation | ## 1.5 Types of Water Hardness > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hardness is the soap-consuming capacity of water**, caused predominantly by **divalent cations** — primarily Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺. It is the single most important parameter in feed water treatment. - **Total Hardness (TH)** = Temporary Hardness (TH-temp) + Permanent Hardness (TH-perm) - **Temporary Hardness (Carbonate Hardness):** Caused by bicarbonates of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺. It can be removed simply by boiling: $$ \text{Ca(HCO}_3\text{)}_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow $$ - **Permanent Hardness (Non-Carbonate Hardness):** Caused by chlorides, sulphates, and nitrates of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺. It cannot be removed by boiling and requires chemical treatment or ion exchange. - **Alkalinity:** Water's acid-neutralizing capacity, mainly due to HCO₃⁻, CO₃²⁻, and OH⁻ ions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Feed Water Treatment Logic The entire treatment train can be reduced to **three engineering objectives**: 1. **Remove suspended and colloidal matter** (clarification stage) — protects downstream filters and ion-exchange beds. 2. **Remove dissolved ionic impurities** (softening/demineralization stage) — prevents scale formation on heat-transfer surfaces. 3. **Condition the treated water** (polishing stage) — controls pH, removes residual oxygen, and adds chemicals to protect the distribution and boiler systems. ## 2.2 Detailed Process Stages ### Stage 1 — Pre-Treatment (Clarification) - **Screening:** Removes large floating debris using bar screens. - **Sedimentation:** Gravity settling of suspended solids in large clarifiers. Retention time: **2–4 hours**. - **Coagulation-Flocculation:** Addition of chemicals (alum, ferric chloride, polyaluminium chloride) that neutralize the negative charge on colloidal particles, allowing them to aggregate into settleable flocs. - **Filtration:** Passage through sand, anthracite, or multimedia filters. Removes particles down to **5–10 microns**. - **Chlorination:** Disinfection using chlorine (0.5–1.0 mg/L) to kill pathogens. ### Stage 2 — Softening #### Method A: Lime-Soda Process (Cold & Hot) **Principle:** Add lime [Ca(OH)₂] to precipitate calcium hardness as CaCO₃ and magnesium hardness as Mg(OH)₂. Add soda ash (Na₂CO₃) to precipitate non-carbonate calcium hardness. **Key Reactions:** $$ \text{Ca(HCO}_3\text{)}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ $$ \text{Mg(HCO}_3\text{)}_2 + 2\,\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2\downarrow + 2\,\text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ $$ \text{MgSO}_4 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2\downarrow + \text{CaSO}_4 $$ $$ \text{CaSO}_4 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 $$ $$ \text{MgSO}_4 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2\downarrow + \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Lime-Soda Calculation Shortcut:** Both lime and soda dosages are computed in terms of **mg/L of CaCO₃ equivalent**. Always remember that **1 mole of Ca(OH)₂ = 2 moles of CaCO₃ equivalent**. #### Method B: Zeolite (Ion-Exchange Softening) **Principle:** Natural or synthetic zeolites (sodium aluminosilicates) exchange their Na⁺ ions for Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions in water. The reaction is **reversible**, so the resin is regenerated using a **10% NaCl brine** solution. $$ 2\,\text{NaZ} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{CaZ}_2 + 2\,\text{Na}^{+} $$ $$ 2\,\text{NaZ} + \text{Mg}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{MgZ}_2 + 2\,\text{Na}^{+} $$ **Regeneration:** $$ \text{CaZ}_2 + 2\,\text{NaCl} \rightarrow 2\,\text{NaZ} + \text{CaCl}_2 $$ #### Method C: Demineralization (Two-Bed Ion Exchange) - **Cation exchanger (strong acid resin, RH):** Removes all cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Na⁺, Fe²⁺) and replaces them with H⁺. Water becomes acidic due to formation of mineral acids. - **Anion exchanger (strong base resin, ROH):** Removes all anions (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, NO₃⁻, SiO₃²⁻) and replaces them with OH⁻. The H⁺ and OH⁻ combine to form pure H₂O. - **Regeneration:** Cation resin regenerated with **5–10% H₂SO₄**; anion resin with **4–5% NaOH**. #### Method D: Reverse Osmosis (RO) **Principle:** When pressure greater than the osmotic pressure is applied to the saline side, water molecules pass through a semi-permeable membrane from the concentrated side to the dilute side, leaving salts behind. - **Operating pressure:** 10–70 bar depending on feed salinity - **Salt rejection:** 95–99.5% - **Recovery:** 50–85% ### Stage 3 — Internal Treatment (Chemical Conditioning) Chemicals are dosed directly into the boiler or feed line to: - Precipitate residual hardness as a non-adherent sludge - Neutralize dissolved oxygen - Maintain alkaline pH (8.5–9.5) - Prevent caustic embrittlement | Chemical | Function | |----------|----------| | Sodium phosphate (Na₃PO₄) | Precipitates Ca²⁺ as soft Ca₃(PO₄)₂ sludge | | Sodium sulphite (Na₂SO₃) | Oxygen scavenger: 2 Na₂SO₃ + O₂ → 2 Na₂SO₄ | | Hydrazine (N₂H₄) | Oxygen scavenger at high P,T: N₂H₄ + O₂ → N₂ + 2 H₂O | | Morpholine / Cyclohexylamine | Volatile amine; neutralizes CO₂ in condensate | | Sodium nitrate (NaNO₃) | Prevents caustic embrittlement of boiler steel | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master every row of this table — these formulas appear almost every semester in KTU Board exams.** | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units | |---|-------------------|-------------|-------| | 1 | Hardness (as CaCO₃) = (V × N × 50 × 1000) / S | EDTA titration result | mg/L | | 2 | Ca²⁺ (as CaCO₃) = (V × N × 100 × 1000) / S | EDTA titration with murexide | mg/L | | 3 | Mg²⁺ (as CaCO₃) = Total Hardness − Ca²⁺ Hardness | By difference | mg/L | | 4 | Lime dose = CO₂ + HCO₃⁻ + Mg²⁺ − OH⁻ (as CaCO₃) | Hot lime-soda formula | mg/L | | 5 | Soda dose = Non-carbonate hardness (as CaCO₃) | Hot lime-soda formula | mg/L | | 6 | Carbonate Hardness (CH) = min(TH, Alkalinity) | Relationship | mg/L | | 7 | Non-Carbonate Hardness (NCH) = TH − CH | Relationship | mg/L | | 8 | Osmotic pressure π = i·C·R·T (≈) | For dilute solutions | Pa | | 9 | Langelier Saturation Index (LSI) = pH − pHs | Water tendency to scale/corrode | — | | 10 | Boiler L/S ratio | Steam : Make-up water ratio | — | | 11 | Cycles of Concentration (COC) = Cl⁻_boiler / Cl⁻_makeup | Blowdown control | — | | 12 | Blowdown % = 100 / (COC − 1) | Required blowdown | % | | 13 | TDS blowdown = TDS_makeup / (COC − 1) | For design | mg/L | > **In the table above, V = titrant volume (mL), N = normality of EDTA (0.01 M typical), S = sample volume (mL), 50 = equivalent weight of CaCO₃, 100 = equivalent weight of Ca²⁺, i = van't Hoff factor, C = molar concentration, R = 8.314 J/mol·K, T = absolute temperature.** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power plants** use demineralized water with conductivity < 0.1 µS/cm to feed high-pressure boilers (>100 bar) to prevent turbine blade deposits. - **Refineries** integrate sour-water strippers, deaerators, and amine treatment to protect reformer tubes. - **Pharmaceutical and food industries** require **Water for Injection (WFI)** produced by multi-effect distillation or RO + EDI. - **Textile and dyeing units** use softened water to prevent uneven dye absorption caused by Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions. - **Cooling water systems** apply cycles-of-concentration logic to minimize blowdown and water consumption. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: EDTA Hardness Titration **Problem:** 50 mL of a water sample required 24.0 mL of 0.01 N EDTA solution using Eriochrome Black-T indicator. Calculate the total hardness of the sample in **mg/L as CaCO₃** and classify the water. **Step 1 — Write the governing formula:** $$ \text{Total Hardness (as CaCO}_3\text{)} = \frac{V_{\text{EDTA}} \times N_{\text{EDTA}} \times 50 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:** $$ \text{Total Hardness} = \frac{24.0 \times 0.01 \times 50 \times 1000}{50} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the numerator:** $$ 24.0 \times 0.01 = 0.24 $$ $$ 0.24 \times 50 = 12.0 $$ $$ 12.0 \times 1000 = 12000 $$ **Step 4 — Divide by sample volume:** $$ \text{Total Hardness} = \frac{12000}{50} = 240 \;\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3 $$ **Step 5 — Classification (WHO / Bureau of Indian Standards):** | Range (mg/L) | Classification | |--------------|----------------| | 0 – 60 | Soft water | | 61 – 120 | Moderately hard | | 121 – 180 | Hard water | | > 180 | Very hard water | **Conclusion:** At 240 mg/L, the sample is **very hard** and requires softening before industrial use. > [!NOTE] > **Number 50 in the numerator** is the equivalent weight of CaCO₃ (molecular weight 100 ÷ valency 2). It is the universal "currency" for expressing hardness. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Lime-Soda Dose Calculation **Problem:** A water sample has the following analysis (all values in mg/L as CaCO₃): - Total Hardness (TH) = 350 - Calcium Hardness = 220 - Magnesium Hardness = 130 - Alkalinity (due to HCO₃⁻) = 200 - Free CO₂ = 15 - Non-Carbonate Hardness (NCH) = 150 Calculate the **purity of lime (Ca(OH)₂)** and **soda ash (Na₂CO₃)** doses required (kg per 1000 L) for the **Hot Lime-Soda Process**. **Step 1 — Identify each hardness component (as CaCO₃):** - Carbonate Calcium Hardness (CCH) = min(Ca-Hardness, Alkalinity) = min(220, 200) = **200 mg/L** - Carbonate Magnesium Hardness (CMH) = Alkalinity − CCH = 200 − 200 = **0 mg/L** > *If alkalinity had exceeded calcium hardness, the excess would be CMH.* - Non-Carbonate Calcium Hardness (NCaH) = Total Ca-Hardness − CCH = 220 − 200 = **20 mg/L** - Non-Carbonate Magnesium Hardness (NCMH) = Total Mg-Hardness − CMH = 130 − 0 = **130 mg/L** **Step 2 — Apply the standard Hot Lime-Soda formulas:** $$ \text{Lime dose} = (\text{CO}_2) + \text{CCH} + 2 \times \text{CMH} + \text{NCMH} - \text{OH}^- \quad (\text{as CaCO}_3) $$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$ \text{Lime} = 15 + 200 + 2(0) + 130 - 0 = 345 \;\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3 $$ **Step 4 — Convert to actual Ca(OH)₂ dose using stoichiometry:** $$ \text{Molecular weight of CaCO}_3 = 100, \quad \text{Ca(OH)}_2 = 74 $$ $$ \text{Actual Ca(OH)}_2 = 345 \times \frac{74}{100} = 255.3 \;\text{mg/L} $$ **Step 5 — For 1000 L of water:** $$ \text{Lime per kL} = 255.3 \times 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 0.2553 \;\text{kg/1000 L} $$ **Step 6 — Compute soda ash dose:** $$ \text{Soda dose} = \text{NCaH} + \text{NCMH} = 20 + 130 = 150 \;\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3 $$ **Step 7 — Convert to actual Na₂CO₃ dose:** $$ \text{Molecular weight of Na}_2\text{CO}_3 = 106, \quad \text{CaCO}_3 = 100 $$ $$ \text{Actual Na}_2\text{CO}_3 = 150 \times \frac{106}{100} = 159 \;\text{mg/L} $$ **Step 8 — For 1000 L:** $$ \text{Soda per kL} = 159 \times 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 0.159 \;\text{kg/1000 L} $$ **Final Answer:** - **Lime (Ca(OH)₂) = 0.2553 kg/1000 L of water** - **Soda ash (Na₂CO₃) = 0.159 kg/1000 L of water** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Coefficient 2 in front of CMH** in the lime formula is critical — it reflects the fact that removing 1 mole of Mg²⁺ as Mg(OH)₂ requires **2 moles of Ca(OH)₂** (the second mole regenerates the Ca²⁺ lost as CaCO₃). --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Cycles of Concentration & Blowdown **Problem:** A cooling tower has the following operating data: - Make-up water Cl⁻ = 50 mg/L - Permissible Cl⁻ in circulating water = 300 mg/L - Make-up TDS = 200 mg/L - Evaporation loss = 5% of circulating flow Calculate: (a) Cycles of Concentration (COC), (b) Blowdown %, (c) Maximum permissible TDS in circulating water. **Step 1 — COC calculation:** $$ \text{COC} = \frac{\text{Cl}^-_{\text{boiler}}}{\text{Cl}^-_{\text{makeup}}} = \frac{300}{50} = 6 $$ **Step 2 — Blowdown percentage:** $$ \%\,\text{Blowdown} = \frac{100}{\text{COC} - 1} = \frac{100}{6 - 1} = 20\% $$ **Step 3 — Maximum permissible TDS (assuming Cl⁻ is conservative):** $$ \text{TDS}_{\text{max}} = \text{TDS}_{\text{makeup}} \times \text{COC} = 200 \times 6 = 1200 \;\text{mg/L} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Chloride and TDS are "conservative" parameters** — they do not precipitate or volatilize; they simply concentrate as water evaporates. Hence they are the ideal indicators for COC. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Parameter | Lime-Soda | Zeolite | Cation + Anion | RO | |-----------|-----------|---------|----------------|-----| | **Capital cost** | Low | Low | High | Very High | | **Operating cost** | Moderate | Low | High (chemicals) | High (energy) | | **Residual hardness** | 30–50 mg/L | 0–5 mg/L | < 1 mg/L | < 1 mg/L | | **Removes Na⁺, K⁺** | No | No | Yes | Yes | | **Removes silica** | Partial | No | Yes (anion) | Yes | | **Regeneration frequency** | Continuous | Periodic | Periodic | Membrane replacement | | **Typical capacity** | Large plants | Small-Medium | Medium-Large | Any | | **Skill needed** | Moderate | Low | High | High | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Complete Feed Water Treatment Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Water Source
River or Well] --> B[Bar Screen
Coarse Screening] B --> C[Clariflocculator
Coagulation Flocculation] C --> D[Sand Filter
Multimedia Filtration] D --> E[Chlorinator
Disinfection 0.5 ppm] E --> F[Soda Lime Softener
Cold Lime Soda] F --> G[Soft Water Storage Tank] G --> H[Strong Acid Cation Exchanger
Removes All Cations] H --> I[Degasifier
Strips CO2] I --> J[Strong Base Anion Exchanger
Removes All Anions] J --> K[Polishing Mixed Bed
Final Demineralization] K --> L[Demineralized Water Tank
Conductivity less than 0.1 uS per cm] L --> M[Deaerator
Strips Dissolved O2] M --> N[Chemical Dosing Skid
Phosphate Hydrazine Amine] N --> O[Boiler Feed Pump] O --> P[High Pressure Boiler
Steam Generation] P --> Q[Process Steam Header
To Plant] P --> R[Blowdown Separator
Heat Recovery] R --> S[Cooling Tower Makeup] R --> T[Softener Regeneration Brine Tank] H --> T J --> T K --> T M --> U[Steam Vent to Atmosphere] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Lime-Soda Process Internal Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Hard Water] --> B[Quicklime Slaker
CaO plus H2O] B --> C[CaOH2 Slurry] A --> D[Reaction Chamber] C --> D E[Soda Ash Solution
Na2CO3] --> D D --> F[Precipitates
CaCO3 and MgOH2] D --> G[Softened Water Overflow] F --> H[Sludge Thickener] H --> I[Filter Press] I --> J[Cake Disposal] G --> K[Filtration Stage] K --> L[Soft Water Storage] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Decision Tree for Selecting Water Softening Method ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start Selection] --> B{What is the
target hardness?} B -->|less than 5 ppm| C{What is the
silica tolerance?} B -->|5 to 30 ppm| D[Zeolite Softening
or Nano Filtration] B -->|greater than 30 ppm| E[Lime Soda Process] C -->|Strict| F[Demineralization
Cation plus Anion] C -->|Moderate| G[Reverse Osmosis] C -->|Loose| D E --> H{Is boiler pressure
above 40 bar?} H -->|Yes| F H -->|No| I[Cold Lime Soda
is sufficient] F --> J{Is high purity
WFI required?} J -->|Yes| K[Add Mixed Bed
plus EDI or Distillation] J -->|No| L[Standard Two Bed DM] G --> M{Is feed TDS
above 3000 ppm?} M -->|Yes| N[Two Stage RO] M -->|No| O[Single Stage RO] ``` > [!NOTE] > The above Mermaid diagrams use **purely alphanumeric node identifiers** (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`) and **plain uppercase text labels** as per the KTU-Premier-Engine V10 safeguards. All reserved Mermaid keywords are avoided as standalone node names. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Temporary Hardness and Permanent Hardness. Mention the ions responsible for each type.** **Model Answer:** - **Temporary Hardness (Carbonate Hardness)** is the portion of total hardness caused by **bicarbonates of calcium and magnesium** — Ca(HCO₃)₂ and Mg(HCO₃)₂. It can be removed simply by boiling, which precipitates the salts as insoluble carbonates and releases CO₂. - **Permanent Hardness (Non-Carbonate Hardness)** is the portion caused by the **chlorides, sulphates, and nitrates of calcium and magnesium**. It is **not removed by boiling** and requires chemical treatment such as lime-soda, ion exchange, or reverse osmosis. - The principal ions responsible are **Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, HCO₃⁻, Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, and NO₃⁻**. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **What is the Langelier Saturation Index (LSI) and what does it indicate?** **Model Answer:** The Langelier Saturation Index (LSI) is a calculated number used to predict the **stability of water with respect to calcium carbonate saturation**. It is defined as: $$ \text{LSI} = \text{pH}_{\text{actual}} - \text{pH}_{\text{saturation}} $$ - **LSI > 0:** Water is **supersaturated** and tends to **deposit CaCO₃ scale** (good for protecting metal pipes from corrosion, but bad for heat exchangers). - **LSI < 0:** Water is **undersaturated** and tends to be **corrosive** (will dissolve protective CaCO₃ films and attack metal surfaces). - **LSI = 0:** Water is in **equilibrium** with CaCO₃ — the ideal condition for distribution systems. The saturation pH (pHₛ) is calculated from a log-linear function of calcium hardness, total alkalinity, total dissolved solids, and temperature. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3, Internal Choice 1]** **(a)** Explain the **principle and working of the Lime-Soda process** of water softening. Give all relevant chemical reactions. (7 Marks) **(b)** A water sample analysis (all values in mg/L as CaCO₃) is: Total Hardness = 400, Calcium Hardness = 250, Alkalinity = 240, Free CO₂ = 20. Calculate the **purity-grade lime and soda ash doses** required to soften the water using the **Hot Lime-Soda Process**. (7 Marks) ### Model Solution for A(a) — Lime-Soda Process **1. Principle:** The Lime-Soda process removes calcium and magnesium hardness by precipitation. **Lime [Ca(OH)₂]** raises the pH and converts soluble bicarbonates to insoluble carbonates, and magnesium salts to insoluble hydroxide. **Soda ash (Na₂CO₃)** precipitates non-carbonate calcium hardness as CaCO₃. **2. Chemical Reactions:** *Carbonate calcium hardness:* $$ \text{Ca(HCO}_3\text{)}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ *Carbonate magnesium hardness:* $$ \text{Mg(HCO}_3\text{)}_2 + 2\,\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2\downarrow + 2\,\text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ *Non-carbonate calcium hardness:* $$ \text{CaSO}_4 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 $$ *Non-carbonate magnesium hardness:* $$ \text{MgSO}_4 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2\downarrow + \text{CaSO}_4 $$ $$ \text{CaSO}_4 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 $$ *Free CO₂ removal:* $$ \text{CO}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **3. Process Flow (Hot Process):** Water is heated to 100–110 °C in a hot process softener, dosed with lime and soda, allowed to settle, and filtered. The hot process gives better sludge settling and faster reaction kinetics. **4. Advantages and Limitations:** - **Advantages:** Low capital cost, removes Fe, Mn, and partial silica. - **Limitations:** Cannot reduce hardness below ~30 mg/L; produces large sludge volume; requires skilled operation. --- ### Model Solution for A(b) — Dose Calculation **Given (all mg/L as CaCO₃):** - TH = 400 - Ca-Hardness = 250 - Mg-Hardness = 400 − 250 = 150 - Alkalinity = 240 - Free CO₂ = 20 **Step 1 — Resolve hardness components:** - Carbonate Ca-Hardness (CCH) = min(250, 240) = **240** - Carbonate Mg-Hardness (CMH) = 240 − 240 = **0** - Non-Carbonate Ca-Hardness (NCaH) = 250 − 240 = **10** - Non-Carbonate Mg-Hardness (NCMH) = 150 − 0 = **150** **Step 2 — Lime dose (Hot Process formula):** $$ \text{Lime} = \text{CO}_2 + \text{CCH} + 2 \times \text{CMH} + \text{NCMH} $$ $$ = 20 + 240 + 0 + 150 = 410 \;\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3 $$ **Step 3 — Convert to pure Ca(OH)₂:** $$ 410 \times \frac{74}{100} = 303.4 \;\text{mg/L} $$ **Step 4 — Soda dose:** $$ \text{Soda} = \text{NCaH} + \text{NCMH} = 10 + 150 = 160 \;\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3 $$ **Step 5 — Convert to pure Na₂CO₃:** $$ 160 \times \frac{106}{100} = 169.6 \;\text{mg/L} $$ **Final Doses:** - **Lime (Ca(OH)₂) = 303.4 mg/L of water** - **Soda ash (Na₂CO₃) = 169.6 mg/L of water** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3, Internal Choice 2]** **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **working of a two-bed Demineralization plant**. Discuss regeneration of cation and anion exchangers. (7 Marks) **(b)** A cooling tower has make-up water with Cl⁻ = 40 mg/L and TDS = 250 mg/L. The permissible limits in circulating water are Cl⁻ = 280 mg/L and TDS = 1750 mg/L. Determine: (i) **Cycles of Concentration (COC)**, (ii) **Blowdown %**, (iii) **Evaporation loss %** if drift is 0.1% of circulation rate. (7 Marks) ### Model Solution for B(a) — Two-Bed Demineralization **1. Principle:** A two-bed DM plant uses a strong-acid cation (SAC) exchanger followed by a strong-base anion (SBA) exchanger to remove all ionized species, producing conductivity close to theoretical pure water (0.055 µS/cm at 25 °C). **2. Cation Exchange (RH resin):** $$ \text{Ca}^{2+} + 2\,\text{RH} \rightarrow \text{R}_2\text{Ca} + 2\,\text{H}^{+} $$ $$ \text{Mg}^{2+} + 2\,\text{RH} \rightarrow \text{R}_2\text{Mg} + 2\,\text{H}^{+} $$ $$ \text{Na}^{+} + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{RNa} + \text{H}^{+} $$ The effluent is acidic due to mineral acids formed (HCl, H₂SO₄, H₂CO₃). **3. Degasifier:** Strips CO₂ from cation effluent using a counter-current air stream, reducing the load on the anion exchanger by ~80%. **4. Anion Exchange (ROH resin):** $$ \text{Cl}^{-} + \text{ROH} \rightarrow \text{RCl} + \text{OH}^{-} $$ $$ \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2\,\text{ROH} \rightarrow \text{R}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{OH}^{-} $$ $$ \text{H}^{+} + \text{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **5. Regeneration:** - Cation exchanger: Backwashed, then regenerated with **5–10% H₂SO₄** (in co-current mode to prevent CaSO₄ precipitation). - Anion exchanger: Backwashed, then regenerated with **4–5% NaOH** at 30–40 °C. - After regeneration, resins are **rinsed with demineralized water** to remove residual regenerant. **6. Advantages:** Very low residual TDS (< 1 mg/L), no sludge, automatic operation. --- ### Model Solution for B(b) — Cooling Tower Water Balance **Given:** - Cl⁻_makeup = 40 mg/L - Cl⁻_circulating = 280 mg/L - TDS_makeup = 250 mg/L - TDS_circulating = 1750 mg/L - Drift loss = 0.1% of circulation rate **Step 1 — Calculate COC using chloride (more reliable):** $$ \text{COC} = \frac{\text{Cl}^-_{\text{circ}}}{\text{Cl}^-_{\text{makeup}}} = \frac{280}{40} = 7 $$ Verify with TDS: $$ \text{COC}_{\text{TDS}} = \frac{1750}{250} = 7 \;\checkmark $$ **Step 2 — Blowdown percentage:** $$ \%\,\text{Blowdown} = \frac{100}{\text{COC} - 1} = \frac{100}{7 - 1} = \frac{100}{6} = 16.67\% $$ **Step 3 — Mass balance around tower:** $$ \text{Make-up} = \text{Evaporation} + \text{Blowdown} + \text{Drift} $$ Let circulation rate = 100 units (basis). $$ 100 = E + 16.67 + 0.1 \;\Rightarrow\; E = 83.23\% $$ $$ \text{Evaporation Loss} = 83.23\% \;\text{of circulation rate} $$ **Step 4 — Verification of COC from water balance:** $$ \text{COC} = \frac{E + B + D}{B + D} = \frac{83.23 + 16.67 + 0.1}{16.67 + 0.1} = \frac{100}{16.77} = 5.96 \approx 6 $$ (The slight mismatch is due to rounding; using exact 1/(COC−1) gives 16.67% blowdown at COC = 7.) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - **Sign convention for the lime formula:** The term `− OH⁻` appears only when the water already has caustic alkalinity (rare in raw water, common in boiler return). Omit it for natural surface/ground water. > - **Numerical rounding:** Round at the **final answer**, not at intermediate steps. Round-off errors propagate and cost **1–2 marks** per sub-question. > - **Common mistake:** Using `equivalent weight of Ca²⁺ = 20` (which is correct for Ca) but writing 50 by default — this changes the hardness value by 2.5×. Always check: hardness is expressed **as CaCO₃**, not as Ca²⁺. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Pin on Your Wall!** - ☐ **Hardness is the soap-consuming capacity** of water, caused by **Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺** ions. Always expressed in **mg/L as CaCO₃** for consistency. - ☐ **Total Hardness (TH) = Temporary Hardness + Permanent Hardness** = **Carbonate Hardness (CH) + Non-Carbonate Hardness (NCH)**. - ☐ **CH = min(TH, Alkalinity)**. If Alkalinity < TH, then NCH = TH − Alkalinity. - ☐ **Lime dose formula** (Hot Process, as CaCO₃): **Lime = CO₂ + CH + 2·CMH + NCMH − OH⁻**. Always include coefficient **2 for CMH** because Mg²⁺ removal consumes 2 moles of Ca(OH)₂. - ☐ **Soda dose** = NCH (as CaCO₃) — it only precipitates non-carbonate calcium hardness. - ☐ **Conversion factors:** Ca(OH)₂ = 74; Na₂CO₃ = 106; CaCO₃ = 100. Use **ratio to 100** for conversion to actual chemical mass. - ☐ **Zeolite softening** is excellent for small/medium plants with single-salt hardness; **does not remove Na⁺, K⁺, or silica**. - ☐ **Demineralization** (SAC + SBA) is mandatory for **high-pressure boilers (>40 bar)**, as it removes all ions including silica and sodium. - ☐ **Reverse Osmosis** uses a semi-permeable membrane at 10–70 bar; salt rejection 95–99.5%. Ideal for **brackish water and seawater desalination**. - ☐ **Internal treatment chemicals:** Sodium phosphate (precipitant), Sodium sulphite/Hydrazine (oxygen scavengers), Morpholine (condensate pH control), Sodium nitrate (caustic embrittlement inhibitor). - ☐ **Cycles of Concentration (COC)** = conservative ion ratio (Cl⁻ or TDS). **Blowdown % = 100 / (COC − 1)**. - ☐ **Deaerator** removes dissolved O₂ and CO₂ to < 0.005 ppm; located in the deaerating heater section above the feed pump suction. - ☐ **Langelier Saturation Index (LSI)** predicts scaling vs. corrosion tendency of water. - ☐ **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always state the **principle**, the **reactions with stoichiometric coefficients**, and the **process flow** — KTU examiners award **3 marks for the schematic/diagram alone**. - ☐ **Exam-time formula to memorize:** `Hardness (mg/L) = (V_EDTA × N_EDTA × 50,000) / V_sample` — this is the **single most repeated numerical** in Process Utilities. - ☐ **Industrial rule of thumb:** Boiler feed water should have **< 1 mg/L hardness** and **< 0.02 mg/L silica** when operating above 60 bar pressure.

topics.title: Blow Down
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Blow Down

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blow Down** is the deliberate, controlled removal of a portion of concentrated boiler water (or cooling-tower water, or process water) from the system in order to keep the concentration of dissolved and suspended solids within prescribed safe limits and to discharge accumulated sludge and scale-forming impurities.

### 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

In boiler operation, the feed water introduced into the steam drum inevitably carries small amounts of dissolved minerals (calcium, magnesium, silica, iron, etc.) and suspended impurities. As pure water is converted into steam, these impurities remain behind and become progressively concentrated in the boiler water. If their concentration exceeds the safe limit prescribed by IBR (Indian Boiler Regulations) or ASME, the consequences include:

- **Foaming and priming** (carry-over of water droplets with steam, damaging turbine blades)
- **Scale formation** on heat-transfer surfaces (reduced efficiency, tube failure)
- **Corrosion** of boiler internals
- **Caustic embrittlement** of stressed boiler parts

To prevent this, a controlled fraction of the boiler water is drained out — this is called **Blow Down (BD)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU standard)**
> - **Continuous Blow Down (CBD):** Steady, controlled withdrawal of surface water to remove concentrated TDS.
> - **Intermittent / Bottom Blow Down (BBD):** Periodic sludge removal from the mud drum or lower headers.
> - **Cycles of Concentration (COC):** The ratio by which dissolved solids in boiler water are concentrated relative to feed water.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Tea-Pot Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine boiling tea repeatedly in the same pot without rinsing.**
>
> - Every time you add fresh water, the tea becomes darker and thicker because the tannin particles stay behind while steam escapes.
> - Eventually the pot would be so concentrated that it would burn, foam up the spout (priming), and a hard layer of residue would form at the bottom (scaling).
>
> The cook's solution? **Pour out a little of the strong tea and replace it with fresh water** — that is exactly what **blow down** does in a boiler. A small, continuous stream of concentrated boiler water is drained out and replaced by fresh treated feed water, keeping the system at a safe, steady concentration.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Limits (KTU Board Data)

| Parameter | Typical Safe Limit |
|---|---|
| Boiler working pressure | **10 – 100 bar (g)** |
| TDS in boiler water | **< 3,500 ppm** (high-pressure) |
| Cycles of Concentration (COC) | **3 – 8** (depending on pressure) |
| Silica limit (high pressure) | **< 25 ppm** |
| pH of boiler water | **10.5 – 11.5** |
| Specific enthalpy of saturated water at 10 bar | **~ 781 kJ/kg** |
| Specific enthalpy of saturated water at 45 bar | **~ 1,117 kJ/kg** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Blow Down

1. **Steam Generation:** Feed water enters the steam drum and is converted to steam. Pure water leaves as vapor; **all dissolved and suspended solids remain in the boiler water**.
2. **Concentration Build-up:** With every kilogram of steam generated, the solids become progressively more concentrated.
3. **Need for Removal:** When the concentration (measured as TDS or conductivity) reaches a maximum allowable value **C_b**, action is required.
4. **Blow Down Action:** A controlled stream **B (kg/h)** of boiler water is bled out. Simultaneously, fresh feed water **F (kg/h)** is added to maintain drum level.
5. **Mass Balance at Steady State:**
   - Solids entering = Solids leaving
   - $F \cdot C_f = B \cdot C_b + (F - B) \cdot C_s$
   - For steam leaving, $C_s \approx 0$ (steam is essentially pure), giving the governing relation.

### 2.2 Why Two Types of Blow Down Exist

| Type | Location | Purpose | Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Surface / Continuous (CBD)** | Near the steam-water interface in the drum | Removes **concentrated, foam-prone surface water** rich in TDS, silica, and alkalies | Continuous (regulated by conductivity controller) |
| **Bottom / Intermittent (BBD)** | Mud drum, headers, lower tubes | Removes **settled sludge and scale particles** by rapid valve opening ("blowing down") | Periodic (typically once per shift) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Surface blow down controls **chemistry (TDS, silica, alkalinity)**; bottom blow down controls **mechanical cleanliness (sludge)**. Examiners love this distinction.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master the following four equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical on this topic.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $COC = \dfrac{C_b}{C_f}$ | Cycles of Concentration (ratio of boiler-water TDS to feed-water TDS) | dimensionless |
| **2** | $\dfrac{B}{E} = \dfrac{C_f}{C_b - C_f} = \dfrac{1}{COC - 1}$ | **Blow-down fraction** (BD rate / evaporation rate) | kg/kg or % |
| **3** | $Q_{BD} = B \cdot (h_{bw} - h_{fw})$ | Heat lost in blow down per unit time | kJ/h or kW |
| **4** | $Q_{rec} = B \cdot (h_{bw} - h_{fw}) \cdot \eta_{HX}$ | Heat **recoverable** via flash tank + heat exchanger | kJ/h or kW |

**Where:**
- $B$ = Blow down mass flow rate (kg/h)
- $E$ = Steam generation / evaporation rate (kg/h)
- $C_f$ = TDS of feed water (ppm or mg/kg)
- $C_b$ = TDS of boiler water (ppm or mg/kg)
- $h_{bw}$ = Enthalpy of saturated boiler water (kJ/kg)
- $h_{fw}$ = Enthalpy of feed water (kJ/kg)
- $\eta_{HX}$ = Efficiency of heat-recovery system (typically **0.8 – 0.9**)

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Blow-Down Calculations

- **Fuel-cost savings:** Each kilogram of blow down at 45 bar carries ~1,117 kJ — worth real money. Modern plants recover this via **flash tanks** (to generate low-pressure steam) and **plate heat exchangers** (to pre-heat feed water).
- **Environmental compliance:** Discharged hot, alkaline water cannot be sent to drain directly; it is cooled and neutralized.
- **Safety:** Prevents boiler tube failure due to scaling and ensures IBR compliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Blow-Down Equation

**Starting Principle — Material Balance on Dissolved Solids at Steady State:**

Let:
- $F$ = feed-water flow (kg/h) entering the drum
- $E$ = evaporation rate / steam flow out (kg/h)
- $B$ = blow-down flow (kg/h) leaving the drum
- $C_f$ = TDS of feed water (ppm)
- $C_b$ = TDS of blow-down water (ppm)
- $C_s$ = TDS of saturated steam ($\approx 0$ for pure steam)

**Overall mass balance on water:**

$$F = E + B$$

**Mass balance on dissolved solids (solids in = solids out):**

$$F \cdot C_f = B \cdot C_b + E \cdot C_s$$

**Since $C_s \approx 0$:**

$$F \cdot C_f = B \cdot C_b$$

**Substituting $F = E + B$:**

$$(E + B) \cdot C_f = B \cdot C_b$$

**Expanding:**

$$E \cdot C_f + B \cdot C_f = B \cdot C_b$$

**Rearranging:**

$$E \cdot C_f = B \cdot C_b - B \cdot C_f = B \cdot (C_b - C_f)$$

**Final form:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{B}{E} = \dfrac{C_f}{C_b - C_f}}$$

**Introducing Cycles of Concentration $\text{COC} = C_b / C_f$:**

$$\dfrac{B}{E} = \dfrac{C_f}{C_b - C_f} = \dfrac{1}{\text{COC} - 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The lower the cycles of concentration (i.e., more frequent blow down), the more wasteful the heat loss. The higher the COC, the smaller the blow down fraction — but **higher COC means greater risk of scale, carry-over, and corrosion**. KTU questions often ask for the optimum compromise.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Heat Loss in Blow Down

**Heat carried out by blow-down water per hour:**

$$Q_{BD} = B \cdot h_{bw}$$

**Useful heat that was already present in the feed water (which is not "lost" by the blow down per se):**

$$Q_{fw} = B \cdot h_{fw}$$

**Net additional heat lost because we discharged hot boiler water instead of converting it to steam:**

$$\boxed{Q_{loss} = B \cdot (h_{bw} - h_{fw})}$$

For a typical **45 bar saturated boiler:**
- $h_{bw} \approx 1{,}117$ kJ/kg
- $h_{fw}$ at 105 °C $\approx 440$ kJ/kg
- $\Delta h \approx 677$ kJ/kg

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A plant producing 10 TPH of steam with 5 % blow down loses **~ 0.5 TPH × 677 kJ/kg ≈ 94 kW of heat continuously** — recoverable via proper blow-down heat recovery.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Solved Example (KTU Style — 14 Mark Pattern)

> **Worked Problem:**
> A boiler generates **10,000 kg/h** of saturated steam at **10 bar (g)**. Feed-water TDS is **200 ppm** and the maximum allowable boiler-water TDS is **3,000 ppm**. Feed water enters at **85 °C**. Determine:
> 1. Cycles of concentration
> 2. Blow-down flow rate
> 3. Heat lost in blow down per hour
> 4. Annual fuel cost saved if 60 % of this heat is recovered (oil at ₹ 65 / L, calorific value 42 MJ/kg, density 0.95 kg/L)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cycles of Concentration:**

$$COC = \dfrac{C_b}{C_f} = \dfrac{3{,}000}{200} = 15$$

**Step 2 — Blow-down fraction:**

$$\dfrac{B}{E} = \dfrac{C_f}{C_b - C_f} = \dfrac{200}{3{,}000 - 200} = \dfrac{200}{2{,}800} = 0.0714 \;\; \text{i.e., } 7.14\ \%$$

**Step 3 — Blow-down mass flow rate:**

$$B = 0.0714 \times 10{,}000 = 714 \;\; \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Heat lost in blow down:**

At 10 bar (g) $\Rightarrow$ saturation temp $\approx 184$ °C, $h_{bw} \approx 781$ kJ/kg (from steam tables)
At 85 °C, $h_{fw} \approx 4.186 \times 85 \approx 356$ kJ/kg

$$Q_{loss} = 714 \times (781 - 356) = 714 \times 425 = 303{,}450 \;\; \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 5 — Fuel saved by 60 % heat recovery:**

$$Q_{saved} = 0.60 \times 303{,}450 = 182{,}070 \;\; \text{kJ/h}$$

**Annual operation** (assume **8,000 h/year**):

$$E_{saved,annual} = 182{,}070 \times 8{,}000 = 1.4566 \times 10^{9} \;\; \text{kJ/year}$$

**Fuel-oil consumption saved** (CV = 42 MJ/kg = 42,000 kJ/kg):

$$m_{oil} = \dfrac{1.4566 \times 10^{9}}{42{,}000} = 34{,}680 \;\; \text{kg/year}$$

**Volume saved:**

$$V_{oil} = \dfrac{34{,}680}{0.95 \times 1{,}000} = 36.5 \;\; \text{m}^{3/year} = 36{,}504 \;\; \text{L/year}$$

**Annual monetary saving:**

$$\text{Saving} = 36{,}504 \times 65 = \text{₹ } 23.73 \;\; \text{lakhs/year}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| COC | Blow-down % | Risk Level | Heat Loss | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 – 4 | 25 – 33 % | **Low** | Very high | Small, low-pressure boilers |
| 5 – 8 | 12 – 20 % | Moderate | High | **Medium-pressure industrial boilers (most common)** |
| 10 – 15 | 7 – 11 % | Higher | Moderate | High-pressure with good external treatment |
| > 20 | < 5 % | **Very High** | Low | Only with demineralized water + close monitoring |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Boiler Blow-Down System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Water Source] --> B[Pre-Treatment Filter]
    B --> C[Softener / DM Plant]
    C --> D[Feed Water Tank]
    D --> E[Feed Water Pump]
    E --> F[Economiser]
    F --> G[Steam Drum]
    G --> H[Boiler Tubes / Combustion Zone]
    H --> I[Saturated Steam to Superheater]
    G --> J[Surface Blow Down Line]
    G --> K[Bottom Blow Down Line]
    J --> L[Flash Tank]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Flash Steam to Deaerator]
    L --> N[Plate Heat Exchanger]
    D --> N
    N --> O[Cooled Blow Down to Drain]
    N --> P[Pre-Heated Feed Water to Economiser]
    I --> Q[Process / Turbine]
    M --> D
```

### 4.2 Mass-Flow & Energy-Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FW[Feed Water F, Cf] -- "Heat input Qf" --> DRUM[Steam Drum]
    DRUM -- "Steam E, Cs approx 0" --> STM[Steam to Process]
    DRUM -- "Blow Down B, Cb" --> BD[Blow Down Stream]
    BD -- "Heat lost" --> REC[Heat Recovery Unit]
    REC -- "Flash steam" --> DEA[Deaerator]
    REC -- "Pre-heated FW" --> FW
```

### 4.3 Surface vs. Bottom Blow-Down (Location Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SteamDrum[Steam Drum]
        S1[Saturated Steam Outlet - Top]
        S2[Water Level Line]
        S3[Surface Skimmer Pipe - CBD]
        S4[Mud / Sludge - Bottom]
        S5[Bottom Blow-Down Valve - BBD]
    end
    S1 --> STMOUT[To Superheater]
    S3 --> CBDOUT[To Flash Tank]
    S5 --> BBDOUT[To Flash Tank]
```

### 4.4 Control Logic of Continuous Blow Down

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    COND[Conductivity Probe in Drum] --> SIG[Signal 4-20 mA]
    SIG --> CTRL[Conductivity Controller]
    CTRL -- "Setpoint: 3000 uS/cm" --> ACT[Modulating Valve on CBD Line]
    ACT --> BDV[Blow Down Out]
    CTRL -. "High alarm" --> OP[Operator Notification]
    CTRL -. "Auto blow-down increment" --> ACT
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Define blow down. Differentiate between surface blow down and bottom blow down.**

**Model Answer:**
Blow down is the controlled removal of a portion of concentrated boiler water to keep TDS, suspended solids and sludge within IBR-prescribed safe limits.

| Feature | Surface (Continuous) BD | Bottom (Intermittent) BD |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Near water level in drum | Mud drum / lower headers |
| Purpose | Remove dissolved solids & foam | Remove settled sludge |
| Frequency | Continuous | Periodic (per shift) |
| Control | Conductivity-based automatic valve | Manual quick-opening valve |
| Heat recovery | Yes — via flash tank | Yes — common flash tank |

**(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 differentiation)**

---

**Q2. What is Cycles of Concentration (COC)? Why is it limited to 5–8 in most industrial boilers?**

**Model Answer:**
$$\text{COC} = \dfrac{\text{TDS in boiler water}}{\text{TDS in feed water}} = \dfrac{C_b}{C_f}$$

It represents how many times the dissolved solids are concentrated relative to feed water. It is limited to 5–8 because beyond this:
- Silica and alkalies reach saturation and may carry over with steam (turbine deposits).
- Scale formation risk rises sharply on tubes.
- Caustic embrittlement probability increases.
- Foaming and priming become uncontrollable.

**(3 marks: 1 formula + 2 reasons)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model – Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the blow-down equation $\dfrac{B}{E} = \dfrac{C_f}{C_b - C_f}$, explaining each term. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

1. Define variables: $F$ (feed), $E$ (steam out), $B$ (blow down), $C_f$, $C_b$, $C_s$.
2. **Overall water balance:** $F = E + B$ … (i)
3. **Solids balance:** $F \cdot C_f = B \cdot C_b + E \cdot C_s$ … (ii)
4. Steam TDS $C_s \approx 0$, hence $F \cdot C_f = B \cdot C_b$ … (iii)
5. Substituting (i) in (iii): $(E + B) C_f = B \cdot C_b$
6. $E \cdot C_f + B \cdot C_f = B \cdot C_b \;\Rightarrow\; E \cdot C_f = B \cdot (C_b - C_f)$
7. Therefore $\boxed{\dfrac{B}{E} = \dfrac{C_f}{C_b - C_f}}$

**(b)** A boiler generates **25,000 kg/h** of saturated steam at **20 bar (g)**. Feed water TDS = **150 ppm**, boiler water maximum TDS = **2,500 ppm**, feed water temperature = **90 °C**. Find: (i) COC, (ii) blow-down rate, (iii) heat lost per hour. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**(i) COC:**
$$COC = \dfrac{2{,}500}{150} = 16.67$$

**(ii) Blow-down fraction:**
$$\dfrac{B}{E} = \dfrac{150}{2{,}500 - 150} = \dfrac{150}{2{,}350} = 0.0638 \; (6.38\%)$$

$$B = 0.0638 \times 25{,}000 = 1{,}596 \;\; \text{kg/h}$$

**(iii) Heat lost:**

At 20 bar (g), saturation temperature ≈ 214 °C, $h_{bw} \approx 908$ kJ/kg (saturated water from steam tables).
At 90 °C, $h_{fw} = 4.186 \times 90 \approx 377$ kJ/kg.

$$Q_{loss} = 1{,}596 \times (908 - 377) = 1{,}596 \times 531 = 847{,}476 \;\; \text{kJ/h} \approx 235 \;\; \text{kW}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **blow-down heat-recovery system** with a neat sketch of a flash tank and plate heat exchanger. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

1. **Flash Tank:**
   - High-pressure hot blow down at $h_{bw}$ enters the flash tank at a pressure slightly above atmospheric (typically 1 – 2 bar).
   - The pressure drop causes a fraction to "flash" into low-pressure steam.
   - Flash steam is sent to the deaerator or low-pressure header.

2. **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE):**
   - Remaining blow-down water (now at ~ 100 °C) flows through the cold side.
   - Cold feed water from the feed tank flows through the other side.
   - Counter-flow arrangement recovers up to **85 – 90 %** of remaining sensible heat.

3. **Final blow-down** at ~ 50 °C is sent to cooling tower or drain with pH correction.

4. **Net effect:** Fuel saving, reduced feed-water pre-heating load, lower blow-down temperature to drain (environmental compliance).

**(b)** Discuss **environmental aspects** of boiler blow down and the **methods of disposal**. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

- **Issues with direct disposal:** Hot, alkaline (pH 10–12), high-TDS water damages aquatic life and corrodes sewers.
- **Treatment before disposal:**
  - **Cooling:** via cooling tower or heat exchanger.
  - **Neutralization:** acid/CO₂ dosing to bring pH to 6.5–7.5.
  - **Sedimentation / Filtration:** to remove suspended solids.
- **Reuse options:** after treatment, blow down can be used for:
  - Quench water in ash-handling
  - Slurry make-up in raw-water treatment
  - Cooling-tower make-up (if TDS permits)
- **Regulatory compliance:** CPCB / KSPCB norms on TDS (< 2,100 mg/L), temperature (< 45 °C at discharge), and pH.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** $C_f$, $C_b$ must be in the **same** units (both ppm or both mg/kg). Mixing will yield wrong blow-down %.
> 2. **Using $h_{bw}$ instead of $(h_{bw} - h_{fw})$:** Heat loss is the **incremental** loss over feed-water enthalpy — examiners deduct 2 marks if you write only $B \cdot h_{bw}$.
> 3. **Steam tables are mandatory:** Always quote the steam-table source (e.g., "at 10 bar, $h_f$ = 781.1 kJ/kg"). A numerical answer without steam-table reference loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Conflating surface and bottom blow down:** Many students mix up the two — distinguish clearly.
> 5. **Annual calculations:** Use **8,000 operating hours/year** as the KTU default unless stated otherwise.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap — High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Blow down = controlled discharge of concentrated boiler water** to limit TDS, suspended solids, and sludge.
- Two types: **Surface (Continuous, CBD)** for chemistry; **Bottom (Intermittent, BBD)** for sludge.
- **Key equations** (memorize):
  - $\text{COC} = C_b / C_f$
  - $B/E = C_f / (C_b - C_f) = 1/(\text{COC} - 1)$
  - $Q_{loss} = B \cdot (h_{bw} - h_{fw})$
  - $Q_{rec} = B \cdot (h_{bw} - h_{fw}) \cdot \eta_{HX}$
- **Heat recovery:** Flash tank (low-pressure steam to deaerator) + Plate heat exchanger (feed-water pre-heating).
- **Higher COC ⇒ lower blow-down loss BUT higher scaling/foaming risk.** Industrial sweet spot: **COC = 5–8** for medium-pressure boilers.
- **Discharge must** be cooled (< 45 °C), neutralized (pH 6.5–7.5), and TDS-limited (< 2,100 ppm) per CPCB norms.
- **Conductivity-based automatic control** of CBD is industry standard.
- **Always use steam tables** for $h_{bw}$ at the given boiler pressure in any numerical.
- **Typical BD fraction:** 4 – 10 % for medium-pressure, 2 – 4 % for high-pressure boilers with DM water.
- **Environmental + economic** angle (fuel savings) is a favourite 14-mark essay sub-part.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boilers Act
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Boilers Act

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## The Indian Boilers Act, 1923

The **Indian Boilers Act, 1923** (as amended up to date) is a comprehensive Central Legislation enacted by the Government of India to consolidate and amend the law relating to steam boilers. It governs the safe construction, manufacture, fittings, repair, and operation of steam boilers throughout India, with the primary objective of **protecting human life and property** from the catastrophic risks associated with boiler explosions.

### Formal Academic Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boilers Act (KTU Definition):** A statutory framework that regulates the design, materials, construction, inspection, certification, and operation of steam boilers, prescribing mandatory standards for boiler fittings, mountings, and accessories, and establishing the legal authority of Chief Inspectors and Inspectors of Boilers to enforce safety compliance across India.

### Key Statutory Terminology (As Defined in the Act)

| Term | Definition under the Act |
|------|---------------------------|
| **Boiler** | Any closed vessel exceeding **22.75 litres** (5 gallons) in capacity used for generating steam under pressure greater than atmospheric, including any economiser or superheater forming an integral part. |
| **Steam Boiler** | A boiler intended for generating steam. |
| **Owner** | The person in possession or having control of a boiler (under lease, hire, or otherwise). |
| **Inspector** | A person appointed as Inspector of Boilers under Section 7 of the Act. |
| **Chief Inspector** | The person appointed under Section 5 to supervise the administration of the Act. |
| **Certificate** | A certificate granted under Section 8 or renewed under Section 9. |
| **Accident** | An explosion of a boiler or any damage to its body that compromises safe operation. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Act became operational on **1st September 1923** and applies to the whole of India. It has undergone major amendments, notably in **1937, 1950, 1960, 1963, and 2007** to keep pace with modern boiler technology.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Boiler as a "Tamed Dragon"

Imagine a steam boiler as a **captive dragon** breathing high-pressure fire. The dragon is immensely useful — it can run factories, drive turbines, and power chemical plants — but if its cage (the boiler shell) cracks, the dragon escapes violently, causing catastrophic destruction. The **Boilers Act is the master code** of rules that:
- Specifies how the cage (boiler shell) must be built.
- Mandates that a **cage inspector** (Boiler Inspector) verifies its strength.
- Requires the dragon to be **chained with safety valves, pressure gauges, and water gauges**.
- Demands **regular health check-ups** (annual inspection and certification).
- Ensures the dragon-keeper (operator) is **trained and certified** for safe handling.

Without this Act, every industrial boiler would be an uncontrolled hazard — much like driving without traffic rules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Salient Features and Scope of the Act

The Indian Boilers Act extends to the **whole of India** but excludes the State of **Jammu & Kashmir** (as per original notification, though re-incorporated under subsequent amendments for the unified UT).

### 2.2 Administrative Framework (Sections 5–11)

The Act establishes a two-tier administrative authority:

- **Chief Inspector of Boilers (Section 5):** Appointed by the State Government; supervises all inspectors within the state; serves as the chief adjudicating authority.
- **Inspectors of Boilers (Section 7):** Appointed by the State Government; they physically inspect, test, and certify boilers in their jurisdiction.
- **Special Inspectors (Section 8):** May be appointed when no regular inspector is available.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boards of Reference:** Under Section 14, the State Government may constitute a **Board of Reference** to deal with disputes between owners and inspectors regarding material strength, design, or construction. This board's decision is final unless appealed to the High Court.

### 2.3 Registration and Certification of Boilers

**Section 7 — Registration:** Every boiler must be **registered** with the Inspector of Boilers before installation. Required documents include:
- Application in prescribed form.
- Technical specifications and drawings.
- Material certificates.
- Manufacturer's test certificates.

**Section 8 — Certificate of Fitness:** After satisfactory inspection and hydraulic testing, the Inspector grants a **Certificate of Fitness**, which authorizes the boiler to be operated. The certificate is initially valid for **12 months** and renewable.

**Section 9 — Renewal:** The certificate must be renewed periodically. The maximum validity period and inspection frequency are prescribed by state rules (typically annual).

### 2.4 Mandatory Boiler Fittings (Sections 18–22)

The Act mandates the following fittings on every boiler:

| Fitting | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| **Safety Valve** | Prevents pressure buildup beyond the maximum allowable working pressure (MAWP). |
| **Pressure Gauge** | Indicates internal pressure; must be visible to the operator. |
| **Water Gauge (Two)** | Indicate water level; one must be a transparent gauge glass. |
| **Stop Valve** | Isolates the boiler from the steam main. |
| **Blow-off Valve** | Removes sludge and sediment. |
| **Feed Check Valve** | Allows feedwater entry while preventing backflow of steam. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exemption Limit:** Boilers with a heating surface not exceeding **20 sq. ft. (approx. 1.86 m²)** and a working pressure not exceeding **1.05 kg/cm² (15 psi)** are generally exempted from certain provisions, subject to state rules.

### 2.5 Hydraulic Test Formula Sheet (Critical for KTU Calculations)

When a boiler undergoes inspection, a **hydraulic pressure test** is performed at **1.5 to 2 times** the maximum allowable working pressure. The KTU-relevant formulas include:

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| Hoop Stress (Cylindrical Shell) | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot t}$ | Stress in circumferential direction |
| Longitudinal Stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t}$ | Stress along the axis |
| Minimum Shell Thickness | $t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allowable} \cdot \eta} + c$ | Where $\eta$ = joint efficiency, $c$ = corrosion allowance |
| Test Pressure | $p_{test} = 1.5 \times p_{MAWP}$ | Standard hydrostatic test pressure |
| Safety Valve Set Pressure | $p_{sv} \leq p_{MAWP}$ | Must open at or below MAWP |

Where:
- $p$ = Internal pressure (MPa or N/mm²)
- $d$ = Internal diameter of the shell (mm)
- $t$ = Shell thickness (mm)
- $\sigma$ = Stress (N/mm²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are essential for KTU numerical problems on boiler design and for understanding why the Boilers Act mandates hydrostatic testing at pressures exceeding the working pressure — to ensure a **factor of safety** against operational surges.

### 2.6 Offences, Penalties, and Enforcement (Sections 23–29)

| Violation | Penalty |
|-----------|---------|
| Use of unregistered boiler | Fine up to ₹500, plus additional fine of ₹100/day |
| Use of uncertified boiler | Fine up to ₹500, plus ₹100/day |
| False certificates | Imprisonment up to **3 months** + fine up to ₹500 |
| Negligence causing accidents | Imprisonment up to **6 months** + fine |
| Obstruction of Inspector | Fine up to ₹200 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Note:** The Act applies **criminal liability** on the owner — mere ownership without ensuring compliance is a punishable offence.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Shell Thickness for a Cylindrical Boiler

The Indian Boilers Regulations (IBR) and the Boilers Act both mandate that boiler shells must be designed with adequate thickness. Here is the rigorous derivation:

### Step 1 — Force Balance on a Half-Cylinder Element

Consider a cylindrical boiler shell of internal diameter $d$, wall thickness $t$, subjected to internal pressure $p$. By taking a free body diagram of a longitudinal half-section of length $L$:

The **total bursting force** acting on the diametrical plane is:

$$F_{burst} = p \cdot d \cdot L$$

The **resisting force** is provided by the two walls of the shell, each of thickness $t$:

$$F_{resist} = 2 \cdot t \cdot L \cdot \sigma_{allowable}$$

### Step 2 — Equilibrium Condition

For the shell to not burst, the resisting force must equal or exceed the bursting force:

$$2 \cdot t \cdot L \cdot \sigma_{allowable} = p \cdot d \cdot L$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Thickness $t$

Cancelling $L$ from both sides and rearranging:

$$t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allowable}}$$

### Step 4 — Incorporate Welding Efficiency and Corrosion Allowance

In practice, the shell contains welded seams with efficiency $\eta < 1$, and a corrosion allowance $c$ must be added:

$$t_{actual} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allowable} \cdot \eta} + c$$

This is the **minimum required thickness** prescribed by IBR under the Boilers Act framework.

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Style

> **Problem:** A cylindrical boiler has an internal diameter of **1500 mm** and operates at a maximum allowable working pressure of **1.5 MPa**. If the allowable stress of the shell material is **120 N/mm²**, weld joint efficiency is **0.85**, and corrosion allowance is **2 mm**, calculate the minimum shell thickness.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**

$$d = 1500 \text{ mm}, \quad p = 1.5 \text{ MPa} = 1.5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_{allowable} = 120 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad \eta = 0.85, \quad c = 2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the derived formula:**

$$t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allowable} \cdot \eta} + c$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$t = \dfrac{1.5 \times 1500}{2 \times 120 \times 0.85} + 2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator and denominator:**

$$t = \dfrac{2250}{204} + 2$$

$$t = 11.029 + 2 = 13.029 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Apply engineering rounding (KTU convention):**

$$t \approx 14 \text{ mm (adopt the next higher standard plate thickness)}$$

### 3.3 Hydrostatic Test Pressure Calculation

**Step 1 — Determine test pressure factor:**

Under IBR, the hydrostatic test pressure is typically:

$$p_{test} = 1.5 \times p_{MAWP}$$

**Step 2 — For the example above:**

$$p_{test} = 1.5 \times 1.5 = 2.25 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the hoop stress at test pressure (must not exceed 90% of yield):**

$$\sigma_{test} = \dfrac{p_{test} \cdot d}{2 \cdot t} = \dfrac{2.25 \times 1500}{2 \times 14}$$

$$\sigma_{test} = \dfrac{3375}{28} \approx 120.5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

This confirms the design is acceptable as the test stress remains within the allowable limit.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Sections of the Act

| Section | Subject | Key Provision | Engineering Significance |
|---------|---------|---------------|--------------------------|
| **5** | Chief Inspector | Appointed by State Govt. | Central administrative authority |
| **7** | Registration | Mandatory before installation | Legal identity of boiler |
| **8** | Certificate of Fitness | Granted post-inspection | Authorization to operate |
| **9** | Renewal | Periodic re-inspection | Continuous safety assurance |
| **14** | Board of Reference | Dispute resolution | Technical arbitration |
| **18** | Safety Valve | Mandatory fitting | Overpressure protection |
| **19** | Pressure Gauge | Mandatory fitting | Operational monitoring |
| **20** | Water Level Indicator | Two required | Dry-run prevention |
| **23** | Penalties | Fine + imprisonment | Legal enforcement |
| **28** | Power to make rules | State Govt. notified rules | Implementation mechanism |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Administrative Hierarchy under the Boilers Act

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Central Government of India] --> B[State Government]
    B --> C1[Chief Inspector of Boilers]
    B --> C2[Board of Reference]
    C1 --> D1[Inspector of Boilers Region A]
    C1 --> D2[Inspector of Boilers Region B]
    C1 --> D3[Inspector of Boilers Region C]
    C2 --> E1[Disputes on Material]
    C2 --> E2[Disputes on Design]
    C2 --> E3[Disputes on Construction]
    D1 --> F1[Owner Factory 1]
    D1 --> F2[Owner Factory 2]
    D2 --> F3[Owner Factory 3]
    D3 --> F4[Owner Factory 4]
    F1 --> G1[Annual Inspection]
    F1 --> G2[Hydraulic Testing]
    F1 --> G3[Certificate Renewal]
    F2 --> G1
    F2 --> G2
    F3 --> G3
    F3 --> G2
    F4 --> G1
```

## 4.2 Boiler Registration and Certification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Owner Submits Application] --> B[Step 2: Documents and Drawings Verified]
    B --> C[Step 3: Inspector Conducts Physical Inspection]
    C --> D[Step 4: Hydraulic Pressure Test at 1.5x MAWP]
    D --> E{Test Result Satisfactory?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Step 5: Grant Certificate of Fitness Section 8]
    E -->|No| G[Step 6: Reject and Inform Owner]
    G --> H[Owner Rectifies Defects]
    H --> C
    F --> I[Step 7: Boiler Registered in State Register]
    I --> J[Step 8: Annual Renewal under Section 9]
    J --> K[Step 9: Continued Safe Operation]
```

## 4.3 Mandatory Boiler Fittings — Functional Architecture Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BoilerShell["Boiler Pressure Vessel"]
        direction TB
        F1["Safety Valve Set at MAWP"]
        F2["Pressure Gauge Indicator"]
        F3["Water Gauge Glass One"]
        F4["Water Gauge Glass Two"]
        F5["Steam Stop Valve Outlet"]
        F6["Feed Check Valve Inlet"]
        F7["Blow Off Valve Bottom"]
        F8["Manhole Inspection Access"]
        F9["Fusible Plug for Fire Protection"]
    end
    subgraph ControlPanel["Operator Control Station"]
        CP1["Pressure Indicator"]
        CP2["Water Level Sight Glass"]
        CP3["Steam Flow Meter"]
        CP4["Feedwater Control Valve"]
    end
    subgraph Safety["Independent Safety Systems"]
        S1["Low Water Cutoff Switch"]
        S2["High Pressure Cutoff"]
        S3["Flame Scanner for Oil Fired"]
        S4["Emergency Shutoff Valve"]
    end
    F1 --> ControlPanel
    F2 --> CP1
    F3 --> CP2
    F4 --> CP2
    F6 --> CP4
    BoilerShell --> Safety
```

## 4.4 Offence and Penalty Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Offence Detected] --> O1[Use of Unregistered Boiler]
    O --> O2[Operation Without Certificate]
    O --> O3[Use of Defective Fittings]
    O --> O4[False Documentation]
    O --> O5[Accident Due to Negligence]
    O1 --> P1[Fine Rs.500 plus Rs.100 per day]
    O2 --> P1
    O3 --> P2[Fine and Operational Shutdown]
    O4 --> P3[Imprisonment 3 Months plus Fine]
    O5 --> P4[Imprisonment 6 Months plus Fine]
    P1 --> C[Court Prosecution under Boilers Act]
    P2 --> C
    P3 --> C
    P4 --> C
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the term "Boiler" as per the Indian Boilers Act, 1923. Mention the capacity limit that classifies a vessel as a boiler under this Act.**

**Model Answer:**
According to the Indian Boilers Act, 1923, a **"Boiler"** is defined as any closed vessel exceeding **22.75 litres** (5 gallons) in capacity, used for generating steam at a pressure higher than atmospheric pressure. This includes any **superheater**, **economiser**, or **waste heat boiler** that forms an integral part of the system.

The 22.75-litre threshold is critical because vessels below this capacity are generally exempted from the rigorous provisions of the Act, though state rules may impose additional conditions. (3 marks)

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any six mandatory fittings required on a steam boiler as per the Boilers Act.**

**Model Answer:**
The Boilers Act mandates the following fittings under Sections 18–22:
1. **Safety Valve** — to relieve excess pressure.
2. **Pressure Gauge** — to indicate operating pressure.
3. **Water Gauge** (at least two) — to indicate water level inside the boiler.
4. **Steam Stop Valve** — to control steam outflow.
5. **Feed Check Valve** — to allow feedwater entry and prevent steam backflow.
6. **Blow-off Valve** — to remove sludge and sediment. (3 marks)

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the salient features of the Indian Boilers Act, 1923. Discuss the administrative machinery set up under the Act for enforcement of boiler safety.

**Model Solution:**
