The Indian Boilers Act, 1923 is a landmark piece of Central Legislation aimed at ensuring the safe design, construction, operation, and maintenance of steam boilers throughout India. Its salient features include:

1. **Comprehensive Scope:** The Act applies to the entire territory of India (with historical exemption of J&K) and governs all boilers exceeding **22.75 litres** in capacity used for steam generation.

2. **Central Legislation with State Implementation:** While the Act is a Central law, its day-to-day enforcement is delegated to State Governments, who appoint Inspectors and Chief Inspectors of Boilers.

3. **Mandatory Registration and Certification:** Every boiler must be **registered** under Section 7 and obtain a **Certificate of Fitness** under Section 8 before operation. The certificate must be **renewed periodically** under Section 9.

4. **Mandatory Fittings:** The Act prescribes essential safety fittings — safety valves, pressure gauges, water gauges, and blow-off valves — to ensure inherent safety.

5. **Regular Inspection:** Inspectors are empowered to enter, inspect, and test any boiler at any reasonable time, ensuring ongoing compliance.

6. **Penal Provisions:** The Act prescribes strict penalties, including fines and imprisonment, for violations, ensuring legal deterrence.

7. **Board of Reference:** Disputes between the Inspector and the Owner regarding technical matters are resolved by an independent **Board of Reference** under Section 14.

**Administrative Machinery:**
- The **State Government** appoints a **Chief Inspector of Boilers** under Section 5, who supervises all inspectors in the state.
- **Inspectors of Boilers** are appointed under Section 7 to conduct inspections and grant certificates.
- **Special Inspectors** may be appointed under Section 8 for emergencies.
- The **Board of Reference** (Section 14) provides technical arbitration for disputes.
- The administrative chain ensures every boiler in India is periodically examined and certified for safe operation.

(7 marks — covering salient features and administrative hierarchy)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A cylindrical boiler shell has an internal diameter of **2000 mm** and operates at a maximum working pressure of **2.0 MPa**. The allowable stress of the material is **125 N/mm²**, weld efficiency is **0.80**, and corrosion allowance is **3 mm**. Calculate: (i) the minimum shell thickness, and (ii) the required hydrostatic test pressure.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$$d = 2000 \text{ mm}, \quad p = 2.0 \text{ MPa} = 2.0 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad \sigma_{allow} = 125 \text{ N/mm}^2$$
$$\eta = 0.80, \quad c = 3 \text{ mm}$$

**(i) Minimum Shell Thickness:**

Applying the formula:

$$t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow} \cdot \eta} + c$$

Substituting values:

$$t = \dfrac{2.0 \times 2000}{2 \times 125 \times 0.80} + 3$$

$$t = \dfrac{4000}{200} + 3$$

$$t = 20 + 3 = 23 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore, the minimum shell thickness required is **23 mm**.

**(ii) Hydrostatic Test Pressure:**

$$p_{test} = 1.5 \times p_{MAWP} = 1.5 \times 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ MPa}$$

Therefore, the hydrostatic test pressure is **3.0 MPa** (or 30 bar).

(7 marks — showing all formula substitutions clearly)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Discuss the offences and penalties prescribed under the Indian Boilers Act, 1923. Why is criminal liability on the boiler owner considered essential for safety?

**Model Solution:**

The Indian Boilers Act, 1923 prescribes stringent offences and penalties under Sections 23–29 to enforce compliance with safety regulations. The key offences and their penalties are:

| Offence | Section | Penalty |
|---------|---------|---------|
| Use of unregistered boiler | 23 | Fine up to ₹500 + ₹100/day continuing |
| Operation without Certificate of Fitness | 24 | Fine up to ₹500 + ₹100/day |
| Negligence causing accidents | 27 | Imprisonment up to 6 months + fine |
| False or misleading certification | 25 | Imprisonment up to 3 months + fine |
| Obstruction of Inspector | 26 | Fine up to ₹200 |
| Failure to report accident | 29 | Fine and additional daily fine |

**Why Criminal Liability is Essential:**

1. **Deterrence:** Steam boilers operate at extreme temperatures and pressures. Without criminal liability, owners might economise on safety inspections and maintenance, risking catastrophic loss of life.

2. **Accountability:** Criminal liability ensures the owner cannot delegate responsibility and escape consequences. It enforces a culture of proactive safety management.

3. **Public Safety:** Boiler explosions can affect neighbouring communities, workers, and the environment. Criminal liability reflects the gravity of the potential harm.

4. **Compliance Enforcement:** Civil penalties alone may be absorbed as a cost of doing business. Criminal liability forces strict adherence to statutory requirements.

(7 marks — comprehensive coverage of offences, penalties, and rationale)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Explain the procedure for registration and certification of a boiler under Sections 7, 8, and 9 of the Indian Boilers Act.

**Model Solution:**

The procedure under the Indian Boilers Act follows a structured statutory pathway:

**Section 7 — Registration of Boilers:**

1. The owner submits an application in the **prescribed form** to the Inspector of Boilers having jurisdiction.
2. The application is accompanied by:
   - Technical drawings and specifications.
   - Material certificates (test certificates of plates, tubes, etc.).
   - Manufacturer's test certificate.
   - Calculation sheets for pressure parts.
3. The Inspector examines the documents and may conduct a **physical inspection** of the boiler.
4. Upon satisfaction, the boiler is entered into the **State Boiler Register** with a unique registration number.

**Section 8 — Certificate of Fitness:**

1. After successful registration and hydraulic testing, the Inspector issues a **Certificate of Fitness**.
2. The certificate authorises the boiler to be **operated** under specified conditions, including:
   - Maximum allowable working pressure (MAWP).
   - Boiler identification number.
   - Period of validity (typically 12 months).
3. The certificate is displayed prominently at the boiler location.

**Section 9 — Renewal of Certificate:**

1. Before expiry, the owner applies for **renewal**.
2. The Inspector conducts a **fresh inspection**, including internal examination and hydraulic test if required.
3. Satisfactory inspection leads to renewal of the Certificate of Fitness for the next validity period.
4. If defects are found, the certificate is withheld until repairs are completed and re-inspection is passed.

This three-tier system (Registration → Certification → Renewal) ensures **continuous monitoring** throughout the boiler's operational life.

(7 marks — complete procedural explanation)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Numerical Problems:** Students often forget to include the **corrosion allowance** when calculating shell thickness — this costs 1 mark. Also, ensure correct unit conversion: **1 MPa = 1 N/mm²**.
> 2. **Theory Questions:** Many students write only the section numbers without explaining the **engineering rationale** behind each provision. KTU examiners award marks for **understanding**, not just listing.
> 3. **Definitions:** Always quote the **22.75-litre capacity limit** when defining a boiler — partial definitions cost marks.
> 4. **Fittings Question:** Don't confuse **mandatory fittings under the Boilers Act** with general boiler mountings and accessories — the Act specifically lists only a few critical ones.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Boilers Act**

- ✅ The **Indian Boilers Act, 1923** governs steam boiler safety across India.
- ✅ A **boiler** under the Act is any closed vessel exceeding **22.75 litres** used for steam generation above atmospheric pressure.
- ✅ The Act establishes a **Chief Inspector** and **Inspectors** appointed by the State Government.
- ✅ **Section 7** mandates **registration** of every boiler before installation.
- ✅ **Section 8** requires a **Certificate of Fitness** before operation.
- ✅ **Section 9** mandates **periodic renewal** through re-inspection.
- ✅ **Section 14** provides for a **Board of Reference** for technical dispute resolution.
- ✅ Mandatory fittings: **Safety valve, Pressure gauge, Two water gauges, Stop valve, Feed check valve, Blow-off valve**.
- ✅ The hydraulic test pressure is **1.5 times the MAWP**.
- ✅ Shell thickness formula: $t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow} \cdot \eta} + c$ — always include **corrosion allowance** $c$.
- ✅ Penalties include **fines up to ₹500** and **imprisonment up to 6 months** for serious violations.
- ✅ **Criminal liability on the owner** is a distinguishing feature of the Act.
- ✅ The Act applies to the **whole of India** with historical exemptions revised by amendments.
- ✅ **Boilers with heating surface ≤ 20 sq. ft. and pressure ≤ 1.05 kg/cm²** may receive exemptions (subject to state rules).
- ✅ KTU expects students to connect statutory provisions with **engineering safety principles** — not just rote memorisation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Handling and Distribution: Steam Distribution System
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Steam Handling and Distribution: Steam Distribution System

# Steam Distribution System — A Comprehensive KTU 2024 Study Module

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Steam Distribution System** is an engineered network of pipes, valves, fittings, headers, traps, pressure-regulating devices, insulation, and condensate-handling equipment used to convey steam at controlled pressure and temperature from the **Steam Generation Source (Boiler House / HRSG / Waste Heat Boiler)** to multiple points of consumption (process heaters, reboilers, ejectors, turbine drives, jacketed reactors, HVAC coils, etc.) within an industrial plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> *“A steam distribution system consists of the piping network that transports steam from the boiler to the points of use, along with auxiliary equipment such as valves, traps, pressure regulators, separators, and condensate return lines, designed to deliver dry steam at the required pressure and temperature with minimum losses.”*

The system is engineered around three governing thermodynamic constraints:
1. **Pressure class** — High Pressure (HP, > 40 barg), Medium Pressure (MP, 10–40 barg), Low Pressure (LP, 3–10 barg).
2. **Quality criterion** — Steam must remain *dry* (moisture content < 0.5%, dryness fraction > 0.98).
3. **Velocity criterion** — Line velocity must stay within **25 m/s – 50 m/s** to prevent erosion, water hammer, and excessive pressure drop.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — “The Steam City”

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Steam Distribution System as the Water Supply Network of an Industrial City:**
>
> Imagine steam as **boiling-hot invisible water** flowing under high pressure through insulated underground mains. Just like a municipal water system:
>
> - The **Boiler** is the **Water Treatment & Pumping Station** that generates and pressurises the fluid.
> - The **Steam Mains (HP/MP/LP)** are the **Trunk Water Mains** carrying the fluid across the plant.
> - **Branch Lines** are the **Street Pipelines** delivering steam to individual buildings (equipment).
> - **Pressure Reducing Valves (PRVs)** are the **District Pressure Regulators** stepping down pressure for different zones.
> - **Steam Traps** are the **Drainage Valves** that automatically discharge the cooled condensate (water formed after steam gives up its heat) while holding back live steam — like a **one-way check valve** for water but intelligent enough to know the difference.
> - The **Condensate Return Line** is the **Sewage/Recycled Water Pipeline** that brings cooled water back to the boiler.
> - The **Insulation Jacket** is the **Winter Coat** preventing the steam from losing its heat (latent energy) before reaching the consumer.
>
> If you do not insulate the pipes, you waste fuel; if you do not trap condensate, you water-hammer your pipes; if you do not regulate pressure, you burst your equipment. Hence the system must be **carefully engineered, not just piped together.**

---

### 1.3 Why Steam Distribution Matters in Chemical Plants

In a typical **refinery, petrochemical, or process plant**, steam constitutes **20%–40% of total plant energy consumption**. A poorly designed distribution system results in:

| Loss Mechanism | Consequence | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Heat loss through uninsulated lines | Fuel wastage | Up to **5%–10%** of total steam energy |
| Carryover of moisture | Erosion of turbine blades, process upsets | **0.5%–2%** efficiency drop |
| Water hammer | Pipe rupture, safety hazard | Catastrophic |
| Excessive pressure drop | Equipment receives low-pressure steam | Capacity loss **15%–25%** |
| Flash steam losses | Energy dumped to drain | **10%–30%** of latent heat recoverable |

> [!TIP]
> A well-designed steam distribution system can recover **15%–25% of energy** through proper trapping, insulation (≥ 90% coverage), and flash steam utilisation — directly impacting the **plant’s Net Operating Cost (NOC)** and **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**.

---

### 1.4 Standard Units & Conventions Used in KTU Problems

| Quantity | SI Unit | FPS Unit (used in Perry's) |
|---|---|---|
| Steam Pressure | barg or kPa | psig |
| Steam Temperature | °C | °F |
| Pipe Diameter | mm (DN) | inches (in) |
| Mass Flow Rate | kg/s | lb/hr |
| Specific Volume | m³/kg | ft³/lb |
| Velocity | m/s | ft/s |
| Heat Loss | W/m | Btu/hr-ft |
| Pressure Drop | kPa or bar | psi |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Steam Distribution System

A modern steam distribution network is **hierarchical**, with steam flowing in a *tree-like* structure from a central generation point outward. The hierarchy is:

**Boiler → HP Main → Pressure Reducing Station → MP Main → Branch Lines → PRV → LP Header → Consumer Equipment → Steam Trap → Condensate Collection → Condensate Return Pump → Deaerator → Boiler.**

Each level has dedicated instrumentation: **PI** (Pressure Indicator), **TI** (Temperature Indicator), **FI** (Flow Indicator), **PDI** (Differential Pressure), **LI** (Level Indicator for condensate pots), and **SI** (Safety/Sight Indicator).

---

## 2.2 Major Components — Engineering Logic

### A. Steam Mains (Trunk Piping)

The mains are oversized carbon-steel pipes (ASTM A106 Gr. B for HP, A53 for LP) sized to keep **velocity ≤ 40 m/s** and **pressure drop ≤ 10% of upstream pressure per 100 m run**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb (KTU):**
> - HP steam (40–100 barg): velocity = **25–35 m/s**
> - MP steam (10–40 barg): velocity = **30–40 m/s**
> - LP steam (3–10 barg): velocity = **35–50 m/s**
> - **Maximum permissible noise level: 90 dB(A)** at 1 m → sets upper velocity.

### B. Pressure Reducing Valves (PRV / PRDS)

PRVs reduce upstream pressure to a controlled downstream pressure. They may be:
1. **Direct-acting** (spring-loaded diaphragm) — for small loads, ± 5% accuracy.
2. **Pilot-operated** — for large flows, ± 1% accuracy.
3. **Two-stage / Series PRV** — for high reduction ratios (e.g., 100 barg → 4 barg).

PRV stations always include a **safety relief valve (SRV)** sized at **110% of maximum PRV capacity** to prevent overpressure in the downstream line if the PRV fails open.

### C. Steam Traps

A steam trap is an **automatic valve** that:
- **Discharges condensate** (and air) when it accumulates.
- **Retains live steam** (does not let it escape).

**Classification of Traps:**

| Type | Operating Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical (Float & Thermostatic)** | Buoyancy of float lifts valve; thermostatic air-vent bleeds air | Continuous condensate load, heat exchangers |
| **Thermostatic (Bimetallic, Bellows)** | Senses temperature differential; opens when condensate is cold | Tracing lines, drip legs |
| **Thermodynamic (Disc Trap)** | Bernoulli + flash steam dynamics; rapid open/close cycles | High-pressure drip legs, header drains |
| **Inverted Bucket** | Buoyancy of inverted bucket; intermittent discharge | Drip legs, moderate loads |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *steam trap* with *check valve*. A trap is *thermodynamically intelligent* — it knows the difference between hot condensate and live steam. A check valve only blocks reverse flow.

### D. Condensate Return System

Condensate contains recoverable energy:
- **Sensible heat** (from saturated liquid at line pressure down to ~ 90°C).
- **Flash steam** (a portion re-evaporates when pressure drops).

A typical installation: **Steam Trap → Drip Leg / Strainer → Flash Tank → Condensate Receiver → Condensate Pump → Deaerator → Boiler Feed**.

Flash steam is the **“free energy bonus”** — for every kg of condensate at 10 barg flashed to atmospheric, ~ **11% of its mass becomes flash steam at 100°C**.

### E. Insulation

Purpose: minimise **heat loss = U × A × ΔT**, where **U** is the overall heat transfer coefficient (typically 0.3–0.5 W/m²·K for 75 mm calcium silicate + aluminium cladding).

Economic insulation thickness balances **fuel cost saved** vs. **capital cost of insulation**, with payback typically **6–18 months**.

---

## 2.3 The Governing Equations — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Below is the complete, exam-ready formula bank for Module 3 – Steam Distribution. Master these before attempting numerical problems.**

### Master Formula Table

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units (SI) | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steam Velocity | $v = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot A} = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\rho \pi D^2}$ | $\dot{m}$ = kg/s, $\rho$ = kg/m³, D = m | Checks if line is over/under-sized |
| 2 | Specific Volume (ideal) | $v_s = \dfrac{R T}{P \cdot M}$ | R = 8.314 J/mol·K, T = K, P = Pa, M = kg/mol | Used for compressible flow check |
| 3 | Darcy-Weisbach Pressure Drop | $\Delta P = f \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | f = friction factor, L = m, D = m | Fundamental pressure loss in pipe |
| 4 | Friction Factor (Colebrook-White) | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log\left[\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right]$ | $\varepsilon$ = roughness, Re = Reynolds | Implicit; iterate for f |
| 5 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $\mu$ = Pa·s | Determines flow regime |
| 6 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot v_s}{3600}$ | v_s = m³/kg | Pipe sizing input |
| 7 | Flash Steam Fraction | $y_{flash} = \dfrac{h_{f1} - h_{f2}}{h_{fg2}}$ | h_f = sat. liquid enthalpy (kJ/kg) | Energy recovered by flashing |
| 8 | Condensate Load | $\dot{m}_{cond} = \dfrac{Q_{load}}{h_{fg}}$ | Q_load = kW | Quantifies trap capacity |
| 9 | Heat Loss (insulated pipe) | $Q_{loss} = \dfrac{2 \pi k L (T_{steam} - T_{amb})}{\ln(D_o/D_i)}$ | k = W/m·K | Sizing insulation thickness |
| 10 | Water Hammer Pressure (Joukowsky) | $\Delta P = a \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta v$ | a = wave speed (~ 1200 m/s for water/condensate) | Critical surge pressure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Velocity Limit Criterion (Critical for KTU Numericals):**
> Maximum steam velocity for noise control: $v_{max} = \dfrac{30}{\sqrt{\rho}}$ where ρ is in kg/m³. This gives ~ **25 m/s** at 10 barg and **~ 12 m/s** at 100 barg.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Steam Distribution System

- **Refineries** use HP steam for feed heating (crude preheat trains), MP steam for reboilers, and LP steam for stripping and tracing.
- **Power Plants** use steam mains to feed multiple turbine-driven pumps and compressors (high reliability, no electrical dependency).
- **Pharmaceutical & Food Plants** use clean steam (CS) — derived from purified water — distributed in stainless steel (SS 316L) lines for sterilisation-in-place (SIP) of reactors and pipelines.
- **District Heating Networks** in Scandinavian countries distribute steam from central CHP plants to entire city blocks.

---

## 2.5 Why and How — Connecting the Physics

> **Q: Why must condensate be removed from steam lines?**
> A: Condensate in a moving steam column acts as a **slug of incompressible water travelling at steam velocity**. When this slug suddenly decelerates (e.g., at a valve or bend), the Joukowsky pressure spike can be **150× the line pressure** — enough to rupture the pipe. This is called **water hammer**.

> **Q: Why is velocity limited?**
> A: At high velocities (> 50 m/s), steam carries entrained water droplets that **erode** valves, elbows, and turbine blades. Velocity also controls **noise** (above ~ 90 dB(A) it violates occupational health limits).

> **Q: Why are pressure reducing stations placed at elevation changes?**
> A: To handle the **static head** due to elevation: $\Delta P_{static} = \rho g \Delta z$. Steam with 1 kg/m³ density gives only ~ 1 kPa per 100 m elevation, but condensate columns can add tens of kPa. PRV locations must drain condensate effectively.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pipe Sizing for Given Steam Load

**Problem Framework (Standard KTU Form):**
A chemical plant requires **W kg/hr** of saturated steam at pressure **P barg** to be delivered over a pipe length **L m** with fittings equivalent to **L_eq m**. Determine the **optimal pipe diameter D** such that velocity stays between 25–40 m/s and pressure drop is ≤ 5% of line pressure.

### Step-by-Step Solution Logic

**Step 1 — Convert mass flow to volumetric flow:**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{steam}} = \frac{W}{3600 \cdot \rho_{steam}}$$

Where $\rho_{steam}$ is obtained from saturated steam tables at pressure P.

**Step 2 — Select trial velocity:**

For LP steam (< 10 barg), take $v_{design} = 40 \text{ m/s}$ as initial guess.

**Step 3 — Calculate required cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{\dot{V}}{v_{design}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute pipe diameter:**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}}$$

**Step 5 — Round up to nearest standard pipe size (DN).**

**Step 6 — Verify pressure drop** (using Darcy-Weisbach, see derivation below).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Drop in a Steam Main (Darcy-Weisbach Form)

**Starting equation** — the Darcy-Weisbach relation for an adiabatic compressible flow is integrated assuming isothermal flow (valid for short lines < 500 m):

$$\Delta P = \frac{f L}{D} \cdot \frac{G^2}{2 \rho}$$

Where G = $\dot{m}/A$ = mass flux (kg/m²·s).

**Expanded form in terms of specific volume v_s (m³/kg):**

$$\Delta P = \frac{f L G^2 v_s}{2 D}$$

For steam, $G = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{A} = \dfrac{4\dot{m}}{\pi D^2}$, so $G^2 = \dfrac{16 \dot{m}^2}{\pi^2 D^4}$. Substituting:

$$\Delta P = \frac{f L}{D} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{16 \dot{m}^2}{\pi^2 D^4} \cdot v_s = \frac{8 f L \dot{m}^2 v_s}{\pi^2 D^5}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{8 f L \dot{m}^2 v_s}{\pi^2 D^5}}$$

This is the **canonical steam-line pressure drop equation** used in process engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Useful approximation (Perry's / Coulson):**
> For carbon steel steam mains with roughness $\varepsilon = 0.046$ mm, at typical Re in the 10⁵–10⁶ range, $f \approx 0.018$ to $0.020$. Use $f = 0.019$ as a working estimate when no iteration is feasible.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Condensate Load and Trap Sizing

A heat exchanger receives saturated steam at $P_1$ and the condensate leaves at $P_2 \approx P_1 - \Delta P_{trap}$. The latent heat transferred:

$$Q = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot h_{fg}(P_1)$$

Therefore, the **condensate mass flow** (assuming no subcooling for now):

$$\dot{m}_{cond} = \dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{Q}{h_{fg}}$$

If the condensate is **subcooled by ΔT°C** before the trap (due to piping losses), the load on the trap is **continuous** with a varying flow rate. Trap capacity is selected at **2× normal load** (safety factor) and matched to the **maximum instantaneous load** during plant startup.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Flash Steam Recovery

Condensate at high pressure $P_1$ (sat. liquid enthalpy $h_{f1}$) is discharged to a flash tank at $P_2 < P_1$. A fraction $y_{flash}$ flashes to steam at $P_2$:

$$y_{flash} = \frac{h_{f1} - h_{f2}}{h_{fg2}}$$

**Energy of flash steam per kg of condensate:**

$$E_{flash} = y_{flash} \cdot h_{fg2}$$

### Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Given:** 5000 kg/hr of saturated condensate at 10 barg (h_f1 = 762.8 kJ/kg) is flashed to 3 barg (h_f2 = 561.5 kJ/kg, h_fg2 = 2163.8 kJ/kg).

**Step 1 — Compute flash fraction:**

$$y_{flash} = \frac{762.8 - 561.5}{2163.8} = \frac{201.3}{2163.8} = 0.0930 \; \text{or} \; 9.30\%$$

**Step 2 — Flash steam mass flow:**

$$\dot{m}_{flash} = 5000 \times 0.0930 = 465 \; \text{kg/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Energy recovered per hour:**

$$E_{flash} = 465 \times 2163.8 = 1,006,167 \; \text{kJ/hr} \approx 279.5 \; \text{kW}$$

> [!TIP]
> At an industrial steam cost of ₹ 4 per kg of steam, recovering 465 kg/hr of flash steam = **₹ 14,88,000 per year saved** (assuming 8000 operating hours).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Insulation Economic Thickness (P1 Method)

For a bare pipe, heat loss is high. Adding insulation of thickness $t$ and conductivity $k$ reduces loss. The economic thickness is where:

$$\text{Incremental saving in fuel} = \text{Incremental cost of insulation}$$

Using a simplified energy balance, the outer radius $r_2$ is given by:

$$r_2 = r_1 \cdot \exp\left[\frac{2 \pi k (T_s - T_a)}{q_{econ}}\right] - 1$$

Where $q_{econ}$ is the economic heat flux (W/m²) based on fuel cost, insulation cost, and operating hours.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Design Options

| Design Parameter | Option 1: Small Dia, High Velocity | Option 2: Large Dia, Low Velocity | KTU Recommended |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capital Cost | Lower | Higher (1.5–2×) | Moderate |
| Pressure Drop | High (10–15%) | Low (2–5%) | **Low (Option 2 wins)** |
| Noise (dB) | 90+ | 70–80 | **Option 2 wins** |
| Erosion Risk | High | Negligible | **Option 2 wins** |
| Insulation Cost | Lower | Higher | Option 1 marginally wins |
| **Verdict** | Risky | **Preferred** | **Use Option 2 for HP/MP mains** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Steam Distribution System (Boiler-to-Condensate)

This diagram maps the **functional flow** of the entire distribution network from steam generation to condensate recovery — the kind of schematic KTU examiners expect students to draw in Module 3 questions.

    D --> F[BRANCH LINES to Reboilers]

    G --> H

    N --> A
    J --> O[LP STEAM CONSUMERS Tracing and HVAC]

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Pressure Reducing Station (PRS)

The Pressure Reducing Station is the **heart** of multi-pressure distribution. Below is a fully detailed functional layout showing the instruments and interlocks.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answers are designed to match KTU's marking key — concise, terminologically accurate, and points-driven.**

---

### **Question A1 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: List any four essential components of a steam distribution system and state the function of each.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Steam Mains** — Carry steam from boiler to the consumer areas; sized for velocity ≤ 40 m/s.
2. **Pressure Reducing Valve (PRV)** — Reduces upstream pressure to required downstream pressure level (e.g., 40 barg → 10 barg).
3. **Steam Trap** — Removes condensate and air automatically while retaining live steam.
4. **Condensate Return Line** — Returns condensate to the deaerator/boiler for reuse, recovering sensible heat and treated water.

*(1 mark per correct component with function, 4 × 0.75 = 3 marks; any four relevant components acceptable.)*

---

### **Question A2 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Why is steam velocity limited to 25–50 m/s in distribution mains? What happens if it exceeds 60 m/s?** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Steam velocity is limited to **25–50 m/s** to:
1. **Prevent erosion** of valves, fittings, and turbine blades by entrained water droplets.
2. **Control noise** below the occupational limit of **90 dB(A)** at 1 m.
3. **Limit pressure drop** to ≤ 5–10% of line pressure.
4. **Avoid water hammer** during load transients.

If velocity exceeds **60 m/s**, the consequences are:
- Severe pipe erosion and noise.
- Excessive pressure drop → equipment receives steam below design pressure.
- Increased condensate carryover → wet steam → process upsets.

*(1 mark for reasons, 2 marks for consequences — KTU valuation key.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — 14 Marks (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, each 14-mark question has internal choice between two sub-options OR two completely different questions. Below we provide two full alternative 14-mark questions.**

---

### **Question B-A — 14 Marks** *(Module 3 Internal Choice Option A)*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

> *A process plant requires 8000 kg/hr of saturated steam at 10 barg to be distributed from the boiler house through a horizontal carbon-steel pipe (ε = 0.046 mm) over a distance of 250 m. The pipe has six 90° standard elbows, two fully-open gate valves, and one check valve. Allowable pressure drop is 5% of line pressure. Steam temperature = 180°C. Density of steam at 10 barg = 5.15 kg/m³, viscosity μ = 1.6 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s.*

#### **Part (a) — Determine the suitable pipe diameter. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert mass flow to volumetric flow.**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{8000}{3600 \times 5.15} = \frac{2.222}{5.15} = 0.4315 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Select design velocity (LP steam).**

For LP steam (10 barg), take $v_{design} = 40 \; \text{m/s}$ (initial trial).

**Step 3: Compute required cross-sectional area.**

$$A = \frac{\dot{V}}{v_{design}} = \frac{0.4315}{40} = 0.01079 \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4: Calculate diameter.**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.01079}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{0.01374} = 0.1172 \; \text{m} \approx 117.2 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 5: Round up to standard size.**

Nearest standard DN = **125 mm (5 inch NB, Sch 40, ID = 128.2 mm)**. ✓

**Step 6: Recalculate actual velocity with D = 0.1282 m.**

$$v_{actual} = \frac{\dot{V}}{A} = \frac{0.4315}{\frac{\pi (0.1282)^2}{4}} = \frac{0.4315}{0.01291} = 33.42 \; \text{m/s}$$

Since 25 ≤ 33.42 ≤ 50 m/s, **velocity check PASSED**. ✓

**(7 Marks: 2 marks for selection of velocity, 2 marks for diameter calculation, 2 marks for standard pipe selection, 1 mark for verification.)**

---

#### **Part (b) — Calculate the actual pressure drop and verify it is within 5% of line pressure. (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Compute Reynolds number.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{5.15 \times 33.42 \times 0.1282}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{22.07}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.379 \times 10^6$$

Since Re >> 4000 → **Fully turbulent flow**.

**Step 2: Compute relative roughness.**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.046}{128.2} = 0.000359$$

**Step 3: Compute friction factor using Colebrook-White (or use Moody chart).**

Using the explicit Haaland approximation:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log\left[\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{Re}\right]$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log\left[(0.0000971)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{1.379 \times 10^6}\right]$$

$$= -1.8 \log[2.46 \times 10^{-5} + 5.00 \times 10^{-6}] = -1.8 \log[2.96 \times 10^{-5}]$$

$$= -1.8 \times (-4.529) = 8.152 \Rightarrow f = (8.152)^{-2} = 0.01504$$

**Step 4: Equivalent length of fittings.**

| Fitting | Quantity | L/D per fitting | L_eq contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| 90° Standard Elbow | 6 | 30 | 180 D |
| Gate Valve (fully open) | 2 | 8 | 16 D |
| Check Valve | 1 | 100 | 100 D |

Total $\sum L/D = 180 + 16 + 100 = 296$

Equivalent length: $L_{eq} = 296 \times 0.1282 = 37.94 \; \text{m}$

Total effective length: $L_{eff} = 250 + 37.94 = 287.94 \; \text{m}$

**Step 5: Apply Darcy-Weisbach.**

$$\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L_{eff}}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = 0.01504 \times \frac{287.94}{0.1282} \times \frac{5.15 \times (33.42)^2}{2}$$

$$= 0.01504 \times 2245.7 \times \frac{5.15 \times 1116.9}{2} = 0.01504 \times 2245.7 \times 2876.0$$

$$\Delta P = 97,135 \; \text{Pa} \approx 97.1 \; \text{kPa} \approx 0.971 \; \text{bar}$$

**Step 6: Convert to % of line pressure.**

$$\% \Delta P = \frac{0.971}{10} \times 100\% = 9.71\%$$

**Step 7: Compare with criterion.**

Since 9.71% > 5%, the **next larger standard size (DN 150, ID = 154.1 mm)** must be selected and re-checked. Iterating with D = 0.1541 m gives v = 22.83 m/s, Re ≈ 1.13 × 10⁶, f ≈ 0.0151, and ΔP ≈ 0.41 bar = **4.1% < 5% ✓ PASSED**.

**(7 Marks: 2 marks for Re & f calculation, 2 marks for equivalent length, 2 marks for pressure drop calculation, 1 mark for verification with 5% criterion.)**

---

### **Question B-B — 14 Marks** *(Module 3 Internal Choice Option B)*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

> *A heat exchanger in a chemical plant condenses 4000 kg/hr of saturated steam at 6 barg (h_fg = 2085.4 kJ/kg). The condensate is discharged through a steam trap into a flash tank operating at 2 barg (h_f = 504.7 kJ/kg, h_fg = 2201.6 kJ/kg).*

#### **Part (a) — Calculate the flash steam generated per hour and the energy recovered. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify condensate enthalpy at 6 barg.**

From steam tables, $h_{f1} = 670.4 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ (saturated liquid at 6 barg).

**Step 2: Apply the flash steam fraction formula.**

$$y_{flash} = \frac{h_{f1} - h_{f2}}{h_{fg2}} = \frac{670.4 - 504.7}{2201.6} = \frac{165.7}{2201.6} = 0.0753$$

So **7.53% of condensate flashes to steam.**

**Step 3: Compute mass of flash steam.**

$$\dot{m}_{flash} = 4000 \times 0.0753 = 301.2 \; \text{kg/hr}$$

**Step 4: Compute energy recovered.**

$$E_{flash} = \dot{m}_{flash} \times h_{fg2} = 301.2 \times 2201.6 = 663,121 \; \text{kJ/hr}$$

Convert to kW: $663,121 / 3600 = 184.2 \; \text{kW}$

**(7 Marks: 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for numerical substitution, 2 marks for flash mass, 1 mark for energy.)**

---

#### **Part (b) — Recommend a suitable type of steam trap for this application, justifying with reasoning. Discuss what happens if the trap fails open. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify load characteristics.**

- Condensate load = **4000 kg/hr (continuous)**.
- Pressure = 6 barg (moderate).
- Temperature = saturated ~ 158.8°C.
- Load is **continuous and substantial**.

**Step 2: Recommend trap type.**

> **Recommended: Float and Thermostatic (F&T) Trap.**
>
> **Justification:**
> - F&T traps are designed for **continuous high condensate loads** — exactly matching this duty.
> - They provide **modulating discharge** (proportional to condensate rate), unlike disc or bucket traps which cycle.
> - Built-in **thermostatic air vent** discharges air at startup without wasting steam.
> - High capacity (typical F&T trap handles up to **10,000 kg/hr** of condensate at 10 barg).
> - Best for heat exchangers where condensate is essentially saturated.

**Step 3: Discuss failure mode.**

> **If the trap fails open (passes live steam):**
> - Massive steam loss through the flash tank → vent.
> - Energy wastage: full line steam (worth Rs. 4–8/kg) escapes → operating cost can jump by **₹ 5–10 lakh per year** per failed trap.
> - Condensate temperature in the receiver drops → lower deaerator efficiency.
> - Pressure drop in flash tank collapses → downstream LP users affected.

**Step 4: Diagnostic recommendation.**

Install a **trap diagnostic sight glass** (or steam trap monitor) on each F&T trap to detect live steam leakage through:

- Temperature rise on the downstream side.
- Acoustic/ultrasonic signature.
- Visual exhaust (steam plume in cold ambient).

**(7 Marks: 2 marks for recommendation with reasoning, 3 marks for failure mode analysis, 2 marks for diagnostic approach.)**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insights — Where Students Lose Marks in Module 3:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Equivalent Length of Fittings (–2 marks):** Many students compute ΔP only for straight pipe, missing the 30–100% extra loss from elbows and valves. Always add $\sum (L/D)_{fittings} \times D$.
>
> 2. **Using Bare Pipe Density Instead of Compressibility (–1 mark):** For steam, use the **actual ρ from steam tables**, not the ideal gas approximation at every pressure. At HP (> 40 barg), steam deviates significantly from ideal gas.
>
> 3. **Wrong Steam Trap Justification (–2 marks):** Never recommend a "disc trap" for a large continuous heat-exchanger load — it cycles rapidly and erodes itself. Always match trap type to load profile.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Pressure Drop Criterion (–1 mark):** KTU questions explicitly state "ΔP ≤ 5%" or similar. After sizing, ALWAYS verify this criterion; otherwise the answer is incomplete.
>
> 5. **No Sketch / Block Diagram (–2 marks):** Every 14-mark question in KTU expects **at least one schematic** (even a simple block diagram of the distribution system). Students who skip diagrams lose marks consistently.
>
> 6. **Mixing Up Flash Steam Formula (–2 marks):** $y_{flash} = (h_{f1} - h_{f2}) / h_{fg2}$ — note it is divided by $h_{fg2}$ (latent heat at LOWER pressure), not $h_{fg1}$. Common error.
>
> 7. **Velocity Limit Neglected (–1 mark):** Even if pressure drop passes, if $v > 50$ m/s for LP steam, the design fails. Always check both criteria.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 — Steam Distribution System: Quick Revision (Last-Minute Prep)**

### ⚡ Core Concepts
- Steam distribution network = **Boiler → Mains → PRV → Branches → Consumers → Traps → Condensate → Return**.
- Three pressure tiers: **HP, MP, LP** — matched to consumer requirements.
- Steam must be **dry** (quality > 0.98) and at **correct pressure** when delivered.

### ⚡ Key Design Rules
- **Velocity limits:** 25–35 m/s (HP), 30–40 m/s (MP), 35–50 m/s (LP).
- **Pressure drop limit:** ≤ 5% of upstream pressure per 100 m equivalent length.
- **Noise limit:** ≤ 90 dB(A) at 1 m from pipe.
- **Steam trap sizing:** at **2× normal load** for safety margin.

### ⚡ Critical Formulas (5 Must-Memorise)
1. $v = 4\dot{m} / (\rho \pi D^2)$
2. $\Delta P = 8 f L \dot{m}^2 v_s / (\pi^2 D^5)$
3. $y_{flash} = (h_{f1} - h_{f2}) / h_{fg2}$
4. $\dot{m}_{cond} = Q / h_{fg}$
5. $v_{max} = 30 / \sqrt{\rho}$

### ⚡ Steam Trap Selection Cheat-Sheet
- **Continuous load + heat exchanger** → **Float & Thermostatic (F&T)**.
- **Drip leg + intermittent** → **Thermodynamic Disc** or **Inverted Bucket**.
- **Tracing lines + small load** → **Bimetallic Thermostatic**.
- **Air venting critical + light load** → **Bellows Thermostatic**.

### ⚡ Common Plant Issues & Remedies
| Symptom | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| Water hammer in lines | Slug of condensate | Install drip legs + traps |
| Equipment receives low-pressure steam | Excessive ΔP or undersized line | Upsize pipe or add booster PRV |
| Excessive fuel consumption | Poor insulation | Re-insulate to 75 mm calcium silicate |
| Trap discharge contains live steam | Worn trap seat | Replace trap; install diagnostic monitor |
| Flash tank overflow | Excess condensate or undersized tank | Re-size flash tank or split flow |

### ⚡ KTU Exam-Day Strategy
- Always draw a **block diagram** before starting numericals.
- State **assumptions** (isothermal flow, roughness, etc.) explicitly.
- Use **steam table values** — do not use ideal gas for steam above 10 barg.
- Verify **both velocity and pressure-drop** criteria before finalising pipe size.
- For trap questions, **always justify** your choice — generic answers get 0.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Steam Distribution System**
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | NEP 2020 Aligned | OBE-Compliant*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assessment of Steam Distribution Losses
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Assessment of Steam Distribution Losses** is the systematic, quantitative engineering procedure used to identify, measure, and evaluate the magnitude of energy and mass losses occurring in a steam generation, distribution, and utilization network. It encompasses the audit of heat losses through uninsulated or poorly insulated pipelines, losses of live steam through leaking joints/valves/steam traps, flash steam losses from hot condensate discharge, and boiler blowdown losses. The assessment provides the baseline data required for energy conservation, insulation retrofitting, and process efficiency improvement in chemical process plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Assessment of steam distribution losses forms the quantitative backbone of any Process Utilities energy audit. The KTU 2024 Scheme course **OECHT611 – Process Utilities** expects students to demonstrate the ability to:
> 1. Categorize every loss type in a steam system.
> 2. Compute each loss numerically using standard heat-transfer correlations.
> 3. Translate losses into equivalent **fuel (coal/furnace oil) cost** in **₹/year** or **kg of steam lost/annum**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are boiling water in a kettle with a long, thin rubber tube connected to the spout, carrying the steam to a radiator in another room.

- If the tube is **bare (no insulation)**, the steam cools down before it reaches the radiator. Some of it condenses into water **en route**, dripping uselessly along the way. That is **heat loss through pipe walls** — you burned fuel, but the energy went into heating the room air, not the radiator.
- If the tube has **small pinholes**, steam hisses out before reaching the radiator. That is a **steam leak** — pure product loss, with no useful work done.
- If the hot water (condensate) arriving at the radiator is suddenly released to atmospheric pressure, part of it violently "flashes" back into steam and escapes into the air. That is **flash steam loss** — energy lost in the discharge flash.
- If you keep flushing some of the hot boiler water down the drain to keep salt concentration low, that is **blowdown loss** — sensible heat of hot water discarded.

So **steam distribution losses** = the *invisible tax* the plant pays every second, because of imperfect insulation, leaking equipment, and uncontrolled discharge. **Assessment** is the act of putting a ruler on each leak, calculating the rupee value lost per year, and ranking the leaks so management can spend retrofit money where it gives the highest return.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used in Industry:**
> - **kg of steam lost per hour (kg/h)**
> - **MJ of heat lost per hour (MJ/h)**
> - **% Boiler fuel wasted**
> - **₹/year cost of loss** (using local fuel tariff and boiler efficiency)
> - **kCal lost per metre of pipe** — used in insulation selection charts (e.g., **CIBSE / IS 9844 / DOE-2**).

---

## 1.3 Why Is This Topic Critical in Chemical Engineering Practice?

Steam is the **largest single energy carrier** in most process plants — petrochemical, refinery, paper, sugar, food, pharmaceutical. Studies published by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India** and the **United Nations Industrial Development Organization (UNIDO)** show that:

- **5–15 %** of generated steam is routinely lost in distribution (typical Indian chemical plant).
- **30–45 %** of uninsulated steam line surface area is found in audited plants.
- A single **failed open steam trap** wastes the equivalent of **30,000–80,000 kg steam/year**, worth **₹2–6 lakh/year** (2024 fuel prices).

Hence, "Assessment" is the *gateway step* to a **Steam Trapping Audit** and **Insulation Retrofit Project**.

---

## 1.4 Categories of Steam Distribution Losses (Master List)

A complete KTU-grade assessment breaks the steam system into **six (6) loss categories**. Each is computed differently:

| # | Loss Category | Physical Form of Loss | Primary Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pipe Wall Heat Loss** | Sensible heat conducted to ambient | Bare / damaged / wet insulation |
| 2 | **Valve & Flange Leakage** | Live steam escaping to atmosphere | Worn gaskets, packing, open bypass |
| 3 | **Steam Trap Loss** | Live steam passing through defective trap | Failed-open trap (blow-through) |
| 4 | **Flash Steam Loss** | Latent heat of high-pressure condensate | Hot condensate discharged to low-pressure flash tank |
| 5 | **Boiler Blowdown Loss** | Sensible heat of hot water | TDS / hardness control |
| 6 | **Distribution Pipe Length & Dead Legs** | Steam sitting idle in un-used branches | Oversized distribution network |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** In KTU exams, *always* state the loss category **explicitly by name** before writing its formula. Examiners award partial credit for correctly identifying the loss type even if a numerical slip occurs.

---

## 1.5 Scope of the Assessment Procedure

A formal assessment follows the **ASME PTC 4 – Fired Steam Generators** and **BEE Energy Audit** methodology:

1. **Survey** — walk the steam network, mark each line on the P&ID.
2. **Measure** — pipe sizes, lengths, surface temperatures, insulation condition.
3. **Compute** — heat loss per metre × length = total heat loss.
4. **Convert** — heat loss → kg of steam equivalent → fuel equivalent.
5. **Cost** — multiply by tariff and operating hours.
6. **Recommend** — list of retrofits with **Simple Pay-Back Period (SPP)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Heat Loss Through Pipe Wall — Theoretical Foundation

For a long, horizontal, cylindrical pipe carrying steam at temperature $T_s$ exposed to ambient air at $T_a$, heat flows radially outward by conduction through the pipe wall and insulation, then by convection and radiation from the outer surface to the surroundings.

For **bare pipe** (single-layer radial conduction in cylinder):

$$
Q_{bare} = \frac{2 \pi L \, k_p \, (T_s - T_a)}{\ln(D_o / D_i)}
$$

Where:
- $Q_{bare}$ = heat loss per pipe, **W (J/s)**
- $L$ = pipe length, **m**
- $k_p$ = thermal conductivity of pipe material, **W/(m·K)** (steel ≈ **45 W/(m·K)**, copper ≈ **385 W/(m·K)**)
- $T_s$ = steam temperature, **°C or K**
- $T_a$ = ambient air temperature, **°C or K**
- $D_i$ = pipe inner diameter, **m**
- $D_o$ = pipe outer diameter, **m**

For **insulated pipe** (radial conduction through *N* concentric cylindrical layers, each in series):

$$
Q_{ins} = \frac{2 \pi L \, (T_s - T_a)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \dfrac{\ln(D_{j+1}/D_j)}{k_j} + \dfrac{1}{h_o \, D_{N+1}}}
$$

Where:
- $k_j$ = thermal conductivity of layer *j* (e.g., calcium silicate ≈ **0.045 W/(m·K)** at 100 °C)
- $D_{j+1}/D_j$ = outer/inner diameter ratio of layer *j*
- $h_o$ = combined surface (convective + radiative) heat-transfer coefficient, **W/(m²·K)**

The **insulation efficiency** is then defined as:

$$
\eta_{ins} = \left(1 - \frac{Q_{ins}}{Q_{bare}}\right) \times 100 \; \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Observation:** A 25 mm thick calcium silicate insulation on a 100 mm NB steel pipe carrying saturated steam at 180 °C reduces heat loss by approximately **85–90 %**. A wet or oil-soaked insulation can drop this to **20–30 %**, making moisture ingress a critical degradation mechanism in chemical plants.

---

## 2.2 Steam Mass Equivalent of Heat Loss

Once $Q$ (in W or kJ/h) is computed, the **equivalent steam mass loss** is found by dividing by the latent heat of vaporization of the steam in the line. The steam in the line gives up **sensible heat** (superheat) first and then **latent heat** during condensation. For simplicity, we use the **total enthalpy drop of steam from its inlet condition to saturated liquid at ambient pressure** — this is the "available energy" per kg:

$$
\dot{m}_{steam,eq} = \frac{Q}{\lambda_{steam}}
$$

Where:
- $\lambda_{steam}$ = latent heat of vaporization of the steam at line pressure, **kJ/kg**
- For **saturated steam at 1.0 MPa (10 bar gauge)**: $T_s = 179.9 \; °C$, $\lambda \approx 2014 \; kJ/kg$
- For **saturated steam at 0.7 MPa**: $T_s = 164.9 \; °C$, $\lambda \approx 2066 \; kJ/kg$
- For **saturated steam at 4.0 MPa**: $T_s = 250.4 \; °C$, $\lambda \approx 1714 \; kJ/kg$

If steam is **superheated**, the superheat enthalpy must be added:

$$
\lambda_{total} = \lambda_{sat} + c_{p,v} \, (T_{super} - T_{sat})
$$

Where $c_{p,v} \approx 2.0 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ for steam vapour.

---

## 2.3 Steam Trap Loss Quantification

A **steam trap** is an automatic valve that discharges condensate but retains live steam. Three failure modes exist:

| Failure Mode | Description | Loss Signature |
|---|---|---|
| **Failed Open (Blow-through)** | Trap is stuck open; live steam vents | Highest loss — equivalent to full bore live steam vent |
| **Failed Closed (Plugged)** | Trap blocks; condensate backs up | No steam loss, but process heating impaired — water hammer risk |
| **Leaking (Passing)** | Trap passes steam intermittently | Moderate loss — small live steam leak |

For a **failed-open trap**, the steam discharge through a sharp-edged orifice of area $A_o$ is:

$$
\dot{m}_{trap} = C_d \, A_o \, P_1 \, \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma \, M}{R_u \, T_1}} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}
$$

For a **choked (sonic) orifice** with $\gamma = 1.3$ (steam), the bracket simplifies, giving:

$$
\dot{m}_{trap,sonic} = 0.65 \, A_o \, P_1 \, \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T_1}}
$$

A practical **BEE rule of thumb** (used by every Indian auditor):

> A single 15 mm failed-open steam trap on a 6 bar line loses **≈ 8–10 kg of steam per hour**, worth **₹1.5–2.0 lakh/year** at 2024 furnace oil prices.

---

## 2.4 Flash Steam Loss from Condensate Discharge

When high-pressure condensate (say at $P_1$, enthalpy $h_{f,1}$) is discharged to a low-pressure flash tank at $P_2$, a fraction flashes to steam:

$$
\% \, Flash = \frac{h_{f,1} - h_{f,2}}{\lambda_2} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $h_{f,1}$ = saturated liquid enthalpy at high pressure, **kJ/kg**
- $h_{f,2}$ = saturated liquid enthalpy at low (flash) pressure, **kJ/kg**
- $\lambda_2$ = latent heat at flash pressure, **kJ/kg**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Reference (Steam Tables excerpt — memorize for KTU exams):**
> - At **6 bar sat. steam**: $h_f = 670.4 \; kJ/kg$, $T_{sat} = 158.8 \; °C$
> - At **0 bar (atmospheric) condensate**: $h_f = 419.0 \; kJ/kg$, $T_{sat} = 100 \; °C$
> - At **0 bar latent heat**: $\lambda = 2256 \; kJ/kg$
> - **Flash % = (670.4 − 419.0)/2256 × 100 = 11.14 %**

This means **11 % of high-pressure condensate** is lost as flash steam when discharged to atmospheric drain.

---

## 2.5 Boiler Blowdown Loss

Blowdown is the deliberate discharge of concentrated boiler water to control **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)**. The mass rate is:

$$
\dot{m}_{BD} = \frac{\dot{m}_{steam} \times TDS_{feed}}{TDS_{limit} - TDS_{feed}}
$$

Where:
- $TDS_{feed}$ = dissolved solids in feedwater, **ppm**
- $TDS_{limit}$ = maximum allowed in boiler water, **ppm** (typically **3500 ppm** for low-pressure, **2500 ppm** for high-pressure boilers)

The **heat lost in blowdown** is:

$$
Q_{BD} = \dot{m}_{BD} \times (h_{f,boiler} - h_{f,feed})
$$

Where $h_{f,boiler}$ is saturated liquid enthalpy at boiler pressure and $h_{f,feed}$ is enthalpy of feedwater at its inlet temperature (≈ 80–105 °C for an **economizer-fed** boiler).

---

## 2.6 Annual Cost Conversion — The Final Step

The total annual cost of a loss is:

$$
C_{annual} = \dot{m}_{loss} \times H_{op} \times C_{steam}
$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_{loss}$ = mass rate of steam loss, **kg/h**
- $H_{op}$ = annual operating hours, **h/year** (typically **8000 h/year** for continuous plant, **6000 h/year** for seasonal)
- $C_{steam}$ = cost of generated steam at boiler battery limits, **₹/kg**

And the **equivalent fuel cost** is:

$$
C_{fuel} = \frac{C_{annual}}{\eta_{boiler}} \times \frac{1}{CV_{fuel}} \times 1
$$

Or more directly:

$$
C_{fuel} = \frac{\dot{m}_{loss} \times H_{op} \times C_{steam}}{\eta_{boiler}}
$$

With $\eta_{boiler} \approx 0.85$ for oil-fired, $0.78$ for coal-fired with no economizer.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Single-Table Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the ONLY cheat sheet you need for steam-loss numerical problems.**

| # | Loss Type | Governing Equation | SI Units | Boundary / Operating Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bare pipe radial conduction | $Q = 2 \pi L k_p (T_s - T_a) \, / \, \ln(D_o / D_i)$ | W | $L \gg D_o$ (long pipe) |
| 2 | Insulated pipe (multi-layer) | $Q = 2 \pi L (T_s - T_a) \, / \, \left[ \sum \ln(D_{j+1}/D_j)/k_j + 1/(h_o D_o) \right]$ | W | $h_o \approx 5\!-\!15 \; W/(m^2 \cdot K)$ including radiation |
| 3 | Insulation efficiency | $\eta_{ins} = (1 - Q_{ins}/Q_{bare}) \times 100$ | % | Higher is better; > **85 %** is industry target |
| 4 | Steam equivalent mass | $\dot{m} = Q / \lambda$ | kg/s | Use $\lambda$ at line pressure |
| 5 | Superheat correction | $\lambda_{tot} = \lambda_{sat} + c_{p,v} \Delta T_{sup}$ | kJ/kg | $c_{p,v} \approx 2.0 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ |
| 6 | Failed-open trap (sonic) | $\dot{m}_{trap} = 0.65 \, A_o \, P_1 \, / \, \sqrt{T_1}$ | kg/s | Choked flow; $T_1$ in K, $P_1$ in Pa |
| 7 | Flash steam fraction | $x_{flash} = (h_{f,1} - h_{f,2}) / \lambda_2$ | kg/kg | Hot condensate → low pressure |
| 8 | Blowdown rate | $\dot{m}_{BD} = \dot{m}_{steam} \, TDS_{feed} / (TDS_{lim} - TDS_{feed})$ | kg/h | Ratio basis; continuous blowdown |
| 9 | Blowdown heat loss | $Q_{BD} = \dot{m}_{BD} (h_{f,boiler} - h_{f,feed})$ | kJ/h | Sensible heat only |
| 10 | Annual cost of loss | $C_{ann} = \dot{m}_{loss} H_{op} C_{steam} / \eta_{boiler}$ | ₹/year | $H_{op}$ = 8000 h typical |
| 11 | Simple Payback | $SPP = C_{retrofit} / C_{annual,saved}$ | years | < **2 years** is the BEE "good retrofit" benchmark |
| 12 | Equivalent fuel mass | $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{loss} \lambda / (CV_{fuel} \, \eta_{boiler})$ | kg/h | $CV_{furnace \, oil} \approx 10200 \; kcal/kg$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** In numerical problems, students routinely **omit** the $\eta_{boiler}$ term in cost conversion. The question usually asks for *fuel-equivalent cost*, not *steam cost*. Always read carefully whether $\eta_{boiler}$ should be included.

---

## 2.8 Why This System Matters in Real Engineering

- **Energy Audit compliance** — Indian **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme** under **BEE** mandates quantified loss reporting.
- **Insurance & Safety** — bare hot lines (> 60 °C) require **personnel-protection insulation** as per **IS 15105**.
- **Carbon Accounting** — every kg of wasted steam ≈ **0.27 kg CO₂** emitted (natural gas basis).
- **Plant Economics** — a 1 % steam loss in a 50 TPH boiler plant costs roughly **₹1.5–2 crore/year** at current FO prices.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bare-Pipe Heat Loss from First Principles

We start from **Fourier's law of heat conduction in cylindrical coordinates**. For steady-state, one-dimensional radial conduction through a cylindrical pipe wall:

$$
\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dT}{dr} \right) = 0
$$

**Step 1 — Integrate once:**

$$
r \frac{dT}{dr} = C_1
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange and integrate again:**

$$
\frac{dT}{dr} = \frac{C_1}{r} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T(r) = C_1 \ln r + C_2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions:**

At $r = r_i$ (inner radius), $T = T_s$ (steam temperature).
At $r = r_o$ (outer radius), $T = T_{s,o}$ (outer surface temperature).

$$
T_s = C_1 \ln r_i + C_2
$$

$$
T_{s,o} = C_1 \ln r_o + C_2
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract to find $C_1$:**

$$
C_1 = \frac{T_s - T_{s,o}}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}
$$

**Step 5 — Heat flux by Fourier's law:**

$$
q_r = -k_p \frac{dT}{dr} = -k_p \cdot \frac{C_1}{r}
$$

The total heat loss through the cylindrical wall (per unit length):

$$
\frac{Q}{L} = \int_{r_i}^{r_o} q_r \, (2 \pi r) \, dr = -2 \pi k_p C_1 \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{dr}{r} \cdot \frac{1}{?}
$$

More cleanly, the **radial heat flow through any cylinder of radius $r$** in this 1-D steady state is **constant** (no internal source), so evaluate at $r = r_i$:

$$
Q = -k_p \, (2 \pi r_i L) \, \frac{dT}{dr}\bigg|_{r_i} = -2 \pi k_p L \, C_1
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $C_1$:**

$$
Q = \frac{2 \pi k_p L \, (T_s - T_{s,o})}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}
$$

Converting $r_i, r_o$ to $D_i, D_o$ (since $\ln(r_o/r_i) = \ln(D_o/D_i)$) and using $T_{s,o} \approx T_a$ for a bare pipe (when convection/radiation is lumped into the ambient temperature — strictly the surface temperature is higher, but in **lumped resistance** form we write $T_{s,o} \to T_a$ to get the **upper-bound** heat loss):

$$
\boxed{\, Q_{bare} = \frac{2 \pi k_p L \, (T_s - T_a)}{\ln(D_o / D_i)} \,}
$$

This is the bare-pipe heat loss used in every KTU-level insulation problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Multi-Layer Insulated Pipe Heat Loss

**Step 1 — For an insulated pipe, the radial path is now a series of resistances:**

Pipe wall ($k_p$, from $D_i$ to $D_o$) → Insulation ($k_{ins}$, from $D_o$ to $D_{ins}$) → Surface convection/radiation ($h_o$, at $D_{ins}$).

**Step 2 — For steady-state 1-D conduction in cylinders, the heat flow is constant across each layer. Equating the heat flow across the whole composite cylinder:**

$$
Q = \frac{2 \pi L \, (T_s - T_a)}{\dfrac{\ln(D_o / D_i)}{k_p} + \dfrac{\ln(D_{ins} / D_o)}{k_{ins}} + \dfrac{1}{h_o \, D_{ins}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation as a thermal-resistance network analogy (Ohm's law for heat):**

$$
Q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{total}}, \quad \text{where} \quad R_{total} = R_{pipe} + R_{ins} + R_{surface}
$$

with each cylindrical resistance $R_{j} = \dfrac{\ln(D_{j+1}/D_j)}{2 \pi k_j L}$ and surface resistance $R_s = 1 / (h_o \, 2 \pi D_{ins} L)$.

**Step 4 — Generalize to N layers:**

$$
\boxed{\, Q_{ins} = \frac{2 \pi L \, (T_s - T_a)}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{N} \frac{\ln(D_{j+1}/D_j)}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_o D_o,total}} \,}
$$

This is the **master equation** used in the **3E Plus** insulation-economic software (NAIMA) and the **BEE Insulation Software** used across India.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalent Steam Mass Loss from a Heat Loss

**Given:** Heat loss $Q$ in kJ/h. Steam at line pressure has latent heat $\lambda$ in kJ/kg.

**Step 1 — Define energy balance on the line.** The steam flowing through the line has enthalpy $h_g$ (saturated vapour) at the inlet. It condenses, releasing latent heat, until it becomes saturated liquid $h_f$ at the same pressure. The available energy per kg of steam in the line is:

$$
\Delta h_{available} = h_g - h_f = \lambda \;\; \text{(for saturated steam)}
$$

**Step 2 — Equate heat lost to mass of steam condensed:**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_{eq} \times \lambda
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\dot{m}_{eq}$:**

$$
\boxed{\, \dot{m}_{eq} = \frac{Q}{\lambda} \,}
$$

**Worked Example — KTU Typical:**
A 50 m length of bare 100 mm NB steel pipe ($D_i = 107.1$ mm, $D_o = 114.3$ mm) carries saturated steam at 6 bar ($T_s = 158.8 \; °C$). Ambient is $30 \; °C$. $k_p = 45 \; W/(m \cdot K)$. Find steam loss per hour.

**Step 1 — Compute heat loss:**

$$
Q = \frac{2 \pi \times 50 \times 45 \times (158.8 - 30)}{\ln(0.1143 / 0.1071)} \;\; W
$$

$$
\ln(0.1143/0.1071) = \ln(1.0672) = 0.06506
$$

$$
Q = \frac{2 \pi \times 50 \times 45 \times 128.8}{0.06506} = \frac{1{,}821{,}051}{0.06506} \approx 2.799 \times 10^7 \; W = 27{,}990 \; kW
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to kJ/h:**

$$
Q = 27{,}990 \times 3600 = 1.0076 \times 10^8 \; kJ/h
$$

**Step 3 — Use $\lambda$ at 6 bar ≈ 2086 kJ/kg:**

$$
\dot{m}_{eq} = \frac{1.0076 \times 10^8}{2086} \approx 48{,}310 \; kg/h
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Note the magnitude:** 50 metres of bare pipe loses ~**48 tonnes of steam per hour** — this is why a steam audit always begins with insulation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Flash Steam Loss

**Step 1 — Mass balance on flash tank.** Hot condensate at pressure $P_1$ enters with mass flow $\dot{m}_1$ and saturated-liquid enthalpy $h_{f,1}$. Flash tank operates at $P_2 < P_1$. The fraction $x$ of incoming mass flashes to vapour, and the rest $(1-x)$ leaves as saturated liquid at $P_2$.

**Step 2 — Component balance on liquid + vapour:**

$$
\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_v + \dot{m}_l
$$

$$
\dot{m}_1 \, h_{f,1} = \dot{m}_v \, h_{g,2} + \dot{m}_l \, h_{f,2}
$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate $\dot{m}_l$:**

$$
\dot{m}_1 \, h_{f,1} = \dot{m}_v \, h_{g,2} + (\dot{m}_1 - \dot{m}_v) \, h_{f,2}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\dot{m}_v$:**

$$
\dot{m}_v = \dot{m}_1 \cdot \frac{h_{f,1} - h_{f,2}}{h_{g,2} - h_{f,2}} = \dot{m}_1 \cdot \frac{h_{f,1} - h_{f,2}}{\lambda_2}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the flash fraction:**

$$
\boxed{\, x_{flash} = \frac{\dot{m}_v}{\dot{m}_1} = \frac{h_{f,1} - h_{f,2}}{\lambda_2} \,}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Boiler Blowdown Rate

**Step 1 — Mass balance on dissolved solids in the boiler.** With continuous blowdown at steady state, solids entering with feedwater = solids leaving with blowdown (assuming no solids leave with steam).

$$
\dot{m}_{feed} \times TDS_{feed} = \dot{m}_{BD} \times TDS_{BD}
$$

**Step 2 — Steam mass balance** (steam leaves dry, blowdown leaves liquid):

$$
\dot{m}_{feed} = \dot{m}_{steam} + \dot{m}_{BD}
$$

**Step 3 — From step 1, $TDS_{BD} \approx TDS_{lim}$ (blowdown water = boiler water at limit):**

$$
\dot{m}_{BD} = \frac{\dot{m}_{feed} \times TDS_{feed}}{TDS_{lim}} \approx \frac{\dot{m}_{steam} \times TDS_{feed}}{TDS_{lim} - TDS_{feed}}
$$

(The last step uses $\dot{m}_{feed} \approx \dot{m}_{steam}$ for small blowdown.)

**Step 4 — Final formula:**

$$
\boxed{\, \dot{m}_{BD} = \frac{\dot{m}_{steam} \times TDS_{feed}}{TDS_{lim} - TDS_{feed}} \,}
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix: How to Choose the Right Equation

| Situation in Question | Loss Type | Equation(s) to Use | Common Mistake to Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pipe length, steam temperature, pipe size given | Pipe wall heat loss | $Q = 2 \pi L k (T_s - T_a) / \ln(D_o/D_i)$ | Forgetting to use **outer** $D_o$ not nominal bore |
| Insulation thickness and material given | Insulated pipe loss | Multi-layer radial conduction | Forgetting to include surface resistance $1/h_o D_o$ |
| Trap orifice size + line pressure given | Steam trap loss | $\dot{m} = 0.65 A_o P_1 / \sqrt{T_1}$ | Forgetting choked flow assumption |
| Condensate from $P_1$ to $P_2$ given | Flash loss | $x_{flash} = (h_{f,1} - h_{f,2}) / \lambda_2$ | Using $h_g$ instead of $h_f$ on the numerator |
| TDS values + steam rate given | Blowdown | $\dot{m}_{BD} = \dot{m}_{steam} TDS_{feed} / (TDS_{lim} - TDS_{feed})$ | Using $TDS_{feed}$ alone in denominator |
| "Equivalent fuel cost" asked | Cost conversion | $C = \dot{m} H_{op} C_{steam} / \eta_{boiler}$ | Omitting $\eta_{boiler}$ |

---

## 3.7 Full Worked Example — Comprehensive KTU-Style 14-Mark Question

> **Problem (Module 3, 14 marks):** A chemical plant distributes saturated steam at 6 bar gauge through a 200 m long insulated line. The pipe is 100 mm NB, schedule 40 steel ($D_i = 102.3$ mm, $D_o = 114.3$ mm). Insulation is 50 mm thick calcium silicate ($k_{ins} = 0.045 \; W/(m \cdot K)$). Ambient is $30 \; °C$. The surface heat-transfer coefficient is $h_o = 10 \; W/(m^2 \cdot K)$.
> Steam cost is **₹0.40/kg** and boiler efficiency is **0.85**. The plant runs **8000 h/year**.
> **(a)** Calculate the heat loss from the line in kW and in kg of steam equivalent per hour.
> **(b)** Estimate the annual cost of the heat loss. Compare with the same line **un-insulated** and compute the simple pay-back period if insulation retrofit costs **₹3,00,000**.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Insulated Pipe Heat Loss**

**Step 1 — Identify all thermal resistances.** $D_i = 0.1023$ m, $D_o = 0.1143$ m, $D_{ins} = 0.1143 + 2(0.05) = 0.2143$ m.

**Step 2 — Pipe wall resistance:**

$$
R_{pipe} = \frac{\ln(D_o / D_i)}{2 \pi k_p L} = \frac{\ln(0.1143/0.1023)}{2 \pi (45) (200)}
$$

$$
= \frac{\ln(1.1173)}{56{,}549} = \frac{0.1108}{56{,}549} = 1.96 \times 10^{-6} \; K/W
$$

**Step 3 — Insulation resistance:**

$$
R_{ins} = \frac{\ln(D_{ins} / D_o)}{2 \pi k_{ins} L} = \frac{\ln(0.2143/0.1143)}{2 \pi (0.045) (200)}
$$

$$
= \frac{\ln(1.875)}{56.55} = \frac{0.6286}{56.55} = 1.111 \times 10^{-2} \; K/W
$$

**Step 4 — Surface resistance:**

$$
R_{surface} = \frac{1}{h_o (2 \pi D_{ins} L)} = \frac{1}{10 \times 2 \pi \times 0.2143 \times 200} = \frac{1}{2692} = 3.71 \times 10^{-4} \; K/W
$$

**Step 5 — Total resistance (sum):**

$$
R_{total} = 1.96 \times 10^{-6} + 1.111 \times 10^{-2} + 3.71 \times 10^{-4} = 1.150 \times 10^{-2} \; K/W
$$

**Step 6 — Heat loss:**

$$
Q = \frac{T_s - T_a}{R_{total}} = \frac{158.8 - 30}{1.150 \times 10^{-2}} = \frac{128.8}{0.01150} = 11{,}200 \; W \approx 11.2 \; kW
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to kJ/h:**

$$
Q = 11{,}200 \times 3.6 = 40{,}320 \; kJ/h
$$

**Step 8 — Steam equivalent at 6 bar (λ ≈ 2086 kJ/kg):**

$$
\dot{m}_{eq} = \frac{40{,}320}{2086} = 19.33 \; kg/h
$$

> **Result (a):** $Q \approx 11.2 \; kW$ corresponding to **19.3 kg steam lost per hour** through the insulated line.

---

**Part (b) — Annual Cost & Payback**

**Step 1 — Annual steam loss:**

$$
m_{annual} = 19.33 \times 8000 = 1.546 \times 10^5 \; kg/year = 154.6 \; tonnes/year
$$

**Step 2 — Annual cost of loss (steam basis):**

$$
C_{annual} = 154{,}600 \times 0.40 = ₹61{,}840 \; / year
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent fuel cost (using $\eta_{boiler} = 0.85$):**

$$
C_{fuel} = \frac{61{,}840}{0.85} = ₹72{,}753 \; /year
$$

> **Result:** Even with insulation, the line is losing **~₹73,000/year** worth of fuel. Without insulation, the loss is **8–10× higher** (since $\eta_{ins} \approx 88\%$ → bare loss is 11.2/0.12 = 93.3 kW → 161 kg/h → 1288 t/year → ₹5,15,000/year).

**Step 4 — Annual savings from insulation:**

$$
C_{saved} = ₹5,15,000 - ₹72,753 = ₹4,42,247 \; / year
$$

**Step 5 — Simple pay-back period:**

$$
SPP = \frac{₹3,00,000}{₹4,42,247} = 0.68 \; years \approx 8 \; months
$$

> **Result (b):** The retrofit pays for itself in **less than 9 months** — a "no-brainer" investment. The BEE classifies this as a **Grade-A retrofit**.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram: Categories of Steam Distribution Losses

The following Mermaid block shows the **complete architecture of an assessment procedure**, from input data to final cost output, and the six categories of losses branching from the central steam system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Steam System at Battery Limit<br/>P_boiler, T_boiler, m_steam_dot"]
    A --> B1["Pipe Wall Heat Loss<br/>Q_radial, kW per metre"]
    A --> B2["Steam Trap Loss<br/>Failed-open, blowing live steam"]
    A --> B3["Valve and Flange Leakage<br/>Gland, gasket failure"]
    A --> B4["Flash Steam Loss<br/>High-P to Low-P condensate"]
    A --> B5["Boiler Blowdown Loss<br/>Hot concentrated water"]
    A --> B6["Idle / Dead-Leg Loss<br/>Steam sitting in unused branch"]

    B1 --> C["Loss Quantification Module<br/>Compute Q in kW"]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    B6 --> C

    C --> D["Convert to kg steam lost per hour<br/>m_eq_dot = Q over lambda"]
    D --> E["Convert to fuel equivalent<br/>C_annual = m_H_op_C_steam over eta_boiler"]
    E --> F["Cost Ranking Matrix<br/>Sort by rupees per year"]
    F --> G["Retrofit Decision<br/>SPP less than 2 years executes"]

    G --> H1["Insulation Upgrade"]
    G --> H2["Steam Trap Replacement"]
    G --> H3["Gland Packing / Flange Repair"]
    G --> H4["Flash Recovery Vessel Installation"]
    G --> H5["Blowdown Heat Recovery"]
    G --> H6["Network Rationalisation"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Heat Loss Through Composite Pipe Wall

The following Mermaid diagram is the **radial thermal-resistance network** of a 3-layer composite pipe — equivalent to an electrical circuit where temperature is voltage, heat is current, and resistances are cylindrical radial terms.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["T_s<br/>Steam at Inner Wall"] --> R1["R_pipe<br/>ln D_o over D_i / 2 pi k_pipe L"]
    R1 --> T2["T_interface_1<br/>Pipe-Insulation Boundary"]
    T2 --> R2["R_insulation<br/>ln D_ins over D_o / 2 pi k_ins L"]
    R2 --> T3["T_interface_2<br/>Insulation-Ambient Boundary"]
    T3 --> R3["R_surface<br/>1 over h_o times 2 pi D_ins L"]
    R3 --> T4["T_a<br/>Ambient Air"]

    T1 -. Heat Flow Q .-> T4
```

---

## 4.3 Steam Trap Failure Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Steam Trap Inspected"]
    S --> C1{"Temperature<br/>on both sides?"}
    C1 -- "Both cold" --> M1["Mode: Failed Closed<br/>Condensate backed up<br/>No steam loss<br/>PROCESS AT RISK"]
    C1 -- "Both hot" --> M2["Mode: Operating OK<br/>or Failed Open"]
    M2 --> C2{"Steam visible<br/>at discharge?"}
    C2 -- "Yes, continuous" --> M3["Mode: Failed Open<br/>LIVE STEAM LOSS<br/>Replace immediately"]
    C2 -- "No visible steam" --> M4["Mode: Healthy<br/>OK to keep"]
    C1 -- "Inlet hot, outlet cold" --> M5["Mode: Healthy<br/>Condensate discharged<br/>No steam loss"]
```

---

## 4.4 Annual Loss Conversion — Input-Output Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        I1["Heat Loss Q in kW"]
        I2["Latent Heat lambda in kJ per kg"]
        I3["Operating Hours H_op in h per year"]
        I4["Steam Cost C_steam in rupees per kg"]
        I5["Boiler Efficiency eta_boiler"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS
        P1["Compute m_eq_dot = Q over lambda"]
        P2["Compute m_annual = m_eq_dot times H_op"]
        P3["Compute C_annual = m_annual times C_steam"]
        P4["Compute C_fuel = C_annual over eta_boiler"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT
        O1["Annual Steam Lost<br/>Tonnes per year"]
        O2["Annual Steam Cost<br/>Rupees per year"]
        O3["Annual Fuel Cost<br/>Rupees per year"]
        O4["Retrofit Payback<br/>Years"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    I3 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    I4 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    I5 --> P4
    P2 --> O1
    P3 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
    O3 --> O4
```

---

## 4.5 Insulation Efficiency vs. Thickness — Conceptual Chart

The following Mermaid block is a qualitative plot of insulation efficiency $\eta_{ins}$ vs. insulation thickness $t_{ins}$, highlighting the three operating regions every chemical engineer must know.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REGION_A["Region A: Under-Insulated"]
        A1["Thickness 0 to 25 mm<br/>Efficiency 0 to 70 percent<br/>Heat Loss: HIGH"]
    end
    subgraph REGION_B["Region B: Economic Optimum"]
        B1["Thickness 25 to 75 mm<br/>Efficiency 70 to 92 percent<br/>Heat Loss: MEDIUM<br/>Industry BEE target: 85 percent"]
    end
    subgraph REGION_C["Region C: Over-Insulated"]
        C1["Thickness greater than 100 mm<br/>Efficiency greater than 95 percent<br/>Diminishing Returns<br/>Cost-Benefit Negative"]
    end
    REGION_A --> REGION_B --> REGION_C
```

---

## 4.6 Steam Loss Audit Procedure — Workflow Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Step 1: Walk the Plant<br/>Mark all steam lines on P and ID"]
    A2["Step 2: Measure Pipe Sizes and Lengths<br/>Use laser or ultrasonic"]
    A3["Step 3: Identify Insulation Status<br/>Bare, Damaged, Wet, OK"]
    A4["Step 4: Survey Steam Traps<br/>Use TL3800 or TLV-type tester"]
    A5["Step 5: Sample TDS<br/>At boiler and feedwater lines"]
    A6["Step 6: Compile Input Data Sheet<br/>per line, per trap, per boiler"]
    A7["Step 7: Apply Formulas<br/>Pipe heat loss, Trap loss, Flash, BD"]
    A8["Step 8: Cost Conversion<br/>Rupees per year for each loss"]
    A9["Step 9: Rank by Loss Magnitude<br/>Pareto Chart"]
    A10["Step 10: Recommend Retrofits<br/>with Simple Payback Period"]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9 --> A10
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Define "Flash Steam Loss" in a steam distribution system. State the governing equation and the units of each variable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Flash steam loss is the fraction of high-pressure hot condensate that spontaneously vaporises (flashes) when discharged to a lower-pressure flash tank or drain. The governing equation is:

$$
x_{flash} = \frac{h_{f,1} - h_{f,2}}{\lambda_2}
$$

Where:
- $h_{f,1}$ = saturated liquid enthalpy at the upstream (high) pressure, **kJ/kg**
- $h_{f,2}$ = saturated liquid enthalpy at the downstream (low/flash) pressure, **kJ/kg**
- $\lambda_2$ = latent heat of vaporization at the flash pressure, **kJ/kg**
- $x_{flash}$ = flash fraction, dimensionless (kg flashed per kg of condensate)

For 6 bar condensate flashed to atmospheric pressure, $x_{flash} \approx 11\%$.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition 1 mark, equation 1 mark, units 1 mark.

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**List any six (6) categories of steam distribution losses that must be assessed in a chemical plant energy audit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Pipe wall heat loss** — conduction through bare or poorly insulated steam lines.
2. **Steam trap loss** — live steam passing through failed-open traps.
3. **Valve and flange leakage loss** — live steam escaping through worn gaskets and gland packings.
4. **Flash steam loss** — spontaneous vaporization of high-pressure condensate discharged to low pressure.
5. **Boiler blowdown loss** — hot concentrated water discharged for TDS control.
6. **Distribution network losses** — steam held in oversized pipes, dead legs, and bypass lines.

**Mark Distribution:** 0.5 mark per correctly stated loss.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Regulation Note:** Part B questions in Module 3 typically carry 14 marks each, with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each, and **internal choice** between two alternative full-length questions.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** A 250 m long steam line carries saturated steam at **10 bar gauge** ($T_s = 179.9 \; °C$, $\lambda = 2014 \; kJ/kg$). The pipe is 150 mm NB schedule 40 steel with $D_i = 154.1 \; mm$ and $D_o = 168.3 \; mm$. The pipe is insulated with **75 mm of mineral wool** ($k_{ins} = 0.040 \; W/(m \cdot K)$). Ambient temperature is **32 °C** and the surface heat-transfer coefficient is **$h_o = 12 \; W/(m^2 \cdot K)$**. Compute the **heat loss in kW** and the **steam loss in kg/h**. (7 marks)

**(b)** The boiler efficiency is **0.82** and the plant runs **8400 h/year**. The cost of furnace oil with CV = **10,200 kcal/kg** is **₹70/kg**. Compute the **annual monetary loss in ₹/year** attributable to the line heat loss. Comment on whether insulation retrofit is justified. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution (Question A)**

**Part (a) — Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the **outer diameter of insulation** $D_{ins} = D_o + 2 t_{ins} = 0.1683 + 2(0.075) = 0.3183 \; m$.

**Step 2:** Calculate **pipe wall thermal resistance**:

$$
R_{pipe} = \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 \pi k_p L} = \frac{\ln(0.1683/0.1541)}{2 \pi (45)(250)} = \frac{\ln(1.0922)}{70{,}686}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.08824}{70{,}686} = 1.248 \times 10^{-6} \; K/W
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate **insulation thermal resistance**:

$$
R_{ins} = \frac{\ln(D_{ins}/D_o)}{2 \pi k_{ins} L} = \frac{\ln(0.3183/0.1683)}{2 \pi (0.040)(250)}
$$

$$
= \frac{\ln(1.891)}{62.83} = \frac{0.6373}{62.83} = 1.014 \times 10^{-2} \; K/W
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate **surface thermal resistance**:

$$
R_{surface} = \frac{1}{h_o \, 2 \pi D_{ins} L} = \frac{1}{12 \times 2 \pi \times 0.3183 \times 250} = \frac{1}{5999} = 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \; K/W
$$

**Step 5:** Sum resistances:

$$
R_{total} = 1.248 \times 10^{-6} + 1.014 \times 10^{-2} + 1.667 \times 10^{-4} = 1.031 \times 10^{-2} \; K/W
$$

**Step 6:** Compute heat loss:

$$
Q = \frac{T_s - T_a}{R_{total}} = \frac{179.9 - 32}{0.01031} = \frac{147.9}{0.01031} = 14{,}345 \; W \approx 14.35 \; kW
$$

**Step 7:** Convert to kJ/h:

$$
Q = 14{,}345 \times 3.6 = 51{,}642 \; kJ/h
$$

**Step 8:** Steam equivalent:

$$
\dot{m}_{eq} = \frac{51{,}642}{2014} = 25.64 \; kg/h
$$

> **Answer (a):** $Q \approx 14.35 \; kW$, equivalent to **25.64 kg of steam lost per hour**.

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1:** Annual steam lost:

$$
m_{annual} = 25.64 \times 8400 = 2.154 \times 10^5 \; kg/year = 215.4 \; tonnes/year
$$

**Step 2:** Annual steam heat lost (in kCal):

$$
Q_{annual} = 51{,}642 \times 8400 = 4.338 \times 10^8 \; kJ/year
$$

Convert to kCal (1 kJ = 0.239 kCal):

$$
Q_{annual} = 4.338 \times 10^8 \times 0.239 = 1.037 \times 10^8 \; kCal/year
$$

**Step 3:** Equivalent fuel mass required (with boiler efficiency):

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{Q_{annual}}{CV_{fuel} \times \eta_{boiler}} = \frac{1.037 \times 10^8}{10{,}200 \times 0.82} = \frac{1.037 \times 10^8}{8364} = 12{,}400 \; kg/year
$$

**Step 4:** Annual fuel cost:

$$
C_{annual} = 12{,}400 \times 70 = ₹8,68,000 \; /year
$$

> **Answer (b):** The line is losing approximately **₹8.68 lakh/year** in fuel cost.

**Retrofit Justification:** If a 25 mm upgrade of insulation (to 100 mm total) reduces heat loss by ~50 %, annual savings = **₹4.34 lakh/year**. Insulation cost for 250 m line ≈ **₹4.5–5.0 lakh** (calcium silicate, 2024 rates). Payback ≈ **1.1 years** — **HIGHLY JUSTIFIED** as per BEE Grade-A retrofit standard.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** A boiler generates **10,000 kg/h** of saturated steam at **10 bar** with feedwater at **80 °C**. The feedwater TDS is **250 ppm** and the maximum boiler water TDS allowed is **3500 ppm**. The calorific value of the fuel is **10,200 kcal/kg** and the boiler efficiency is **0.85**. Compute: **(i)** the continuous blowdown rate in **kg/h**, **(ii)** the **heat lost in blowdown** in **kCal/h**, and **(iii)** the **annual cost of blowdown** if the plant runs **8000 h/year** and fuel cost is **₹65/kg**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 15 mm failed-open steam trap on a **6 bar line** ($T_1 = 158.8 \; °C = 431.95 \; K$) has a discharge orifice of area **$A_o = 1.77 \times 10^{-4} \; m^2$** (15 mm diameter). Using the choked-orifice equation, calculate the **steam loss in kg/h** and the **annual cost** if steam costs **₹0.45/kg** and the plant runs **8000 h/year**. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution (Question B)**

**Part (a) — Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate blowdown rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{BD} = \frac{\dot{m}_{steam} \times TDS_{feed}}{TDS_{lim} - TDS_{feed}} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 250}{3500 - 250} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{3250} = 769.2 \; kg/h
$$

**Step 2:** Find enthalpies. At 10 bar, $T_{sat} = 179.9 \; °C$, $h_{f,boiler} = 762.8 \; kJ/kg$. Feedwater at 80 °C: $h_{f,feed} = 4.18 \times 80 = 334.4 \; kJ/kg$ (using water cp).

**Step 3:** Heat loss in blowdown:

$$
Q_{BD} = \dot{m}_{BD} \times (h_{f,boiler} - h_{f,feed}) = 769.2 \times (762.8 - 334.4)
$$

$$
= 769.2 \times 428.4 = 3.295 \times 10^5 \; kJ/h
$$

Convert to kCal/h:

$$
Q_{BD} = 3.295 \times 10^5 \times 0.239 = 78{,}750 \; kCal/h
$$

**Step 4:** Equivalent fuel mass:

$$
