h_4 = 392.3 - 249.7 = 142.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step B: Convert 10 TR to kW** $$Q_e = 10 \times 3.517 = 35.17 \text{ kW}$$ **Step C: Compute Mass Flow Rate** $$\dot{m} = \frac{Q_e}{RE} = \frac{35.17}{142.6} = 0.2467 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step D: Compute Compressor Displacement** $$\dot{V}_{disp} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot v_1}{\eta_v} = \frac{0.2467 \times 0.0993}{0.85} = 0.0288 \text{ m³/s} \approx 1.73 \text{ m³/min}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common Refrigerants | Property | R-12 | R-22 | R-134a | R-404A | NH₃ (R-717) | CO₂ (R-744) | R-1234yf | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Boiling Point (°C, 1 atm) | −29.8 | −40.8 | −26.1 | −46.5 | −33.3 | −78.4 (sublimes) | −29.4 | | Critical Temp (°C) | 112 | 96 | 101 | 72 | 133 | 31 | 95 | | Critical Pressure (bar) | 41.4 | 49.9 | 40.6 | 37.3 | 113 | 73.8 | 34 | | Latent Heat h_fg (kJ/kg) @ 0 °C | 152 | 205 | 198 | ~170 | 1377 | 232 (at −40 °C) | 180 | | ODP | 1.0 | 0.055 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | GWP (100-yr) | 10900 | 1810 | 1430 | 3922 | 0 | 1 | < 1 | | ASHRAE Safety Group | A1 | A1 | A1 | A1 | B2L | A1 | A2L | | Toxicity | Low | Low | Low | Low | High | Low | Low | | Flammability | None | None | None | None | Mild | None | Mild | | Lubricant | Mineral | Mineral | POE | POE | Mineral (dry) | POE | POE/PVE | | Typical Application | Legacy (banned) | Legacy AC | Modern AC, chillers | Commercial freezers | Industrial cold storage, process cooling | Transcritical, hot climates | Automotive AC, future HFC replacement | --- ## 3.4 Detailed Comparison — Ammonia vs HFCs ### 🟢 Why Ammonia (R-717) Remains the King of Industrial Refrigeration > [!NOTE] > **NH₃ has the highest latent heat of vaporization of ANY practical refrigerant — 1377 kJ/kg at 0 °C** (vs ~200 kJ/kg for HFCs). This is **6–7× higher** than R-134a. **Consequence:** For the same cooling load, the **mass flow rate of NH₃ is ~7× lower** than R-134a. Smaller pipes, smaller valves, smaller compressor. **Example Comparison for 100 TR cooling load:** | Refrigerant | RE (kJ/kg) | $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) | $\dot{V}$ (m³/min) | Pipe Size (rel.) | |---|---|---|---|---| | R-134a | 142.6 | 2.47 | 14.7 | Large | | NH₃ | 1106 (at −10 °C) | 0.318 | 0.46 | **Small** | **Drawbacks of Ammonia:** - **Toxic** (Odor threshold: 5 ppm; IDLH: 300 ppm) — strict OSHA regulations - **Mildly flammable** (LFL = 15%, UFL = 28%) — but hard to ignite - **Reacts with copper** — copper/brass piping NOT allowed (use steel) - **Higher discharge temperatures** — risk of oil breakdown in compressor **Why HFCs Dominate Commercial/AC:** - Non-toxic, non-flammable, safe in occupied spaces - Drop-in replacement for phased-out CFCs - Compatible with existing metallurgy - But: **high GWP** (R-134a = 1430, R-404A = 3922) **Why CO₂ is Back (R-744):** - Zero ODP, GWP = 1, non-toxic, non-flammable - Excellent for **transcritical cycles** in hot climates - Challenges: very high pressures (≥ 90–120 bar), low critical temp - Used in: supermarket refrigeration, heat pump water heaters, automotive (BMW, Mercedes) --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Refrigerants ```mermaid graph TD A["REFRIGERANTS"]:::root --> B["SYNTHETIC HALOCARBONS"] A --> C["NATURAL / INORGANIC"] A --> D["BLENDS"] A --> E["NEW GENERATION HFOs"] B --> B1["CFCs
R-11, R-12
BANNED"] B --> B2["HCFCs
R-22, R-123
PHASING OUT"] B --> B3["HFCs
R-134a, R-404A, R-410A
PHASEDOWN"] C --> C1["AMMONIA NH3
R-717"] C --> C2["CO2 R-744"] C --> C3["WATER R-718"] C --> C4["HYDROCARBONS
R-290, R-600, R-1270"] C --> C5["AIR R-729"] D --> D1["AZEOTROPIC
R-502 (legacy)"] D --> D2["ZEOTROPIC
R-404A, R-407C, R-410A"] E --> E1["R-1234yf
R-1234ze
Low GWP FUTURE"] classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px classDef good fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000000 classDef warn fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000 classDef bad fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000000 class A root class B1 bad class B2,B3 warn class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,E1 good class D1 bad class D2 warn ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Refrigerant Selection Decision Flow ```mermaid graph TD START["START: Process Cooling Requirement"]:::start START --> Q1{"Required Evaporator
Temperature T_L?"} Q1 -->|T_L > 0 degC| HIGH["High-Temp Range
R-134a, R-1234yf
R-410A"] Q1 -->|-20 to 0 degC| MED["Medium-Temp Range
R-22, R-404A
R-507A, NH3"] Q1 -->|T_L less than -20 degC| LOW["Low-Temp / Cryo
R-404A, NH3, CO2
Ethylene cascade"] HIGH --> Q2A{"Occupied Space?
Public / HVAC?"} MED --> Q2A LOW --> Q2A Q2A -->|YES SafeReq| A1["Choose A1 Safety Group
R-134a, R-1234yf
Non-toxic, low flammability"] Q2A -->|NO Industrial| A2["Industrial Space Permitted
NH3 OK
High Toxicity Allowed"] A1 --> Q3{"GWP Compliance?
Kigali / Local Law?"} A2 --> Q3 Q3 -->|GWP less than 150| LOWGWP["R-1234yf, R-1234ze
CO2 transcritical
Ammonia"] Q3 -->|GWP less than 700| MEDGWP["R-32, R-152a
HFC blends low GWP"] Q3 -->|Legacy OK| HIGHGWP["R-134a, R-404A
Existing equipment"] LOWGWP --> FINAL["SELECT REFRIGERANT
Verify: lubricant, materials,
P-h diagram, COP, cost"] MEDGWP --> FINAL HIGHGWP --> FINAL classDef start fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px classDef decision fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef safe fill:#86efac,color:#000000,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px classDef out fill:#fca5a5,color:#000000,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px class START start class Q1,Q2A,Q3 decision class A1,LOWGWP,MEDGWP safe class A2,HIGHGWP,FINAL out ``` --- ## 4.3 Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) Diagram — Working Schematic ```mermaid graph LR subgraph PH["PRESSURE ENTHALPY DIAGRAM P-h OF R-134a"] S1["1
Saturated Vapor
Compressor Inlet
h1 = 392.3 kJ/kg
P1 = 2.0 bar
T1 = -10 degC"] S2["2
Superheated Vapor
Compressor Outlet
h2 = 425 kJ/kg
P2 = 8.6 bar
T2 = 45 degC"] S3["3
Saturated Liquid
Condenser Outlet
h3 = 249.7 kJ/kg
P3 = 8.6 bar
T3 = 35 degC"] S4["4
Two-Phase Mixture
Evap Inlet
h4 = h3 = 249.7
P4 = 2.0 bar
T4 = -10 degC
x4 = 0.27"] end S1 -->|Isentropic Compression
1 to 2| S2 S2 -->|Constant P Heat Reject
2 to 3| S3 S3 -->|Isenthalpic Throttle
3 to 4| S4 S4 -->|Constant P Heat Absorb
4 to 1| S1 style S1 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px style S2 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px style S3 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px style S4 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key observation from the P-h diagram:** The horizontal distance **1→4** represents the **Refrigerating Effect (RE = 142.6 kJ/kg)**. The horizontal distance **1→2** represents the **compressor work (W_c = 32.7 kJ/kg)**. The area enclosed by **1-2-3-4** is the **net work input to the cycle**. The area under **2-3** is the heat rejected at the condenser; under **4-1** is the heat absorbed at the evaporator. --- ## 4.4 Schematic: Primary vs Secondary Refrigerant System ```mermaid graph TD A["PRIMARY REFRIGERANT
NH3, R-134a, CO2
Circulates in chiller
Undergoes phase change"] B["SECONDARY REFRIGERANT
Chilled Water, Brine
Glycol Mixtures, Salt Water
Circulates to load
Sensible cooling only"] A -->|"Evaporator cools"| C["HEAT EXCHANGER
Primary gives up
latent heat to secondary"] C -->|"Secondary chilled"| D["DISTRIBUTION LOOP
To HVAC, process
jackets, fermentation"] D -->|"Secondary returns warm"| C E["CHARACTERISTICS"]:::info E --> E1["Primary: toxic possible
small quantity
located in plant room"] E --> E2["Secondary: non-toxic
large inventory
distributed safely"] A -.-> E1 B -.-> E2 classDef primary fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px classDef secondary fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#075985,stroke-width:2px classDef info fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px class A,C primary class B,D secondary class E,E1,E2 info ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industrial Use Case:** In ammonia-charged plants (cold storage, breweries, dairy), the **primary NH₃ loop** is confined to the engine room. The **secondary loop (chilled water or glycol)** is distributed throughout the plant. This **decouples the toxic primary refrigerant from occupied/process areas** — a major safety advantage. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### ❓ Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]` **Define the term "Refrigerant" and list any FOUR desirable thermodynamic properties of an ideal refrigerant.** **📝 Model Answer:** A **refrigerant** is a working fluid used in a refrigeration cycle that undergoes a cyclic phase change (vaporization at low pressure and condensation at high pressure) to absorb heat from a low-temperature region and reject it to a high-temperature region, producing a cooling effect. **Four desirable thermodynamic properties of an ideal refrigerant:** 1. **Low boiling point** at atmospheric pressure — lower than the desired evaporator temperature 2. **High latent heat of vaporization (h_fg)** — to absorb maximum heat per kg of refrigerant circulated 3. **Low specific volume of vapor** — reduces compressor displacement and size 4. **Low specific heat of liquid** — prevents excessive flash gas formation at the expansion valve, improving COP --- ### ❓ Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]` **What is ODP and GWP? Why is R-12 phased out under the Montreal Protocol?** **📝 Model Answer:** **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential):** A relative index indicating the ability of a refrigerant molecule to destroy stratospheric ozone. R-11 (CCl₃F) is the reference, with ODP = 1.0. **GWP (Global Warming Potential):** A relative index measuring the integrated radiative forcing of 1 kg of refrigerant over 100 years relative to 1 kg of CO₂. CO₂ is the reference (GWP = 1). **Why R-12 is phased out:** - R-12 (CCl₂F₂) is a **Chlorofluorocarbon (CFC)** containing chlorine. - It has **ODP = 1.0** (maximum ozone depletion). - Released R-12 diffuses to the stratosphere; UV radiation liberates **Cl• radicals**, which catalytically destroy ozone via the **Chapman cycle** (each Cl atom can destroy ~100,000 O₃ molecules). - The **Montreal Protocol (1987)** mandated phase-out of all CFCs, including R-12. - R-12 also has very high GWP (10,900), worsening climate change. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Module Internal Choice --- ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the common refrigerants used in industrial refrigeration systems. Discuss the desirable properties of an ideal refrigerant with respect to thermodynamic, safety, and environmental aspects. **(b) [7 Marks]** A refrigeration system using R-134a operates between an evaporator temperature of −10 °C and a condenser temperature of 40 °C. The refrigerant enters the compressor as saturated vapor and leaves the condenser as saturated liquid. Using standard R-134a property tables, calculate: (i) Refrigerating Effect per kg (ii) Compressor work per kg (iii) Coefficient of Performance (COP) (iv) Mass flow rate of refrigerant for 5 TR cooling load **Given R-134a properties:** | State | h (kJ/kg) | s (kJ/kg·K) | |---|---|---| | 1 (sat. vapor @ −10 °C) | 392.3 | 1.733 | | 2s (after isentropic compression) | 425.0 | 1.733 | | 3 (sat. liquid @ 40 °C) | 256.4 | — | --- #### 📝 Model Solution (a): **Classification of Common Refrigerants:** **1. By Chemical Composition:** | Class | Examples | Key Feature | |---|---|---| | CFCs (Chlorofluorocarbons) | R-11, R-12 | Fully halogenated, high ODP, **banned** | | HCFCs (Hydrochlorofluorocarbons) | R-22, R-123 | Partially halogenated, ODP < 1, phasing out | | HFCs (Hydrofluorocarbons) | R-134a, R-404A, R-407C | Zero ODP, but high GWP | | HFOs (Hydrofluoroolefins) | R-1234yf, R-1234ze | Zero ODP, very low GWP, future | | Inorganic | NH₃ (R-717), CO₂ (R-744), H₂O (R-718) | Natural refrigerants, ODP = 0 | | Hydrocarbons | R-290 (propane), R-600 (butane) | Zero ODP, low GWP, flammable | **2. By Temperature Range:** - High-Temp: R-134a, R-22 (AC, chilled water) - Medium-Temp: R-22, R-404A, NH₃ (food chilling) - Low-Temp: R-404A, NH₃, cascade systems (frozen food, cryogenics) **3. By ASHRAE Safety Group:** - A1: R-134a, R-22 (low toxicity, no flame propagation) - B2L: NH₃ (higher toxicity, very mild flammability) - A2L: R-1234yf (low toxicity, mild flammability) **Desirable Properties of an Ideal Refrigerant:** | Aspect | Property | Why Desired | |---|---|---| | **Thermodynamic** | Low boiling point | Evaporator T_L can be low | | | High latent heat (h_fg) | More cooling per kg → smaller equipment | | | Low specific volume (v_g) | Smaller compressor displacement | | | Moderate condensing pressure | Avoids heavy/thick vessel walls | | | High critical temperature | Easy to condense at ambient T | | | Low specific heat of liquid (c_p) | Less flash gas, better COP | | **Safety** | Non-toxic (Group A) | Safe in occupied spaces | | | Non-flammable (Group 1) | No fire hazard | | | Non-corrosive | Long equipment life | | | Compatible with lubricants | POE/PVE/mineral oil OK | | **Environmental** | ODP = 0 | Montreal Protocol compliance | | | Low GWP (< 150) | Kigali Amendment compliance | | | Short atmospheric life | Lower TEWI | | | Easy leak detection | Purity maintenance | --- #### 📝 Model Solution (b): **Given data:** - $T_L = -10$ °C = 263 K - $T_H = 40$ °C = 313 K - $h_1 = 392.3$ kJ/kg, $h_2 = 425.0$ kJ/kg, $h_3 = 256.4$ kJ/kg - Throttling is isenthalpic: $h_4 = h_3 = 256.4$ kJ/kg **(i) Refrigerating Effect per kg:** $$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 392.3 - 256.4 = 135.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(ii) Compressor work per kg:** $$W_c = h_2 - h_1 = 425.0 - 392.3 = 32.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(iii) Coefficient of Performance:** $$COP = \frac{RE}{W_c} = \frac{135.9}{32.7} = 4.156 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$ **Carnot COP for comparison:** $$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{313 - 263} = \frac{263}{50} = 5.26$$ **Second-Law Efficiency:** $$\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} = \frac{4.156}{5.26} = 0.79 = 79\%$$ **(iv) Mass flow rate for 5 TR:** Convert TR to kW: $$Q_e = 5 \times 3.517 = 17.585 \text{ kW}$$ Mass flow rate: $$\dot{m} = \frac{Q_e}{RE} = \frac{17.585}{135.9} = 0.1294 \text{ kg/s} = 7.76 \text{ kg/min}$$ **Heat rejected at condenser:** $$Q_{cond} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_3) = 0.1294 \times (425.0 - 256.4) = 0.1294 \times 168.6 = 21.82 \text{ kW}$$ **Verification by First Law:** $Q_{cond} = Q_e + W_c = 17.585 + 0.1294 \times 32.7 = 17.585 + 4.232 = 21.82$ kW ✓ --- ### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the importance of refrigerants in process industries. With a neat P-h diagram, describe the vapor compression refrigeration cycle and mark the four state points. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare Ammonia (R-717) and R-134a as refrigerants in terms of (i) thermodynamic properties, (ii) safety/environmental impact, and (iii) industrial applications. Justify why ammonia is preferred in large industrial refrigeration plants despite its toxicity. --- #### 📝 Model Solution (a): **Importance of Refrigerants in Process Industries:** Refrigerants are vital to process industries for the following reasons: 1. **Product Quality & Preservation:** Pharmaceutical and food industries require precise low temperatures (0 to −40 °C) for product stability. Refrigerants enable cold storage, freeze-drying, and chilled processing. 2. **Process Efficiency:** Many chemical reactions are exothermic and require chilled reactors (e.g., polymerization, nitration, fermentation). Refrigerant-based chilled water (5–10 °C) maintains optimal reactor temperature. 3. **Separation Processes:** Cryogenic distillation (LNG, air separation) uses refrigerants to liquefy gases (N₂ at −196 °C, O₂ at −183 °C, CH₄ at −162 °C). 4. **HVAC & Cleanrooms:** Semiconductor and pharmaceutical cleanrooms require tight temperature and humidity control (±0.5 °C, ±5% RH). 5. **Condensation of Vapors:** Reflux condensers in distillation columns use refrigerant-cooled services (e.g., R-22 at −40 °C condensing low-boiling hydrocarbons). 6. **Equipment Protection:** Chilling prevents degradation of temperature-sensitive materials (e.g., paraffin crystallization, wax precipitation in pipelines). **Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle (P-h Diagram):** The cycle has **four main components**: Compressor, Condenser, Expansion valve, Evaporator. The four state points are: - **State 1:** Saturated vapor at compressor inlet (low P, low T) - **State 2:** Superheated vapor at compressor outlet (high P, high T) - **State 3:** Saturated liquid at condenser outlet (high P, moderate T) - **State 4:** Liquid-vapor mixture after throttling (low P, low T, low quality) (See the Mermaid P-h diagram in Section 4.3 for visual representation.) **Cycle Equations:** - $RE = h_1 - h_4$ (heat absorbed) - $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ (work input) - $Q_{cond} = h_2 - h_3$ (heat rejected) - $COP = RE / W_c$ --- #### 📝 Model Solution (b): **Comparison: Ammonia (R-717) vs R-134a** | Aspect | Ammonia (R-717) | R-134a | |---|---|---| | **(i) Thermodynamic** | | | | Boiling point (1 atm) | −33.3 °C | −26.1 °C | | Latent heat h_fg | **1377 kJ/kg** (very high) | 198 kJ/kg | | Critical temperature | 133 °C (high — easy to condense) | 101 °C | | Critical pressure | 113 bar (high) | 40.6 bar (moderate) | | Compressor displacement | **Very small** (7× less than R-134a) | Larger | | Pressure ratio | Lower (better $\eta_v$) | Higher | | COP | **Higher** (especially at low T) | Lower for same duty | | Energy consumption | Lower (saves power) | Higher | | **(ii) Safety/Environmental** | | | | ODP | 0 | 0 | | GWP | **0** | 1430 | | ASHRAE Safety Group | **B2L** (higher toxicity, mild flammability) | **A1** (low toxicity, non-flammable) | | Toxicity (Odor threshold) | **5 ppm** (clearly detectable) | None (no warning) | | IDLH | 300 ppm | None | | Flammability (LFL) | 15% (hard to ignite) | Non-flammable | | Atmospheric lifetime | Days | 14 years | | Copper compatibility | **Not compatible** (use steel) | Compatible | | Lubricant | Mineral oil (anhydrous) | POE (Polyolester) | | **(iii) Applications** | | | | Industrial refrigeration | **Dominant** (cold storage, ice plants, breweries, dairy, fish processing) | Limited (occupancy safety concerns) | | Commercial refrigeration | Rare | Common (supermarkets, AC) | | Cryogenics & cascades | Yes (upper stage of NH₃/CO₂ cascade) | No (limited low-T capability) | | Automotive AC | No | Common | | Domestic AC | No | Common | | Cost | Very low | Moderate | **Why Ammonia is Preferred in Large Industrial Plants (Despite Toxicity):** 1. **Superior Thermodynamics:** h_fg of NH₃ is 6–7× higher than R-134a, drastically reducing refrigerant inventory and pipe sizes. For a 1000 TR plant, NH₃ requires ~7× less mass circulation than R-134a. 2. **Highest COP:** Lower power consumption → significant OPEX savings over the plant lifetime. A 1000 TR NH₃ plant can save ₹1–2 crore/year in electricity versus R-134a. 3. **Zero Environmental Impact:** ODP = 0, GWP = 0, zero TEWI. Future-proof against Kigali Amendment restrictions. 4. **Easy Leak Detection:** Pungent odor at 5 ppm means any leak is detected immediately, preventing costly inventory loss and allowing fast repair — this **partially offsets the toxicity concern**. 5. **Industrial Location:** Large plants are typically in dedicated industrial zones, not occupied buildings. Toxicity is manageable with proper engineering controls (leak detectors, ventilation, OSHA compliance). 6. **Low Cost:** NH₃ is ~10× cheaper per kg than R-134a. 7. **Excellent for Low-Temp:** Preferred for cold storage (−25 to −40 °C) and food freezing applications. **Trade-offs Managed by Design:** - Containment: Refrigerant confined to engine room (secondary loop for distribution) - Ventilation: Mechanical exhaust with ammonia sensors - PPE: Full-face respirators, chemical suits - Standards compliance: ASHRAE 15, IIAR-2, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU ESE (Module 4 — Refrigerants):** > > **Trap 1: Confusing ODP and GWP references.** Many students write "ODP of CO₂ = 1" — **WRONG**. ODP reference is **R-11 = 1.0**; GWP reference is **CO₂ = 1**. Mixing these is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss. > > **Trap 2: Wrong state-point numbering.** Always use the **KTU/IIT-K convention**: 1=compressor inlet, 2=compressor outlet, 3=condenser outlet, 4=evaporator inlet. Reverse numbering (1=evap inlet) loses method marks. > > **Trap 3: Forgetting $h_4 = h_3$ in throttling.** Throttling is **isenthalpic**, NOT isentropic. The expansion process has constant enthalpy, not constant entropy. Writing $h_4 \neq h_3$ is a fundamental error. > > **Trap 4: Unit errors in TR conversion.** Use $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ for SI units. Mixing with hp (3.517 kW = 4.715 hp) without conversion loses 1 mark. > > **Trap 5: COP dimension.** COP is **dimensionless** (kJ/kJ). Writing COP = "kW/kW" is acceptable; "kW of cooling / kW input" is required phrasing. > > **Trap 6: Confusing primary and secondary refrigerants.** Primary = undergoes phase change (NH₃, R-134a). Secondary = sensible heat transfer only (chilled water, brine). A common 3-mark short-answer trap. > > **Trap 7: Not specifying refrigerant on P-h diagram.** Examiner expects labeled diagram with state-point enthalpies and pressures. > > **Trap 8: Missing second-law comparison.** When COP is asked, always quote **Carnot COP** for second-law efficiency context — adds 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Types of Refrigerants and Their Importance** - ✅ **Refrigerant** = working fluid in vapor-compression / absorption cycle; undergoes phase change to provide cooling. - ✅ **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min = 12,600 kJ/hr** — **memorize this.** - ✅ **State-point convention:** 1 → 2 (compression), 2 → 3 (condensation), 3 → 4 (throttling, $h_4 = h_3$), 4 → 1 (evaporation). - ✅ **Key formulas:** $RE = h_1 - h_4$; $W_c = h_2 - h_1$; $COP = RE/W_c$; $COP_{Carnot} = T_L/(T_H - T_L)$. - ✅ **CFCs (R-11, R-12):** ODP = 1.0, **banned** under Montreal Protocol 1987. - ✅ **HCFCs (R-22):** ODP ≈ 0.055, phasing out; still common in legacy systems. - ✅ **HFCs (R-134a, R-404A, R-410A):** ODP = 0, but **high GWP** (Kigali phasedown). - ✅ **HFOs (R-1234yf, R-1234ze):** ODP = 0, **GWP < 1** — future of refrigeration. - ✅ **ODP reference = R-11 (CFC-11), value 1.0**; **GWP reference = CO₂, value 1**. - ✅ **Ammonia (R-717):** Industrial king — $h_{fg} = 1377$ kJ/kg, ODP=0, GWP=0, B2L safety group, no copper. - ✅ **R-134a:** Domestic/commercial king — ODP=0, GWP=1430, A1 safety group, POE lubricant. - ✅ **CO₂ (R-744):** Transcritical cycles, hot climates, high pressure (90+ bar). - ✅ **ASHRAE Safety Group Codes:** Letter = toxicity (A < B), Number = flammability (1 < 2 < 3), L = mild flammability. - ✅ **Montreal Protocol (1987)** = ozone protection; **Kigali Amendment (2016)** = HFC phasedown. - ✅ **ASHRAE numbering rule:** R-number = (MW − 40) for saturated hydrocarbons; suffix "a/b/c" = isomers. - ✅ **Primary refrigerant** = phase change occurs (toxic OK in industrial); **Secondary refrigerant** = chilled water/brine (sensible cooling, non-toxic distribution). - ✅ **Selection criteria hierarchy:** Temperature → Capacity → Safety → ODP/GWP → Lubricant → Cost. - ✅ **TEWI** = total climate impact (direct GWP leak + indirect energy CO₂ emission over equipment life). - ✅ **Why NH₃ over HFCs industrially:** High h_fg → small equipment; low GWP → regulatory safe; easy leak detection (5 ppm odor); 10× cheaper. - ✅ **Why HFCs over NH₃ in AC:** Non-toxic, non-flammable, safe in occupied spaces, drop-in replacement for phased CFCs. - ✅ **Carnot COP** is the upper limit; real COP is 40–80% of Carnot. Lower T_L → sharply lower COP → cryogenic refrigeration is power-intensive. - ✅ **P-h diagram (Mollier diagram)** is the **standard tool** for refrigeration cycle analysis — expect a labeled sketch in 7-mark questions. ---

topics.title: Exhaust and Ventilation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Exhaust and Ventilation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Industrial Ventilation** is the engineering discipline concerned with the controlled movement of air within industrial and process-plant environments to achieve one or more of the following objectives: - Removal of contaminated air, vapors, dust, fumes, or heat from the **point of generation**. - Supply of clean (make-up) air to replace extracted volumes. - Maintenance of occupational health, fire safety, and process-quality conditions. **Exhaust ventilation** is the *active removal* of air from a localized point or a general enclosure by means of mechanical draft (fans, blowers, or ejectors), typically ducted to an atmospheric discharge stack after appropriate treatment (scrubbers, filters, cyclones, or thermal oxidizers). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag (CO4):** *"Apply the principles of process utilities such as ventilation, exhaust, and waste heat recovery in the design of chemical process plants."* — Exhaust and ventilation form the backbone of *occupational hygiene* and *emission control* in any chemical plant. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Think of a **kitchen chimney (exhaust hood)** sitting above a gas stove. When you fry something, smoke, oil mist, and water vapor are generated *right at the burner*. The hood captures these contaminants *before* they spread into the room. - **General (Dilution) Ventilation** = Opening all the windows of the house. Works only if the contaminant concentration is low and spread out. - **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** = The chimney hood. It captures contaminants *exactly where they are born*, using ducts and a blower. > [!NOTE] > **Core insight for students:** *Dilution ventilation* treats the **room**; *Local exhaust ventilation* treats the **source*. LEV is always preferred when a contaminant has a well-defined source because it is **10–1000× more efficient** than dilution and prevents worker exposure rather than merely reducing it. ## 1.3 Why It Matters in Process Plants | Application | Typical Contaminant | Typical System | |-------------|--------------------|----------------| | Reactor charging/transfer | VOC vapors | LEV at charging port | | Reactor venting | Process gases | Vent pipe + scrubber + stack | | Solvent storage tanks | VOC emissions | Conservation vent / vent stack | | Crushing / milling | Particulate dust | LEV + bag filter | | Laboratory fume hoods | Acid vapors, solvents | LEV with bypass | | Process area HVAC | Heat, humidity | Dilution ventilation | **Standard Reference Parameters (ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual):** - Capture velocity at the source: **50 – 500 ft/min** (0.25 – 2.5 m/s) - Transport velocity in duct: **3,500 – 4,500 ft/min** (17.8 – 22.9 m/s) for dusts - Vent velocity for vapors: **1,500 – 3,000 ft/min** (7.6 – 15.2 m/s) - Air Changes per Hour (ACH) for general areas: **6 – 12 ACH** - ACH for laboratories / control rooms: **10 – 20 ACH** - Design relative humidity: **40 – 60 %** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Ventilation Systems ### A. Based on **Scope of Operation** 1. **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** — captures contaminant at source. 2. **General (Dilution) Ventilation** — dilutes contaminated air with clean air. 3. **Push-Pull Ventilation** — combination of supply and exhaust air curtains. ### B. Based on **Air Movement Driver** 1. **Natural Draft Ventilation** — driven by density (stack effect) / wind. 2. **Mechanical Ventilation** — driven by fans / blowers. ### C. Based on **Process Function** 1. **Process Vent** (vent on reactor / tank for pressure relief). 2. **Combustion Vent** (flue gas discharge). 3. **Hygiene Ventilation** (occupational comfort). 4. **Emergency Vent** (pressure relief under runaway conditions). ## 2.2 Components of a Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) System ``` Hood → Ductwork → Air-Cleaning Device → Fan/Blower → Discharge Stack (capture) (transport) (scrubber/filter) (push) (release) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this order matters (KTU examiner's favourite):** Air-cleaning devices are placed **AFTER** the duct but **BEFORE** the fan. This protects the fan from corrosion/erosion and reduces maintenance. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | **Velocity Pressure (VP)** | $VP = \dfrac{\rho V^{2}}{2 g_{c}}$ | in. H₂O | $\rho$ = density (lb/ft³), V = velocity (ft/min) | | 2 | **VP in SI** | $VP = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$ | Pa | $\rho$ in kg/m³, V in m/s | | 3 | **Static Pressure (SP)** | $SP = HSP + VP$ | in. H₂O | HSP = Hood Static Pressure | | 4 | **Hood Entry Loss** | $HSP = K \cdot VP$ | in. H₂O | $K$ = entry loss coefficient | | 5 | **Duct Friction Loss (Darcy)** | $\Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^{2}}{2}$ | Pa | $f$ = Darcy friction factor | | 6 | **Equivalent Diameter (Round)** | $D_e = D$ | m | Circular duct | | 7 | **Equivalent Diameter (Rectangular)** | $D_e = \dfrac{2ab}{a+b}$ | m | $a$, $b$ = side lengths | | 8 | **Air Changes per Hour** | $ACH = \dfrac{Q}{V_{room}} \times 60$ | hr⁻¹ | Q in ft³/min, $V_{room}$ in ft³ | | 9 | **Hood Capture Velocity** | Empirical from ACGIH tables | fpm | Depends on contaminant type | | 10 | **Fan Law — Flow** | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | — | N = fan speed | | 11 | **Fan Law — Pressure** | $\dfrac{\Delta P_1}{\Delta P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^{2}$ | — | — | | 12 | **Fan Law — Power** | $\dfrac{Pwr_1}{Pwr_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^{3}$ | — | — | | 13 | **Fan Law — Air Density** | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho} = \text{constant for same N}$ | — | Density-corrected pressure | | 14 | **Vent Stack Height (Briggs equation)** | $H_s = 1.5 \, V_s \cdot D_s + 10 \, D_s$ | m | $V_s$ = stack exit velocity, $D_s$ = stack dia. | | 15 | **Effective Draft (Natural Ventilation)** | $\Delta P = H \, g \, (\rho_a - \rho_g)$ | Pa | Stack effect | | 16 | **Brake Horsepower (Fan)** | $BHP = \dfrac{Q \cdot \Delta P}{\eta \cdot 6356}$ | hp | Q in CFM, $\Delta P$ in in. H₂O, $\eta$ = fan efficiency | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** The fan laws are valid for the **same fan** at different speeds or for **geometrically similar fans**. Do *not* apply them across fans of different impeller designs. ## 2.4 Hood Types and Entry-Loss Coefficients (K) | Hood Type | Diagram (concept) | Entry Loss Coefficient $K$ | |-----------|-------------------|---------------------------| | Plain opening | Open duct end | **0.93** | | Plain flared (bell) | Tapered end | **0.25** | | Flanged opening | With rim flange | **0.40** | | Slotted hood | Narrow slot | **1.78** | | Canopy hood | Inverted cup | **0.50** | | Booth hood | Enclosed chamber | **0.25 – 1.0** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The flared hood (K = 0.25) gives 73 % lower entry loss than a plain opening (K = 0.93).** This is why industrial LEV hoods are always designed with a flare or flange. ## 2.5 Selection of Capture Velocity (ACGIH Manual, 30th Ed.) | Contaminant / Process | Capture Velocity (fpm) | Capture Velocity (m/s) | |-----------------------|------------------------|------------------------| | Vapors, gases (low toxicity) | 50 – 100 | 0.25 – 0.50 | | Solvent vapors (high toxicity) | 100 – 200 | 0.50 – 1.00 | | Welding fumes | 100 – 200 | 0.50 – 1.00 | | Grinding, polishing dust | 200 – 500 | 1.00 – 2.50 | | Hot processes (rising plume) | 100 – 200 | 0.50 – 1.00 | | Very fine dust (e.g., silica) | 200 – 500 | 1.00 – 2.50 | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pharmaceutical plants**: LEV around tablet-coating pans to capture organic solvents (Class I Div I explosive atmospheres — spark-proof fans mandated). - **Oil refineries**: Process vents from distillation columns routed to flare stacks; hydrogen sulfide → caustic scrubber. - **Acid plants**: SO₂ absorption towers vented via 60-m stacks; Briggs equation governs minimum height. - **Nuclear / heavy water plants**: Highly specialized HEPA filtration trains with 99.97 % DOP efficiency. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Velocity Pressure and Static Pressure Relationship Consider air of density $\rho$ flowing through a duct at uniform velocity $V$. **Step 1 — Kinetic energy per unit volume of air:** The kinetic energy of a mass $m$ moving at velocity $V$ is $\tfrac{1}{2} m V^{2}$. **Step 2 — Energy per unit volume:** Divide by volume $v$, so mass per unit volume is $\rho = m / v$. $$ KE_{\text{per vol}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to pressure using Bernoulli's principle:** In a flowing fluid, the kinetic-energy-per-unit-volume is equal to the velocity head, which is the **velocity pressure (VP)**: $$ VP = \frac{\rho V^{2}}{2 g_c} $$ **Step 4 — Apply English Engineering Units (lb·mass, lb·force, ft, min):** To obtain VP in inches of water (in. H₂O), use $g_c = 32.174 \dfrac{\text{lbm·ft}}{\text{lbf·s}^{2}}$ and the conversion $1\, \text{in. H}_2\text{O} = 5.202\, \text{lbf/ft}^{2}$. For air at standard conditions ($\rho = 0.075\, \text{lbm/ft}^3$): $$ VP \,(\text{in. H}_2\text{O}) = \left(\frac{V \, (\text{fpm})}{4005}\right)^{2} $$ **Step 5 — Verify for V = 4005 fpm (i.e., V = 1 in. H₂O):** $$ VP = \left(\frac{4005}{4005}\right)^{2} = 1.00 \; \text{in. H}_2\text{O} \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 6 — Static Pressure relationship:** Static pressure is the pressure measured by a tap perpendicular to flow: $$ SP = HSP + VP $$ where $HSP$ is the hood static pressure that *drives* the air into the hood (since air cannot enter unless a slight vacuum exists). ## 3.2 Derivation: Hood Entry Loss Coefficient **Step 1 — Energy balance between ambient (1) and hood entrance (2):** $$ P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^{2} = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^{2} + K \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Ambient air is essentially still, so $V_1 \to 0$ and $P_1 = P_{atm}$.** $$ P_{atm} = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^{2} (1 + K) $$ **Step 3 — Define Hood Static Pressure (HSP) as the depression below ambient:** $$ HSP = P_{atm} - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^{2} (1 + K) $$ **Step 4 — For most practical hoods, $K \gg 1$, so the "1" is sometimes dropped:** $$ HSP \approx K \cdot VP = K \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} $$ **Step 5 — Verification for a flared hood with K = 0.25 at V = 3000 fpm:** $$ VP = \left(\frac{3000}{4005}\right)^{2} = 0.561\, \text{in. H}_2\text{O} $$ $$ HSP = 0.25 \times 0.561 = 0.140\, \text{in. H}_2\text{O} \quad \checkmark $$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Fan Affinity Laws (Mechanical & Pneumatic) The fan laws are derived from **dimensional similarity** for geometrically similar fans. **Step 1 — Dimensionless flow coefficient:** For geometric similarity, the flow coefficient is constant: $$ \Phi = \frac{Q}{N D^{3}} = \text{constant} $$ Therefore: $$ \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} \cdot \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^{3} $$ For **same fan** ($D_1 = D_2$): $$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\;} $$ **Step 2 — Dimensionless pressure coefficient:** $$ \Psi = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho N^{2} D^{2}} = \text{constant} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{\Delta P_1}{\Delta P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^{2} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}\;} $$ **Step 3 — Power coefficient:** $$ \Pi = \frac{Pwr}{\rho N^{3} D^{5}} = \text{constant} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{Pwr_1}{Pwr_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^{3} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}\;} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation: Air-Changes-per-Hour Formula **Step 1 — Definition of volumetric flow rate:** $$ Q = V_{room} \cdot \frac{ACH}{60} $$ where Q is in volume per minute (e.g., CFM), $V_{room}$ in cubic units (ft³), and ACH is the dimensionless air-change rate per hour. **Step 2 — Solve for ACH explicitly:** $$ ACH = \frac{Q \times 60}{V_{room}} $$ **Step 3 — Numerical example:** A laboratory of dimensions 10 m × 8 m × 3 m requires 12 ACH. Compute required supply airflow. $$ V_{room} = 10 \times 8 \times 3 = 240\, \text{m}^{3} $$ $$ Q = \frac{12 \times 240}{60} = 48\, \text{m}^{3}/\text{min} = 0.80\, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \quad \checkmark $$ ## 3.5 Derivation: Stack Height (Briggs Equation for Plume Rise) **Step 1 — Momentum rise of a buoyant plume:** The plume rises due to vertical momentum after leaving the stack: $$ \Delta h = 1.5 \cdot V_s \cdot D_s $$ **Step 2 — Add safety margin for downwash dispersion (downwind distance ≤ 10 stack diameters):** $$ H_s = 1.5 \, V_s D_s + 10 \, D_s $$ **Step 3 — Numerical evaluation (typical values):** $V_s = 12\, \text{m/s}$, $D_s = 1.2\, \text{m}$: $$ H_s = 1.5 \times 12 \times 1.2 + 10 \times 1.2 = 21.6 + 12.0 = 33.6\, \text{m} \quad \checkmark $$ ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Design Strategy Summary | Problem Type | Key Equation | Step Sequence | |--------------|--------------|---------------| | Hood sizing | $Q = V_c \cdot A_{hood}$ | (i) Choose $V_c$ from ACGIH, (ii) Compute hood area, (iii) Compute Q | | Duct sizing | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi V_t}}$ | (i) Choose $V_t$, (ii) Compute D, (iii) Round to standard pipe | | Pressure drop | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^{2}}{2}$ | (i) Compute Re, (ii) Read f from Moody chart, (iii) Compute ΔP | | Fan selection | $Q_{fan} \geq \Sigma Q$, $SP_{fan} \geq \Sigma \Delta P$ | (i) Sum branches, (ii) Add stack loss, (iii) Pick fan from curve | | ACH problem | $Q = \dfrac{ACH \cdot V}{60}$ | (i) Identify contaminant, (ii) Use ACGIH ACH table, (iii) Compute Q | | Stack height | $H_s = 1.5 V_s D_s + 10 D_s$ | (i) Get $V_s$ from fan, (ii) Compute $H_s$, (iii) Add plume rise if applicable | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[SOURCE OF CONTAMINANT
Reactor / Tank / Process Equipment] --> B[HOOD OR CAPTURE ELEMENT
Plain, Flared, Booth or Canopy Type] B --> C[DUCTWORK NETWORK
Main Header plus Branches] C --> D[AIR CLEANING UNIT
Scrubber, Cyclone, Bag Filter or HEPA] D --> E[FAN OR BLOWER
Centrifugal, Axial or Inline Type] E --> F[EXHAUST STACK
Vertical Discharge with Rain Cap] F --> G[ATMOSPHERIC DISPERSION
Buoyant Plume and Downwash Zone] H[CLEAN MAKE UP AIR INLET] --> I[PROCESS ROOM OR WORK ZONE] I --> A D -.filtered dust and liquor.-> J[WASTE TREATMENT FACILITY] ``` ## 4.2 Hood Classification Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB H[INDUSTRIAL HOODS] --> H1[ENCLOSING TYPE
Booth, Cabinet, Fume Hood] H --> H2[EXTERNAL OR RECEIVING TYPE
Canopy, Slot, Side Draft] H --> H3[FLANGED TYPE
Plain Flanged and Bell Mouth Hood] H1 --> H1a[Best capture efficiency
Encloses source fully] H2 --> H2a[Used above hot processes
Requires rising plume] H3 --> H3a[Lowest K value
Energy efficient] ``` ## 4.3 Ventilation System Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S[CONTAMINANT IDENTIFICATION] --> Q1{Has defined point source?} Q1 -- YES --> LEV[DESIGN LOCAL EXHAUST VENTILATION
Hood plus Duct plus Fan plus Stack] Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is contaminant toxic or explosive?} Q2 -- YES --> Q3[Increase ACH and add dedicated exhaust] Q2 -- NO --> DIV[DESIGN DILUTION VENTILATION
General supply and exhaust fans] LEV --> AC[Compute capture velocity from ACGIH tables] AC --> AD[Compute duct transport velocity and diameter] AD --> AP[Compute static pressure losses through hood, duct, fittings] AP --> AF[Select fan using combined Q and SP] AF --> AS[Design stack height using Briggs equation] ``` ## 4.4 Pressure-Loss Cascade in a Typical LEV System ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Hood Entry Loss
HSP equals K times VP] --> P2[Straight Duct Friction
f times L over D times VP] P2 --> P3[Fitting Losses
Elbows, Tees, Reducers] P3 --> P4[Air Cleaning Device
Scrubber, Cyclone, Filter] P4 --> P5[Discharge Loss
VP at stack exit] P5 --> P6[Stack Effect
Plus natural draft assist] P6 --> FAN[FAN TOTAL PRESSURE RISE
Sum of all above components] ``` ## 4.5 Process Plant Vent Header Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR V1[Reactor 1
Vent Valve] --> VH[Common Vent Header
Sized for all relief cases] V2[Reactor 2
Vent Valve] --> VH V3[Storage Tank 1
Pressure Vent] --> VH V4[Storage Tank 2
Pressure Vent] --> VH VH --> FB[Flame Arrestor
or Knockout Drum] FB --> SC[Scrubber or Absorber
Removes soluble or reactive components] SC --> FS[Flare Stack
or Thermal Oxidizer] FS --> AT[Atmospheric Discharge
Above grade and prevailing wind zone] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why a vent header (KTU board favourite):** Multiple reactor and tank vents are manifolded into a single header so that treatment (scrubber / flare) is centralized — saving capital cost and improving emissions accounting. Each vessel has a **pressure safety valve (PSV)** upstream to protect against runaway. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between local exhaust ventilation (LEV) and dilution (general) ventilation. State when each is preferred in a process plant.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) | Dilution Ventilation | |--------|----------------------------------|----------------------| | Scope | Captures contaminant **at source** | Dilutes contaminant in the **entire room** | | Efficiency | High (90–99 %) | Low (often < 50 %) | | Airflow volume | Small, focused | Large, generalized | | Cost | Higher capital, lower operating | Lower capital, higher operating | | Preferred when | Source is **well-defined**, contaminant is **toxic / flammable / carcinogenic** | Source is **diffuse**, contaminant is **low-toxicity**, heat removal is the goal | **Preferred in process plants:** - LEV is preferred at charging ports, reactors, grinding mills, welding stations. - Dilution ventilation is used for general process-area comfort and as **make-up air** to compensate for LEV extraction. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is the significance of "capture velocity" in the design of a local exhaust hood? Mention typical values for solvent vapors and grinding operations as per ACGIH.** **Model Answer:** **Capture velocity** is the air velocity that must be created at the **point of contaminant generation** (just outside the hood) so that the air stream overcomes any cross-drafts and entrains the contaminant into the hood opening. If $V_c$ is too low, contaminants escape into the breathing zone. If it is too high, energy is wasted and turbulence increases. **Typical ACGIH values:** - Solvent vapors (low toxicity) : **50 – 100 fpm** (0.25 – 0.50 m/s) - Welding fumes : **100 – 200 fpm** (0.50 – 1.00 m/s) - Grinding and polishing dust : **200 – 500 fpm** (1.00 – 2.50 m/s) > [!WARNING] > **Common mistake (3-mark penalty risk):** Students often confuse **capture velocity** with **transport velocity**. Capture velocity is at the *source* (50–500 fpm). Transport velocity is *inside the duct* (1,500–4,500 fpm). These are **not** interchangeable. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set A]** **Design a local exhaust ventilation system for a solvent-charging operation in a pharmaceutical reactor. The hood is a flared bell-mouth type located 0.5 m above the charging port. The duct is 25 m long, 0.20 m diameter mild steel circular pipe. Volumetric flow is 0.50 m³/s. Air density = 1.20 kg/m³. Darcy friction factor = 0.02. There are two standard 90° elbows. Determine:** **(a) Capture velocity at the charging port and verify against ACGIH. (7 marks)** **(b) Total static pressure the fan must overcome. (7 marks)** --- #### Solution to A(a): **Step 1 — Volumetric flow:** $$ Q = 0.50\, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} = 30\, \text{m}^{3}/\text{min} = 1060\, \text{CFM} $$ **Step 2 — Velocity at the hood opening (transport velocity $V_t$):** For a flared hood with K = 0.25 and assuming standard hood area $A_h$ chosen such that $V_t = 15\, \text{m/s}$ (typical for vapour exhaust): $$ A_h = \frac{Q}{V_t} = \frac{0.50}{15} = 0.0333\, \text{m}^{2} $$ Assuming circular hood of diameter $D_h$: $$ D_h = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_h}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.0333}{\pi}} = 0.206\, \text{m} $$ **Step 3 — Capture velocity at the source (0.5 m away):** Empirical ACGIH equation for axial hood (from Sutton / ACGIH Manual): $$ \frac{V_c}{V_h} = \frac{A_h}{10 \cdot X^{1.5}} $$ where X = distance (ft). Converting 0.5 m = 1.64 ft, and $V_h = V_t = 15\, \text{m/s} \approx 2950\, \text{fpm}$: $$ V_c = \frac{2950 \times A_h}{10 \times (1.64)^{1.5}} $$ With $A_h = 0.0333\, \text{m}^{2} \approx 0.358\, \text{ft}^{2}$: $$ V_c = \frac{2950 \times 0.358}{10 \times 2.10} = \frac{1056}{21.0} \approx 50.3\, \text{fpm} \approx 0.25\, \text{m/s} $$ **Step 4 — Verify against ACGIH:** Solvent vapour, low toxicity, requires **50 – 100 fpm**. The calculated $V_c \approx 50$ fpm is at the **lower limit — borderline acceptable**. Recommended improvement: increase $V_t$ to 18 m/s, which raises $V_c$ to ≈ 60 fpm. ✓ --- #### Solution to A(b): **Step 1 — Velocity pressure in duct:** $$ V_{duct} = \frac{Q}{A_{duct}} = \frac{0.50}{\pi \times (0.10)^{2}} = 15.92\, \text{m/s} $$ $$ VP = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.20 \times (15.92)^{2} = 152.1\, \text{Pa} $$ **Step 2 — Hood entry loss (K = 0.25 for flared hood):** $$ \Delta P_{hood} = K \cdot VP = 0.25 \times 152.1 = 38.0\, \text{Pa} $$ **Step 3 — Straight duct friction (Darcy):** $$ \Delta P_{duct} = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^{2}}{2} = 0.02 \times \frac{25}{0.20} \times 152.1 = 0.02 \times 125 \times 152.1 = 380.3\, \text{Pa} $$ **Step 4 — Elbow losses (two 90° standard elbows, K = 0.75 each):** $$ \Delta P_{elbows} = 2 \times 0.75 \times 152.1 = 228.2\, \text{Pa} $$ **Step 5 — Discharge (exit) loss (K = 1.0, full velocity into atmosphere):** $$ \Delta P_{exit} = 1.0 \times 152.1 = 152.1\, \text{Pa} $$ **Step 6 — Total static pressure:** $$ SP_{total} = 38.0 + 380.3 + 228.2 + 152.1 = 798.6\, \text{Pa} \approx 3.20\, \text{in. H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 7 — Add 10 % safety margin:** $$ SP_{design} = 1.10 \times 798.6 \approx 878\, \text{Pa} \approx 3.52\, \text{in. H}_2\text{O} $$ **Fan selection:** Choose a centrifugal fan rated at Q = 0.50 m³/s (≈ 1060 CFM) and SP ≥ 878 Pa (≈ 3.5 in. H₂O). ✓ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set B]** *(Alternative Choice)* **A reactor process area has dimensions 25 m × 15 m × 6 m. The designer recommends 12 air changes per hour (ACH) due to mild solvent vapours.** **(a) Calculate the volumetric flow rate of supply and exhaust air, and verify using the dilution ventilation equation. (7 marks)** **(b) The fan selected runs at 1450 rpm. If the speed is increased to 1750 rpm, find the new flow, new pressure, and new power consumption using fan affinity laws. Assume air density unchanged. (7 marks)** --- #### Solution to B(a): **Step 1 — Room volume:** $$ V_{room} = 25 \times 15 \times 6 = 2250\, \text{m}^{3} $$ **Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate required:** $$ Q = \frac{ACH \cdot V_{room}}{60} = \frac{12 \times 2250}{60} = 450\, \text{m}^{3}/\text{min} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to m³/s:** $$ Q = \frac{450}{60} = 7.5\, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to CFM (1 m³/s = 2119 CFM):** $$ Q = 7.5 \times 2119 \approx 15,890\, \text{CFM} $$ **Step 5 — Verification using dilution equation:** $$ C = \frac{G}{Q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = \frac{G}{C} $$ where $G$ = contaminant generation rate (g/s) and $C$ = desired concentration (g/m³). For $G = 0.5\, \text{g/s}$ and desired concentration $C = 0.067\, \text{g/m}^{3}$ (TWA limit): $$ Q = \frac{0.5}{0.067} \approx 7.46\, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 7.5\, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \quad \checkmark $$ **Result:** Supply fan and exhaust fan each sized for **7.5 m³/s (15,890 CFM)**. The exhaust rate must equal supply rate to maintain balanced room pressure. ✓ --- #### Solution to B(b): **Given:** $N_1 = 1450\, \text{rpm}$, $N_2 = 1750\, \text{rpm}$, original $Q_1 = 7.5\, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$, original $\Delta P_1 = 1000\, \text{Pa}$, original $P_1 = 5\, \text{kW}$. **Fan Law 1 — New Flow:** $$ \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1750}{1450} = 1.207 $$ $$ Q_2 = 1.207 \times 7.5 = 9.05\, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 19,170\, \text{CFM} $$ **Fan Law 2 — New Pressure:** $$ \frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2} = (1.207)^{2} = 1.456 $$ $$ \Delta P_2 = 1.456 \times 1000 = 1456\, \text{Pa} \approx 5.84\, \text{in. H}_2\text{O} $$ **Fan Law 3 — New Power:** $$ \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3} = (1.207)^{3} = 1.758 $$ $$ P_2 = 1.758 \times 5 = 8.79\, \text{kW} $$ **Step 4 — Verification (Sanity check):** A 20 % increase in speed produces a **20 %** flow increase, a **45.6 %** pressure increase, and a **75.8 %** power increase. This dramatic cubic dependence on speed is **why speed reduction is the most effective fan energy-saving strategy**. ✓ --- ## ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks (Examiners' pitfall list):** > 1. **Confusing VP and SP:** Velocity pressure is the *kinetic* component; static pressure is the *depression* below ambient. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks per sub-question. > 2. **Ignoring hood entry loss K:** Many students compute only duct friction. The hood is a significant contributor (~5–10 % of total SP) — do not omit it. > 3. **Wrong units for VP:** Use **in. H₂O** for English units (with V in fpm and 4005 factor), and **Pa** for SI units. Mixing units is a guaranteed 0.5-mark cut. > 4. **Forgetting fan safety margin:** Industry practice (and KTU valuation key) requires 10–15 % margin on SP. Don't compute the bare total. > 5. **Not stating ACH origin:** When solving ACH problems, mention that 12 ACH is **typical for chemical process areas with solvent vapours** (per ACGIH / ISHRAE). > 6. **Skipping discharge / exit loss:** Velocity at stack exit is lost as kinetic energy — always include K = 1.0 × VP at discharge. > 7. **Wrong density assumptions:** For hot exhaust gases, ρ changes — use $\rho_g$ (gas density at the operating temperature), not 1.20 kg/m³. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **LEV (Local Exhaust Ventilation)** captures contaminant *at source* — preferred when the source is defined and the contaminant is toxic/flammable. - ✅ **Dilution ventilation** dilutes contaminant in the entire room — used for heat removal, low-toxicity contaminants, and as make-up air. - ✅ **Capture velocity** ($V_c$, 50–500 fpm) is at the *source*. **Transport velocity** ($V_t$, 1,500–4,500 fpm) is in the *duct*. **Don't confuse.** - ✅ **Components order:** Hood → Duct → Cleaner → Fan → Stack. *Cleaner must precede the fan* to protect the fan. - ✅ **Velocity Pressure formula:** $VP = (\rho V^{2})/2$ in SI; $VP = (V/4005)^{2}$ in English (in. H₂O). - ✅ **Static Pressure:** $SP = HSP + VP$. Hood static pressure drives air into the hood. - ✅ **Hood entry loss coefficient K:** 0.25 for flared (best), 0.93 for plain opening. Lower K → lower energy cost. - ✅ **Fan laws (same fan):** $Q \propto N$, $\Delta P \propto N^{2}$, $Pwr \propto N^{3}$ — speed control is the most powerful energy-saving knob. - ✅ **ACH (Air Changes per Hour):** Typical values: 6–12 ACH for process areas, 10–20 ACH for labs, 15–25 ACH for paint booths. - ✅ **Briggs equation** for stack height: $H_s = 1.5 V_s D_s + 10 D_s$ — accounts for momentum rise and downwash. - ✅ **Discharge loss at stack exit:** $\Delta P_{exit} = 1.0 \times VP$ — never omit this. - ✅ **Always add 10–15 % safety margin** to fan SP before selection. - ✅ **Process vent headers** manifold multiple vessels into a common scrubber/flare for centralized treatment. - ✅ **Natural draft (stack effect):** $\Delta P = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{air} - \rho_{gas})$ — drives ventilation without fans in tall stacks. - ✅ **Captures velocity table (ACGIH):** Evaporation 50–100 fpm; Solvents 100–200 fpm; Welding 100–200 fpm; Grinding 200–500 fpm. - ✅ **Fan selection criteria:** $Q_{fan} \geq \Sigma Q_{branches}$ and $SP_{fan} \geq \Sigma \Delta P_{losses} \times 1.10$.

topics.title: Introduction to Piping: Classification of Pipes, Pipe Materials, Pipe Sizing, Schedule Number, Codes and Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Piping: Classification of Pipes, Pipe Materials, Pipe Sizing, Schedule Number, Codes and Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Piping — The Arterial Network of a Process Plant

A **piping system** is an integrated assembly of pipes, fittings, flanges, gaskets, bolts, valves, and supporting elements designed to convey fluids (liquids, gases, vapours, slurries, or multiphase mixtures) safely and economically between process units, storage facilities, and utility systems within an industrial plant. In the context of **Process Utilities (OECHT611)**, piping forms the critical backbone that interconnects boilers, cooling towers, compressors, heat exchangers, distillation columns, reactors, and storage tanks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A pipe is a tubular section, usually circular in cross-section, used for the conveyance of fluids. Piping refers to the complete network comprising pipes, fittings, flanges, valves, and accessories along with their supports and insulation, designed and installed as per recognized codes and standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):** The learner is expected to classify pipes by service and material, determine pipe dimensions using schedule numbers and sizing criteria, and identify the governing codes (ASME B31 series, ASTM, ANSI, API, ISO, IS) applicable to industrial piping systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Piping as the "Circulatory System" of a Plant

Imagine a chemical plant as a **living human body**:
- **Pipes** are the *arteries and veins* that transport vital fluids.
- **Pumps and compressors** act as the *heart*, providing the driving pressure.
- **Valves** are the *sphincters* that regulate flow.
- **Heat exchangers** function as the *lungs and skin*, exchanging thermal energy.
- **Pipe supports and hangers** are the *skeletal system*, holding everything in place.

Just as a cardiologist must choose the right vessel diameter, material, and pressure rating for each artery, a process engineer must select the correct **pipe size, schedule, material, and code** to ensure safe, leak-tight, and energy-efficient operation. A mismatched artery causes a stroke; a wrongly specified pipe causes a rupture, fire, or toxic release.

## 1.3 Why Pipe Selection Matters — The Engineering Stakes

The selection of a pipe is governed by **four fundamental design parameters**, often remembered by the mnemonic **F-M-S-P** (Fluid, Material, Size, Pressure):

| Parameter | Engineering Concern | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Fluid (F)** | Corrosivity, toxicity, flammability, viscosity, density | Aggressive acids, hydrocarbons, steam, water |
| **Material (M)** | Compatibility, temperature, mechanical strength | Carbon steel, SS-304, SS-316, Hastelloy, HDPE, FRP |
| **Size (S)** | Flow rate, allowable velocity, pressure drop | NPS ½" to NPS 48"+ (DN 15 to DN 1200) |
| **Pressure (P)** | Design pressure, allowable stress, temperature rating | Up to 2500 bar in critical service |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single miscalculation in **Schedule Number** (wall thickness) for a high-pressure hydrogen line, for instance, can lead to catastrophic failure — the **Hindustan Petroleum Visakh Refinery fire (1997)** and the **Texas City Refinery explosion (2005)** were both partially attributed to piping non-conformance with ASME B31.3.

## 1.4 The "Piping Triad" — The Three Pillars of Pipe Specification

Every pipe specification in a Process and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) consists of three inseparable components, written as a "**Piping Class**" or "**Pipe Specification**":

$$\text{Pipe Specification} = \underbrace{\text{Material}}_{\text{chemical/mechanical}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Schedule}}_{\text{thickness}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{End Connection}}_{\text{joint type}}$$

For example: **"1½" Sch 40, A106-Gr.B, BW (Butt Weld), SMLS (Seamless)"** fully describes a pipe used for high-temperature hydrocarbon service.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Pipes — A Multi-Dimensional Taxonomy

Pipes are classified along **seven independent axes**, each serving a specific engineering purpose.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Manufacturing Method

| Category | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Seamless (SMLS)** | Made by piercing solid billets of steel | High-pressure, high-temperature service (boiler tubes, hydrocracker piping) |
| **Welded (ERW / EFW)** | Made by rolling plate/sheet and welding the seam | Low-to-medium pressure, large diameters (water mains, air ducts) |
| **Fabricated (FAB)** | Built from rolled plates welded longitudinally | Very large diameters (>24"), low pressure (chutes, ducting) |
| **Cast (CI / DI)** | Manufactured by casting molten iron | Underground water/sewage networks |

> [!NOTE]
> **ERW (Electric Resistance Welded)** uses induction or contact welding; **EFW (Electric Fusion Welded)** uses an external filler metal. **EFW** is preferred for larger diameters and thicker walls per ASME B36.10M.

### 2.1.2 Classification by Wall Thickness / Schedule

| Category | Wall Thickness (relative) | Common Schedules |
|---|---|---|
| **Thin-wall** | Low | Sch 5, Sch 10, Sch 10S, Sch 20 |
| **Standard** | Medium | Sch 40 (STD) |
| **Extra Strong** | High | Sch 80 (XS) |
| **Double Extra Strong** | Very high | Sch 160, XXS |

### 2.1.3 Classification by Service / Application

| Service Class | Fluid | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Piping** | Raw materials, intermediates, products | CS, SS, alloy steels |
| **Utility Piping** | Steam, water, air, nitrogen, fuel | Carbon steel (ASTM A53, A106) |
| **Power Piping** | High-pressure steam, boiler feed water | Alloy steels (A335 P11/P22/P91) |
| **Pipeline** | Crude oil, natural gas (cross-country) | API 5L X-grade steels |
| **Hygienic / Sanitary** | Food, pharma, water-for-injection (WFI) | SS-316L, electropolished |

### 2.1.4 Classification by Material (Broad Families)

1. **Ferrous Metals**: Carbon Steel (CS), Cast Iron (CI), Ductile Iron (DI), Alloy Steels, Stainless Steels (austenitic, ferritic, duplex, super-duplex).
2. **Non-Ferrous Metals**: Copper, Aluminium, Brass, Titanium, Nickel alloys (Inconel, Monel, Hastelloy).
3. **Non-Metallic**: PVC, CPVC, HDPE, PP, PVDF, FRP, glass, ceramics.
4. **Lined Pipes**: Metallic outer with internal lining (PTFE, rubber, glass, PE).

### 2.1.5 Classification by Joining Method

- **Butt-Welded (BW)** — strongest, used for high-pressure.
- **Socket-Welded (SW)** — for small sizes (≤ NPS 2"), non-critical.
- **Threaded (NPT/BSP)** — low pressure, fire-water, instrument air.
- **Flanged** — for detachable joints, equipment nozzles.
- **Compression / Push-fit** — for utilities, instrumentation.

### 2.1.6 Classification by Cross-Sectional Geometry

- **Circular** (universal, structurally efficient).
- **Square / Rectangular** (used in HVAC ducting, low-pressure gas streams).
- **Special (annular, finned, oval)** — heat transfer applications.

### 2.1.7 Classification by Operating Pressure / Temperature

| Class | Pressure Range (psig) | Typical Service |
|---|---|---|
| **Class 150** | Up to 285 psig @ 100°F | Low-pressure utilities |
| **Class 300** | Up to 740 psig | General process |
| **Class 600 / 900** | Up to 1480–2220 psig | Hydrocarbon process |
| **Class 1500 / 2500** | Up to 3700–6170 psig | High-pressure reactors |

## 2.2 Pipe Materials — Detailed Engineering Catalog

### 2.2.1 Carbon Steel (CS) — The Workhorse of the Industry

- **ASTM A53 (Gr. A/B)**: General service, welded and seamless, up to 750 °F.
- **ASTM A106 (Gr. A/B/C)**: Seamless, high-temperature (up to 850 °F), refinery service.
- **ASTM A333 (Gr. 1/3/6)**: Low-temperature carbon steel (down to -50 °F).
- **ASTM A671 / A672**: Electric-Fusion-Welded for atmospheric/lower temperatures.
- **ASTM A691**: EFW for high-pressure, high-temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanical Property Anchor (ASTM A106 Gr. B):**
> - Tensile Strength: **415 MPa (60,000 psi) minimum**
> - Yield Strength: **240 MPa (35,000 psi) minimum**
> - Elongation: **30% minimum in 2 inches**

### 2.2.2 Stainless Steel (SS) — The Corrosion Champion

| Type | Composition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **SS 304** | 18% Cr, 8% Ni | General food, dairy, low-temp chemical |
| **SS 316** | 16% Cr, 10% Ni, 2% Mo | Chloride-bearing, marine, pharma |
| **SS 316L** | Low-carbon 316 | Welded fabrication, resistance to sensitization |
| **SS 321** | Ti-stabilized | High-temperature (up to 870 °C) |
| **SS 347** | Nb-stabilized | High-temp, weld-heavy service |
| **Duplex 2205** | 22% Cr, 5% Ni, 3% Mo | Seawater, oil & gas, chloride stress corrosion |
| **Super-Duplex 2507** | 25% Cr, 7% Ni, 4% Mo | Severe corrosive, HPHT wells |

> [!NOTE]
> The "**L**" in 304L/316L denotes **Low Carbon** (≤ 0.03%) — essential for welded fabrication to prevent *intergranular corrosion* caused by chromium carbide precipitation at grain boundaries.

### 2.2.3 Non-Ferrous Alloys

- **Copper & Copper Alloys (Cu-Ni 90/10, 70/30)**: Seawater, desalination.
- **Titanium Gr. 2 / Gr. 7**: Wet chlorine, hot oxidizing acids, deep-sea.
- **Nickel Alloys**:
  - **Inconel 600/625** — high temp, oxidation.
  - **Monel 400** — hydrofluoric acid, sea water.
  - **Hastelloy C-276** — most severe chemical service (HCl, H₂SO₄, Cl₂).

### 2.2.4 Non-Metallic Pipes

| Material | Max Temp (°C) | Max Pressure | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PVC** | 60 | 16 bar | Drainage, water distribution |
| **CPVC** | 93 | 16 bar | Hot water, chemical transfer |
| **HDPE** | 60 | 16 bar | Gas distribution, marine outfalls |
| **PP (Polypropylene)** | 100 | 10 bar | Chemical drainage |
| **PVDF (Kynar®)** | 140 | 16 bar | Ultra-pure water, aggressive acids |
| **FRP / GRP** | 120 | 25 bar | Corrosive chemical, sea water intake |
| **PTFE-lined steel** | 230 | 25 bar | Highly corrosive chemicals (HCl, H₂SO₄) |

## 2.3 Pipe Sizing — The Heart of Hydraulic Design

Pipe sizing determines the **nominal diameter (NPS/DN)** such that the line operates within:
1. **Acceptable velocity limits** (to prevent erosion, water hammer, noise).
2. **Acceptable pressure drop** (per unit length of pipe).
3. **Two-phase flow regime** considerations.

### 2.3.1 Sizing for Single-Phase Liquids

The fundamental relationship is:

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot \dot{m}}{\pi \cdot \rho \cdot v}}$$

where:
- $D$ = inside diameter (m)
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)
- $v$ = average velocity (m/s)

Or, in volumetric form:

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot v}}$$

where $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Rules of Thumb (KTU-Standard Practice):**
> - **Liquids (general)**: 1.0 – 3.0 m/s (use 1.5 – 2.5 m/s for hydrocarbon liquids).
> - **Water (pump suction)**: 0.6 – 1.5 m/s (to avoid cavitation).
> - **Water (pump discharge)**: 1.5 – 3.5 m/s.
> - **Saturated steam**: 25 – 40 m/s.
> - **Superheated steam**: 35 – 60 m/s.
> - **Compressed air / gases**: 15 – 30 m/s.
> - **High-pressure gas (>50 bar)**: 8 – 20 m/s.

### 2.3.2 Sizing for Compressible Gases

For gases, a more rigorous approach uses the **general flow equation** based on isothermal compressible flow:

$$\dfrac{Q_{sc}}{P_{sc}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2/5} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{5 \cdot D}{8 \cdot f \cdot L \cdot G \cdot T_{sc} \cdot Z}} \cdot \dfrac{(P_1^2 - P_2^2)^{1/2}}{T} \quad \text{(Weymouth) }$$

A simpler **pressure-correction** method uses the volumetric flow rate at operating conditions:

$$D = \left[ \dfrac{8 \cdot f \cdot L \cdot \dot{m}^{2} \cdot R \cdot T}{\pi^{2} \cdot (P_1^{2} - P_2^{2}) \cdot M} \right]^{1/5}$$

where $f$ is the Darcy friction factor, $L$ the line length, $M$ the molecular mass, $R$ the universal gas constant, and $Z$ the compressibility factor.

### 2.3.3 Pressure Drop — Darcy–Weisbach Equation

The most general and rigorous pressure-drop equation is:

$$\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \cdot v^{2}}{2}$$

where:
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop (Pa)
- $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless, related to Reynolds number and relative roughness)
- $L$ = equivalent length (m), including fittings
- $D$ = internal diameter (m)
- $\rho$ = density (kg/m³)
- $v$ = average velocity (m/s)

Equivalent length contributions from fittings are added to the straight length:

$$L_{eq,total} = L_{straight} + \sum_{i} K_i \cdot \dfrac{D}{f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For fully turbulent flow in commercial steel pipe ($Re > 10^5$, $\varepsilon/D \approx 0.0002$), the **Fanning friction factor** $f_{Fanning} \approx 0.005$, and the **Darcy friction factor** $f_{Darcy} = 4 \cdot f_{Fanning} \approx 0.020$.

## 2.4 Schedule Number — The Wall-Thickness DNA

### 2.4.1 Historical Origin

The Schedule Number was introduced in **1939 by the American Standards Association (ASA)**, derived from the **Birmingham Wire Gauge (BWG)** formula to standardize pipe wall thickness independently of material grade:

$$\text{Schedule Number} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot P}{S}$$

where:
- $P$ = design internal pressure (psi)
- $S$ = allowable fiber stress (psi) at the design temperature

### 2.4.2 Relationship to Wall Thickness (Modern Form)

For a given **Schedule Number (Sch)** and **Nominal Pipe Size (NPS)** with outside diameter $D_o$:

$$t = \dfrac{\text{Sch} \times D_o}{1000 \cdot 12.5 + \text{Sch} \times 0.5} \quad \text{(approximate, in inches)}$$

A more accurate ASME B36.10M closed-form relationship is:

$$t_{\text{inch}} = \dfrac{\text{Sch} \times D_o}{1000 \times (1 + \text{Sch}/2000)}$$

The standardized inside diameter then becomes:

$$D_i = D_o - 2 \cdot t$$

### 2.4.3 Standard Schedule Numbers (ASME B36.10M / B36.19M)

| Schedule No. | Wall Designation |
|---|---|
| 5, 10, 10S, 20, 30 | Thin-wall |
| 40, **STD** (Standard) | Medium-wall |
| 60 | Medium-heavy |
| 80, **XS** (Extra Strong) | Heavy-wall |
| 100, 120, 140 | Extra-heavy |
| 160 | Very heavy |
| **XXS** (Double Extra Strong) | Heaviest standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The suffixes **"S"** in 10S, 40S, 80S denote **Stainless Steel Schedule** as per **ASME B36.19M**, which uses a thinner-wall philosophy (originally for corrosion allowance only) than B36.10M carbon-steel pipes.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \sqrt{4Q / (\pi v)}$ | Inside diameter from flow rate and velocity | m, m³/s, m/s |
| 2 | $D = \sqrt{4\dot{m} / (\pi \rho v)}$ | Diameter from mass flow rate and density | m, kg/s, kg/m³, m/s |
| 3 | $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\Delta P = f \cdot (L/D) \cdot (\rho v^{2}/2)$ | Darcy-Weisbach pressure drop | Pa |
| 5 | $v = Q / A = 4Q / (\pi D^{2})$ | Average velocity | m/s |
| 6 | $A = \pi D^{2}/4$ | Cross-sectional area | m² |
| 7 | $\text{Sch No} = 1000 \cdot P / S$ | Schedule number | dimensionless |
| 8 | $D_i = D_o - 2t$ | Inside diameter from OD and wall thickness | m |
| 9 | $t = (P \cdot D_o)/(2 \cdot S \cdot E - 1.2 \cdot P)$ | Barlow's wall thickness (pressure design) | m, Pa |
| 10 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | Mass flow rate | kg/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **E** in the Barlow formula is the **joint efficiency** (1.0 for seamless, 0.85 for ERW, 0.80 for EFW, per ASME B31.3). Always convert all units to a **single system (SI or FPS)** before substitution — examiners award zero credit for unit-mismatch errors.

## 2.6 Codes and Standards — The Legal and Engineering Framework

Codes and standards form the **"Rulebook"** of piping design, ensuring safety, interchangeability, and global acceptance. Major organizations and their roles:

| Organization | Code | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **ASME** (American Society of Mechanical Engineers) | B31.1, B31.3, B31.4, B31.5, B31.8, B31.9 | Pressure piping, power, process, gas, building services |
| **ANSI** (American National Standards Institute) | Coordinates U.S. standards | Accreditation, harmonization |
| **ASTM** (American Society for Testing and Materials) | A53, A106, A312, A333, A335, B16.9, B16.11, B16.34 | Material specifications, fittings, valves |
| **API** (American Petroleum Institute) | API 5L, 5CT, 5L X-grade | Line pipe, casing, tubing for oil & gas |
| **MSS** (Manufacturers Standardization Society) | SP-43, SP-75, SP-97 | Valves, fittings, branch connections |
| **ISO** (International Organization for Standardization) | ISO 4200, ISO 1127, ISO 14692 | Plain-end steel tubes, stainless, GRP |
| **DIN** (Deutsches Institut für Normung) | DIN 2448, DIN 2527, DIN 2573 | German/European pipe and flange standards |
| **BS** (British Standards) | BS 1600, BS 1640, BS 534 | UK piping standards (older) |
| **IS** (Indian Standards, BIS) | IS 1239, IS 3589, IS 1978, IS 2062 | Indian pipe and steel standards |

### 2.6.1 The ASME B31 Series — The Crown Jewels of Piping Codes

| Code | Title | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **ASME B31.1** | Power Piping | Power plants, boiler external piping, high-pressure steam |
| **ASME B31.3** | Process Piping | Refineries, chemical, pharmaceutical, cryogenic, plants — **THE** most-used code |
| **ASME B31.4** | Pipeline Transportation Systems for Liquid Hydrocarbons and Other Liquids | Cross-country crude/product pipelines |
| **ASME B31.5** | Refrigeration Piping and Heat Transfer Components | HVAC, ammonia/CO₂ refrigerant lines |
| **ASME B31.8** | Gas Transmission and Distribution Piping Systems | Natural gas transmission/distribution |
| **ASME B31.9** | Building Services Piping | HVAC, water, gas in commercial buildings |
| **ASME B31.12** | Hydrogen Piping and Pipelines | Hydrogen economy (emerging) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** For chemical/refinery process plants (the focus of OECHT611), the **dominant code is ASME B31.3 (Process Piping)**. It categorizes fluids into **Normal Fluid Service, Category M (high-toxicity), Category D (non-flammable, non-toxic), and High Pressure**, each with progressively stricter design, testing, and inspection requirements.

### 2.6.2 Indian Standards (BIS) — The National Context

| IS Code | Title | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 1239 (Part 1 & 2)** | Mild Steel Tubes, Tubulars and Other Wrought Steel Fittings | General low-pressure water, air, gas |
| **IS 3589** | Steel Pipes for Water and Sewage | Large-diameter water mains |
| **IS 1978** | Line Pipe for Petroleum Industry | Cross-country hydrocarbon lines |
| **IS 2062** | Hot Rolled Medium and High Tensile Structural Steel | Pipe supports, structural fabrication |
| **IS 4267** | Socket-Weld and Threaded Carbon Steel and Stainless Steel Fittings | Forged fittings |
| **IS 6392** | Steel Pipe Flanges | Pipe flange dimensions (PN ratings) |

## 2.7 Why This System Matters — Real-World Engineering Utility

The pipe-classification-schedule-codes triad is not academic bureaucracy; it is a **lives-saving, money-saving framework** with concrete applications:

1. **Procurement and Logistics**: A single ASME B36.10M Sch 40 pipe of NPS 6" is interchangeable worldwide — no custom fabrication needed.
2. **Safety and Liability**: Adherence to ASME B31.3 is a legal defense in incident investigations. Non-adherence is often the proximate cause of accidents.
3. **Cost Optimization**: Selecting Sch 10 over Sch 40 where allowable saves ~50% in pipe material cost and ~30% in welding labour for a 10-km pipeline.
4. **Inter-discipline Coordination**: Process, Mechanical, Civil, and Instrumentation engineers speak the same "pipe-spec language" on the P&ID.
5. **Inspection and Quality**: ASTM material test certificates (MTCs) and ASME stamp-markings enable traceability and quality assurance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Schedule Number Formula from First Principles

We start from the **thin-wall pressure vessel assumption** (valid when $t < D_o/6$, true for most process piping), where hoop stress in a cylindrical shell under internal pressure is:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot D_i}{2 \cdot t} \quad \text{(membrane stress)}$$

For design, the allowable stress $S$ must be greater than or equal to the hoop stress divided by a safety/quality factor. Including the **joint efficiency $E$** and the **corrosion allowance $c$**:

$$S \cdot E \geq \dfrac{P \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot (t - c)}$$

Solving for the wall thickness $t$ (neglecting the corrosion allowance for the schedule-number definition):

$$t = \dfrac{P \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot S \cdot E}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For seamless pipe, $E = 1.0$. Substitute this into the equation and rewrite the **Schedule Number** as the **dimensionless pressure-to-stress ratio** multiplied by 1000:

$$\text{Schedule Number} \equiv \dfrac{P}{S} \times 1000$$

This is the **historical BWG/ASA definition** (1939). The 1000 factor makes typical pressure-stress ratios (which are small, of order 10⁻³) come out as convenient integers (40, 80, 160).

**Step 1**: Define the pressure–stress ratio:
$$\dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{\text{Schedule Number}}{1000}$$

**Step 2**: Substitute back into the thin-wall formula (with $E = 1$, $c = 0$):
$$t = \dfrac{P \cdot D_o}{2 S} = \dfrac{D_o}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{D_o}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\text{Sch}}{1000}$$

**Step 3**: Express the wall thickness in inches (using $D_o$ in inches):
$$t_{\text{inch}} = \dfrac{D_{o,\text{inch}}}{2000} \cdot \text{Sch}$$

**Step 4**: Example verification for NPS 4" Sch 40:
$$D_o = 4.500 \text{ in} \;\Rightarrow\; t = \dfrac{4.500 \times 40}{2000} = 0.0900 \text{ in}$$
This underestimates the standard Sch 40 wall (0.237 in) — the simple formula is for **BWG gauge**; the modern ASME B36.10M uses the corrected equation.

**Step 5**: Modern ASME B36.10M wall thickness equation:
$$t = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot D_o \cdot \text{Sch}}{1000 + 0.5 \cdot \text{Sch}}$$

**Step 6**: Re-verify NPS 4" Sch 40 with the modern formula:
$$t = \dfrac{0.5 \times 4.500 \times 40}{1000 + 0.5 \times 40} = \dfrac{90}{1020} = 0.0882 \text{ in (BWG basis)}$$

**Step 7**: The actual ASME B36.10M wall thickness for NPS 4" Sch 40 is **0.237 in** — this corresponds to a **Schedule Number of 80** under the BWG gauge convention. Hence, modern Sch 40 ≈ BWG Sch 80 ≈ "STD" wall. **The 1939 Schedule Number was redefined to retain familiar labels** (Sch 40, 80) while assigning them the wall thicknesses that the 1939 industry was using as "Standard" and "Extra Strong" (XS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The numerical schedule numbers today are *labels*, not strict mathematical outputs of $1000 P/S$. They map to standardized wall thicknesses published in ASME B36.10M tables.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pipe Sizing from Velocity and Flow Rate

We derive the relationship between pipe diameter, volumetric flow rate, and average velocity.

**Step 1**: Define volumetric flow rate $Q$ as the volume passing per unit time:
$$Q = \dfrac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Time}} = A \cdot v$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area and $v$ the average velocity.

**Step 2**: For a circular pipe, the area is:
$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2}$$

**Step 3**: Substitute into the continuity equation:
$$Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot v$$

**Step 4**: Solve for the diameter $D$:
$$D^{2} = \dfrac{4 \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot v}$$

$$\boxed{D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot v}}}$$

**Step 5**: For mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$, the equivalent form is:
$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot \dot{m}}{\pi \cdot \rho \cdot v}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reynolds Number for Flow Regime Identification

**Step 1**: Start with the dimensionless group defining the ratio of inertial to viscous forces:
$$Re = \dfrac{\text{Inertial forces}}{\text{Viscous forces}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot L_c}{\mu}$$

**Step 2**: For internal pipe flow, the characteristic length is the **inside diameter $D_i$**:
$$Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D_i}{\mu}$$

**Step 3**: Substitute $v = 4Q/(\pi D_i^2)$ to obtain:
$$Re = \dfrac{4 \cdot \rho \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot \mu \cdot D_i}$$

**Step 4**: For water at 20 °C ($\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s), with $Q = 50$ m³/h = 0.0139 m³/s, $D_i = 0.10$ m:
$$v = \dfrac{4 \times 0.0139}{\pi \times 0.10^{2}} = 1.77 \text{ m/s}$$
$$Re = \dfrac{998 \times 1.77 \times 0.10}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.76 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 5**: $Re > 4000$ implies **fully turbulent flow**. Friction factor $f$ (Darcy) is read from the **Moody diagram** or the **Colebrook–White equation**:
$$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)$$

For commercial steel ($\varepsilon = 0.045$ mm) at $Re = 1.76 \times 10^5$:
$$f \approx 0.0194 \; \text{(from Moody chart)}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example — Complete Pipe Sizing Calculation

**Problem**: A pipe must carry **120 m³/h of kerosene** (density 780 kg/m³, viscosity 1.92 × 10⁻³ Pa·s) over a 250 m straight run with two 90° elbows and one fully-open gate valve. Determine the **minimum NPS, schedule number, and pressure drop**.

**Step 1**: Convert flow rate:
$$Q = 120 / 3600 = 0.0333 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step 2**: Assume initial velocity $v = 1.5$ m/s (typical for kerosene):
$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.0333}{\pi \times 1.5}} = 0.168 \text{ m} = 168 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3**: Standardize to nearest NPS:
- NPS 6" (DN 150), OD 168.3 mm, ID (Sch 40) = 154.1 mm.
- NPS 8" (DN 200), OD 219.1 mm, ID (Sch 40) = 202.7 mm.

**Step 4**: Check velocity for NPS 6" Sch 40 (D_i = 0.1541 m):
$$v = \dfrac{4 \times 0.0333}{\pi \times 0.1541^{2}} = 1.785 \text{ m/s}$$
This is within the recommended 1.0 – 2.5 m/s range — **NPS 6" Sch 40 is selected**.

**Step 5**: Compute Reynolds number:
$$Re = \dfrac{780 \times 1.785 \times 0.1541}{1.92 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.124 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 6**: Friction factor (commercial steel, $\varepsilon = 0.045$ mm, $\varepsilon/D = 2.92 \times 10^{-4}$):
Using the Swamee–Jain explicit approximation of Colebrook–White:
$$f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} \right) \right]^{2}}$$

$$f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{2.92 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{(1.124 \times 10^{5})^{0.9}} \right) \right]^{2}}$$

$$f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10} \left( 7.89 \times 10^{-5} + 1.13 \times 10^{-4} \right) \right]^{2}} = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10} (1.92 \times 10^{-4}) \right]^{2}}$$

$$f = \dfrac{0.25}{(-3.717)^{2}} = \dfrac{0.25}{13.82} = 0.0181$$

**Step 7**: Equivalent length of fittings (using $L/D$ method):
- 90° elbow (standard) → $L/D \approx 30$ → 30 × 0.1541 = 4.62 m (each), total 2 elbows = 9.24 m.
- Gate valve fully open → $L/D \approx 8$ → 8 × 0.1541 = 1.23 m.
- Total equivalent length:
$$L_{eq} = 250 + 9.24 + 1.23 = 260.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8**: Pressure drop (Darcy–Weisbach):
$$\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L_{eq}}{D_i} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \cdot v^{2}}{2}$$

$$\Delta P = 0.0181 \times \dfrac{260.5}{0.1541} \times \dfrac{780 \times 1.785^{2}}{2}$$

$$\Delta P = 0.0181 \times 1690 \times 1242 = 3.80 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa} = 38.0 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 9**: Check against allowable pressure drop (typical: 30 kPa per 100 m = 75 kPa for 250 m). Our 38 kPa is acceptable.

**Step 10**: Final specification: **NPS 6" Sch 40, ASTM A106 Gr. B, SMLS, BW**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Pipe Class
